Sites must have prevailing winds blowing **away from** populated areas and an average wind speed ≥ 3 m/s to avoid stagnation.

3. **Hydrological Buffering (1 mark):** Proximity to drinking-water reservoirs must be ≥ 150 m, with the plant located **downstream** of any public water intake. This prevents contamination of potable water during a spill.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **ALARP**. Why is the concept critical in site layout decisions?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**ALARP** stands for **As Low As Reasonably Practicable** — a risk-reduction principle from UK HSE (1 mark). It is the level beyond which the cost of further risk reduction becomes *grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit gained (1 mark).

In site layout, ALARP is invoked to justify why a particular separation distance was chosen — e.g., *"We chose 200 m between the LPG sphere and admin block because further separation would require ₹ 50 crore land acquisition for marginal risk reduction from $10^{-5}$/year to $10^{-6}$/year."* (1 mark for engineering reasoning).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **factors influencing the safety-driven site selection** of a process plant. Classify them as primary and secondary. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **different types of plant layouts** and explain which type is most preferred for a **petrochemical complex** handling flammable hydrocarbons. Justify your answer using escalation-risk arguments. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Site selection is the macro-level decision that fixes the geographic coordinates of the plant. It is the *first* safety decision and constrains all subsequent design choices.

**Primary (Mandatory) Factors (4 marks):**

1. **Topography & Soil Mechanics**
   - Bearing capacity and slope stability
   - Flood plain avoidance
   - Seismic zone classification
2. **Meteorology**
   - Wind rose analysis (frequency × speed × direction)
   - Atmospheric stability class frequency
   - Temperature inversions
3. **Hydrology**
   - Distance from water bodies
   - Groundwater table depth
   - Drainage pattern
4. **Demography & Population Density**
   - Density of population within 1 km, 5 km, 10 km
   - Critical receptors (schools, hospitals)

**Secondary (Desirable) Factors (2 marks):**

1. **Economic**
   - Land cost, taxes, transport links, labour availability
2. **Logistical**
   - Port proximity, pipeline corridors, rail spurs

**Conclusion (½ mark):** A safe site minimises baseline risk; even the best layout cannot compensate for a flawed site.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Refer Section 4.3 for the four-tier architecture.

**Layout types (3 marks):**

| Layout | When Used | Safety Profile |
|---|---|---|
| Open/Outdoor | Most petrochemical units | Excellent dispersion; weather-exposed |
| Semi-open | Compressor houses | Compromise |
| Closed/Indoor | Control rooms, toxic units | Risk of confinement-induced overpressure |
| Modular | Offshore, remote sites | Compact, easy relocation |

**Preferred Layout for Flammable Petrochemical Complex — Justification (1 mark):**

The **combined (hybrid) layout** is preferred. It uses **open layout** for reactors, columns, and storage (allowing fire/blast energy to dissipate upward and outward) and **closed, blast-resistant construction** for control rooms and admin blocks. This segmentation, recommended by **API RP 752**, limits the **escalation potential** — the probability that a primary incident in Unit A propagates to Unit B.

---

### QUESTION B — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **TNT-equivalency method** for estimating blast damage radius. A storage sphere contains **2,000 kg** of methane. Compute the damage radius for **heavy structural damage** ($k = 4.6$) and **lethal range** ($k = 14.8$). Take $\eta = 0.10$, $H_c = 55,500$ kJ/kg, $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4,680$ kJ/kg. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **wind-rose method** for siting administrative and emergency facilities. Include a sketch and discuss how the prevailing wind influences placement of the flare stack. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Numerical

**Theory (2 marks):**

The TNT-equivalency method normalises the energy released by a hydrocarbon explosion to an equivalent mass of TNT. This allows the use of standardised overpressure-distance charts (Baker-Strehlow, TNO).

**Equation:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{\text{TNT}}}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{0.10 \times 2{,}000 \times 55{,}500}{4{,}680}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{11{,}100{,}000}{4{,}680} \approx 2{,}372 \text{ kg-TNT}
$$

**Cube root:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} = 2{,}372^{1/3} \approx 13.32
$$

**Damage Radius — Heavy Structural Damage ($k = 4.6$):**

$$
R_{\text{heavy}} = k \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} = 4.6 \times 13.32 \approx 61.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Damage Radius — Lethal Range ($k = 14.8$):**

$$
R_{\text{lethal}} = 14.8 \times 13.32 \approx 197.1 \text{ m}
$$

**Result statement (1 mark):** All personnel should remain beyond ~200 m of the sphere; occupied buildings must be ≥ 62 m away.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Qualitative

**Wind Rose Basics (2 marks):**
A wind rose is a polar plot showing the frequency of wind blowing FROM each compass direction, classified by speed bins. The dominant direction (longest petal) is the prevailing wind.

**Siting Rule (3 marks):**
- **Upwind (relative to prevailing wind)** — Admin block, control room, fire station, air-intake for HVAC, occupied buildings.
- **Downwind** — Flare stack, vent stack, effluent treatment plant, cooling tower drift zone, ammonia storage (heavy gas).

**Flare Stack Placement (2 marks):**
The flare must be located **downwind** of all occupied and process units. It must also be elevated so that the flame plume does not impinge on the ground or any equipment within the **radiant heat envelope** (typically 4.7 kW/m² for emergency exposure per API 521).

**Sketch (refer Section 4.2).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callouts:**
> 1. **Confusing "upwind" and "downwind":** Many students state "place admin block on the downwind side". This is **incorrect** and costs **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always include kJ/kg, m, kg-TNT. KTU scripts without units lose **½ to 1 mark** per step.
> 3. **Omitting the efficiency factor:** Forgetting $\eta$ in TNT-equivalency is the single most common error — **1 mark penalty**.
> 4. **Sketch without labels:** A diagram without axis labels or legends receives **0 of 3 marks** for the diagram component. Always label: wind direction, north arrow, zone names.
> 5. **No justification for layout choice:** Stating "open layout is best" without escalation-risk reasoning scores only **1 of 2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] Site selection is **macro-level**; layout is **micro-level** — address macro first.
- [ ] Primary site factors: topography, meteorology, hydrology, demography.
- [ ] Secondary site factors: economics, logistics, labour.
- [ ] Five layout types: Open, Semi-open, Closed, Modular, Combined.
- [ ] **Combined layout** is industry-preferred for petrochemical complexes.
- [ ] Control rooms → **upwind + up-hill + far perimeter** from process units.
- [ ] Flare stack → **downwind + elevated**, outside the 4.7 kW/m² radiant envelope.
- [ ] TNT-equivalency: $W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{\text{TNT}}}$
- [ ] Scaled distance: $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}}$
- [ ] Damage radius: $R = k \cdot W^{1/3}$; with $k = 4.6$ (heavy), $k = 14.8$ (lethal).
- [ ] Dow F&EI = MF × GPH × SPH; F&EI > 158 → "Very Severe Hazard".
- [ ] Minimum drainage slope = **1:100** for spill control (API RP 753).
- [ ] Muster points: upwind, uphill, away from all hazardous units.
- [ ] ALARP = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — UK HSE origin.
- [ ] Reference standards: API RP 752/753, NFPA 30, IEC 61511, IS 1893.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU:** **"SITE-FLOP"** →
> **S**oil • **I**nfrastructure • **T**opography • **E**nvironment • **F**lare location • **L**ayout type • **O**ffsite population • **P**revalent wind
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of Industrial Accidents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Industrial Accidents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Industrial Accident

An **Industrial Accident** is defined (per KTU 2024 syllabus, OEBBT723) as an *unplanned, uncontrolled event* in a chemical/petrochemical/process plant that results in—or has the potential to result in—harm to human life, property damage, environmental degradation, or business interruption. In **Process Safety Engineering**, an accident is the culmination of a chain of initiating events (often called the *accident pyramid* or *domino sequence*) that propagates through process equipment, instrumentation, and human systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> An industrial accident is an undesired sudden event that releases unwanted energy or hazardous material into the environment, arising from a loss of containment (LOC) or loss of process control in a chemical/physical plant operation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Domino Stack"

Imagine a row of 1000 dominoes standing in a line. Each domino represents a *safety barrier* (instrumentation, training, maintenance, relief valve, SOPs). In a well-managed plant, an **upstream disturbance** (a small leak, a stuck valve, a slip by an operator) is *caught* by the first few dominoes and the cascade stops.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Domino Analogy of Heinrich (1931) — still the cornerstone of accident causation theory taught in KTU Module 1.**
> Now imagine a plant where the *first 10 dominoes* are weak or missing. A small hiccup (a faulty weld) is no longer contained — it topples domino 11, 12, 13… and within minutes, you have a **Bhopal-scale catastrophe**. **An industrial accident is, therefore, not a single failure; it is the failure of a chain of defenses.**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

- **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** = (Number of fatalities × 10⁸) / (Total hours worked by the group)
- **Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR)** = (Recordable injuries × 200,000) / Total hours worked
- **Loss of Containment (LOC)** — the release rate `Q (kg/s)` of hazardous material
- **TNT Equivalent Mass of Explosion** `W_TNT (kg)`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benchmark constant to memorize for KTU:** **1 kg TNT ≈ 4,520 kJ of energy released**. Used universally in explosion consequence calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Industrial Accidents

Industrial accidents are broadly classified into **six canonical categories** (this exact six-fold classification appears repeatedly in KTU previous question papers):

1. **Fire** — pool fire, jet fire, flash fire
2. **Explosion** — confined vapor cloud explosion (CVCE), unconfined vapor cloud explosion (UVCE), boiling liquid expanding vapor explosion (BLEVE), dust explosion
3. **Toxic Release** — acute (Bhopal MIC) vs. chronic exposure
4. **Mechanical Failure** — vessel rupture, pipe whip, rotating equipment failure
5. **Runaway Reaction** — exothermic loss of thermal control (e.g., T2 Laboratories, 2007)
6. **Transportation Accident** — road, rail, pipeline, marine (e.g., Halifax, 1917; Lac-Mégantic, 2013)

### 2.2 The Accident Pyramid (Bird's Triangle)

A foundational KTU Module 1 concept. For every **1 fatal accident**, statistically there are:

- 30 minor injury accidents
- 300 property-damage incidents
- 3,000 near-misses (no injury, no damage, but *latent hazard* present)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Process Safety:** Near-misses are *free lessons*. The cost of fixing a near-miss is orders of magnitude lower than fixing a catastrophe. A robust Process Safety Management (PSM) system captures near-misses aggressively.

### 2.3 The Logical Causation Chain — "Why Accidents Happen"

Every industrial accident investigated by CSB (US Chemical Safety Board), HSE (UK), and FACTORIES & BOILERS (Kerala) follows the same logical structure:

- **Step 1 — Immediate Cause:** The unsafe act/condition directly triggering the event (e.g., open flange left unattended).
- **Step 2 — Basic Cause:** Personal factors (fatigue, training gap) or job factors (poor procedure).
- **Step 3 — Root Cause:** Management system failure — absence of a Permit-to-Work system, inadequate MOC (Management of Change).
- **Step 4 — Lack of Control:** The specific barrier (engineering, administrative, PPE) that should have stopped the cascade.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | TNT Equivalent Mass | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | kg | $\eta$ = yield factor (typically 0.02–0.05 for VCE) |
| 2 | TNT Reference Energy | $E_{TNT} = 4{,}520 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ | kJ/kg | Memorize for KTU numericals |
| 3 | Fatal Accident Rate | $FAR = \dfrac{N_f \times 10^{8}}{H_{total}}$ | fatalities per 10⁸ man-hours | Compare industry benchmarks |
| 4 | TRIR | $TRIR = \dfrac{N_{rec} \times 200{,}000}{H_{total}}$ | injuries per 200,000 hrs | OSHA standard |
| 5 | Discharge through hole (liquid) | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 g h}$ | m³/s | Torricelli's for steady leak |
| 6 | Discharge through hole (gas) | $Q = C_d A P \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma M}{R T} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}^{\,(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)}}$ | kg/s | Choked flow for sonic discharge |
| 7 | BLEVE Threshold | Liquid above $T \geq 0.9 \cdot T_c$ (critical) | K | Superheat limit |
| 8 | Probit for thermal radiation | $Pr = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln \left( t \cdot I^{4/3} \right)$ | dimensionless | For fatality probability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Notice that in row 1, the yield factor $\eta$ is *not* a constant. It is **empirically determined** by the TNO Multi-Energy method: typically **$\eta \approx 0.03$ for unconfined VCE** and up to **$\eta \approx 0.40$ for partially confined VCE** (e.g., Flixborough).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The TNO Multi-Energy Method and TNT-equivalent method are the **backbone of Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** in the KTU Module 3 syllabus. By converting complex flame propagation phenomena into a single equivalent mass, the engineer can predict blast overpressure, damage radius, and fatality zones — directly applicable to plant layout, occupied building rule (OBR), and emergency shutdown (ESD) design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: TNT-Equivalent Mass for a Vapor Cloud Explosion

This is the most frequently asked **Part B derivation in KTU Module 1** previous papers. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Energy released by combustion of the released hydrocarbon mass `m` (kg):**

$$E_{released} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$$

Where $\Delta H_c$ is the heat of combustion in kJ/kg. *This is the maximum theoretical chemical energy if 100 % of the vapor burns.*

**Step 2 — Apply the yield (explosibility) factor `η`:**

$$E_{effective} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c$$

$\eta$ accounts for the fact that, in a real VCE, the flame front is partially quenched, the cloud is not stoichiometric everywhere, and shock-wave energy is only a fraction of combustion energy.

**Step 3 — Equate to TNT energy release:**

$$E_{effective} = W_{TNT} \cdot E_{TNT}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for the TNT-equivalent mass:**

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$

This is the final KTU-board formula.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

> **Problem:** *A 1,500 kg cloud of cyclohexane vapor is released and undergoes a partially confined vapor cloud explosion in a process module. Using the multi-energy assumption, take $\eta = 0.10$ and $\Delta H_c = 43{,}900 \ \text{kJ/kg}$. Compute the TNT equivalent mass and the peak side-on overpressure at a distance of **200 m** using the standard KTU chart correlation.*

**Given:**
- $m = 1{,}500 \ \text{kg}$
- $\eta = 0.10$
- $\Delta H_c = 43{,}900 \ \text{kJ/kg}$
- $E_{TNT} = 4{,}520 \ \text{kJ/kg}$
- $R = 200 \ \text{m}$

**Step 1 — Compute $W_{TNT}$:**

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1{,}500 \times 43{,}900}{4{,}520}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{6{,}585{,}000}{4{,}520}$$

$$W_{TNT} = 1{,}457.0 \ \text{kg TNT}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the scaled distance `Z`** (KTU standard for blast correlation):

$$Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$$

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} = (1{,}457)^{1/3} = 11.34 \ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

$$Z = \dfrac{200}{11.34} = 17.64 \ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the KTU-board correlation (Hopkinson-Cranz / Baker):**

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{8{,}080 \left(1 + Z^{1.5}\right)}{\sqrt{1 + Z^{1.5}} \cdot \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{4.5}\right)^{2}\right]} \quad \text{(kPa — correlation, not memorised) — for KTU, use the chart}$$

Using the KTU chart, $Z = 17.64$ corresponds to $\Delta P \approx 13 \ \text{kPa}$ (≈ 1.9 psi).

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**

> At $\Delta P = 13$ kPa, **partial demolition of buildings** occurs and **injury threshold** for eardrum rupture is reached. The plant control room at 200 m is *unsafe* unless blast-rated.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Learning from the "Big Four" Catastrophes

| Accident | Year | Location | Trigger Event | Loss of Containment Mass | Key Lesson (KTU Module 1 emphasis) |
|----------|------|----------|---------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Flixborough (Nypro)** | 1974 | UK | Bypass pipe rupture (cyclohexane) — 30-inch missing section | ~40,000 kg cyclohexane | Plant modification without MOC; absence of HAZOP; **MOC is now law** |
| **Bhopal (Union Carbide)** | 1984 | India | MIC (Methyl Isocyanate) tank water ingress — runaway reaction | ~40,000 kg MIC | 8,000+ deaths; refrigeration system defunct; **Siting of hazardous plants in populated areas** |
| **Seveso (ICMESA)** | 1976 | Italy | Reactor rupture during trichlorophenol manufacture | ~2 kg TCDD (dioxin) | Birth of the **Seveso Directive** (EU 1982, revised 1996, 2012) — *Seveso III* is a KTU reference |
| **Piper Alpha** | 1988 | North Sea, UK | Condensate pump A — maintenance error + blocked ESD | Fire → explosion on platform | 167 deaths; **Permit-to-Work failure**; **SMART** (Safety Management and Reliability of Technology) |
| **BP Texas City Refinery** | 2005 | USA | Raffinate splitter — heavy liquid overfill → vapor flash | ~50,000 lb hydrocarbon vapor | 15 deaths; 180 injured; **cultural safety failure** — Baker Panel report |
| **AZF Toulouse** | 2001 | France | Off-spec ammonium nitrate in storage | ~300 tons AN | 31 deaths; **incompatibility of stored chemicals** |
| **Buncefield** | 2005 | UK | Petrol storage tank overflow — tank gauge failure | ~300,000 L petrol | Largest peacetime fire in UK; **tank overfill protection (OFP)** systems |
| **West Pharmaceutical** | 2003 | USA | Cross-contamination of polyethylene powder — dust explosion | n/a | 6 deaths; **NFPA dust hazard classification** |
| **Lac-Mégantic** | 2013 | Canada | Unattended freight train, parked on mainline | ~6 million L crude oil | 47 deaths; **railway safety & remote isolation** |
| **T2 Laboratories** | 2007 | USA | Methylcyclopentadienyl manganese tricarbonyl — runaway | n/a | 4 deaths; **calorimetry / reactive chemistry screening failure** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Repeated Trivia:** The "founding fathers" of modern process safety legislation are **Flixborough** and **Bhopal**. The **Seveso Directive**, **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)**, and **COMAH Regulations 2015** (UK) trace their lineage to these four events.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Process Safety Accident Causation Pyramid (Bird's Triangle, Mermaid-Adapted)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["FATALITIES AND MAJOR DISASTERS"] --> nodeB["30 MAJOR INJURIES"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["300 MINOR INJURIES AND PROPERTY DAMAGE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["3000 NEAR MISSES AND UNSAFE ACTS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["30000 UNSAFE CONDITIONS AND LATENT HAZARDS"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ROOT CAUSES — MANAGEMENT SYSTEM GAPS"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["DESIGN AND ENGINEERING FLAWS"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["LOSS OF CONTAINMENT EVENT"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["INDUSTRIAL ACCIDENT — Bhopal, Flixborough, Piper Alpha"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Accident Causation Logic Chain (Loss of Containment Model)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["LATENT HAZARD Inherent Process Property e.g. Flammable, Toxic, High Pressure"] --> nodeB["INITIATING EVENT Equipment Failure, Human Error, External Event"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PROPAGATING EVENT Escalation by Domino Effect"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["LOSS OF CONTAINMENT LOC Release Rate Q kg per s"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["HAZARDOUS PHENOMENA Fire, Explosion, Toxic Cloud"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["CONSEQUENCES Human, Asset, Environment, Reputation"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["BARRIERS PREVENTED  Detection, Isolation, Blowdown, MOC, Permit"]
    nodeG -.->|If barriers fail| nodeC
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Six Major Accident Phenomena (KTA / KTU Module 1 Reference Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["INDUSTRIAL ACCIDENT CLASSIFICATION"] --> nodeB["FIRE Pool Fire, Jet Fire, Flash Fire"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["EXPLOSION VCE, BLEVE, Dust, Confined"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["TOXIC RELEASE Acute, Chronic, Environmental"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["MECHANICAL FAILURE Vessel, Pipe, Pump, Compressor"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["RUNAWAY REACTION Exothermic Loss of Control"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["TRANSPORTATION Road, Rail, Sea, Pipeline"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Process Safety Management (PSM) Loop & Its Linkage to Accident Review

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["ACCIDENT OR NEAR MISS OCCURS"] --> nodeB["INCIDENT INVESTIGATION Root Cause Analysis"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["CORRECTIVE AND PREVENTIVE ACTIONS CAPA"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT 14 ELEMENTS OSHA PSM"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PROCESS HAZARD ANALYSIS PHA, HAZOP, LOPA, QRA"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["DESIGN REVIEW, MOC, PERMIT TO WORK, TRAINING"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT SAFETY CULTURE"]
    nodeG -.->|Feed-back to PSM| nodeD
    nodeG -.->|Reduces likelihood| nodeA
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define "Loss of Containment" (LOC). Why is it considered the central concept in process safety accident analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Loss of Containment (LOC) is the unintended release of a hazardous material (flammable, toxic, or reactive) from its primary containment system — typically a vessel, pipe, pump, or valve — into the surrounding atmosphere or workplace.

It is central to process safety because the **majority of all major industrial accidents** (Bhopal, Flixborough, Buncefield) are initiated by a LOC event. The release of material creates a hazardous phenomenon (fire, explosion, toxic cloud) that propagates damage. Hence, preventing LOC and mitigating its consequences is the **core engineering objective** of process safety. **(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — explanation, 1 mark — example).**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the Heinrich Bird's Triangle (accident pyramid) and explain its significance in process safety management.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Heinrich Bird's Triangle proposes that for every 1 fatal/major accident, there exist **30 minor injuries, 300 property-damage incidents, and 3,000 near-misses.** **(1 mark)**

**Significance:** It establishes the principle that **near-misses are early-warning indicators** of latent hazards and management system failures. A robust PSM system uses near-miss reporting to identify root causes *before* they escalate into catastrophes — directly reducing fatality probability. **(2 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** [7 marks] *With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the accident causation logic chain in process industries. Use the Flixborough disaster (1974) as a case study to illustrate each step.*

**(b)** [7 marks] *A leak of 800 kg of propane occurs in a partially congested area. Compute the TNT equivalent mass, given the heat of combustion is 46,000 kJ/kg and the yield factor is 0.08. Discuss the engineering implications.*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Accident Causation Chain — Flixborough (1974):**

The accident causation chain typically progresses through **latent hazard → initiating event → propagating event → LOC → hazardous phenomenon → consequences**. In Flixborough:

- **Latent hazard:** Large inventory (~240 tons) of cyclohexane in atmospheric storage, with a 20-inch bypass pipe already existing.
- **Initiating event:** Cracking of a temporary 20-inch bypass pipe (installed to bypass a faulty reactor). The bypass was *never pressure-tested* and *never subjected to MOC review*.
- **Propagating event:** Massive release of ~40,000 kg cyclohexane formed a vapor cloud that drifted across the plant.
- **LOC event:** Vapor cloud of flammable hydrocarbon at concentrations between LEL and UEL.
- **Hazardous phenomenon:** Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion (UVCE) with $\eta \approx 0.10$.
- **Consequences:** 28 fatalities, 36 injuries, total plant destruction.

**Engineering lesson:** The disaster directly led to the establishment of the **Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** in the UK and the inclusion of **MOC (Management of Change)** as a mandatory element of the OSHA PSM standard. **Diagram as in Section 4.2 above should be reproduced by the student for full marks.**

**(b) TNT-Equivalent Calculation:**

**Given:** $m = 800$ kg, $\eta = 0.08$, $\Delta H_c = 46{,}000$ kJ/kg, $E_{TNT} = 4{,}520$ kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Effective energy released:**

$$E_{eff} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c$$

$$E_{eff} = 0.08 \times 800 \times 46{,}000 = 2{,}944{,}000 \ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2 — TNT equivalent mass:**

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{E_{eff}}{E_{TNT}} = \dfrac{2{,}944{,}000}{4{,}520}$$

$$W_{TNT} \approx 651.3 \ \text{kg TNT}$$

**Engineering implications:** A 651 kg TNT-equivalent explosion in a congested area generates peak overpressure of approximately **50–80 kPa within 50 m radius**, leading to total destruction of conventional buildings and certain fatality to personnel without blast-rated shelters. This mandates:

- **Control room blast-rated design** (per ASCE/SEI 59-11).
- **Minimum separation distance** of 100+ m between LPG storage and occupied buildings.
- **Gas detection and isolation (ESD)** systems on the leaking section.
- **Vapor cloud exclusion zones** maintained by ventilation and operational discipline.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a)** [7 marks] *Explain the "Bow-Tie" concept in process safety. How does it integrate accident causation and consequence modeling in a single diagram?*

**(b)** [7 marks] *Compare the Bhopal (1984) and Piper Alpha (1988) disasters with respect to (i) initiating cause, (ii) barrier failures, and (iii) regulatory aftermath.*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) The Bow-Tie Concept:**

The Bow-Tie is a risk-assessment visualization that places a **central "Top Event"** (typically a Loss of Containment event) in the middle. **On the left** are *threats* (initiating causes — equipment failure, human error, external event). **On the right** are *consequences* (fire, explosion, toxic release, fatality). **Connecting the two sides are *preventive barriers*** (on the left — e.g., maintenance, inspection, training) **and *mitigative barriers*** (on the right — e.g., gas detection, ESD, deluge systems, evacuation).

**Why it is KTU-relevant:** It integrates accident causation (left) and consequence modeling (right) into a *single* diagram. Each barrier has a *function*, a *performance standard* (SIL), and a *degradation mode*. Loss of any one barrier shifts the top-event probability upward — directly supporting **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** in KTU Module 3.

**(b) Comparative Analysis:**

| Dimension | Bhopal (1984) | Piper Alpha (1988) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **(i) Initiating cause** | Water ingress into MIC storage tank 610 → runaway exothermic reaction | Condensate pump A's relief valve removed for maintenance; pump A started without it — the chain of events led to explosion on platform |
| **(ii) Barrier failures** | Refrigeration system defunct (cannot cool MIC); scrubber neutralization tower off-line; flare tower under maintenance; public evacuation plan missing | Permit-to-Work system failed (valve tagged-out but not physically isolated); ESD valves operable but platform already lost power; temporary refuge damaged |
| **(iii) Regulatory aftermath** | Indian Factories Act Amendment 1987; Hazardous Waste Management Rules 1989; EPA Act 1986 enforcement; **Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster Act** | Cullen Report 1990 → UK Safety Case Regulations 1992 (offshore); 167 fatalities; **Offshore Installations (Safety Case) Regulations** |

The student should present a **detailed table of comparison** and conclude with: *"Both disasters emphasize that the loss of multiple independent safety barriers is the true cause — not a single failure."* **(1 mark for conclusion synthesis).**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 (Review of Industrial Accidents):**
> 1. **Citing accidents without *dates* and *specific chemicals*** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if Bhopal is mentioned without "1984" or "MIC" (Methyl Isocyanate).
> 2. **Skipping the role of MOC** — for Flixborough, *always* explicitly state "MOC (Management of Change) was not followed."
> 3. **Wrong unit of $W_{TNT}$** — it must be in **kg TNT**, not kJ.
> 4. **Conflating $\Delta H_c$ and $\Delta H_f$** — heat of *combustion*, not *formation*.
> 5. **Forgetting the Seveso Directive year (1982) and Seveso III year (2012)** — a KTU-favourite.
> 6. **Misidentifying Piper Alpha cause** — it is *not* simply "fire on platform"; it is a *sequence of permit-to-work failures* that converted a small pump fire into a *catastrophic* explosion.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Industrial accident = uncontrolled event** causing harm to people, property, environment.
- ✅ **Six canonical accident types:** Fire, Explosion, Toxic Release, Mechanical Failure, Runaway Reaction, Transportation.
- ✅ **Bird's Triangle ratio:** 1 : 30 : 300 : 3,000 (fatality : major : minor : near-miss).
- ✅ **Memorize $E_{TNT} = 4{,}520$ kJ/kg** — appears in nearly every numerical.
- ✅ **TNT-equivalent formula:** $W_{TNT} = (\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c) / E_{TNT}$.
- ✅ **Yield factor $\eta$** for UVCE ≈ 0.02–0.05; for partially-confined VCE ≈ 0.10–0.40.
- ✅ **Flixborough (1974, UK, cyclohexane, 28 dead)** → birth of MOC and HSE.
- ✅ **Bhopal (1984, India, MIC, 8,000+ dead)** → birth of Indian EPA, PSM emphasis, siting rules.
- ✅ **Seveso (1976, Italy, dioxin/TCDD)** → **Seveso Directive 1982 → Seveso II 1996 → Seveso III 2012**.
- ✅ **Piper Alpha (1988, North Sea, condensate)** → 167 dead; **Cullen Report**; UK Safety Case Regime.
- ✅ **BP Texas City (2005, USA)** → 15 dead; **Baker Panel** report on safety culture.
- ✅ **Buncefield (2005, UK)** → tank overfill; **Overfill Protection Regulations**.
- ✅ **Bow-Tie diagram** = central top event, threats left, consequences right, barriers in between.
- ✅ **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)** has **14 elements** — listed in KTU Module 5.
- ✅ **LOC is the central event** of almost every accident chain — focus your analysis on barrier failures around LOC.
- ✅ **Permit-to-Work (PTW), Management of Change (MOC), HAZOP, LOPA, QRA** are the *recurring* KTU keywords for Module 1.
- ✅ **Always cite Seveso III Directive 2012/18/EU** (not "Seveso Directive" generically) for maximum KTU marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major Chemical Industry Accidents (Bhopal, Flixborough, Seveso)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Major Chemical Industry Accidents (Bhopal, Flixborough, Seveso)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Major Chemical Industry Accident?

A **Major Chemical Industry Accident** (often termed a **Process Safety Incident** or **Major Hazard Accident**) is defined under KTU 2024 syllabus and the **OECD Working Group on Chemical Accidents (WGCA)** as a sudden, unintended occurrence involving one or more hazardous chemicals (flammable, toxic, reactive, or explosive), resulting in serious danger to human life, health, the environment, or property, on an immediate or delayed basis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Process Safety Engineering Module 1):**
> A *major accident* is an occurrence such as a major emission, fire, or explosion resulting from uncontrolled developments and leading to serious danger to human life, health, environment, or property, whether inside or outside the establishment. The three landmark cases — **Bhopal (1984), Flixborough (1974), and Seveso (1976)** — form the empirical foundation upon which modern process safety legislation (OSHA PSM, EU Seveso Directive, Indian Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, MSIHC 1989) is built.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Modern Process Safety (the "Safety Triangle"):**
> 1. **Engineering Design Integrity** (inherently safer design)
> 2. **Operational Discipline** (procedures, training, management of change)
> 3. **Emergency Preparedness & Response** (mitigation, evacuation, medical)

---

## 1.2 Why Study These Three Accidents?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** Nearly every KTU ESE question on this topic expects students to demonstrate the **Causal Chain Analysis** — i.e., *Root Cause → Trigger → Escalation → Catastrophic Outcome → Engineering Lesson*.

| Accident | Year | Country | Primary Hazard | Direct Fatalities |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flixborough | 1974 | United Kingdom | Vapor Cloud Explosion (Cyclohexane) | 28 |
| Seveso | 1976 | Italy | Toxic Release (TCDD Dioxin) | 0 (immediate) |
| Bhopal | 1984 | India | Toxic Gas Release (MIC) | 3,800+ immediate, 15,000+ total |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Boiling Milk Pan" Analogy:**
> Imagine a kitchen with three stoves, each representing one of these accidents:
> - **Flixborough's stove** = A pressure-cooker lid held by a temporary paper clip. The clip failed → the entire contents exploded across the kitchen (mechanical/structural failure of a bypass pipe).
> - **Seveso's stove** = A chemical "soup" was overcooked past its safe temperature, producing a deadly, invisible toxin that seeped into the neighborhood (thermal runaway leading to dioxin formation).
> - **Bhopal's stove** = A sealed bottle of poisonous vapor was left unattended for weeks with multiple safety valves deliberately disabled. Water entered the bottle → massive toxic release (multiple safeguard failures + sabotage/management neglect).

All three accidents share a common theme: **multiple independent safety systems failed simultaneously because management, design, and maintenance systems were inadequate.** They are textbook examples of the **Swiss Cheese Model** of accident causation — where the holes in each defensive layer momentarily aligned, allowing a hazard to pass through.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990):** Each layer of defense (engineering, operational, emergency) has latent weaknesses ("holes"). A major accident occurs only when the holes in all layers momentarily align, permitting the hazard to reach the public.

---

## 1.4 Pre-Accident Industry Context (Setting the Stage)

Before 1974, the chemical industry largely operated under the philosophy of *"safety by engineering codes"* — i.e., compliance with codes was believed to guarantee safety. The **Flixborough disaster** shattered this assumption by revealing that:

1. Plant modifications can be made without proper engineering review.
2. Existing plants can harbor unknown structural vulnerabilities.
3. The quantity of inventory itself is the dominant variable in disaster magnitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:**
> All three accidents contributed to the recognition of **Process Safety Management (PSM)** as a distinct discipline, separate from occupational/personnel safety. The **Cumulative Inventory** of hazardous materials on-site is now treated as a **strategic design parameter**, not merely an operational detail.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Flixborough Disaster (June 1, 1974)

### 2.1.1 Plant Background

- **Company:** Nypro (UK) Ltd., Flixborough, Lincolnshire, England.
- **Process:** Production of **caprolactam** (raw material for Nylon-6) via the oxidation of **cyclohexane** in a series of six reactors in series.
- **Operating Conditions:** Cyclohexane at ~8.5 bar (≈ **8.5 × 10⁵ Pa**) and 155 °C (**428 K**).
- **Reactor Capacity:** Each reactor held ~20 tonnes of cyclohexane; total reactor section inventory ≈ **120 tonnes**.

### 2.1.2 The Failed Modification (Trigger Event)

In March 1974, a cyclohexane leak was detected in the **5th reactor** (Reactor 5). The reactor was removed for repair. To maintain production, management installed a **20-inch (≈ **0.508 m**) diameter temporary "dog-leg" bypass pipe** to connect Reactor 4 directly to Reactor 6, **bypassing Reactor 5**.

### 2.1.3 Engineering Failure Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Lesson:** The bypass pipe was **NOT** designed with adequate support. It formed a **thin-walled, unsupported 20-inch "candy cane"** — a flexible hose-like arrangement exposed to the full internal pressure of **8.5 bar** with NO expansion loop, NO adequate support, and NO proper stress analysis.

The bypass pipe failed by **buckling** — its unrestrained straight section underwent **elastic instability** under internal pressure, ruptured catastrophically, and released approximately **120 tonnes of hot cyclohexane** in ~50 seconds.

### 2.1.4 Consequences

1. A massive **vapor cloud** (≈ **40–50 tonnes** of cyclohexane vapor) formed and drifted to the plant's hydrogen compressor house.
2. The cloud ignited, producing a **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** equivalent to approximately **15–45 tonnes of TNT**.
3. **28 workers killed** on-site; **36 injured**.
4. **1,821 houses and 167 shops** damaged within a **3-mile (≈ **4.83 km**) radius**.
5. Total damage: ≈ **£100 million** (1974 GBP).

### 2.1.5 Causal Chain (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Root Cause:** Management decision to bypass Reactor 5 without proper Management of Change (MOC) review.
2. **Engineering Failure:** Bypass pipe had no proper engineering stress analysis, no support, no pressure rating certification.
3. **Process Failure:** Inventory of ~120 tonnes cyclohexane above its boiling point was the key amplification factor.
4. **Consequence Multiplier:** Plant layout — control room, occupied buildings — placed too close to process units.
5. **Missing Safeguards:** No gas detection, no isolation valves, no emergency shutdown (ESD) system.

### 2.1.6 Regulatory Outcome

- UK Government appointed **HSC (Health and Safety Commission)** to investigate.
- Resulted in the **Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974**.
- Birth of the **"Major Hazard" concept** and **CIMAH Regulations (1984)** in the UK.

---

## 2.2 The Seveso Disaster (July 10, 1976)

### 2.2.1 Plant Background

- **Company:** ICMESA (Industrie Chimiche Meda Società Azionaria), Meda, Italy (owned by Givaudan, a Swiss subsidiary of Hoffmann-La Roche).
- **Process:** Production of **2,4,5-trichlorophenol (TCP)** — an intermediate for herbicide manufacturing.
- **Reactor:** Stirred batch reactor (Reactor R1) of **10,000 L** capacity.
- **Reaction:** 1,2,4,5-tetrachlorobenzene + sodium hydroxide → sodium trichlorophenate + ... (exothermic, requires temperature control < **200 °C**).

### 2.2.2 The Failed Reaction (Trigger Event)

The batch reactor was being heated via a **steam coil**. On July 10, 1976, the reactor's **temperature control and pressure-relief system failed**. The exothermic reaction went into **thermal runaway**:
- Temperature rose from ~150 °C to over **400 °C**.
- Pressure exceeded design limits.
- The **rupture disc** burst at ~4 bar.
- A toxic plume containing **2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD)** — one of the most toxic synthetic chemicals known — was released into the atmosphere.

### 2.2.3 Quantities Released

- Approximately **2 kg of TCDD** released over the populated area.
- Contaminated area: **~18 km²**; **> 3,000 farm animals** died or had to be slaughtered.
- **~200,000 people** exposed; **~600** evacuated; **~193** cases of **chloracne** reported.
- **Zero immediate fatalities** — the population was evacuated from contaminated zones within days.

### 2.2.4 Engineering Failure Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Lesson:** A runaway exothermic reaction in a batch reactor requires a **fail-safe emergency cooling system** AND a **vent sizing adequate for the worst-case runaway scenario**. Seveso had neither in proper working order.

### 2.2.5 Causal Chain (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Root Cause:** Maintenance shutdown reduced weekend staffing — critical process control was not monitored continuously.
2. **Engineering Failure:** Rupture disc set at too high a pressure; safety valve undersized for runaway relief.
3. **Process Failure:** The exotherm was insufficiently characterized; thermal runaway was a "known unknown" in the chemistry.
4. **Consequence Multiplier:** Plant located **only ~150 m** from residential areas; no buffer zone.
5. **Missing Safeguards:** No automatic temperature cutoff, no emergency dump tank, no containment dike around the reactor.

### 2.2.6 Regulatory Outcome

- The **Seveso Directive (82/501/EEC)** of the European Community, 1982.
- Subsequently updated as **Seveso-II Directive (96/82/EC)** and **Seveso-III Directive (2012/18/EU)**.
- Established the **"Land Use Planning"** principle: hazardous installations must maintain minimum separation distances from population centers.

---

## 2.3 The Bhopal Disaster (December 2–3, 1984)

### 2.3.1 Plant Background

- **Company:** Union Carbide India Limited (UCIL), Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, India.
- **Process:** Production of **Sevin (carbaryl) pesticide** via the intermediate **Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)**.
- **MIC Storage:** Three **stainless-steel tanks** holding **~41 tonnes**, **~20 tonnes**, and **~15 tonnes** of MIC, respectively.
- **MIC Properties:** Boiling point **39.1 °C (312.25 K)**; vapor density ≈ **2× air** (vapor sinks); highly toxic — inhalation **LCt₅₀ ≈ 200 ppm·min** for humans.

### 2.3.2 The Failed Tank (Trigger Event)

On the night of December 2–3, 1984:
- Water entered **Tank E610** containing ~42 tonnes of MIC.
- The water–MIC reaction is **highly exothermic**:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2$$
- This released **CO₂ and heat**, accelerating further reactions including the runaway formation of **trimers, dimers, and cyanide-bearing compounds** including **HCN**.
- Temperature rose above **200 °C**, vapor pressure exceeded **400 kPa (4 bar)**.
- The relief valves lifted — toxic MIC vapor escaped for **~2 hours** before the residual inventory was consumed.

### 2.3.3 Scale of the Catastrophe

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bhopal is the worst industrial accident in world history.**
> - **3,800+ immediate deaths** (some estimates 8,000+); 15,000+ total deaths over the years.
> - **500,000+ people exposed** to MIC.
> - **Tons of MIC** released (estimated ~30–40 tonnes).
> - Animals died within minutes; survivors reported blindness, respiratory failure, and long-term neurological damage.
> - Toxic plume spread **up to 8 km downwind** from the plant, affecting dense slums where over **100,000 people lived**.

### 2.3.4 Engineering Failure Analysis — The "Layers of Failure"

| Layer | What Failed | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigeration | MIC tanks not refrigerated (designed at 0 °C; actual: ambient) | Cost-cutting, plant in shutdown mode |
| Vent Gas Scrubber (VGS) | Sodium hydroxide scrubber was OFF for maintenance | Maintenance schedule not protected |
| Flare Tower | Connecting pipe disconnected during maintenance | Poor maintenance coordination |
| Water Spray System | Inadequate reach; water in fact *fed* the runaway reaction | Design & management errors |
| Pressure Relief | Relief valves lifted but discharge was unscrubbed | Improper design of vent treatment |
| Isolation Valves | Slip-blind plates missing on interconnecting lines | MOC failure |
| Plant Staffing | Plant on shutdown; only 6 operators at night | Understaffing |

### 2.3.5 Causal Chain (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Root Cause:** Corporate cost-cutting led to operation with **multiple critical safety systems disabled simultaneously**.
2. **Engineering Failure:** Tank design allowed water ingress; refrigeration bypassed; scrubber not running.
3. **Process Failure:** The MIC–water reaction chemistry was not adequately modeled for runaway scenarios.
4. **Consequence Multiplier:** Plant located **adjacent to densely populated slums** with no buffer zone.
5. **Missing Safeguards:** No community alarm system, no real-time gas detection network, no emergency evacuation plan for the surrounding population.

### 2.3.6 Regulatory Outcome

- India enacted the **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC Rules), 1989**.
- Strengthened the **Factories Act, 1948** amendments.
- Globally, accelerated adoption of **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)** in the USA and **COMAH Regulations** in the UK.
- Led to the **"Responsible Care"** initiative (1985) by the global chemical industry.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Process Safety Quantification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Students:** While these accidents are case studies, the KTU 2024 syllabus expects numerical proficiency in consequence analysis. The following formulas appear regularly in ESE questions.

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TNT Equivalence: $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | $W_{TNT}$ = TNT equivalent mass [kg]; $\eta$ = empirical yield factor (typically **0.02–0.05**); $M$ = mass of released hydrocarbon [kg]; $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion [kJ/kg] (cyclohexane ≈ **46,000 kJ/kg**); $E_{TNT}$ = **4,680 kJ/kg** | Estimating VCE blast damage from a flammable release (e.g., Flixborough) |
| 2 | Pasquill–Gifford Dispersion: $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$ | $Q$ = release rate [g/s]; $u$ = wind speed [m/s]; $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = dispersion coefficients [m]; $H$ = effective release height [m] | Predicting toxic vapor ground concentration downwind (e.g., Bhopal MIC plume) |
| 3 | Probit Equation for Toxicity: $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ | $Y$ = probit; $C$ = concentration [ppm]; $t$ = exposure time [min]; $a, b, n$ = substance-specific constants (e.g., for HCN: $a=-29.42$, $b=3.008$, $n=1.0$) | Estimating fractional mortality from toxic release |
| 4 | Probability of Death: $P = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp(-u^2/2)\,du$ | $P$ = mortality fraction (0 to 1); $Y$ = probit value | Converting probit to fatality percentage |
| 5 | Heat Radiation Threshold: $q'' = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{4\pi r^2}$ | $q''$ = heat flux [kW/m²]; $\dot{Q}$ = total heat release rate [kW]; $r$ = stand-off distance [m] | Estimating thermal radiation intensity from a fireball |
| 6 | Overpressure Damage: $\dfrac{p'}{p_a} \propto \left(\dfrac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}\right)^{-n}$ | $p'$ = peak overpressure [Pa]; $p_a$ = ambient ≈ **101,325 Pa**; $r$ = distance [m]; $n$ = decay exponent (typically **0.9–1.4**) | Estimating blast damage from VCE |
| 7 | Inerting Molar Fraction: $M_{air/inert} = \dfrac{\sum x_i M_i}{\sum x_i}$ | $x_i$ = mole fraction; $M_i$ = molecular weight [g/mol] | Verifying tank inert atmosphere to prevent Bhopal-type MIC reactions |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Markdown Note:** The vertical pipe symbol has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in all formulas above to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.5 Cross-Cutting Lessons (KTU High-Yield Synthesis)

| Lesson | Flixborough | Seveso | Bhopal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventory is the dominant hazard variable** | 120 tonnes cyclohexane | 10,000 L reactor + TCDD | 41 tonnes MIC in Tank E610 |
| **Management of Change (MOC)** is critical | Bypass pipe mod without review | Rupture disc spec change | Scrubber offline, refrigeration bypass |
| **Plant siting & land-use planning** matters | Plant in rural area (lessons later) | 150 m from residential | Adjacent to dense slums |
| **Emergency isolation & relief design** must be fail-safe | No isolation valves | Rupture disc undersized | Relief vented unscrubbed MIC |
| **Operating discipline** (procedures, training) | Poor engineering judgment | Weekend understaffing | Plant in shutdown, safety systems off |
| **Community awareness & emergency response** | Minimal | 600 evacuated | No alarm; thousands died unaware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "STAMPS":** The six pillars of process safety derived from these three accidents are:
> **S** — Siting & Land-Use Planning
> **T** — Training & Operating Discipline
> **A** — Atmospheric dispersion & consequence analysis
> **M** — Management of Change (MOC)
> **P** — Pressure relief & emergency isolation
> **S** — Safeguard integrity (no simultaneous disablement)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 3 Focus:** KTU 2024 syllabus requires *quantitative analysis* of these accidents. The derivations below provide the standard board-exam numerical framework for estimating damage from VCE and toxic gas dispersion — the two primary consequence modes in Flixborough and Bhopal respectively.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: TNT Equivalence for the Flixborough Vapor Cloud Explosion

### Problem Setup

At Flixborough, **~50 tonnes** of cyclohexane vapor was involved in the explosion cloud. Estimate the **TNT equivalent**, **peak overpressure at 200 m**, and **expected damage**.

### Given Data

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 50{,}000 \text{ kg (vapor mass)} \\
\Delta H_c &= 46{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg (cyclohexane heat of combustion)} \\
E_{TNT} &= 4{,}680 \text{ kJ/kg (TNT energy of detonation)} \\
\eta &= 0.04 \text{ (typical explosion yield factor for hydrocarbon vapor clouds)} \\
r &= 200 \text{ m (distance of interest)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute the TNT Equivalent Mass

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{0.04 \times 50{,}000 \times 46{,}000}{4{,}680}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c = 0.04 \times 50{,}000 \times 46{,}000 = 9.2 \times 10^{10} \text{ kJ}$$

Dividing by $E_{TNT}$:

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{9.2 \times 10^{10}}{4{,}680} = 1.966 \times 10^{7} \text{ kg TNT}$$

$$\boxed{W_{TNT} \approx 19{,}660 \text{ tonnes of TNT equivalent}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The Flixborough explosion released energy comparable to ~**20 kilotons of TNT** — roughly the yield of the Hiroshima bomb's blast-only equivalent scaled down by a factor of 100. (This explains the catastrophic damage within a 3-mile radius.)

### Step 2 — Compute the Scaled Distance

The "scaled distance" $Z$ is the standard TNT-equivalence normalization:

$$Z = \dfrac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$$

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} = (1.966 \times 10^{7})^{1/3} = 271 \text{ m}$$

$$Z = \dfrac{200}{271} = 0.738 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Peak Overpressure Using the Brode Equation

For a surface-burst TNT explosion, the **Brode (1957)** correlation gives:

$$\dfrac{p'}{p_a} = \dfrac{0.975}{Z} + \dfrac{1.455}{Z^2} + \dfrac{5.85}{Z^3} - 0.019$$

Substituting $Z = 0.738$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{p'}{p_a} &= \dfrac{0.975}{0.738} + \dfrac{1.455}{(0.738)^2} + \dfrac{5.85}{(0.738)^3} - 0.019 \\[6pt]
&= 1.321 + 2.672 + 14.557 - 0.019 \\[6pt]
&= 18.531
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{p' = 18.531 \times 101{,}325 \approx 1.88 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 1.88 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Step 4 — Damage Classification

| Peak Overpressure (kPa) | Expected Damage |
|---|---|
| **2–7 kPa** | Window glass breakage (light damage) |
| **15–20 kPa** | Partial demolition of houses |
| **35–70 kPa** | Reinforced concrete buildings damaged |
| **> 100 kPa** | Total destruction |
| **~1,880 kPa (computed)** | **Total devastation** at 200 m |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Many students forget to use the **scaled distance** and directly substitute $r$ into Brode's equation. The scaled distance $Z = r/W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ is **mandatory**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Probit-Based Toxicity Estimate for the Bhopal MIC Release

### Problem Setup

Estimate the **fractional mortality** in the exposed population assuming **C = 30 ppm** of MIC for **t = 60 minutes** of exposure.

### Probit Constants for MIC (per AEGLs/AIHA references)

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= -7.41 \\
b &= 3.69 \\
n &= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Calculate the Probit Value

$$Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$$

$$C^n \cdot t = 30^{1.0} \times 60 = 1{,}800$$

$$\ln(1{,}800) = 7.496$$

$$Y = -7.41 + 3.69 \times 7.496 = -7.41 + 27.66 = 20.25$$

### Step 2 — Convert Probit to Probability of Death

$$P = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp(-u^2/2)\,du$$

With $Y - 5 = 20.25 - 5 = 15.25$:

Since $P(Y \ge 15.25)$ is essentially **1.0**:

$$\boxed{P \approx 1.0 \text{ (100\% mortality)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 1-hour exposure to 30 ppm MIC is essentially **always fatal**. The actual Bhopal concentrations in the densely populated slums near the plant were estimated to be in the **hundreds of ppm for tens of minutes**, fully consistent with the catastrophic mortality figures.

---

| **Country** | UK | Italy | India |

| **Release Mass** | ~50 tonnes (vapor cloud) | ~2 kg TCDD + intermediates | ~30–40 tonnes MIC |
| **Energy Equivalent** | ~20 kilotons TNT | None (toxic release) | None (toxic release) |
| **Trigger Event** | Mechanical failure of bypass pipe | Thermal runaway in batch reactor | Water ingress into MIC tank |
| **Root Cause** | No MOC; no stress analysis | Inadequate relief sizing; understaffing | Multiple safeguards disabled |
| **Primary Consequence** | Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) | Toxic plume / contamination | Toxic gas cloud |
| **Fatalities (immediate)** | 28 | 0 | 3,800+ |
| **Long-term Impact** | Plant destroyed | Chloracne, environmental contamination | 500,000+ exposed, chronic illness |
| **Key Engineering Lesson** | Bypass piping MUST be engineered to same code as original | Relief vent sizing must match runaway chemistry | Never operate with multiple safeguards disabled |
| **Regulatory Outcome** | UK HSWA 1974; HSC; CIMAH | EU Seveso Directive (1982) | Indian MSIHC Rules 1989; OSHA PSM |
| **KTU Concept Tag** | MOC; Mechanical Integrity | Thermal Runaway; Relief Design | Siting; Multi-Layer Safeguard Failure |

## 3.4 The Swiss Cheese Model Applied to Bhopal (Causal Alignment Diagram)

The classic Reason Swiss Cheese Model is illustrated with the Bhopal accident as follows — each slice represents a defensive layer, and the aligned holes allowed the accident to occur:

| Layer | Defense | Hole (Failure) at Bhopal |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inherent Safety** (low inventory, less hazardous chemistry) | 41 tonnes MIC stored in single tank; alternative non-MIC pesticides ignored |
| 2 | **Engineering Controls** (refrigeration, scrubbing) | Refrigeration OFF; caustic scrubber OFF; flare disconnected |
| 3 | **Procedural Controls** (MOC, permits) | No permit-to-work system; unauthorized maintenance |
| 4 | **Operating Discipline** (operator training, staffing) | 6 operators at night; plant in "shutdown" status |
| 5 | **Emergency Response** (gas detection, evacuation) | No public alarm; no real-time detection; dense population unaware |
| 6 | **Community / Land-Use Planning** | Plant adjacent to slums; no buffer zone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Discuss the Bhopal accident in light of Swiss Cheese Model," present it as a **6-layer Swiss Cheese model** with each failed layer enumerated. This demonstrates OBE competency in **CO-1 (Identify process hazards)** and **CO-4 (Apply safety management principles)**.

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Bank Tagging Convention:**
> - **Part A** = 3 marks each, Bloom Level L1 (Remember) / L2 (Understand)
> - **Part B** = 14 marks each, Bloom Level L3 (Apply) / L4 (Analyze) / L5 (Evaluate)
> - Sub-parts (a) and (b) each carry **7 marks**, as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern
> - **Internal Choice**: Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a "Major Hazard Installation" with reference to the Bhopal disaster. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **Major Hazard Installation (MHI)** is any industrial facility that manufactures, processes, stores, or handles one or more hazardous chemicals in quantities exceeding the threshold quantities specified under the Indian **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC), 1989**, such that a failure could result in serious injury, fatality, or major environmental damage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Standard Definition (3 marks):**
> 1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Threshold-based installation handling >X tonnes of hazardous substances.
> 2. **Reference to Bhopal (1 mark):** Union Carbide India Limited plant at Bhopal stored **41 tonnes of MIC** — vastly exceeding thresholds; its failure caused >3,800 deaths.
> 3. **Identifying characteristic (0.5 mark):** Domino effect potential; large single-failure consequence radius.

---

### Question 2: List the three primary causes of the Flixborough disaster and state one engineering lesson derived. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

**Three primary causes of Flixborough:**
1. **Mechanical failure of the temporary 20-inch bypass pipe** (no stress analysis, no proper support).
2. **Inadequate Management of Change (MOC)** procedure for the bypass installation.
3. **Large inventory of hot cyclohexane (~120 tonnes)** above its boiling point, providing the fuel for the VCE.

**Engineering lesson:** Any **temporary modification** to a pressure-containing system must be engineered to the same code and reviewed through formal MOC before commissioning. The bypass pipe should have been analyzed for stress, supports, and thermal expansion — just like the original reactor nozzles.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Answer **ONE** of the two questions (Q-A or Q-B) in full. Each carries 14 marks split as **7 + 7**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]

**(a) Discuss in detail the Bhopal disaster of December 1984, identifying the root causes, the engineering failures, and the consequence multipliers that turned a plant upset into the world's worst industrial accident.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Plant Context (1 mark):**
The Union Carbide India Limited (UCIL) plant in Bhopal produced the pesticide **Sevin (carbaryl)** via the intermediate **Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)**. The plant stored MIC in three stainless-steel tanks totaling ~**76 tonnes**, with Tank E610 containing ~**41 tonnes**.

**(ii) Trigger Event — Water Ingress (1.5 marks):**
On the night of **December 2–3, 1984**, water entered Tank E610 through a faulty or improperly bled connection. The water–MIC reaction is highly exothermic:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{Heat}$$

The heat released drove the temperature above **200 °C**, accelerating further runaway reactions producing **HCN, trimers, and dimers**.

**(iii) Multiple Engineering Failures (2 marks):**
- **Refrigeration system OFF** — designed to keep MIC at ~0 °C.
- **Vent gas scrubber (NaOH)** OFF for maintenance — designed to neutralize escaping MIC.
- **Flare tower disconnected** — designed to burn off vented vapors.
- **Pressure relief valves** lifted but discharged unscrubbed MIC directly to atmosphere.
- **No gas detection or alarm** system on the perimeter.

**(iv) Consequence Multipliers (1.5 marks):**
- Plant located **adjacent to densely populated slums** (population density >100,000 nearby).
- **No buffer zone or land-use planning** between the plant and residences.
- **No community emergency plan** — population was unaware of the gas.
- Weather conditions: cool night, low wind near ground, causing the dense MIC vapor (vapor density ~2× air) to **settle into the slums**.

**(v) Outcome and Regulatory Reform (1 mark):**
- **3,800+ immediate deaths**, **500,000+ exposed**.
- India enacted **MSIHC Rules, 1989**, amended the **Factories Act 1948**, and required buffer zones, emergency planning, and PSM in hazardous industries.

---

**(b) Explain how the Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990) applies to the Bhopal accident, identifying at least five defensive layers and the corresponding holes (failures) that aligned.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The **Swiss Cheese Model** posits that every organization has multiple defensive layers, each with latent weaknesses ("holes"). A major accident occurs only when the holes in all layers momentarily align, permitting the hazard to reach the public.

**Five Defensive Layers and Their Holes at Bhopal:**

| Layer | Defense | Hole at Bhopal |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Inherent Safety | Low-inventory storage; safer chemistry alternatives | 41 tonnes MIC stored in single atmospheric-pressure tank; non-MIC routes deprioritized |
| 2. Engineering Controls | Refrigeration, scrubbing, flare, isolation valves | Refrigeration OFF; scrubber OFF; flare disconnected; isolation valves lacked slip plates |
| 3. Procedural Controls | MOC, Permit-to-Work, Lockout/Tagout | No formal MOC; safety systems disabled without formal risk assessment |
| 4. Operating Discipline | Trained operators; adequate staffing | Only 6 operators at night; plant in "shutdown" status |
| 5. Emergency Response | Gas detection; public alarm; evacuation plan | No public alarm sounded; no real-time detection; dense slum population unaware |

**Concluding Statement (1 mark):**
At Bhopal, **all five defensive layers had aligned holes** because of corporate cost-cutting, lack of MOC discipline, and inadequate emergency planning. The accident could have been prevented at any single layer if even one safeguard had functioned. This is the most important lesson: **a robust process safety system requires redundancy at every layer, never the disabling of multiple safeguards simultaneously.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]

**(a) With reference to the Flixborough disaster of 1974, explain the engineering failure of the 20-inch bypass pipe, the consequence of the vapor cloud explosion, and the four major regulatory reforms that followed.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Plant Context (1 mark):**
The Nypro (UK) plant at Flixborough produced **caprolactam** (for Nylon-6) by oxidizing **cyclohexane** in **six reactors in series** at ~**8.5 bar** and **155 °C**.

**(ii) Bypass Pipe Failure — Engineering Analysis (2.5 marks):**
- Reactor 5 developed a leak and was removed.
- A **20-inch bypass pipe** was installed to connect Reactor 4 directly to Reactor 6.
- The pipe was a **thin-walled temporary spool**, **unrestrained**, with no expansion loop or proper support.
- It was **NOT subjected to formal stress analysis** under the plant's Management of Change procedure.
- Under internal pressure (8.5 bar) and thermal expansion loads, the pipe **buckled and ruptured**.
- Approximately **120 tonnes of hot cyclohexane** (above its boiling point of **80.7 °C**) released within ~50 seconds, forming a massive vapor cloud.

**(iii) Vapor Cloud Explosion Consequence (2 marks):**
- Approximately **40–50 tonnes of cyclohexane vapor** formed a cloud drifting toward the hydrogen compressor house.
- The cloud ignited, producing a **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** equivalent to ~**15–20 kilotons of TNT**.
- Peak overpressure at the plant center exceeded **1 MPa**, destroying all buildings within ~200 m and damaging **1,821 houses and 167 shops** within a 3-mile radius.
- **28 workers killed on-site**, **36 injured**.

**(iv) Regulatory Reforms (1.5 marks):**
1. **Health and Safety at Work etc. Act, 1974 (UK)** — established the Health and Safety Commission (HSC).
2. **Adoption of the CIMAH Regulations (1984)** — Control of Industrial Major Accident Hazards.
3. **Mandatory Management of Change (MOC)** procedures for any process modification.
4. **Concept of Major Hazard Installation** introduced — inventory as the dominant design variable.

---

**(b) The Seveso disaster involved a runaway exothermic reaction in a batch reactor producing 2,4,5-trichlorophenol. Using a simple heat balance, derive an estimate of the peak temperature that the reactor could have reached if cooling had completely failed, given the following data, and explain why this caused TCDD formation.** [7 Marks]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Students:** This is a KTU 2024 typical "applied thermodynamics + chemistry" sub-question.

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reaction enthalpy (TCP synthesis)}: &\quad \Delta H_{rxn} = -250 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\text{Total reactant moles in batch}: &\quad n = 5{,}000 \text{ mol} \\
\text{Reactor mass (including reactants)}: &\quad m = 12{,}000 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Average specific heat capacity}: &\quad C_p = 3.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \\
\text{Initial temperature}: &\quad T_i = 423 \text{ K (150 °C)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the total heat released if cooling completely fails:**

$$Q_{released} = n \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}$$

$$Q_{released} = 5{,}000 \text{ mol} \times 250 \text{ kJ/mol} = 1.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the energy balance to find the temperature rise:**

Assuming all heat is absorbed by the reactor mass:

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{Q_{released}}{m \cdot C_p}$$

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{6}}{12{,}000 \times 3.0} = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{6}}{36{,}000} = 34.7 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute peak temperature:**

$$T_{peak} = T_i + \Delta T = 423 + 34.7 \approx 458 \text{ K} \approx 185 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Why this is dangerous (TCDD formation chemistry) (3 marks):**

While 185 °C may seem modest, the **actual** temperature was much higher (>400 °C) because:

1. **Autocatalytic decomposition:** Above ~200 °C, the trichlorophenol itself begins to decompose **exothermically**, releasing additional heat (a positive feedback loop).
2. **TCDD formation pathway:** 2,4,5-TCP undergoes an intramolecular condensation reaction forming **2,3,7,8-TCDD (dioxin)** at temperatures above **~250 °C**, especially in the presence of sodium hydroxide salts acting as catalysts:

$$2 \text{ mol TCP} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{NaOH}} 1 \text{ mol TCDD} + 2 \text{ mol NaCl} + 2 \text{ mol H}_2\text{O}$$

3. **TCDD toxicity:** TCDD is one of the most toxic synthetic compounds — its **LD₅₀ in rats is ~0.02 mg/kg**, with **long-term carcinogenic, teratogenic, and endocrine-disrupting effects**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The 1976 accident demonstrated that **thermal runaway relief design must account for secondary decomposition reactions**, not just the primary reaction. The rupture disc at Seveso was sized only for the primary reaction's relief requirement and was grossly inadequate once secondary decomposition began.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "safety systems disabled" with "plant in shutdown"** — Students often describe Bhopal as "the plant was shut down, so no risk." WRONG. A shutdown plant with **disabled safeguards** is **MORE** dangerous than an operating plant with all safeguards active. Always state this distinction explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting MOC** — Many students discuss the bypass pipe at Flixborough but forget to mention **Management of Change** as the **systemic** root cause. MOC is worth at least 1.5 marks in any Flixborough question.
>
> 3. **No numerical work** — Even in a "discussion" question, KTU 2024 examiners reward **quantitative reasoning** (e.g., estimating TNT equivalence, probit, dispersion distances). Add at least one number per 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 4. **Skipping regulatory mapping** — Always map each accident to its regulatory outcome (HSWA 1974, Seveso Directive, MSIHC 1989). Examiners view this as the "applied" component.
>
> 5. **Ignoring land-use planning** — The single most overlooked element. Always mention that Bhopal and Seveso were **too close to population centers**, and that modern safety standards mandate **buffer zones** based on consequence analysis.
>
> 6. **Memorizing without causal linking** — Don't just list facts. Use the phrase *"this caused… which led to… resulting in…"* to demonstrate causal chain thinking. This earns Bloom Level 4 (Analyze) credit.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1 / Topic: Major Chemical Industry Accidents**

- ☐ **Flixborough (1974, UK):** Caprolactam plant, cyclohexane, VCE, 28 deaths. **Key lesson: Management of Change (MOC)**.
- ☐ **Seveso (1976, Italy):** 2,4,5-TCP plant, thermal runaway, TCDD release, 0 immediate deaths, 193 chloracne. **Key lesson: Relief system adequacy for runaway reactions.**
- ☐ **Bhopal (1984, India):** MIC pesticide plant, water ingress, 3,800+ deaths, 500,000+ exposed. **Key lesson: Never operate with multiple safeguards simultaneously disabled; land-use planning.**
- ☐ All three accidents share the **Swiss Cheese alignment** phenomenon.
- ☐ All three led to **major regulatory reforms**: UK HSWA 1974, EU Seveso Directive, Indian MSIHC Rules 1989.
- ☐ **Inventory is the dominant hazard variable** — design plants for minimal storage of hazardous materials.
- ☐ **TNT Equivalence formula** $W_{TNT} = \eta M \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$ — use for VCE damage estimation.
- ☐ **Pasquill–Gifford dispersion** — use for toxic gas concentration downwind.
- ☐ **Probit equation** $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ — use for fatality estimation.
- ☐ **Scaled distance** $Z = r / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ — mandatory before applying Brode blast correlation.
- ☐ **STAMPS mnemonic** for process safety pillars: **S**iting, **T**raining, **A**tmospheric dispersion, **M**OC, **P**ressure relief, **S**afeguard integrity.
- ☐ The **broader lesson** of these three accidents is the emergence of **Process Safety Management (PSM)** as a discipline distinct from occupational safety.
- ☐ **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** — KCC Trevor Kletz's principle of *Minimize, Moderate, Substitute, Simplify* arose directly from these accidents.
- ☐ Each accident is a **Causal Chain** — be able to articulate **Root Cause → Engineering Failure → Process Escalation → Consequence Multiplier → Outcome → Reform**.
- ☐ For numerical questions, the KTU 2024 ESE typically expects the **TNT equivalence** or **probit** calculation in any 14-mark question on this topic.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 Notes — Topic: Major Chemical Industry Accidents (Bhopal, Flixborough, Seveso).**
