# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **flash fire** with **pool fire** because both involve liquids. The board-distinguishing answer is: **flash fire = pre-mixed vapor cloud burning transiently**; **pool fire = sustained diffusion flame above a liquid pool**. Marks lost here are common.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a pool fire and a flash fire with two key points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Pool Fire | Flash Fire |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel state during combustion** | Liquid pool, vapor burns above it | Pre-mixed vapor–air cloud |
| **Mixing mechanism** | Buoyancy-induced (natural) | Pre-mixed by atmospheric dispersion |
| **Duration** | Long (until fuel exhausted) | Short (seconds) |
| **Hazard type** | Thermal radiation | Flame contact + VCE precursor |

*(Any two points, 1.5 marks each = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Burgess–Hertzberg equation for pool fire burning rate and explain the physical significance of $k_{\beta}$.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-k_{\beta}D}\right)
$$

- $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$ is the **maximum (optically thin) burning rate** of the fuel.
- $k_{\beta}$ is the **flame optical extinction / absorption coefficient** representing how effectively the flame re-radiates heat back to the pool surface to drive evaporation.
- Physically, $k_{\beta}$ characterizes the **thermal feedback strength** — a higher $k_{\beta}$ means faster burning rate saturation with pool diameter.

*(Equation 1.5 marks + explanation 1.5 marks = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Part B carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **classification of process fires** into pool, jet, and flash fires. Compare their damage mechanisms.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A circular pool of gasoline (octane equivalent) of diameter **$D = 8 \text{ m}$** catches fire. Given $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.055 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$, $k_{\beta} = 1.1 \text{ m}^{-1}$, $\Delta H_c = 44{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$, and combustion efficiency $\eta_c = 0.85$. Calculate: (i) Mass burning rate per unit area, (ii) Total heat release rate in MW.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

A labeled sketch (recreate in exam):

```
      FLAME (H)
        ▓▓▓▓▓
       ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓
      ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓
     ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓
    ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓
   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━   ← Pool surface (D)
    ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░    ← Liquid fuel
```

**Classification:**

1. **Pool Fire** — buoyancy-driven diffusion flame above a liquid pool; heat transfer primarily by radiation.
2. **Jet Fire** — momentum-dominated turbulent diffusion flame from a pressurized release; damage by impingement and high local heat flux.
3. **Flash Fire** — short-duration propagating flame through a pre-mixed vapor cloud; damage by direct flame contact over the cloud envelope.

**Damage Mechanism Comparison:**

- Pool fire → **thermal radiation** damage to distant equipment.
- Jet fire → **direct convective + radiative impingement** damage locally.
- Flash fire → **burn injury + asphyxiation** within cloud; escalates to VCE.

*(Sketch 2 marks + classification 3 marks + damage comparison 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**(i) Mass burning rate:**

Apply Burgess–Hertzberg:

$$
\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-k_{\beta}D}\right)
$$

$$
k_{\beta} D = (1.1)(8) = 8.8
$$

$$
e^{-8.8} = 1.51 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\dot{m}'' = 0.055(1 - 0.000151) = 0.05499 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}
$$

**(ii) Heat release rate:**

$$
A_{p} = \frac{\pi (8)^2}{4} = 50.27 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}'' A_{p} \Delta H_c \eta_c
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = (0.05499)(50.27)(44000)(0.85)
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
(0.05499)(50.27) = 2.764 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
(2.764)(44000) = 121{,}616 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
(121{,}616)(0.85) = 103{,}374 \text{ kW} \approx 103.4 \text{ MW}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\dot{m}'' = 0.05499 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$; $\dot{Q} = 103.4 \text{ MW}$

*(Formula 1 mark + step (i) 2 marks + step (ii) 3 marks + final answer with units 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a **jet fire**. Derive the **sonic (choked) mass discharge rate equation** for a gas leaking from a pressurized vessel through an orifice. State clearly the condition under which choking occurs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Methane leaks from a 3-cm diameter orifice at $P_0 = 8 \text{ bar abs}$, $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$. Given $\gamma = 1.31$, $M = 16 \text{ kg/kmol}$, $C_d = 0.85$, and $R = 8314 \text{ J/(kmol·K)}$:
(i) Verify whether the flow is choked.
(ii) Calculate the mass discharge rate.
(iii) Estimate the jet flame length using Chamberlain's correlation, assuming $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Definition:** A jet fire is a turbulent diffusion flame produced by the continuous combustion of a fuel released with significant momentum from a pressurized source.

**Derivation of Choked Mass Flow:**

Starting from **steady, isentropic flow** of an ideal gas through an orifice, the mass flow rate is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho\, u\, A
$$

Applying Bernoulli's isentropic relation and the continuity equation, one obtains:

$$
\dot{m} = A\, P_{0}\,\sqrt{\frac{M}{\gamma R T_{0}}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
$$

Multiplied by discharge coefficient:

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{g} = C_{d}\, A\, P_{0}\,\sqrt{\frac{M}{\gamma R T_{0}}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}}
$$

**Choking Condition:** Flow is choked when downstream pressure $P_{\text{atm}} \le P_c$, where:

$$
P_{c} = P_{0}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}
$$

*(Definition 2 marks + derivation 3 marks + choking condition 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**(i) Verify Choking:**

Critical pressure ratio:

$$
\frac{P_{c}}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}} = \left(\frac{2}{2.31}\right)^{4.226} = (0.866)^{4.226} = 0.546
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_c = 0.546 \times 8 = 4.37 \text{ bar abs}
$$

Since $P_{\text{atm}} = 1 \text{ bar} < 4.37 \text{ bar}$, **flow is choked**. ✓

**(ii) Mass Discharge Rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_g = C_d A P_0 \sqrt{\frac{M}{\gamma R T_0}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
$$

Compute:

$$
\left(\frac{2}{2.31}\right)^{7.452} = (0.866)^{7.452} = 0.535
$$

$$
\frac{M}{\gamma R T_0} = \frac{16}{(1.31)(8314)(300)} = \frac{16}{3.27 \times 10^{6}} = 4.89 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
(4.89 \times 10^{-6})(0.535) = 2.62 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2.62 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.62 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Orifice area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi(0.03)^2}{4} = 7.07 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{m}_g = (0.85)(7.07 \times 10^{-4})(8 \times 10^{5})(1.62 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
= (0.85)(7.07 \times 10^{-4})(1296) = 0.779 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(iii) Jet Flame Length:**

$$
L = 8.5 D_o \left(\frac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_a \sqrt{2gD_o^5}}\right)^{0.347}
$$

$$
D_o = 0.03 \text{ m}, \quad D_o^5 = 2.43 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^5
$$

$$
\sqrt{2gD_o^5} = \sqrt{(19.62)(2.43 \times 10^{-8})} = \sqrt{4.77 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.91 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\rho_a \sqrt{2gD_o^5} = (1.2)(6.91 \times 10^{-4}) = 8.29 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_a\sqrt{2gD_o^5}} = \frac{0.779}{8.29 \times 10^{-4}} = 940
$$

$$
(940)^{0.347} = e^{0.347 \ln(940)} = e^{0.347(6.845)} = e^{2.375} = 10.75
$$

$$
L = (8.5)(0.03)(10.75) = 2.74 \text{ m}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Choked ✓ ; $\dot{m}_g = 0.779 \text{ kg/s}$ ; $L = 2.74 \text{ m}$

*(Choking verification 2 marks + mass flow 3 marks + flame length 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

- [ ] **Pool fire** = buoyancy-driven, sustained, **radiation-dominated** hazard.
- [ ] **Jet fire** = momentum-driven, sustained, **impingement-dominated** hazard.
- [ ] **Flash fire** = pre-mixed, **transient**, cloud-bounded; **VCE precursor**.
- [ ] Burgess–Hertzberg: $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty}(1 - e^{-k_\beta D})$; large pools → optically thin limit.
- [ ] Thomas flame height: $H/D = 42(\dot{m}''/\rho_a\sqrt{gD})^{0.61}$.
- [ ] Heat release rate: $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}'' A_p \Delta H_c \eta_c$ — pool fire cornerstone.
- [ ] Sonic discharge: $\dot{m}_g = C_d A P_0\sqrt{\frac{M}{\gamma R T_0}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$ — applies when $P_{\text{atm}} < P_c$.
- [ ] Critical pressure ratio: $P_c/P_0 = (2/(\gamma+1))^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$.
- [ ] Chamberlain jet flame length: $L = 8.5 D_o (\dot{m}_g/\rho_a\sqrt{2gD_o^5})^{0.347}$.
- [ ] Flash fire mass: $M_f = (\text{LFL}/100)(M_w/V_m)V_{\text{cloud}}$ — use dispersion model for $V_{\text{cloud}}$.
- [ ] **Standard constants to memorize:** $k_\beta = 1.1 \text{ m}^{-1}$, $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $R = 8314 \text{ J/(kmol·K)}$, $C_d = 0.85$.
- [ ] **Damage hierarchy to remember:** Pool fire = *area* damage; Jet fire = *localized* damage; Flash fire = *people inside cloud + domino risk*.
- [ ] **Domino scenario:** Flash fire → ignites pool → sustained pool fire → escalation to BLEVE/VCE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosion - UVCE, BLEVE, Dust Explosion, Deflagration, Detonation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Explosion - UVCE, BLEVE, Dust Explosion, Deflagration, Detonation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What is an Explosion?

An **explosion** is a rapid, violent release of energy accompanied by a sudden generation of high-temperature gases and a shock wave (overpressure front) that propagates into the surrounding environment. In process safety engineering, explosions are classified primarily by the **physical state of the fuel**, the **confinement conditions**, and the **speed of the combustion front** relative to the speed of sound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Process Safety Engineering):**
> An explosion is a sudden, almost instantaneous release of energy that produces a shock wave (or blast wave) in the surrounding medium, accompanied by a loud noise, generation of high-temperature gases, and a rapid rise in pressure.

The energy released during an explosion can manifest in three forms:
1. **Pressure Energy (Blast Wave)** — the most destructive form
2. **Thermal Energy (Fireball / Heat Radiation)**
3. **Fragment Energy (Missile Projection)**

---

### 1.2 The Five Major Explosion Phenomena Covered in KTU Module 2

#### 🔹 A. Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion (UVCE)

**Formal Definition:**
A **UVCE** occurs when a large quantity of flammable vapor (or a rapidly vaporizing flammable liquid) escapes into the open atmosphere, disperses, and forms a flammable cloud. Upon encountering an ignition source, the cloud burns, producing a **transient overpressure** that propagates as a blast wave.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Gas Leak & Spark" Scenario:**
> Imagine a giant gasoline tanker rupturing on a highway. The liquid gasoline instantly vaporizes into a huge flammable mist cloud drifting toward a parked truck. When the mist contacts the truck's hot exhaust, the *entire cloud* ignites almost simultaneously. The result is a massive fireball and a pressure wave that can flatten nearby buildings — that is a UVCE. Classic real-world examples: **Flixborough Disaster (1974, UK)** and **Skikda, Algeria (2004)**.

**Key Numerical Constants:**
- **Typical Cloud Size for Damage:** Mass of fuel in the cloud is usually **≥ 10,000 kg** to produce damaging overpressure.
- **Typical Flame Speed in UVCE:** **100 – 400 m/s** (faster than deflagration due to turbulence).
- **Reference TNT Energy Density:** $E_{TNT} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

---

#### 🔹 B. Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)

**Formal Definition:**
A **BLEVE** is a catastrophic failure of a pressurized vessel containing a liquid stored at a temperature **above its atmospheric boiling point**. When the vessel ruptures, the sudden drop in pressure causes the superheated liquid to flash into vapor almost instantaneously, producing a massive pressure wave and a large fireball (if the liquid is flammable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Shaken Soda Can on Fire" Scenario:**
> Picture a propane tank near a house fire. The fire heats the tank, and the liquid propane inside is now far above its normal boiling point (–42 °C) but held liquid by the tank's pressure. When the metal weakens and ruptures, the pressure drops to atmospheric in a millisecond. The propane liquid instantly boils — billions of bubbles form at once — and the resulting vapor explosion tears the tank apart into shrapnel while launching a giant fireball. This is a BLEVE.

**Key Numerical Constants:**
- **Critical Condition:** Vessel failure occurs when the **vapor pressure exceeds the vessel's relief set pressure** combined with weakened metal at high temperature.
- **Critical Temperature Range:** Liquid temperature must be **above the superheat limit temperature (SIT)**, often approximated as **0.89 × T_critical** (where $T_c$ is the critical temperature of the substance).
- **Fireball Duration (typical):** **10 – 30 seconds**.
- **Fireball Diameter:** Approximately **5 – 10 × vessel diameter**.

---

#### 🔹 C. Dust Explosion

**Formal Definition:**
A **dust explosion** is the rapid combustion of fine solid particles suspended in air (or another oxidizing gas) within a confined or semi-confined space, producing a damaging pressure rise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Flour Mill Catastrophe" Scenario:**
> Imagine a grain silo. Finely powdered wheat dust is suspended in the air inside. A spark from a faulty motor ignites a tiny portion. The flame front sweeps through the dust cloud, each tiny particle burning like miniature gunpowder. The pressure rises so fast that the silo explodes like a bomb. Famous historical example: the **2008 Imperial Sugar Refinery explosion, Georgia, USA**.

**Key Numerical Constants:**
- **Minimum Explosible Concentration (MEC):** Typically **30 – 500 g/m³** (depends on dust type).
- **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE):** Often **1 – 1000 mJ** (much lower than gases).
- **Particle Size Threshold:** Particles **< 500 μm** are typically explosible.
- **Kst Value:** Deflagration index, ranges **Kst < 200 bar·m/s (St-1)**, **200–300 (St-2)**, **> 300 (St-3)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Dust Explosion Pentagon:**
> Unlike gas explosions (which need a fire triangle), dust explosions require **five elements**: **Fuel (dust) + Oxygen + Confinement + Dispersion + Ignition Source**.

---

#### 🔹 D. Deflagration

**Formal Definition:**
**Deflagration** is a mode of combustion in which the **flame front propagates by thermal conduction and diffusion of radicals** through the unreacted medium at a velocity **less than the speed of sound** in that medium. The combustion zone moves by subsonic heat transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Bunsen Burner" Scenario:**
> When you light a Bunsen burner, the flame travels *upward* into the gas jet at a few meters per second — much slower than the speed of sound (340 m/s). The flame is being pushed forward by hot combustion products heating up the unburned gas ahead. This slow, push-driven burning is **deflagration**.

**Key Numerical Constants:**
- **Deflagration Flame Speed:** **1 – 1000 m/s** (typically much less than Mach 1).
- **Pressure Rise:** Moderate, **up to ~8 bar** in confined systems.
- **Pressure Wave Velocity:** Subsonic.

---

#### 🔹 E. Detonation

**Formal Definition:**
**Detonation** is a mode of combustion in which the **flame front is coupled with a shock wave**, propagating at a velocity **greater than the speed of sound** in the unreacted medium. The shock compresses and heats the unreacted material, auto-igniting it instantaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Knock in a Car Engine" Scenario:**
> In a faulty car engine, the air-fuel mixture can sometimes ignite in an uncontrolled, near-instantaneous shock-driven manner rather than a controlled flame-front push. This is called "engine knock." The resulting combustion front moves at supersonic speed, sending a violent shock wave through the cylinder. That is a **detonation** in miniature.

**Key Numerical Constants:**
- **Detonation Velocity:** **1500 – 9000 m/s** in gases (typically 5–10× sound speed).
- **Pressure Rise:** Extreme, can exceed **20 bar**.
- **Characteristic Mach Number:** $\text{Mach} > 1$ relative to unburned gas.

---

### 1.3 Comparative Summary Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Snapshot — Master Comparison for KTU Board Exam:**

| Phenomenon | Fuel State | Confinement | Flame/Velocity | Damage Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| UVCE | Flammable vapor/air | Open/Partial | 100–400 m/s | Blast wave + Fireball |
| BLEVE | Superheated liquid | Vessel rupture | Instantaneous | Pressure wave + Fragment + Fireball |
| Dust Explosion | Solid powder suspended | Confined/Partial | 1–500 m/s | Pressure rise + Flame |
| Deflagration | Gas/Vapor/Mist | Any | Subsonic | Pressure wave |
| Detonation | Gas/Vapor | Confined | Supersonic | Violent shock wave |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Explosion Phenomena

#### 2.1.1 The Physics of a Blast Wave

When an explosion occurs in open air, the rapidly expanding combustion products compress the surrounding atmosphere, creating a **blast wave** characterized by:

1. **Overpressure ($\Delta P$)** — the pressure rise above ambient atmospheric pressure.
2. **Dynamic Pressure ($q$)** — the kinetic energy per unit volume of the moving air behind the shock front.
3. **Impulse ($i$)** — the time-integrated overpressure (important for structural damage).
4. **Duration of Positive Phase ($t^+$)** — time during which pressure exceeds ambient.

The blast wave decays as it travels outward because the energy is distributed over an ever-growing spherical surface (or hemispherical for ground bursts).

#### 2.1.2 Why UVCE Produces Severe Overpressure

In a UVCE, the combustion of the vapor cloud is **not instantaneous**. As the flame front propagates, it generates turbulence that progressively accelerates the flame. This **flame-induced turbulence** is the key feature that distinguishes UVCE from a simple pool fire:

- Initial flame speed is low (a few m/s).
- Turbulence generated by obstacles and the rising combustion products accelerates the flame.
- The faster flame compresses unburned gas ahead, increasing the local flame speed further — a positive feedback loop.
- The result is a "piston-like" expansion that drives a strong blast wave.

#### 2.1.3 Why BLEVE is so Destructive

BLEVE combines **three damage mechanisms simultaneously**:
1. **Pressure Pulse** — from the rapid phase change.
2. **Missile Projection** — vessel fragments propelled at high velocity.
3. **Fireball Thermal Radiation** — if the liquid is flammable (e.g., LPG).

The superheat limit theory explains the BLEVE phenomenon:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Superheat Limit Temperature (SIT):**
> For a liquid under pressure, when the vessel fails and pressure drops suddenly, the liquid is at a temperature higher than the new saturation temperature. The liquid is said to be **superheated**. The maximum temperature to which a liquid can be superheated without spontaneous nucleation is the **Superheat Limit Temperature (SIT)**. Above this, violent bulk boiling occurs. This bulk boiling is the source of the explosive vapor generation in BLEVE.

#### 2.1.4 Why Dust Explosions are Uniquely Dangerous

Dust explosions are governed by the **Dust Explosion Pentagon** and exhibit a critical phenomenon called **secondary explosion**:
- A small primary explosion inside equipment (e.g., a mill) disturbs settled dust on rafters, floors, and pipes.
- This creates a secondary, much larger dust cloud.
- The primary explosion's flame ignites the secondary cloud, producing a catastrophic secondary explosion — often the main cause of structural collapse and fatalities.

#### 2.1.5 Deflagration vs Detonation — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism Difference — KTU Board Favorite:**
>
> - **Deflagration**: Heat transfer drives the reaction front. The unburned gas is heated by **conduction and diffusion** from the burned gas. Subsonic.
> - **Detonation**: A shock wave drives the reaction front. The unburned gas is **adiabatically compressed and auto-ignited** by the leading shock. Supersonic.

This distinction has massive practical implications:

| Property | Deflagration | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Heat conduction | Shock compression |
| Speed | Subsonic | Supersonic |
| Pressure | 1.1 – 10 bar | 20 – 100 bar |
| Energy density | Distributed | Highly concentrated |
| Damage | Pushing/displacement | Crushing/shattering |

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — All Critical Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the single most important block for KTU exam preparation.** Memorize every formula in this table.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TNT Equivalent Mass | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | kg | Standard blast modeling |
| 2 | Energy of TNT | $E_{TNT} = 4680$ | kJ/kg | Reference constant |
| 3 | Yield Factor (η) | $\eta = 0.01 - 0.10$ (typ.) | — | Empirical, depends on confinement |
| 4 | Heat of Combustion | $\Delta H_c$ | kJ/kg | From material data tables |
| 5 | Scaled Distance (Hopkinson) | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | m/kg$^{1/3}$ | Used in blast scaling |
| 6 | Scaled Overpressure | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{P_0}{P_a}$ | dimensionless | Use with Sachs/Brode curves |
| 7 | Sachs Scaled Overpressure | $\bar{P}_s = \dfrac{0.34}{\bar{R}^{3/4}}$ | — | Valid for $0.3 < \bar{R} < 1$ (approx.) |
| 8 | Incident Overpressure | $P_0 = P_a \cdot \left(\dfrac{0.34}{\bar{R}^{3/4}}\right)$ | Pa | Free-field blast |
| 9 | Reflected Overpressure | $P_r = 2P_0 \cdot \dfrac{8P_0 + 4P_a}{P_0 + 7P_a}$ | Pa | When blast hits a surface |
| 10 | Dynamic Pressure | $q = \dfrac{5P_0^2}{2(P_0 + 7P_a)}$ | Pa | Wind/gust loading |
| 11 | BLEVE Fireball Diameter | $D = 5.8 \cdot m^{1/3}$ | m | Empirical, m = mass in kg |
| 12 | BLEVE Fireball Duration | $t = 0.45 \cdot m^{1/3}$ | s | Empirical |
| 13 | BLEVE Thermal Dose | $Q = \text{SEP} \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | kJ/m² | For radiation injury |
| 14 | Superheat Limit Temp (approx.) | $T_{SIT} \approx 0.89 \cdot T_c$ | K | $T_c$ = critical temperature |
| 15 | Dust Explosion Pressure Ratio | $K_{st} = \left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ | bar·m/s | Deflagration index |
| 16 | Cube-Root Law | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$ | — | Geometric scaling |
| 17 | Mach Number (Detonation) | $M = \dfrac{v_{det}}{c}$ | dimensionless | $c$ = local sound speed |
| 18 | Chapman-Jouguet Velocity | $v_{CJ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma} R T_c}$ | m/s | Idealized detonation velocity |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** In the formula table, the **pipe symbol `|`** is forbidden inside markdown tables. For absolute value expressions (e.g., $\vert P_0 - P_a \vert$), always use `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the table structure.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Phenomenon | Industry Application / Hazard Control |
|---|---|
| UVCE | Storage tank farm layout design, vapor cloud exclusion zones |
| BLEVE | LPG transport safety, fire-water cooling deluge systems |
| Dust Explosion | Grain handling, pharmaceutical, coal mining, NFPA 652 compliance |
| Deflagration | Vent panel sizing for dust collectors, flameless venting |
| Detonation | Explosion suppression systems, detonation arrestors in pipelines |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: TNT Equivalent Mass for UVCE

**Problem Statement:** A process plant releases **20,000 kg** of propane into the atmosphere, forming a vapor cloud that ignites. Calculate the **TNT equivalent** of the explosion.

**Given:**
- Mass of fuel in cloud: $m = 20{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- Heat of combustion of propane: $\Delta H_c = 46{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Yield factor: $\eta = 0.05$ (typical for open-air vapor cloud)
- TNT reference energy: $E_{TNT} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Solution:**

Step 1: Write the governing formula.

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$

Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{0.05 \times 20{,}000 \times 46{,}000}{4680}$$

Step 3: Multiply numerator step by step.

$$\eta \cdot m = 0.05 \times 20{,}000 = 1000 \text{ kg}$$

$$(\eta \cdot m) \cdot \Delta H_c = 1000 \times 46{,}000 = 4.6 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ}$$

Step 4: Divide by $E_{TNT}$.

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{4.6 \times 10^7}{4680}$$

$$W_{TNT} = 9830.6 \text{ kg TNT equivalent}$$

Step 5: Express the final answer with appropriate precision.

$$\boxed{W_{TNT} \approx 9830 \text{ kg of TNT} \approx 9.83 \text{ tonnes TNT}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
An explosion equivalent to nearly 10 tonnes of TNT produces severe damage at hundreds of meters radius. The yield factor ($\eta$) of 5% indicates that only a fraction of the chemical energy converts to blast — most of the energy escapes as thermal radiation and unburned fuel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Blast Overpressure at a Given Distance

**Problem Statement:** Using the TNT equivalent from Derivation 1, calculate the **incident (side-on) overpressure** at a distance of **200 m** from the explosion epicenter.

**Given:**
- $W_{TNT} = 9830 \text{ kg}$
- $R = 200 \text{ m}$
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_a = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the **Hopkinson Scaled Distance**.

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$$

Step 2: Compute $W_{TNT}^{1/3}$.

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} = (9830)^{1/3}$$

$$(9830)^{1/3} = 21.4 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} \text{ (approx.)}$$

Step 3: Substitute into the scaled distance formula.

$$\bar{R} = \frac{200}{21.4} = 9.35 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

Step 4: Apply the Sachs empirical equation for scaled overpressure.

$$\bar{P}_s = \frac{0.34}{\bar{R}^{3/4}}$$

Step 5: Compute $\bar{R}^{3/4}$.

$$\bar{R}^{3/4} = (9.35)^{0.75} = 5.41$$

Step 6: Compute the scaled overpressure.

$$\bar{P}_s = \frac{0.34}{5.41} = 0.0629$$

Step 7: Convert to incident overpressure.

$$P_0 = P_a \cdot \bar{P}_s = 101{,}325 \times 0.0629$$

$$P_0 = 6372 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.4 \text{ kPa}$$

Step 8: Present the result clearly.

$$\boxed{P_0 \approx 6.4 \text{ kPa (gauge overpressure)}}$$

**Damage Interpretation:**
At ~6 kPa overpressure, **windows shatter** and **light structures sustain damage**. People at this distance may suffer eardrum rupture.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reflected Overpressure on a Building Wall

**Problem Statement:** A control building is located **150 m** from the explosion. Calculate the **reflected overpressure** it would experience if the blast wave hits the wall head-on.

**Given:**
- $W_{TNT} = 9830 \text{ kg}$
- $R = 150 \text{ m}$
- $P_a = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the scaled distance.

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{150}{21.4} = 7.01 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

Step 2: Compute the scaled overpressure.

$$\bar{P}_s = \frac{0.34}{(7.01)^{0.75}} = \frac{0.34}{4.40} = 0.0773$$

Step 3: Compute the incident overpressure.

$$P_0 = 101{,}325 \times 0.0773 = 7831 \text{ Pa} \approx 7.8 \text{ kPa}$$

Step 4: Apply the reflected overpressure formula.

$$P_r = 2P_0 \cdot \frac{8P_0 + 4P_a}{P_0 + 7P_a}$$

Step 5: Substitute (use Pa for consistency).

$$P_r = 2 \times 7831 \times \frac{8 \times 7831 + 4 \times 101{,}325}{7831 + 7 \times 101{,}325}$$

Step 6: Evaluate the numerator.

$$8 \times 7831 = 62{,}648$$

$$62{,}648 + 4 \times 101{,}325 = 62{,}648 + 405{,}300 = 467{,}948$$

Step 7: Evaluate the denominator.

$$7 \times 101{,}325 = 709{,}275$$

$$709{,}275 + 7831 = 717{,}106$$

Step 8: Compute the full ratio.

$$\frac{467{,}948}{717{,}106} = 0.6526$$

Step 9: Final reflected overpressure.

$$P_r = 2 \times 7831 \times 0.6526 = 10{,}222 \text{ Pa} \approx 10.2 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_r \approx 10.2 \text{ kPa (reflected overpressure)}}$$

**Engineering Note:**
The reflected overpressure ($P_r$) is approximately **30% higher** than the side-on overpressure ($P_0$). This is why buildings near explosions experience worse damage on the side facing the blast.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: BLEVE Fireball Diameter and Duration

**Problem Statement:** A railway tanker carrying **40,000 kg of LPG** undergoes BLEVE. Estimate the fireball diameter and duration.

**Given:**
- Mass of LPG: $m = 40{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- Empirical constants: $D = 5.8 \, m^{1/3}$ (for diameter); $t = 0.45 \, m^{1/3}$ (for duration)

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $m^{1/3}$.

$$m^{1/3} = (40{,}000)^{1/3} = 34.2$$

Step 2: Compute fireball diameter.

$$D = 5.8 \times 34.2 = 198.4 \text{ m}$$

Step 3: Compute fireball duration.

$$t = 0.45 \times 34.2 = 15.4 \text{ s}$$

Step 4: Express the result.

$$\boxed{D \approx 198 \text{ m}, \quad t \approx 15.4 \text{ seconds}}$$

**Interpretation:** A fireball **~200 m in diameter** lasting **~15 seconds** is catastrophic. Anyone within 200 m receives lethal thermal radiation.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Chapman-Jouguet Detonation Velocity for Methane

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the **detonation velocity** for a stoichiometric methane-air mixture using the Chapman-Jouguet (C-J) approximation.

**Given:**
- Ratio of specific heats for combustion products: $\gamma \approx 1.30$
- Gas constant for combustion products: $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$
- Adiabatic flame temperature: $T_c \approx 2800 \text{ K}$

**Solution:**

Step 1: Write the C-J velocity formula.

$$v_{CJ} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma} R T_c}$$

Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.

$$v_{CJ} = \sqrt{\frac{1.30 + 1}{1.30} \times 287 \times 2800}$$

Step 3: Compute $\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma}$.

$$\frac{2.30}{1.30} = 1.769$$

Step 4: Multiply by $R T_c$.

$$287 \times 2800 = 803{,}600$$

$$1.769 \times 803{,}600 = 1{,}421{,}600 \text{ (J/kg)}$$

Step 5: Take the square root.

$$v_{CJ} = \sqrt{1{,}421{,}600} = 1192 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 6: Compare to the local sound speed at flame temperature.

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_c} = \sqrt{1.30 \times 287 \times 2800} = \sqrt{1{,}045{,}160} = 1022 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 7: Verify supersonic condition.

$$\text{Mach} = \frac{v_{CJ}}{c} = \frac{1192}{1022} = 1.17$$

Step 8: Present the final answer.

$$\boxed{v_{CJ} \approx 1190 \text{ m/s}, \quad \text{Mach number} \approx 1.17 \text{ (supersonic — confirms detonation)}}$$

**Interpretation:** Methane detonates at ~1190 m/s in air — about 3.5× the speed of sound at standard conditions. This explains the catastrophic damage potential of confined gas detonations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Classification of Explosions — Hierarchical Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[EXPLOSION PHENOMENA] --> B[Based on Fuel State]
    A --> C[Based on Flame Velocity]
    A --> D[Based on Confinement]
    B --> B1[Gas Vapor Explosion]
    B --> B2[Dust Explosion]
    B --> B3[Liquid Vapor Explosion BLEVE]
    B --> B4[Physical Explosion Non Chemical]
    B1 --> B11[UVCE Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion]
    B1 --> B12[Confined Gas Explosion]
    B3 --> B31[BLEVE Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion]
    C --> C1[Deflagration Subsonic]
    C --> C2[Detonation Supersonic]
    D --> D1[Unconfined Open]
    D --> D2[Partially Confined]
    D --> D3[Fully Confined]
    D1 --> D11[UVCE]
    D2 --> D21[BLEVE during rupture]
    D3 --> D31[Dust collector detonation]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: UVCE Blast Wave Formation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Liquid Fuel Spill 20000 kg] --> B[Pool Formation and Vaporization]
    B --> C[Vapor Cloud Generation]
    C --> D[Cloud Dispersion into Atmosphere]
    D --> E[Ignition Source Encounter]
    E --> F1[Flame Nucleation Slow Start]
    F1 --> G[Flame Induced Turbulence]
    G --> H[Accelerating Flame Front 100 to 400 m/s]
    H --> I[Piston Like Expansion]
    I --> J[Blast Wave Generation]
    J --> K[Overpressure Propagation Spherical]
    K --> L1[Side On Overpressure P0]
    K --> L2[Reflected Overpressure Pr]
    K --> L3[Dynamic Pressure q]
    J --> M[Fireball and Thermal Radiation]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: BLEVE Initiation Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Pressurized Vessel Contains Superheated Liquid] --> B[External Fire Exposure]
    B --> C[Vessel Wall Temperature Rises]
    C --> D[Metal Strength Loss at Elevated T]
    D --> E{Vessel Pressure Exceeds Rupture Strength}
    E -->|Yes| F[Rapid Pressure Drop to Atmospheric]
    F --> G[Liquid Now Superheated Above SIT]
    G --> H[Bulk Nucleation and Explosive Boiling]
    H --> I[Rapid Vapor Volume Expansion 200 to 500x]
    I --> J1[Pressure Wave Blast]
    I --> J2[Vessel Fragment Missiles]
    I --> J3[Fireball If Flammable Liquid]
```

### 4.4 Dust Explosion Pentagon and Secondary Explosion Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Dust Explosion Pentagon] --> A1[Fuel Combustible Dust Cloud]
    A --> A2[Oxygen in Air]
    A --> A3[Confinement Vessel Silo Duct]
    A --> A4[Dispersion Suspended Particles]
    A --> A5[Ignition Source Spark Hot Surface]
    A1 --> B[Primary Explosion Inside Equipment]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    A5 --> B
    B --> C[Pressure Wave Disturbs Settled Dust]
    C --> D[Secondary Dust Cloud Forms in Plant]
    D --> E[Primary Flame Ignites Secondary Cloud]
    E --> F[Catastrophic Secondary Explosion]
    F --> G[Major Structural Damage and Casualties]
```

### 4.5 Deflagration vs Detonation — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DEFLAGRATION_Subsonic
        D1[Unburned Gas Front] -->|Heat Conduction| D2[Reaction Zone]
        D2 -->|Subsonic Push| D3[Burned Gas]
    end
    subgraph DETONATION_Supersonic
        T1[Unreacted Gas] -->|Shock Compression| T2[Auto Ignition]
        T2 -->|Coupled Shock and Flame| T3[Violent Pressure Rise]
    end
```

### 4.6 Explosion Damage Mechanism Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Explosion Energy Release] --> R1[Pressure Energy ~50 percent]
    R --> R2[Thermal Energy ~35 percent]
    R --> R3[Fragment Energy ~15 percent]
    R1 --> S1[Lung Damage and Eardrum Rupture]
    R1 --> S2[Building Collapse and Window Breakage]
    R1 --> S3[Vessel and Equipment Rupture]
    R2 --> T1[Skin Burns and Thermal Radiation Injury]
    R2 --> T2[Secondary Fire Ignition]
    R3 --> F1[Penetration Injury by Missiles]
    F1 --> F2[Equipment and Pipe Rupture]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Answers should be precise, 3–5 sentences, with diagrams where applicable.

---

#### **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Distinguish clearly between **deflagration** and **detonation** with respect to flame propagation mechanism, velocity, and pressure rise.

**Model Answer:**

Deflagration and detonation are two distinct modes of combustion propagation.

1. **Propagation Mechanism:** In **deflagration**, the flame front advances by **heat conduction and diffusion of active species** from the burned gas into the unburned gas. In **detonation**, the flame front is **coupled with a leading shock wave** that compresses and auto-ignites the unburned gas.

2. **Velocity:** Deflagration propagates at a **subsonic velocity** (typically 1 – 1000 m/s), whereas detonation propagates at a **supersonic velocity** (1500 – 9000 m/s), exceeding the local speed of sound.

3. **Pressure Rise:** Deflagration produces a **moderate pressure rise** (typically up to ~8 bar in confined systems), while detonation produces an **extreme pressure rise** (20 – 100 bar or more), due to the shock-coupled compression.

| Parameter | Deflagration | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Heat transfer | Shock compression |
| Velocity | Subsonic | Supersonic (Mach > 1) |
| Pressure | Low to moderate | Very high |

---

#### **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Explain the **Dust Explosion Pentagon** and why it differs from the conventional fire triangle.

**Model Answer:**

The **Dust Explosion Pentagon** describes the five simultaneous conditions required for a dust explosion, extending the classical fire triangle (Fuel + Oxygen + Heat) by adding two extra elements essential to dust combustion:

1. **Fuel** — Combustible dust (e.g., grain, flour, coal, sugar, metals) suspended as fine particles.
2. **Oxygen** — From air, typically ≥ 21% by volume.
3. **Confinement** — A vessel, silo, duct, or room that allows pressure to build up.
4. **Dispersion** — Dust must be **suspended in air** at the right concentration (between MEC and upper explosive limit).
5. **Ignition Source** — Spark, hot surface, electrostatic discharge, etc.

The fire triangle omits **confinement** and **dispersion** because bulk solid fuel can burn without being suspended, and gas fires need only confinement for pressure buildup. Dust requires suspension (dispersion) for its high surface area to interact with oxygen, and confinement for pressure rise — hence the pentagon, not triangle.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and may offer internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Model solutions below mirror actual KTU valuation key patterns.

---

### ⭐ Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question A]**

**Q. (a)** Define **UVCE** (Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion). With a neat sketch, describe the **mechanism of blast wave formation** in a UVCE. Discuss the **role of flame-induced turbulence** in producing damaging overpressure. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** A process unit releases **15,000 kg** of cyclohexane ($\Delta H_c$ = 43,900 kJ/kg) into the atmosphere. Assuming a yield factor of **η = 0.04**, calculate the **TNT equivalent mass**. Also calculate the **incident overpressure** at **250 m** from the blast center. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Definition:** A **UVCE** is an explosion that occurs when a large mass of flammable vapor (or rapidly vaporizing flammable liquid) disperses into the open atmosphere and forms a flammable cloud, which upon ignition produces a transient overpressure that propagates as a blast wave.

**Mechanism of Blast Wave Formation (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Release Phase:** Flammable liquid (or vapor) escapes from process equipment.
2. **Vaporization and Dispersion:** Liquid vaporizes; vapor disperses downwind forming a large flammable cloud (often > 10,000 kg of fuel).
3. **Ignition:** Cloud encounters an ignition source (spark, hot surface, flame).
4. **Flame Initiation:** Combustion begins at the ignition point with low flame speed (~1–5 m/s).
5. **Turbulence Generation:** As the flame propagates, it encounters obstacles, shear layers, and buoyancy-induced flow, generating turbulence in the unburned gas.
6. **Flame Acceleration:** Turbulence increases flame surface area and local burning velocity, accelerating the flame to 100–400 m/s.
7. **Piston Effect:** The accelerating flame acts like a piston compressing unburned gas ahead.
8. **Blast Wave Formation:** This compression generates an outward-propagating pressure wave — the blast wave.

**Role of Flame-Induced Turbulence:**

- Turbulence **increases flame surface area** by folding and stretching the flame.
- It enhances **mass and heat transfer rates** between burned and unburned gases.
- The flame velocity $S_L$ is enhanced by a turbulence factor: $S_{turb} = S_L \cdot \alpha \cdot \text{Re}^{0.5}$ (semi-empirical).
- The positive feedback between flame acceleration and turbulence is what allows UVCE to transition from a benign fire to a damaging explosion.

> **Marks Allocation (per KTU Key):** Definition 2 M, Mechanism 3 M, Role of Turbulence 2 M.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Given:**
- $m = 15{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- $\Delta H_c = 43{,}900 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $\eta = 0.04$
- $E_{TNT} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $R = 250 \text{ m}$
- $P_a = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 — Compute TNT Equivalent Mass:**

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$

$$\eta \cdot m = 0.04 \times 15{,}000 = 600 \text{ kg}$$

$$(\eta \cdot m) \cdot \Delta H_c = 600 \times 43{,}900 = 2.634 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{2.634 \times 10^7}{4680} = 5628 \text{ kg} \approx 5.63 \text{ tonnes TNT}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Scaled Distance:**

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} = (5628)^{1/3} = 17.78 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{250}{17.78} = 14.06 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Sachs Equation:**

$$\bar{P}_s = \frac{0.34}{(14.06)^{0.75}} = \frac{0.34}{7.46} = 0.0456$$

**Step 4 — Compute Incident Overpressure:**

$$P_0 = 101{,}325 \times 0.0456 = 4620 \text{ Pa} \approx 4.6 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{W_{TNT} \approx 5628 \text{ kg TNT}; \quad P_0 \approx 4.6 \text{ kPa at 250 m}}$$

> **Marks Allocation (per KTU Key):** Formula 1 M, Substitution 1 M, Numerical Evaluation 2 M, Final Answer + Units 1 M, Overpressure formula 1 M, Final Pressure 1 M.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

#### **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question B]**

**Q. (a)** What is **BLEVE**? Explain the phenomenon with reference to the **Superheat Limit Temperature (SIT)**. Describe the **three damage mechanisms** of a BLEVE. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss **dust explosion** with the **Dust Explosion Pentagon**. Define **Kst** and explain the dust **explosion severity classes (St-1, St-2, St-3)** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Definition:** A **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion)** is the catastrophic failure of a vessel containing a liquid stored above its atmospheric boiling point. Upon rupture, the liquid flashes explosively into vapor, generating a destructive pressure wave, missile projection, and (if flammable) a fireball.

**Superheat Limit Temperature (SIT):**

The **SIT** is the maximum temperature to which a liquid can be superheated above its normal boiling point without spontaneous nucleation of bubbles. Empirically:

$$T_{SIT} \approx 0.89 \cdot T_c$$

where $T_c$ is the critical temperature. When the liquid temperature exceeds SIT and pressure is suddenly released (vessel failure), bulk nucleation occurs — billions of bubbles form simultaneously, producing explosive vapor expansion.

**Three Damage Mechanisms:**

1. **Pressure Pulse (Blast Wave):** Rapid vaporization produces a sharp pressure rise that propagates outward.
2. **Fragment Projection (Missile Hazard):** Vessel shatters; metal fragments are propelled at high velocity, causing impact damage.
3. **Fireball (if flammable):** A large, intensely hot fireball forms (D ≈ 5.8 × m$^{1/3}$) lasting up to 15–20 seconds, causing severe thermal radiation injury.

> **Marks Allocation:** Definition 2 M, SIT 2 M, Three Mechanisms 3 M.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Dust Explosion Pentagon:**
Dust combustion requires five simultaneous elements: **Fuel + Oxygen + Confinement + Dispersion + Ignition Source.** Removal of any one element prevents the explosion.

**Deflagration Index Kst:**

$$K_{st} = \left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$$

where $(dP/dt)_{max}$ is the maximum rate of pressure rise (bar/s) and $V$ is the vessel volume (m³).

**Severity Classes:**

| Class | Kst Range (bar·m/s) | Hazard Level | Example Dust |
|---|---|---|---|
| St-1 | < 200 | Weak | Cocoa, sugar, wood dust |
| St-2 | 200 – 300 | Strong | Cellulose, polyethylene |
| St-3 | > 300 | Very Strong | Aluminum, magnesium, sulfur |

> **Marks Allocation:** Pentagon 2 M, Kst definition + formula 2 M, Classes table 3 M.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Explosion Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Yield Factor (η):** Many students write $W_{TNT} = m \cdot \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$ without including η. For UVCE, this **always loses 1–2 marks** because η is essential to the calculation. Always include η ≈ 0.02–0.10 for UVCE.
>
> 2. **Unit Confusion in Kst:** Kst is in **bar·m/s**, not bar/s. Mixing units loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing Up Deflagration with Combustion:** Deflagration is *subsonic flame propagation*, not just any burning. Write "subsonic" explicitly.
>
> 4. **Skipping the SIT in BLEVE Answers:** Many students describe BLEVE as just a vessel rupture. Always mention **superheat limit temperature** and the phase-change mechanism to score full marks.
>
> 5. **Pentagon vs Triangle:** Writing "fire triangle" for dust explosion is incorrect. Always write **"dust explosion pentagon"** with all five elements listed.
>
> 6. **No Diagram in UVCE:** For 7-mark questions, a labeled diagram of UVCE blast wave formation is **mandatory**. Missing diagram = lose 2 marks.
>
> 7. **Cube-Root Law Application:** When comparing two explosions, students often forget to take the cube root. The correct ratio is $R_1/R_2 = (W_1/W_2)^{1/3}$, not $(W_1/W_2)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Explosion** = rapid energy release + blast wave + overpressure + heat + fragments.
- ☐ **UVCE** = open-air flammable vapor cloud + ignition → blast wave; uses TNT equivalence with η ≈ 0.02–0.10.
- ☐ **TNT Equivalent Formula:** $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$, with $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg.
- ☐ **Hopkinson Scaled Distance:** $\bar{R} = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$.
- ☐ **Sachs Overpressure:** $\bar{P} = 0.34 / \bar{R}^{3/4}$; valid in mid-range.
- ☐ **Reflected Overpressure** is ~1.5–8× the incident overpressure depending on Mach stem formation.
- ☐ **BLEVE** = vessel rupture + superheated liquid + explosive vaporization; $T_{SIT} \approx 0.89 T_c$.
- ☐ **BLEVE Fireball Diameter:** $D = 5.8 \cdot m^{1/3}$ (m), Duration: $t = 0.45 \cdot m^{1/3}$ (s).
- ☐ **Dust Explosion Pentagon:** Fuel + Oxygen + Confinement + Dispersion + Ignition (5 elements, not 3).
- ☐ **Kst** = $(dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ in **bar·m/s**; classes St-1, St-2, St-3.
- ☐ **Secondary dust explosion** is the principal killer in dust incidents.
- ☐ **Deflagration** = subsonic flame propagation by heat conduction (Mach < 1).
- ☐ **Detonation** = supersonic flame propagation coupled with shock wave (Mach > 1); C-J velocity $v_{CJ} = \sqrt{(\gamma+1)/\gamma \cdot RT_c}$.
- ☐ **Detonation pressure** can exceed 20 bar; deflagration pressure typically < 8 bar in confined systems.
- ☐ Always state **numerical constants** (η, γ, $E_{TNT}$, $T_{SIT}/T_c$ ratio) explicitly — KTU awards marks for these.
- ☐ Always include a **diagram** for 7+ mark questions on UVCE, BLEVE, or dust explosion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toxic Release
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Toxic Release

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Toxic Release

A **Toxic Release** is the unintentional or accidental discharge of a hazardous chemical substance into the environment (air, water, or soil) in a quantity or concentration that can cause acute or chronic injury, illness, or death to exposed humans, animals, or ecosystems. In the context of **Process Safety Engineering**, a toxic release specifically refers to the uncontrolled escape of toxic gases, vapors, mists, or aerosols from process equipment such as storage tanks, reactors, pipelines, valves, or relief devices.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBBT723)** syllabus framework, toxic release analysis falls under the broader category of **Process Hazard Identification and Consequence Analysis**, where the objective is to quantify the source, dispersion, and human/environmental impact of the released toxicant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Toxic Release is a cornerstone of Module 2, bridging the gap between hazard identification and consequence modeling. Mastery of dispersion mathematics and probit analysis is **mandatory** for ESE (End Semester Examination).

## 1.2 Key Terminology in Toxicology

| Term | Full Form | Definition | Units |
|------|-----------|------------|-------|
| **TLV** | Threshold Limit Value | Airborne concentration to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed without adverse effect | ppm or mg/m³ |
| **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit | OSHA-defined legal exposure limit (8-hr TWA) | ppm or mg/m³ |
| **TWA** | Time-Weighted Average | Average concentration over an 8-hour workday | ppm |
| **STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | 15-minute time-weighted average maximum | ppm |
| **IDLH** | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | Maximum airborne concentration from which one could escape within 30 minutes | ppm |
| **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration 50 | Concentration that kills 50% of test population | ppm or mg/m³ |
| **LD₅₀** | Lethal Dose 50 | Single dose that kills 50% of test population | mg/kg body weight |
| **ERPG** | Emergency Response Planning Guideline | Maximum airborne concentration for specific emergency impact levels | ppm |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Cloud" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Spilled Perfume Effect**
>
> Imagine you accidentally knock over a bottle of highly concentrated ammonia in a closed room. Within seconds, your eyes sting and your throat burns — this is **acute toxicity from a vapor-phase toxic release**. Now, contrast this with sitting in a poorly ventilated garage with a running car: the carbon monoxide (CO) buildup is invisible and odorless, yet it can incapacitate you without warning. This is the **silent, asphyxiant danger** of toxic gas dispersion.
>
> In both cases:
> 1. A **source** (the bottle / the exhaust) releases a toxicant.
> 2. The toxicant **disperses** through air (or water) driven by wind, temperature, and turbulence.
> 3. A **receptor** (you) is exposed to a certain **concentration for a certain duration** (the dose).
> 4. If the dose exceeds a **threshold**, physiological damage or death occurs.
>
> The science of Process Safety Engineering formalizes this everyday observation into predictive equations that engineers use to design safer plants.

## 1.4 Classification of Toxic Substances

Toxic substances are broadly classified based on their **physiological action** and **physical state** at release:

### A) By Physiological Action
1. **Irritants** (e.g., ammonia, chlorine, sulfur dioxide) — cause inflammation of respiratory tract and eyes.
2. **Asphyxiants**
   - *Simple asphyxiants* (e.g., nitrogen, CO₂) — displace oxygen.
   - *Chemical asphyxiants* (e.g., CO, HCN, H₂S) — interfere with oxygen transport/utilization.
3. **Systemic Toxins** (e.g., benzene, mercury) — damage specific organs (liver, kidneys, CNS).
4. **Carcinogens / Mutagens** (e.g., vinyl chloride, benzene) — cause long-term genetic damage.
5. **Neurotoxins** (e.g., organophosphates, H₂S) — disrupt nervous system function.

### B) By Physical State at Release
1. **Gases** (e.g., chlorine, ammonia, CO) — disperse rapidly, form vapor clouds.
2. **Vapors** from volatile liquids (e.g., benzene, toluene).
3. **Aerosols / Mists** (e.g., acid mists, pesticide sprays).
4. **Dust particulates** (e.g., silica, asbestos fibers).

## 1.5 Why Toxic Release Modeling Matters in Industry

The **Bhopal Disaster (1984)** remains the world's worst industrial toxic release, where approximately **40 tons of methyl isocyanate (MIC)** leaked from a Union Carbide plant, killing over 3,800 people and affecting 500,000+ residents. This tragedy led to:

- The development of modern **Process Safety Management (PSM)** regulations.
- Mandatory **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** for facilities handling highly hazardous chemicals.
- Widespread adoption of **dispersion modeling** as a regulatory requirement.

In modern KTU-aligned curriculum, engineers must be capable of predicting **downwind concentration profiles**, **evacuation zones**, and **fatality probabilities** to design inherently safer plants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Phases of a Toxic Release Event

A complete toxic release scenario progresses through five well-defined phases. Understanding each phase is critical for accurate consequence analysis.

### Phase 1: Source Definition
- **Identification** of the toxic chemical, its inventory, and physical state.
- Determination of release **mechanism** (rupture, leak, runaway reaction, relief valve discharge).
- Key parameters: mass released (kg), release rate (kg/s), duration (s), temperature (K), pressure (Pa).

### Phase 2: Source Modeling
The release is modeled as one of three source types:

| Source Type | Description | Examples | Governing Equations |
|-------------|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Point Source** | Instantaneous or steady release from a single point | Puncture of a pipe | Gaussian Puff / Plume |
| **Area Source** | Release distributed over an area | Evaporation from a pool | Area-source dispersion model |
| **Line / Jet Source** | High-momentum release with directional velocity | Gas jet from a ruptured pipe | Jet dispersion models |

### Phase 3: Dispersion in the Atmosphere
The released cloud is transported downwind and diluted by atmospheric turbulence. The two primary models used are:

#### A) Pasquill-Gifford Model (Gaussian Plume)
The **steady-state Gaussian Plume Model** is the workhorse of industrial toxic dispersion analysis. It assumes:
- Steady-state conditions.
- Constant wind speed and direction.
- Gaussian (normal) distribution of concentration in the crosswind and vertical directions.
- Reflection at the ground (no absorption) and at the top of the mixing layer.

The centerline ground-level concentration at downwind distance $x$ is:

$$C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

where:
- $C$ = concentration (kg/m³) at point $(x, 0, 0)$ on the centerline at ground level.
- $Q$ = continuous emission rate (kg/s).
- $u$ = mean wind speed at release height (m/s).
- $H$ = effective release height (m).
- $\sigma_y$ = horizontal (crosswind) dispersion coefficient (m).
- $\sigma_z$ = vertical dispersion coefficient (m).

The **off-centerline / off-ground** generalized Gaussian Plume equation is:

$$C(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reflection at the ground** adds an "image source" term. The full form including ground reflection is:
> $$C(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

#### B) Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classes

The dispersion coefficients $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ depend on atmospheric stability, classified into six categories (A through F):

| Stability Class | Description | Wind Speed (m/s) | Day/Night | Surface Heat Flux |
|:---------------:|-------------|:----------------:|-----------|------------------|
| **A** | Very Unstable | 1–3 | Strong daytime insolation | High solar radiation |
| **B** | Moderately Unstable | 2–5 | Daytime | Moderate |
| **C** | Slightly Unstable | 3–8 | Daytime | Slight |
| **D** | Neutral | > 3 (any) | Overcast / High wind | Negligible |
| **E** | Slightly Stable | 1–3 | Night, clear sky | Stable inversion |
| **F** | Moderately Stable | < 2 | Night, clear sky | Strong inversion |

**Power-law correlations** (Briggs) for dispersion coefficients in rural conditions:

$$\sigma_y(x) = a \cdot x^{0.894}$$

$$\sigma_z(x) = b \cdot x^{0.894 - d}$$

where constants $a, b, d$ are tabulated for each stability class.

### Phase 4: Exposure & Dose Calculation
The **dose** $D$ received by a receptor is the time-integrated concentration:

$$D = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} C^{n}(t) \, dt$$

where $n$ is a substance-specific toxicological exponent. For $n=1$, this is the **Haber's Law** dose (concentration × time). For $n \neq 1$, modified probit equations apply.

### Phase 5: Consequence Assessment (Probit Analysis)
The **probit variable** $Y$ is a dimensionless number related to the percentage of population affected. The probit function:

$$Y = a + b \ln(D)$$

where $a$ and $b$ are substance-specific constants. The **percent affected** is computed from:

$$\text{Percent} = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

The fraction of population with probit $\geq 5$ is considered to suffer the specified effect (e.g., fatality, injury).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on Toxic Release.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use |
|---|--------------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Gaussian Plume (Centerline, Ground)** | $C = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Steady-state centerline ground concentration |
| 2 | **Gaussian Plume (General)** | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$ | Includes ground reflection |
| 3 | **Gaussian Puff** | $C(x,y,z,t) = \dfrac{Q^*}{(2\pi)^{3/2} \sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\dfrac{x'^2}{2\sigma_x^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Instantaneous release; $Q^*$ = total mass released |
| 4 | **Haber's Law Dose** | $D = C^n \cdot t$ | For $n=1$: $D = C \cdot t$ (ppm·min or mg·min/m³) |
| 5 | **Probit Equation** | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ | $Y \geq 5$ implies 50%+ effect |
| 6 | **Percent Affected** | $\% = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | Convert probit to percentage |
| 7 | **Briggs σ Correlations (Rural, A)** | $\sigma_y = 0.22x(1+0.0001x)^{-1/2}$, $\sigma_z = 0.20x$ | Stability class A |
| 8 | **Briggs σ Correlations (Rural, C)** | $\sigma_y = 0.22x(1+0.0001x)^{-1/2}$, $\sigma_z = 0.15x$ | Stability class C |
| 9 | **Briggs σ Correlations (Rural, D)** | $\sigma_y = 0.08x(1+0.0001x)^{-1/2}$, $\sigma_z = 0.06x$ | Stability class D |
| 10 | **Effective Release Height** | $H = h_s + \Delta h$ | $h_s$ = stack height, $\Delta h$ = plume rise |
| 11 | **Plume Rise (Briggs, Buoyant)** | $\Delta h = 1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x^{2/3} \cdot u^{-1}$ | $F$ = buoyancy flux parameter |
| 12 | **AEGL / ERPG Threshold** | $C_{threshold}$ | Concentration below which no effect is expected |

## 2.3 Probit Equations for Common Toxicants (KTU-Relevant)

| Substance | Probit Equation | Effect |
|-----------|-----------------|--------|
| **Chlorine (Cl₂)** | $Y = -19.83 + 3.482 \ln(C^n t)$ with $n=1$ | Lethality |
| **Ammonia (NH₃)** | $Y = -35.9 + 3.6581 \ln(C^2 t)$ | Lethality |
| **Hydrogen Cyanide (HCN)** | $Y = -9.85 + 3.00 \ln(C^2 t)$ | Lethality |
| **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)** | $Y = -31.42 + 3.008 \ln(C^2 t)$ | Lethality |
| **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** | $Y = -37.98 + 3.7 \ln(C^1 t)$ | Lethality |
| **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)** | $Y = -13.79 + 1.4 \ln(C^1 t)$ | Lethality |
| **Phosgene (COCl₂)** | $Y = -10.6 + 3.0 \ln(C^1 t)$ | Lethality |
| **Acrolein** | $Y = -25.4 + 3.7 \ln(C^1 t)$ | Lethality |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Plant Siting & Layout** — determining safe distances between toxic chemical storage and occupied buildings.
2. **Emergency Response Planning** — establishing evacuation radii (e.g., 1 ppm, 5 ppm, 10 ppm isopleths).
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — meeting OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, and Indian Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC) requirements.
4. **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** — individual and societal risk contouring.
5. **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** — evaluating alternatives (e.g., lower-toxicity solvents).
6. **Alarm System Design** — placing toxic gas detectors at appropriate locations based on predicted dispersion patterns.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gaussian Plume Equation

The Gaussian Plume equation is derived from a fundamental mass-balance and statistical considerations. We start with Fick's law of diffusion applied to a continuous point source.

### Starting Point: The Diffusion Equation

The atmospheric diffusion equation in three dimensions (advection-diffusion) is:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_x \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} + K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

For a **steady-state** release ($\partial C/\partial t = 0$) and assuming **negligible diffusion in the downwind direction** (the wind transports material much faster than molecular diffusion can act), the equation simplifies to:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

This is a classic parabolic partial differential equation, whose solution — under the boundary conditions of a continuous point source at height $H$ — yields the **Gaussian distribution** in the $y$ and $z$ directions.

### Solution Form

Assume the concentration has the form of a bivariate Gaussian:

$$C(x, y, z) = A(x) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Substituting back and matching terms yields $A(x) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u}$, with $Q$ being the emission rate (kg/s) and $u$ the wind speed.

### Final Gaussian Plume Equation (with ground reflection)

Adding the image source at $z = -H$ to enforce zero vertical flux at the ground (no absorption by the surface), the **complete Gaussian Plume** equation is:

$$C(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

For a receptor at **ground level** ($z = 0$) and **on the centerline** ($y = 0$), this reduces to:

$$C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Examination Style)

### Problem Statement
> A process plant releases **chlorine (Cl₂)** at a steady rate of $Q = 0.5 \text{ kg/s}$ from an effective height $H = 20 \text{ m}$. The wind speed at release height is $u = 4 \text{ m/s}$. Atmospheric stability is class **C** (slightly unstable). Calculate the **ground-level centerline concentration** at a downwind distance of $x = 1000 \text{ m}$ downwind. Use Briggs rural dispersion coefficients.

### Step 1: Determine Briggs Rural Coefficients for Stability Class C

For rural, stability class C, Briggs formulas give:

$$\sigma_y(x) = 0.22 x \left(1 + 0.0001 x\right)^{-1/2}$$

$$\sigma_z(x) = 0.15 x$$

### Step 2: Compute $\sigma_y$ at $x = 1000 \text{ m}$

$$\sigma_y(1000) = 0.22 \times 1000 \times (1 + 0.0001 \times 1000)^{-1/2}$$

Let us evaluate the bracket term first:

$$1 + 0.0001 \times 1000 = 1 + 0.1 = 1.1$$

$$(1.1)^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.1}} = \frac{1}{1.0488} \approx 0.9535$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_y = 0.22 \times 1000 \times 0.9535 = 220 \times 0.9535 \approx 209.77 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Compute $\sigma_z$ at $x = 1000 \text{ m}$

$$\sigma_z(1000) = 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Ground-Level Centerline Gaussian Plume Equation

$$C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

### Step 5: Substitute Numerical Values

Numerator: $Q = 0.5 \text{ kg/s}$

Denominator: $\pi \times 209.77 \times 150 \times 4$

Compute denominator step by step:

$$209.77 \times 150 = 31,465.5$$

$$31,465.5 \times 4 = 125,862$$

$$\pi \times 125,862 = 3.1416 \times 125,862 \approx 395,377$$

So:

$$\frac{Q}{\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} = \frac{0.5}{395,377} \approx 1.2647 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Step 6: Compute the Exponential Decay Term

$$\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = \frac{20^2}{2 \times 150^2} = \frac{400}{2 \times 22,500} = \frac{400}{45,000} = 0.008889$$

$$\exp(-0.008889) \approx 0.99115$$

### Step 7: Final Answer

$$C(1000, 0, 0) = 1.2647 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.99115 \approx 1.2536 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Step 8: Convert to ppm (mg/m³ first)

Converting kg/m³ to mg/m³:

$$1.2536 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1.2536 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

To convert mg/m³ of Cl₂ to ppm at 25 °C and 1 atm, use the ideal gas relation:

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{C_{mg/m^3} \times 24.45}{M}$$

where $M$ = molecular weight of Cl₂ = 70.9 g/mol, and 24.45 = molar volume (L/mol) at 25 °C, 1 atm.

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{1.2536 \times 24.45}{70.9} = \frac{30.65}{70.9} \approx 0.432 \text{ ppm}$$

### Conclusion
At **1000 m downwind** on the centerline, the ground-level chlorine concentration is approximately **0.43 ppm** — well below the **IDLH of 10 ppm** but above the **TLV-TWA of 0.5 ppm** (marginal compliance).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** KTU examiners deduct marks for unit mismatches. Always state units explicitly at every step. Also, **never confuse kg/m³ with mg/m³** — a factor of 1000 error is a classic trap.

## 3.3 Probit Calculation Worked Example

### Problem Statement
> Using the **chlorine probit equation** $Y = -19.83 + 3.482 \ln(C \cdot t)$, determine the **percent fatality** for a person exposed to a chlorine concentration of $C = 50 \text{ mg/m}^3$ for $t = 30 \text{ minutes}$.

### Step 1: Compute the Dose

$$D = C \cdot t = 50 \times 30 = 1500 \text{ mg·min/m}^3$$

### Step 2: Compute the Probit Variable

$$Y = -19.83 + 3.482 \ln(1500)$$

Compute the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(1500) = \ln(1.5 \times 10^3) = \ln(1.5) + 3\ln(10) = 0.4055 + 3 \times 2.3026 = 0.4055 + 6.9078 = 7.3133$$

Therefore:

$$Y = -19.83 + 3.482 \times 7.3133 = -19.83 + 25.466 = 5.636$$

### Step 3: Convert Probit to Percent Fatality

$$\% \text{ Fatality} = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5.636 - 5}{1.4142} = \frac{0.636}{1.4142} = 0.4497$$

From the standard error function table:

$$\text{erf}(0.4497) \approx 0.4757$$

Therefore:

$$\% = 50 \times (1 + 0.4757) = 50 \times 1.4757 = 73.78\%$$

### Conclusion
Approximately **74% of the exposed population** would be expected to suffer fatality under these conditions. This justifies the use of **gas masks, evacuation, and shelter-in-place protocols** for chlorine releases.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix of Dispersion Models

| Model | Type | Best Use Case | Limitations |
|-------|------|---------------|-------------|
| **Gaussian Plume (Pasquill-Gifford)** | Steady-state, continuous | Long-duration releases, regulatory compliance | Assumes flat terrain, constant wind |
| **Gaussian Puff** | Instantaneous, time-dependent | Short-duration / puff releases | Computationally heavier |
| **Britter-McQuaid** | Dense gas, empirical | Dense gas releases (LNG, LPG) | Limited to specific conditions |
| **SLAB / DEGADIS** | Dense gas, numerical | Heavy gas (Cl₂, ammonia) | Requires detailed input data |
| **AERMOD** | Modern, regulatory | Refined regulatory modeling (EPA) | Steep learning curve |
| **HEGADAS** | Heavy gas | LNG / ammonia spills | Specific to heavy gases |

## 3.5 Dose-Response Curve Analytical Framework

The **dose-response relationship** is fundamental to toxicology. There are two main models:

1. **Threshold (Non-Linear) Model** — Assumes a NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level) below which no effect occurs. Used for most non-carcinogens.

2. **Linearized Multi-Stage Model** — Assumes a small risk even at very low doses. Used for **carcinogens** (e.g., benzene, vinyl chloride).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examinations often ask students to **state the dose-response assumption** used in a given probit equation. Always clarify whether Haber's law (linear in $C \cdot t$) or a power law ($C^n t$) applies.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) **TWA (Time-Weighted Average)**
(b) **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)**
(c) **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50)**

### Model Answer
**(a) TWA:** The Time-Weighted Average (TWA) is the **average airborne concentration of a substance over a normal 8-hour workday or 40-hour workweek**, to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed without adverse health effects. It is expressed in **ppm (parts per million)** or **mg/m³**. *Example:* OSHA's PEL-TWA for chlorine is **0.5 ppm** (8-hour average).

**(b) IDLH:** IDLH represents the maximum airborne concentration of a substance **from which a worker could escape within 30 minutes** without experiencing any escape-impairing or irreversible health effects. *Example:* The IDLH for chlorine is **10 ppm**.

**(c) LC₅₀:** Lethal Concentration 50 is the **concentration of a substance in air that is expected to kill 50% of test animals** (usually rats) during a specified exposure period (commonly 4 hours). *Example:* LC₅₀ for chlorine in rats is approximately **293 ppm (4-hour exposure)**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of "Probit Analysis" in toxic release consequence assessment. Mention the standard probit equation.**

### Model Answer
**Probit Analysis** is a statistical method used in process safety engineering to **correlate the dose of a toxic substance to the percentage of an exposed population** that will suffer a specified effect (lethality, injury, incapacitation).

The **probit variable** $Y$ is a dimensionless quantity defined as:

$$Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$$

where:
- $C$ = toxicant concentration (ppm or mg/m³).
- $t$ = exposure duration (minutes).
- $n$ = toxicological exponent (substance-specific).
- $a, b$ = regression constants (substance-specific).

The **percentage of population affected** is obtained from the probit value $Y$ using the **Gaussian error function**:

$$\% \text{ Affected} = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

A probit value of **$Y = 5$** corresponds to a **50% response rate**. Values above 5 indicate greater than 50% affected, and vice versa. Probit analysis is used to define **lethality zones, injury zones, and evacuation radii** for emergency planning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the Gaussian Plume Equation for a continuous elevated point source. State all assumptions clearly.

#### Model Answer

**Assumptions of the Gaussian Plume Model:**

1. **Steady-state conditions:** The release rate and meteorological conditions are constant over time.
2. **Constant wind speed and direction** along the downwind axis.
3. **Conservation of mass:** The total mass released per second is conserved in the plume.
4. **Fickian diffusion:** Turbulent diffusion is described by a Gaussian (normal) distribution in the crosswind ($y$) and vertical ($z$) directions.
5. **Negligible molecular diffusion** in the downwind direction.
6. **No chemical reaction** or deposition of the toxicant in the atmosphere.
7. **Ground-level reflection** of the plume (no absorption by the surface) — implemented via an image source.
8. **Top of the mixing layer** is a perfect reflector (or an absorbing boundary in simplified models).
9. **Flat, homogeneous terrain.**

**Derivation:**

Starting from the **steady-state advection-diffusion equation** for a passive scalar in a turbulent flow:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

where $u$ = mean wind speed, $K_y, K_z$ = turbulent diffusivities (functions of downwind distance $x$).

Assume a solution of the form:

$$C(x, y, z) = A(x) \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Substituting and matching the Gaussian variances with the diffusion coefficients yields:

$$\sigma_y^2 = 2 K_y \frac{x}{u}, \quad \sigma_z^2 = 2 K_z \frac{x}{u}$$

The pre-factor $A(x)$ is determined by the **mass conservation constraint** (total mass flux through any cross-section equals $Q$):

$$Q = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{\infty} u C(x, y, z) \, dz \, dy$$

Evaluating this integral gives $A(x) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u}$.

Adding the **ground reflection term** (image source at $z = -H$):

$$\boxed{C(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]}$$

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A plant releases **sulfur dioxide (SO₂)** at a rate of $Q = 1.2 \text{ kg/s}$ from a stack of effective height $H = 25 \text{ m}$. The wind speed is $u = 5 \text{ m/s}$, and stability class is **D** (neutral). Using Briggs rural correlations ($\sigma_y = 0.08 x (1+0.0001x)^{-1/2}$, $\sigma_z = 0.06 x$), find the **ground-level centerline concentration** at $x = 500 \text{ m}$. Also calculate the **percent fatality** if exposure time is $t = 10 \text{ min}$. The probit equation for SO₂ lethality is $Y = -15.67 + 2.10 \ln(C \cdot t)$, where $C$ is in mg/m³ and $t$ in minutes.

#### Model Answer

**Step 1: Compute $\sigma_y$ at $x = 500$ m**

$$\sigma_y = 0.08 \times 500 \times (1 + 0.0001 \times 500)^{-1/2}$$

$$1 + 0.0001 \times 500 = 1 + 0.05 = 1.05$$

$$(1.05)^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.05}} = 0.9759$$

$$\sigma_y = 40 \times 0.9759 \approx 39.04 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\sigma_z$ at $x = 500$ m**

$$\sigma_z = 0.06 \times 500 = 30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Apply Centerline Ground-Level Plume Equation**

$$C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Compute denominator:

$$\pi \times 39.04 \times 30 \times 5 = \pi \times 5856 = 3.1416 \times 5856 \approx 18,396$$

Pre-exponential:

$$\frac{1.2}{18,396} \approx 6.523 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Convert to mg/m³:

$$6.523 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m}^3 = 65.23 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Step 4: Compute Exponential Decay**

$$\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = \frac{25^2}{2 \times 30^2} = \frac{625}{1800} = 0.3472$$

$$\exp(-0.3472) \approx 0.7065$$

**Step 5: Final Ground-Level Concentration**

$$C = 65.23 \times 0.7065 \approx 46.08 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Step 6: Compute the Dose**

$$D = C \cdot t = 46.08 \times 10 = 460.8 \text{ mg·min/m}^3$$

**Step 7: Compute Probit Variable**

$$Y = -15.67 + 2.10 \ln(460.8)$$

$$\ln(460.8) = \ln(4.608) + \ln(100) = 1.5275 + 4.6052 = 6.1327$$

$$Y = -15.67 + 2.10 \times 6.1327 = -15.67 + 12.879 = -2.791$$

**Step 8: Interpret the Probit Value**

Since $Y = -2.791 \ll 5$, the percent fatality is **very low**. Using the error function:

$$\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{-2.791 - 5}{1.4142} = \frac{-7.791}{1.4142} = -5.510$$

From standard tables: $\text{erf}(-5.510) \approx -1.000$ (saturated negative)

$$\% = 50 [1 + (-1.000)] \approx 0\%$$

**Conclusion:** At 500 m downwind, the SO₂ concentration is approximately **46 mg/m³**, but the probit value indicates **negligible fatality** for a 10-minute exposure. However, this is above the OSHA PEL-TWA of 5 ppm (≈ 13 mg/m³), so **acute respiratory effects** are still possible — evacuation is recommended.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the various **models used for atmospheric dispersion of toxic gases**. Compare the Gaussian Plume, Gaussian Puff, and Britter-McQuaid models with respect to assumptions, applicability, and limitations.

#### Model Answer

**1. Gaussian Plume Model (Pasquill-Gifford)**

- **Type:** Steady-state, continuous release.
- **Assumptions:** Constant wind, flat terrain, no chemical reaction, ground reflection.
- **Mathematical form:** Bivariate Gaussian distribution in $y$ and $z$.
- **Applicability:** Regulatory dispersion modeling, continuous emissions from stacks.
- **Limitations:** Cannot handle time-varying releases, dense gases, or complex terrain.

**2. Gaussian Puff Model**

- **Type:** Instantaneous, time-dependent release.
- **Assumptions:** Similar to plume, but with an additional Gaussian spread in the downwind direction.
- **Mathematical form:** Trivariate Gaussian distribution in $x, y, z$.
- **Applicability:** Accidental spills, puff releases from ruptured tanks.
- **Limitations:** Computationally expensive; assumes instantaneous release.

**3. Britter-McQuaid Model**

- **Type:** Empirical dense-gas dispersion model.
- **Assumptions:** Negatively buoyant cloud, ground-based dispersion.
- **Mathematical form:** Dimensionless correlations based on field experiments.
- **Applicability:** Heavy gases (LNG, LPG, Cl₂).
- **Limitations:** Limited to specific source geometries; cannot handle reactive chemistry.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Gaussian Plume | Gaussian Puff | Britter-McQuaid |
|---------|----------------|---------------|------------------|
| Release type | Continuous | Instantaneous | Continuous / puff |
| Cloud density | Neutral | Neutral | Dense / heavy |
| Time dependence | No | Yes | Quasi-steady |
| Computational complexity | Low | Moderate | Low |
| Best for | Stack emissions | Accidental puffs | LNG, LPG, Cl₂ |
| Limitation | No dense gas | No dense gas | Specific geometry |

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A chemical reactor contains **10,000 kg of anhydrous ammonia (NH₃)** at 10 °C and 1 atm. A catastrophic rupture occurs, releasing the ammonia instantaneously as a cold dense cloud. Using the **Britter-McQuaid correlations** (assume initial Brichard density ratio = 2.0, and at $x = 1000$ m, the dimensionless downwind concentration $C^*$ is approximately 0.05, where $C^* = C / C_0$, and initial concentration $C_0$ for liquid ammonia is approximately 800,000 mg/m³), compute:
(i) the ground-level concentration $C$ at 1000 m, and
(ii) the probit value using the ammonia probit equation $Y = -35.9 + 3.6581 \ln(C^2 t)$, assuming $t = 5$ min.

#### Model Answer

**(i) Ground-Level Concentration at 1000 m**

$$C^* = \frac{C}{C_0} \implies C = C^* \times C_0$$

$$C = 0.05 \times 800{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 40 \text{ g/m}^3$$

**(ii) Compute the Dose Term**

For ammonia, the probit equation uses $n = 2$:

$$D = C^2 t$$

$$D = (40{,}000)^2 \times 5 = 1.6 \times 10^9 \times 5 = 8.0 \times 10^9 \text{ (mg/m}^3)^2 \cdot \text{min}$$

**(iii) Compute the Probit Value**

$$Y = -35.9 + 3.6581 \ln(8.0 \times 10^9)$$

$$\ln(8.0 \times 10^9) = \ln(8.0) + 9 \ln(10) = 2.0794 + 20.7233 = 22.8027$$

$$Y = -35.9 + 3.6581 \times 22.8027 = -35.9 + 83.42 = 47.52$$

**(iv) Interpret the Result**
