Since $Y = 47.52 \gg 5$, the percent fatality is **essentially 100%**. The cumulative probit curve saturates at $Y = 8$ (which corresponds to ~99.99% affected). Hence, the result indicates **catastrophic lethality** at 1000 m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mismatched units:** A frequent mistake is mixing ppm with mg/m³ in the probit equation. Always confirm which units the probit constants assume.
> 2. **Ignoring ground reflection:** Many students forget the image source term in the Gaussian Plume equation, leading to wrong ground-level concentrations.
> 3. **Skipping dose calculation:** Jumping directly to a probit value without computing $C^n t$ loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Wrong stability class:** Mixing up Pasquill-Gifford classes A–F costs full credit on stability-related questions.
> 5. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** In derivations, the assumptions list is **mandatory** and carries 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing $C^*$ with $C$:** In Britter-McQuaid, the dimensionless concentration $C^*$ is **not** the absolute concentration — multiply by $C_0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Toxic Release**

- [x] **Toxic Release** = uncontrolled discharge of harmful chemical → governed by KTU Module 2 of OEBBT723.
- [x] **Five phases:** Source → Source Modeling → Dispersion → Exposure → Consequence.
- [x] **Three source types:** Point, Area, Line/Jet.
- [x] **Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classes A–F** determine $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$.
- [x] **Gaussian Plume Equation** (centerline, ground level):
      $C = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$
- [x] **Briggs Rural Formulas** (most commonly tested coefficients):
      - A: $\sigma_y = 0.22x(1+0.0001x)^{-1/2}$, $\sigma_z = 0.20x$
      - C: $\sigma_y = 0.22x(1+0.0001x)^{-1/2}$, $\sigma_z = 0.15x$
      - D: $\sigma_y = 0.08x(1+0.0001x)^{-1/2}$, $\sigma_z = 0.06x$
- [x] **Haber's Law:** $D = C \cdot t$ (for $n = 1$).
- [x] **General Dose:** $D = C^n \cdot t$.
- [x] **Probit Equation:** $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$.
- [x] **% Affected:** $\% = 50[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$.
- [x] **Probit values:** $Y = 5$ = 50% affected; $Y < 5$ = less than 50%.
- [x] **Unit conversion:** $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{mg/m^3} \times 24.45}{M_{g/mol}}$ at 25 °C, 1 atm.
- [x] **Briggs-McQuaid / Britter-McQuaid** → use $C^*$ dimensionless for dense gas.
- [x] **Common toxicants & probits:** Cl₂, NH₃, HCN, H₂S, CO, NO₂, COCl₂, acrolein.
- [x] **Real-world benchmark:** Bhopal 1984 (MIC) — 40 tons released, 3,800+ deaths.
- [x] **Emergency thresholds:** ERPG-1, ERPG-2, ERPG-3.
- [x] **Regulatory standards:** OSHA PEL, TLV-TWA, STEL, IDLH, EPA RMP, Indian MSIHC Rules.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** Plant siting, evacuation planning, QRA, alarm placement, ISD.
- [x] **Diagram to remember:** Source → Plume → Receptors → Probit → Consequence.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Mismatched units, skipping the image source, wrong stability class.

> **End of Module 2 — Toxic Release Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runaway Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Runaway Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Runaway Reaction** is an undesired, self-accelerating exothermic reaction in a chemical vessel where the rate of heat generation by the chemical reaction exceeds the rate of heat removal by the cooling system. This imbalance causes a continuous and uncontrolled rise in temperature and pressure, ultimately leading to vessel rupture, fire, or explosion.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a runaway reaction is fundamentally characterized by the violation of the **Thermal Stability Criterion**:

$$q_{gen}(T) > q_{rem}(T)$$

The system is considered thermally stable only when:

$$q_{gen}(T) \leq q_{rem}(T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> A runaway reaction is initiated when an operating disturbance (loss of cooling, overcharge, contamination) shifts the system into a state where the **positive feedback loop** of temperature → reaction rate → temperature becomes self-sustaining and irreversible.

### 1.2 Key Technical Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Temperature of No Return | $T_{NR}$ | The lowest temperature at which a runaway cannot be stopped, even with maximum cooling |
| Time to Maximum Rate | $TMR$ | The time (isothermal) for an exothermic decomposition to reach its peak rate |
| Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature | $SADT$ | The lowest ambient temperature at which self-accelerating decomposition occurs in a package |
| Critical Temperature | $T_{crit}$ | Threshold temperature above which heat generation > heat removal |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy: The Snowball Down the Mountain**
> Imagine rolling a small snowball down a snowy slope. At first, it's small and slow. But as it rolls, it picks up more snow (more material reacts) and gains momentum. The longer it rolls, the faster it goes. Now imagine your hands (cooling jacket) trying to stop it. If the slope is gentle, you can catch it. But if the slope is steep enough, the snowball grows so fast that no matter how hard you push back, it escapes.
>
> **Mapping the Analogy:**
> - **Slope steepness** = Activation energy / temperature sensitivity
> - **Snowball** = Reacting mixture generating heat
> - **Your hands** = Cooling system (heat removal)
> - **Avalanche** = Runaway reaction → vessel explosion
> - **Falling off the cliff** = Loss of containment (BLEVE, VCE)

### 1.4 Why Runaway Reactions Are the #1 Concern in Process Safety

> [!WARNING] **Industry Reality**
> Approximately **45% of major chemical incidents** worldwide (per CCPS and CSB databases) originate from uncontrolled exothermic runaway reactions, including infamous disasters like **Bhopal (MIC, 1984)**, **AZF Toulouse (Ammonium Nitrate, 2001)**, and **Texas City BP Refinery (2005)**.

The engineering challenge is that **Arrhenius kinetics** create exponential temperature sensitivity: a 10 °C rise can **double or triple** the reaction rate, which then generates more heat, which further accelerates the rate — a classic **thermal positive feedback loop**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Heat Balance Framework (Semenov's Theory)

The Semenov model of thermal explosion treats the reactor contents as a **spatially uniform lumped system** with a single bulk temperature $T$, exchanging heat with an ambient/cooling jacket at temperature $T_a$.

The transient energy balance on the reactor contents is:

$$V \rho C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = q_{gen} - q_{rem}$$

Where:

$$q_{gen} = -(\Delta H_r) \cdot V \cdot k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{RT}\right) \cdot C^n$$

$$q_{rem} = UA\,(T - T_a)$$

| Variable | Physical Meaning | Units |
|----------|------------------|-------|
| $V$ | Reactor volume | m³ |
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | kg/m³ |
| $C_p$ | Specific heat capacity | J/(kg·K) |
| $\Delta H_r$ | Heat of reaction (negative for exothermic) | J/mol |
| $k_0$ | Pre-exponential (Arrhenius) factor | (concentration)$^{1-n}$/s |
| $E$ | Activation energy | J/mol |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | J/(mol·K) |
| $C$ | Reactant concentration | mol/m³ |
| $n$ | Reaction order | dimensionless |
| $UA$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient × area | W/K |
| $T_a$ | Ambient/cooling temperature | K |

### 2.2 Stability Analysis — Critical Temperature Derivation Concept

At steady state, $q_{gen} = q_{rem}$. But **stability** requires:

$$\dfrac{dq_{gen}}{dT} < \dfrac{dq_{rem}}{dT} \quad \text{at the intersection point}$$

Because $q_{rem}$ is **linear** in $T$ (slope = $UA$), and $q_{gen}$ is a **convex exponential** in $T$, there are three regimes:

- **Below $T_{crit}$** → $q_{gen}$ and $q_{rem}$ intersect at a **stable** point
- **At $T_{crit}$** → Curves are **tangent** (threshold case)
- **Above $T_{crit}$** → $q_{gen} > q_{rem}$ **always** → runaway is inevitable

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mandatory Formulas for KTU University Exam**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Arrhenius Rate} \quad & k = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{RT}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{Heat Generation} \quad & q_{gen} = -\Delta H \cdot V \cdot k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{RT}\right) \cdot C^n \\[6pt]
\text{Heat Removal} \quad & q_{rem} = UA\,(T - T_a) \\[6pt]
\text{Critical Temperature (Semenov)} \quad & T_{crit} = \dfrac{E}{2R} \quad \text{(when } T_{crit} \gg T_a) \\[6pt]
\text{Temperature of No Return} \quad & T_{NR} = T_a + \dfrac{R\,T_a^{2}}{E} \\[6pt]
\text{Time to Maximum Rate (TMR)} \quad & TMR = \dfrac{C_p \, R \, T^{2}}{q_{gen} \, E} \\[6pt]
\text{Adiabatic Temperature Rise} \quad & \Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{-\Delta H \, C_0}{\rho \, C_p} \\[6pt]
\text{Induction Period} \quad & t_{ind} = \dfrac{\rho \, C_p \, R \, T^{2}}{q_{gen} \, E} \\[6pt]
\text{SADT Approximation} \quad & SADT \approx T_{NR} - 5\ \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This framework is used in:

- **Reactor design**: Sizing cooling jackets, emergency relief vents
- **Hazard identification (HAZOP)**: Evaluating "loss of cooling" deviations
- **Storage/transportation**: Determining SADT for hazardous chemicals
- **Insurance & regulatory compliance**: OSHA, EPA, KTU-aligned PSM (Process Safety Management) audits
- **Pharmaceutical batch reactors**: Predicting exotherm onset temperatures

### 2.5 Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature (SADT) — Deep Dive

SADT is the lowest ambient temperature at which a substance of given packaging undergoes self-accelerating decomposition. It is the operational proxy for $T_{NR}$ in bulk storage. Below SADT, the heat losses keep up with the slow decomposition; above SADT, runaway ensues.

> [!NOTE] **Practical Note**
> For ammonium nitrate transport, SADT is approximately **160 °C** in standard 50 kg drums, but effective SADT drops with contamination, confinement, and insulation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Semenov's Critical Temperature ($T_{crit}$)

**Starting Point:** At the threshold of runaway, the heat generation curve is **tangent** to the heat removal line.

**Step 1: Equality condition** — At tangent point $T_{crit}$:

$$q_{gen}(T_{crit}) = q_{rem}(T_{crit})$$

$$-\Delta H \, V \, k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{R\,T_{crit}}\right) C^n = UA\,(T_{crit} - T_a) \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 2: Slope condition** — The derivative equality:

$$\dfrac{dq_{gen}}{dT}\bigg|_{T_{crit}} = \dfrac{dq_{rem}}{dT}$$

$$\left[-\Delta H \, V \, k_0 \, C^n \cdot \dfrac{E}{R\,T^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{R\,T}\right)\right]_{T_{crit}} = UA \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 3: Substituting $q_{gen}(T_{crit})$ into (2)**:

$$\dfrac{E}{R\,T_{crit}^{2}} \cdot q_{gen}(T_{crit}) = UA$$

**Step 4: Replace $UA$ from (1)**:

$$\dfrac{E}{R\,T_{crit}^{2}} \cdot UA\,(T_{crit} - T_a) = UA$$

Cancel $UA$ (assuming $UA \neq 0$):

$$\dfrac{E}{R\,T_{crit}^{2}} (T_{crit} - T_a) = 1$$

**Step 5: Solve for $T_{crit}$**:

$$E\,(T_{crit} - T_a) = R\,T_{crit}^{2}$$

**Step 6: Apply the Semenov limit $T_{crit} \gg T_a$**, so $T_{crit} - T_a \approx T_{crit}$:

$$E \cdot T_{crit} = R \cdot T_{crit}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{crit} = \dfrac{E}{2R}\,}$$

**Step 7: Apply the more general exact form** (Frank-Kamenetskii limit) where $T_{crit} = T_a + RT^{2}/E$:

$$T_{NR} = T_a + \dfrac{R\,T_a^{2}}{E}$$

This is the **Temperature of No Return** — the absolute minimum temperature from which runaway cannot be arrested, even with maximum cooling.

### 3.2 Derivation II: Time to Maximum Rate (TMR)

The TMR represents the **isothermal induction period** before runaway becomes uncontrollable. It is derived by linearizing the energy balance near peak rate.

**Step 1: Starting from the transient energy balance:**

$$V \rho C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = q_{gen} - q_{rem}$$

**Step 2: Apply the worst-case adiabatic assumption** (no cooling, $q_{rem} = 0$):

$$V \rho C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = q_{gen}(T) = -\Delta H \, V \, k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{RT}\right) C^n$$

**Step 3: Introduce a reference rate $q_{gen}(T_0)$ evaluated at the operating temperature $T_0$:**

$$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{q_{gen}(T_0)}{\rho \, C_p} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4: The maximum rate occurs at a small temperature rise $\Delta T$ above $T_0$.** For the runaway peak condition, the time integral yields the classical Townsend-style result:

$$TMR = \dfrac{C_p \, R \, T^{2}}{q_{gen}(T) \, E}$$

**Step 5: Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\,\text{TMR}(T) = \dfrac{\rho \, C_p \, R \, T^{2}}{-\Delta H \, k_0 \exp(-E/RT) \, C^n \, E}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight**
> A **golden rule** in process safety: For every 10 °C rise in temperature, TMR decreases by approximately a **factor of 2 to 2.5** for typical organic peroxides — this is why thermal inertia margins are designed at **20 °C below the maximum safe process temperature**.

### 3.3 Worked Example: TMR Calculation

**Given:** A styrene polymerization reactor has $C_p = 1.8 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $E = 80 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, $T = 400 \text{ K}$, $q_{gen}(400\text{ K}) = 2.5 \text{ kW/m}^3$.

**Solution:**

$$TMR = \dfrac{1800 \times 8.314 \times (400)^{2}}{2500 \times 80{,}000}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TMR & = \dfrac{1800 \times 8.314 \times 160{,}000}{2.0 \times 10^{8}} \\
& = \dfrac{2.39 \times 10^{9}}{2.0 \times 10^{8}} \\
& \approx 11.96 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING] **Examiner Pitfall**
> Students frequently forget to **convert units consistently**: $C_p$ in J/(kg·K), $E$ in J/mol, $q_{gen}$ in W/m³ = J/(s·m³). Mixing kJ with J is the #1 cause of 2-mark deductions in KTU Part A numerical questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Heat Balance Stability Diagram (Semenov Curves)

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Note:** The following Mermaid block represents the classical **Semenov Thermal Explosion Stability Diagram** — mapping the intersection regimes of $q_{gen}(T)$ (exponential curve) and $q_{rem}(T)$ (straight line) versus reactor temperature.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reactor Temperature T (K)"] --> B{"Compare dQ_gen/dT vs dQ_rem/dT"}
    B --> C["STABLE REGIME: T less than T_crit"]
    B --> D["MARGINAL REGIME: T equal to T_crit (Tangent Point)"]
    B --> E["UNSTABLE REGIME: T greater than T_crit (RUNAWAY)"]
    C --> F["Q_gen Curve - Exponential Convex Upward"]
    D --> G["Tangent Condition satisfied: T_crit equals E by 2R"]
    E --> H["Q_rem Straight Line Slope equals UA lies below Q_gen"]
    F --> I["Heat Removal Q_rem Line Slope equals UA"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Intersection at T_crit divides safe and unsafe operating zones"]
    J --> K["Operating point T_op must remain on left side of T_crit for safety"]
```

### 4.2 Runaway Reaction Progression Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA["Stage A: Normal Operation T less than T_safe"] --> initB["Reactor at design temperature with stable exotherm"]
    initB --> distA["Disturbance Trigger Event"]
    distA --> distB["Loss of Cooling OR Overcharge OR Contamination OR Agitator Failure"]
    distB --> riseA["Stage B: Temperature Rises Above T_safe"]
    riseA --> riseB["Arrhenius Kinetics: Rate doubles every 10 degree Celsius"]
    riseB --> critA["Stage C: T reaches T_crit equals E by 2R"]
    critA --> critB["Q_gen exceeds Q_rem by design margin"]
    critB --> nrA["Stage D: Temperature of No Return T_NR crossed"]
    nrA --> nrB["Cooling can no longer arrest the reaction"]
    nrB --> peakA["Stage E: Adiabatic Self-heating to T_final"]
    peakA --> peakB["Pressure spikes from vapor pressure and gas evolution"]
    peakB --> failA["Stage F: Relief Valve Activation OR Vessel Rupture"]
    failA --> failB["Loss of Containment leading to Fire Explosion or Toxic Release"]
    failB --> endX["DISASTER Phase"]
```

### 4.3 TMR vs Temperature Operational Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zone1["SAFE ZONE: TMR greater than 24 hours"] --> z1a["Normal operation with operator response time available"]
    zone2["CAUTION ZONE: TMR between 1 hour and 24 hours"] --> z2a["Enhanced monitoring and emergency cooling required"]
    zone3["DANGER ZONE: TMR between 10 minutes and 1 hour"] --> z3a["Automatic injection of inhibitor or quench required"]
    zone4["CRITICAL ZONE: TMR less than 10 minutes"] --> z4a["Runaway is imminent: activate deluge systems and evacuate"]
    zone5["CATASTROPHIC: TMR less than 1 minute"] --> z5a["Vessel failure unavoidable: only blast walls and shelters prevent loss of life"]
```

### 4.4 Counter-Measures Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["LAYER 1: INHERENT SAFETY"] --> L1a["Minimize inventory"]
    L1a --> L1b["Use less hazardous chemistry"]
    L1b --> L1c["Operate at lower concentration and temperature"]
    L2["LAYER 2: PASSIVE ENGINEERING"] --> L2a["Double-walled vessels"]
    L2a --> L2b["Insulation to prevent heat loss accumulation"]
    L3["LAYER 3: ACTIVE ENGINEERING"] --> L3a["Emergency relief valves sized by DIERS methodology"]
    L3a --> L3b["Quench systems and dump tanks"]
    L3b --> L3c["Inhibitor injection lines"]
    L4["LAYER 4: PROCEDURAL CONTROLS"] --> L4a["HAZOP and LOPA studies"]
    L4a --> L4b["Operator training and alarm management"]
    L5["LAYER 5: EMERGENCY RESPONSE"] --> L5a["On-site fire brigades"]
    L5a --> L5b["Community evacuation plans"]
    L1c --> L2
    L2b --> L3
    L3c --> L4
    L4b --> L5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define a **runaway reaction**. List **four common causes** that may initiate it in a chemical reactor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A runaway reaction is an uncontrolled exothermic reaction in which the rate of heat generation by the chemical process exceeds the rate of heat removal by the cooling system, leading to a self-accelerating rise in temperature and pressure.

**Four common causes (½ mark each):**
1. Failure of cooling water supply (pump failure, valve closure)
2. Overcharge of reactant or catalyst
3. Presence of contaminants (e.g., rust, incompatible material)
4. Loss of agitation leading to local hot spots
5. External fire heating adjacent vessels
6. Incorrect reagent addition sequence

> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for definition + ½ × 4 = 2 marks for causes = **3 marks total**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between **SADT** and **TNR** (Temperature of No Return). State their relationship.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | SADT (Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature) | TNR (Temperature of No Return) |
|-----------|---------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Definition | Lowest ambient temperature at which a packaged substance undergoes self-accelerating decomposition | Lowest temperature from which a runaway cannot be arrested, even with maximum cooling |
| Context | Storage and transportation of reactive chemicals | Reactor thermal analysis (Semenov framework) |
| Math form | Approximated from TNR minus 5 K safety margin | $T_{NR} = T_a + RT_a^{2}/E$ |

**Relationship (1 mark):** $SADT \approx T_{NR} - 5\ \text{K}$ (rule of thumb for safety margin).

---

### PART B — Full-Question (14-Mark Module Choice)

> **Instructions:** Answer **ONE** of the following full questions. Each carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a) Derive the expression for the critical temperature $T_{crit}$ of a thermal runaway reaction using Semenov's theory. State clearly the assumptions made.** (7 marks)

**(b) In a batch reactor, the following exothermic reaction A → B is conducted:**
- $E = 90 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- $T_a = 350 \text{ K}$
- $\Delta H_r = -55 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $k_0 = 10^{10}\ \text{s}^{-1}$
- $C_{A,0} = 2000 \text{ mol/m}^3$

**Calculate:**
- (i) $T_{crit}$ (1.5 marks)
- (ii) $T_{NR}$ (1.5 marks)
- (iii) Comment on the safety margin if the operating temperature is 380 K. (1 mark)
- (iv) If the temperature overshoots to 420 K, qualitatively describe the time to runaway. (1 mark)
- (v) Suggest **two engineering controls** to prevent runaway. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the step-by-step derivation. The final result is:

$$T_{crit} = \dfrac{E}{2R}$$

**Assumptions (½ mark each, total 2 marks):**
1. Lumped parameter model (uniform temperature throughout reactor)
2. Single, irreversible, first-order reaction
3. Heat removal is purely by convection to ambient (Newton's cooling law)
4. Reactant concentration remains approximately constant during the runaway onset
5. Ambient temperature $T_a$ is constant
6. Physical properties ($\rho, C_p, UA$) are temperature-independent

> **Marking split:** 5 marks for derivation steps (1 mark each for: equality, slope, substitution, cancellation, Semenov limit) + 2 marks for assumptions.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) $T_{crit}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{crit} = \dfrac{E}{2R} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{2 \times 8.314} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{16.628} \approx 5412\ \text{K}
$$

> **Note for examiner:** This unrealistically high value signals that **$T_a$ is the limiting factor** — the correct "operational" critical temperature is governed by the tangent-point solution of the exact equations, not the asymptotic Semenov limit. The textbook value should be computed using $T_{NR}$.

**(ii) $T_{NR}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$T_{NR} = T_a + \dfrac{R\,T_a^{2}}{E} = 350 + \dfrac{8.314 \times (350)^{2}}{90{,}000}$$

$$T_{NR} = 350 + \dfrac{8.314 \times 122{,}500}{90{,}000} = 350 + 11.31 \approx 361.3\ \text{K}$$

**(iii) Safety margin comment (1 mark):**
Operating at 380 K is **above $T_{NR}$ (361.3 K)**, leaving **negative safety margin**. Runaway is unavoidable; immediate process modification required.

**(iv) Qualitative TMR at 420 K (1 mark):**
TMR decreases **exponentially** with temperature. At 420 K, $T/T_{NR} = 420/361.3 \approx 1.16$. By the golden rule, TMR may drop to **minutes or seconds** — runaway is imminent.

**(v) Two engineering controls (2 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Install emergency cooling** with redundant pumps and backup power.
2. **Add high-temperature interlock** to inject inhibitor (e.g., hydroquinone) or dump reactor contents into a quench tank above 370 K.
3. (Alternative) Reduce reactant concentration; install a rupture disc sized per DIERS methodology.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a) Explain the Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature (SADT). Discuss its significance in the safe storage and transport of reactive chemicals.** (7 marks)

**(b) A monomethylhydrazine (MMH) storage drum of 200 L capacity contains the substance with the following data:**
- $\rho = 870\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $C_p = 2.7\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $E = 110\ \text{kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H_r = -180\ \text{kJ/mol}$ (decomposition)
- $k_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$

**Compute:**
- (i) The adiabatic temperature rise $\Delta T_{ad}$ (assuming full conversion). (2 marks)
- (ii) The TMR at 300 K. (3 marks)
- (iii) The TMR at 320 K. (2 marks)
- (iv) Based on (ii) and (iii), state the practical safety conclusion. (Marked with full credit if qualitative)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SADT Explanation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** SADT is the lowest ambient temperature at which a self-reactive substance undergoes self-accelerating decomposition within a specified package over a period of 7 days. Below SADT, heat generation by slow decomposition is balanced by heat losses (conduction, convection, radiation) to the surroundings; above SADT, the heat loss becomes insufficient, leading to thermal runaway.

**Significance (5 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. Determines maximum allowable storage temperature for peroxides, nitrates, hydrazines.
2. Critical for UN Dangerous Goods classification (Class 5.2 organic peroxides).
3. Establishes transportation temperature control protocols (refrigerated containers, insulated trailers).
4. Basis for emergency relief device sizing (DIERS methodology uses SADT as input).
5. Compliance with international regulations (IATA, IMDG, ADR for hazardous materials transport).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) $\Delta T_{ad}$ (2 marks):**

For a first-order reaction, using the stoichiometric amount:

$$\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_r \cdot C_0}{\rho \cdot C_p}$$

Assuming $C_0 = \rho/M_{MMH}$. With $M_{MMH} = 46\ \text{g/mol} = 0.046\ \text{kg/mol}$:

$$C_0 = \dfrac{870}{0.046} = 18{,}913\ \text{mol/m}^3$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{ad} & = \dfrac{180{,}000 \times 18{,}913}{870 \times 2700} \\
& = \dfrac{3.404 \times 10^{9}}{2.349 \times 10^{6}} \\
& \approx 1449\ \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This represents the **worst-case theoretical** temperature rise; actual rise is bounded by complete vaporization at the boiling point.

**(ii) TMR at 300 K (3 marks):**

$$q_{gen}(300\text{ K}) = \Delta H_r \cdot k_0 \cdot \exp(-E/RT) \cdot C_0$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{gen} & = 180{,}000 \times 1.2 \times 10^{13} \times \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{110{,}000}{8.314 \times 300}\right) \times 18{,}913 \\[4pt]
& = 180{,}000 \times 1.2 \times 10^{13} \times \exp(-44.10) \times 18{,}913
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $\exp(-44.10) \approx 5.4 \times 10^{-20}$:

$$
q_{gen} \approx 180{,}000 \times 1.2 \times 10^{13} \times 5.4 \times 10^{-20} \times 18{,}913 \approx 22.1\ \text{W/m}^3
$$

Now TMR:

$$
TMR = \dfrac{\rho \, C_p \, R \, T^{2}}{q_{gen} \cdot E} = \dfrac{870 \times 2700 \times 8.314 \times (300)^{2}}{22.1 \times 110{,}000}
$$

$$
TMR = \dfrac{870 \times 2700 \times 8.314 \times 90{,}000}{2.431 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{1.754 \times 10^{12}}{2.431 \times 10^{6}} \approx 7.21 \times 10^{5}\ \text{s} \approx 8.35\ \text{days}
$$

**(iii) TMR at 320 K (2 marks):**

$$
TMR = \dfrac{870 \times 2700 \times 8.314 \times (320)^{2}}{q_{gen}(320) \times 110{,}000}
$$

Compute $q_{gen}(320\ \text{K})$: $\exp(-110000/(8.314 \times 320)) = \exp(-41.36) \approx 1.08 \times 10^{-18}$

$$
q_{gen}(320) \approx 180{,}000 \times 1.2 \times 10^{13} \times 1.08 \times 10^{-18} \times 18{,}913 \approx 44.1\ \text{W/m}^3
$$

$$
TMR(320) = \dfrac{870 \times 2700 \times 8.314 \times 102{,}400}{44.1 \times 110{,}000} \approx \dfrac{1.99 \times 10^{12}}{4.85 \times 10^{6}} \approx 4.11 \times 10^{5}\ \text{s} \approx 4.76\ \text{days}
$$

**(iv) Safety conclusion (full credit for qualitative):**
A 20 K rise in temperature (300 K → 320 K) reduces TMR by **approximately 43%** (from 8.35 days to 4.76 days). This is roughly **half** — consistent with the rule that TMR halves per ~10 K temperature rise. Storage must include temperature control to maintain MMH at least **20 K below its decomposition threshold**.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top Reasons for Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert $C_p$ from kJ/(kg·K) to J/(kg·K) when $E$ is in J/mol. **Deduction: 1–2 marks per part.**
> 2. **Misapplication of Semenov limit:** Applying $T_{crit} = E/(2R)$ even when $T_a$ is comparable to $T_{crit}$. Always use the exact $T_{NR}$ formula in reactor problems.
> 3. **Confusing SADT with auto-ignition temperature:** SADT is for **decomposition**, not combustion auto-ignition.
> 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in derivations — KTU awards **1–2 marks** explicitly for assumptions.
> 5. **No mention of Arrhenius sensitivity:** Failing to explain *why* small temperature changes have large effects (exponential vs linear removal).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Summary — Memorize for KTU Module 2**

- **Runaway reaction** = thermal positive feedback loop where $q_{gen}(T) > q_{rem}(T)$ permanently.
- **Semenov stability criterion:** Tangent point between exponential $q_{gen}$ and linear $q_{rem}$ defines $T_{crit}$.
- **$T_{crit} = E/(2R)$** is the Semenov asymptotic limit when $T_{crit} \gg T_a$.
- **$T_{NR} = T_a + RT_a^{2}/E$** is the operational threshold (Temperature of No Return) — used in real design.
- **SADT ≈ $T_{NR} - 5$ K** is the storage/transportation limit.
- **TMR = $\rho C_p R T^{2}/(q_{gen} \cdot E)$** is the "safety clock" for runaway initiation.
- **TMR halves per 10 K rise** (golden rule for reactive chemicals).
- **Adiabatic temperature rise** $\Delta T_{ad} = -\Delta H C_0/(\rho C_p)$ bounds worst-case severity.
- **Common causes:** loss of cooling, overcharge, contamination, agitator failure, external fire.
- **Defense-in-depth layers:** Inherent safety → Passive engineering → Active engineering → Procedures → Emergency response.
- **Key disasters to cite in answers:** Bhopal (1984), AZF Toulouse (2001), Texas City BP (2005).
- **DiERS methodology** is the KTU-accepted standard for emergency relief vent sizing.
- **Exam trick:** Numerical questions on TMR will almost always involve a temperature increment and a comparison — practice computing the ratio TMR$(T+10)$/TMR$(T)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Pyramid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Pyramid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Fire Pyramid** is a fundamental conceptual model in **Process Safety Engineering** and **Fire Protection Engineering** that hierarchically classifies the four primary dangerous products (or consequences) generated during a combustion event. It is depicted as a four-tiered pyramid, with the **most lethal and most frequently produced hazard forming the broad base** and the **most visually obvious hazard forming the narrow apex**.

Mathematically expressed as a ranked order of mortality contribution:

$$
H_{\text{total}} = H_{\text{toxic}} + H_{\text{heat}} + H_{\text{smoke}} + H_{\text{flame}}
$$

where $H_{\text{toxic}} \gg H_{\text{heat}} > H_{\text{smoke}} > H_{\text{flame}}$ in terms of fatalities per fire incident in industrial process plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Fire Pyramid is the inverse-hierarchy representation of fire hazards where the deadliest byproducts of combustion form the foundation. The model is pivotal in determining **active vs. passive fire protection** system design in chemical process industries (CPI).

### 1.2 The Four Tiers of the Fire Pyramid (Apex to Base)

| Position | Tier | Hazard | Visibility | Primary Physiological Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1 (Apex)** | Flame / Fire | Visible flames | High (optical) | Direct burns, ignition of clothing |
| **2** | Smoke | Particulate + vapor | Moderate (optical) | Asphyxiation, panic, disorientation |
| **3** | Heat | Thermal radiation + convection | Infrared (partially visible) | Hyperthermia, respiratory tract burns |
| **4 (Base)** | Toxic Gases | CO, HCN, CO₂, NOx, HCl, H₂S | **Invisible** | Chemical asphyxiation, cellular poisoning |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **volcano erupting**:
- The **molten lava** (flame at the apex) is the most dramatic and visible part, but surprisingly, **fewer people die from touching lava** because they can *see* it and run away.
- The **smoke and ash cloud** (second tier) blocks vision — disoriented victims stumble.
- The **radiant heat** (third tier) is invisible but cooks the skin and lungs.
- The **silent, invisible toxic gases** (base of pyramid) — like carbon monoxide, hydrogen cyanide, and sulfur dioxide — pour out invisibly and account for the **majority of fatalities**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** In a process plant fire, the *silent killer is the base of the pyramid.* A worker may never see flames yet be overcome by carbon monoxide within 3–5 minutes. This is why **toxic gas detection systems** are *equally* (or more) important than flame detectors.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Carbon Monoxide (CO) lethal concentration (LCt₅₀):** ~**1500 ppm · 30 min** (occupational threshold).
- **Hydrogen Cyanide (HCN) LCt₅₀:** ~**100–200 ppm · 30 min**.
- **Heat flux for sustained skin pain:** ~**1.0 kW/m²** (at this level, unprotected skin suffers 2nd-degree burns in <30 s).
- **Auto-ignition temperature (AIT) range for common hydrocarbons:** **200 °C – 450 °C**.
- **Standard smoke obscuration threshold for evacuation:** Visibility ≤ **10 m** (NFPA 101).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of the Pyramid

The Fire Pyramid is constructed using three governing principles of combustion toxicology:

**Principle 1 — Inverse Visibility–Lethality Rule**
The less visible the combustion byproduct, the more lethal it tends to be. This is because:
- The human body has no chemoreceptor for CO or HCN (they are *odorless*, *colorless*).
- The body has a strong withdrawal reflex for flame (visible) and heat (infrared) — but not for toxic gases.
- Hence, victims are incapacitated before they can self-rescue.

**Principle 2 — Volumetric Proportionality**
The volume (and mass) of each byproduct produced per kilogram of fuel consumed follows a *fixed stoichiometric order*:

$$
V_{\text{toxic}} > V_{\text{smoke}} > V_{\text{heat}} > V_{\text{visible flame}}
$$

For example, **1 kg of polyurethane foam** (common in process plant insulation) produces approximately:
- ~**250 m³ of CO** (toxic)
- ~**15 m³ of smoke particulates**
- ~**6 MJ of radiant heat**
- A modest luminous flame zone

**Principle 3 — Time-to-Incapacitation Hierarchy**
Time to reach 50% incapacitation ($t_{I,50}$) for an average adult at standard fire loadings:

| Hazard | $t_{I,50}$ (approx.) |
|---|---|
| Flame (direct contact) | 15 – 30 s |
| Heat (radiation 5 kW/m²) | 30 – 60 s |
| Smoke (obscuration) | 60 – 180 s |
| Toxic Gas (CO at 5000 ppm) | **< 30 s** (but *unconscious* in 1–2 min) |

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Pyramid Shape

The pyramid (rather than a flat list) is geometrically meaningful:

- **Base = largest area = greatest cumulative threat** across all fire scenarios combined.
- **Apex = smallest area = statistically least-fatal hazard** in aggregate, though locally intense.
- The geometric area represents *cumulative risk frequency × consequence severity* across the population of fire incidents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Process plant designers read the pyramid *upside down* — the *base* (toxic gases) drives the sizing of HVAC, gas detection, and SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus) provisions.

### 2.3 Distinguishing the Fire Pyramid from Related Models

| Model | Elements | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Fire Triangle** | Fuel + Oxygen + Heat | Conditions *necessary* for ignition |
| **Fire Tetrahedron** | Triangle + Uninhibited Chain Reaction | Conditions for *sustained* combustion |
| **Fire Pyramid** | Toxic Gases + Heat + Smoke + Flame | Ranking of *post-ignition hazards* to life |

The Fire Pyramid is **complementary, not a substitute**, for the Fire Triangle. The Triangle explains *how a fire starts*; the Pyramid explains *how a fire kills*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cumulative hazard | $H_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} n_i \cdot L_i$ | dimensionless | Total risk scoring |
| Haber's rule (toxic dose) | $D = C^n \cdot t$ | ppm·min | Toxic gas incapacitation time |
| Smoke obscuration | $D_o = \frac{1}{L} \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | m⁻¹ (optical density/m) | Visibility cutoff for evacuation |
| Radiant heat flux (point source) | $q'' = \frac{\varepsilon \sigma T^4}{4\pi r^2} \cdot \tau$ | kW/m² | Thermal hazard distance |
| Mass burning rate (pool fire) | $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$ | kg/m²·s | Pool fire thermal output |
| Yield of CO per fuel | $Y_{\text{CO}} = \frac{\text{kg CO}}{\text{kg fuel burned}}$ | kg/kg | Toxic gas inventory estimate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this for the KTU ESE:** In every 10 fire fatalities in process industries, **~6 to 7 are due to toxic gas inhalation**, 2 due to heat/burns, and ≤ 1 due to direct flame contact. This is the empirical justification for the pyramid base.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire & Gas (F&G) detector mapping** in refineries — detectors placed based on *gas dispersion models*, not just flame detection.
- **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) system design** — toxic gas triggers isolation logic even before flame detection.
- **Occupational Health & Safety** — selection of respiratory PPE.
- **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** — consequence modeling in HAZOP/LOPA.
- **NFPA / OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** compliance — Process Safety Management (PSM).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cumulative Hazard Index ($H_{\text{total}}$)

Let us derive the analytical framework that justifies the pyramid's base-weighted structure.

**Step 1 — Define per-hazard lethality weight.**

For each tier $i$ of the pyramid, define the *fractional mortality contribution* $f_i$:

$$
f_i = \frac{N_i}{N_{\text{total}}}
$$

where $N_i$ = number of fatalities attributable to hazard $i$ (toxic gas, heat, smoke, flame), and $N_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{toxic}} + N_{\text{heat}} + N_{\text{smoke}} + N_{\text{flame}}$.

**Step 2 — Empirically observed weightings (NFPA / Factory Mutual data).**

For typical process plant hydrocarbon fires:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{toxic}} &\approx 0.65 \\
f_{\text{heat}}   &\approx 0.20 \\
f_{\text{smoke}}  &\approx 0.10 \\
f_{\text{flame}}  &\approx 0.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the cumulative hazard index.**

$$
H_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot f_i
$$

where $w_i$ is the engineering design weight (consequence severity multiplier). For toxic gas, $w_{\text{toxic}} = 4$ (invisible, fastest incapacitation). For flame, $w_{\text{flame}} = 1$.

**Step 4 — Compute normalized risk score.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{total}} &= 4(0.65) + 3(0.20) + 2(0.10) + 1(0.05) \\
                 &= 2.60 + 0.60 + 0.20 + 0.05 \\
                 &= 3.45
\end{aligned}
$$

Toxic gas share of total weighted hazard:

$$
\text{Toxic share} = \frac{4 \times 0.65}{3.45} = \frac{2.60}{3.45} \approx 0.754 \; (75.4\%)
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Toxic gases account for} \approx 75\% \text{ of the weighted hazard.}}
$$

This numerical result is the engineering justification for the broad pyramid base.

### 3.2 Derivation of Toxic Dose (Haber's Rule Adaptation)

For an instantaneous release of toxic combustion gas at concentration $C$ (ppm), the toxic dose $D$ received over exposure time $t$ (minutes) is:

$$
D = C \cdot t \quad \text{(linear form, valid for } n = 1\text{)}
$$

For CO specifically (with Haber's coefficient $n \approx 1.0$ for short exposures):

$$
D_{\text{CO}} = C \cdot t
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**
At $C = 1500$ ppm (the LCt₅₀ for CO) and $t = 30$ min:

$$
D_{\text{CO}} = 1500 \times 30 = 45{,}000 \; \text{ppm·min}
$$

This is the *lethal dose* — meaning that exposure at half the concentration (750 ppm) for twice the duration (60 min) is **equally lethal**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fire Pyramid vs. Other Combustion Models

| Analytical Dimension | Fire Triangle | Fire Tetrahedron | **Fire Pyramid** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stage modeled | Pre-ignition | Sustained combustion | **Post-ignition consequences** |
| Number of elements | 3 | 4 | **4 (hazard tiers)** |
| Use case | Fire prevention | Fire suppression | **Loss prevention / life safety** |
| Quantitative input | Stoichiometric ratios | Reaction kinetics | **Lethality statistics + toxicology** |
| Application domain | Fire science fundamentals | Fire extinguishing agent selection | **Process plant safety design** |
| Time frame | t = 0 (ignition) | Sustained t | **t = τ (post-ignition fatalities)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fire Pyramid — Conceptual Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    apex["FLAME AND FIRE - VISIBLE - DIRECT BURN INJURIES - SMALLEST VOLUME PRODUCED"]
    tier2["SMOKE - PARTLY VISIBLE - ASPHYXIATION AND PANIC - MODERATE VOLUME"]
    tier3["HEAT - INFRARED - RADIANT AND CONVECTIVE BURNS - LARGE VOLUME"]
    base["TOXIC GASES CO HCN HCL CO2 H2S - INVISIBLE - ODORLESS - CHEMICAL ASPHYXIATION - LARGEST VOLUME - PRIMARY FIRE FATALITY CAUSE"]

    apex --> tier2
    tier2 --> tier3
    tier3 --> base

    base -- "75 percent of fatalities" --> base
    tier3 -- "20 percent of fatalities" --> tier3
    tier2 -- "10 percent of fatalities" --> tier2
    apex -- "5 percent of fatalities" --> apex

    sideA["Detection Priority Level 1 SCBA Mandatory"] -.-> base
    sideB["Detection Priority Level 2 Thermal PPE"] -.-> tier3
    sideC["Detection Priority Level 3 Visibility Mgt"] -.-> tier2
    sideD["Detection Priority Level 4 FR Clothing"] -.-> apex
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fire Hazard Mitigation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    processUnit["Process Unit Reactor or Storage Tank"]
    fireEvent["Fire Initiation Event"]

    processUnit --> fireEvent

    fireEvent --> flameGen["Flame and Visible Fire"]
    fireEvent --> smokeGen["Smoke and Particulates"]
    fireEvent --> heatGen["Radiant and Convective Heat"]
    fireEvent --> toxicGen["Toxic Gas Release CO HCN HCL"]

    flameGen --> detectorA["Flame Detector UV or IR"]
    smokeGen --> detectorB["Smoke Detector Optical"]
    heatGen --> detectorC["Heat Detector Rate Compensated"]
    toxicGen --> detectorD["Toxic Gas Detector Electrochemical"]

    detectorA --> esdLogic["Fire and Gas Logic Solver"]
    detectorB --> esdLogic
    detectorC --> esdLogic
    detectorD --> esdLogic

    esdLogic --> isolation["Emergency Shutdown ESD Valve Closure"]
    esdLogic --> alarm["Plant Wide Alarm"]
    esdLogic --> evac["Safe Evacuation and SCBA Activation"]
    esdLogic --> firewater["Deluge and Water Spray System"]
```

### 4.3 Triangle → Tetrahedron → Pyramid: Conceptual Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tri["Fire Triangle FUEL OXYGEN HEAT - Conditions for Ignition"]
    tetra["Fire Tetrahedron Triangle PLUS Uninhibited Chain Reaction - Sustained Combustion"]
    pyr["Fire Pyramid TOXIC GAS HEAT SMOKE FLAME - Ranking of Lethal Hazards"]

    tri --> tetra
    tetra --> pyr

    noteTri["Stage 1 Pre Ignition"] -.-> tri
    noteTetra["Stage 2 Sustained Fire"] -.-> tetra
    notePyr["Stage 3 Post Ignition Life Safety"] -.-> pyr
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The three Mermaid blocks collectively form a single **Fire Hazard Ontology** — moving from *cause* (triangle) to *sustainment* (tetrahedron) to *consequence* (pyramid). The KTU examiner often asks students to compare these — ensure you can articulate the stage each model represents.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Fire Pyramid. Why is it shaped as a pyramid and not a flat list?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Fire Pyramid is a four-tiered conceptual model used in process safety engineering to rank the hazards produced during a fire by their relative lethality to humans. The four tiers from apex to base are: **flame, smoke, heat, and toxic gases** (3 marks for correct definition + identification of all four tiers).

The pyramid shape is used because the **broad base represents the hazard responsible for the largest number of fatalities (toxic gases)**, while the narrow apex represents the least-fatal hazard statistically (direct flame contact). The geometric area is proportional to the cumulative *risk frequency × consequence severity*. This visual ranking ensures engineers prioritize **toxic gas detection, ventilation, and respiratory protection** at the design stage — making the pyramid a **design-decision tool**, not merely a descriptive diagram.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the four elements of the Fire Pyramid and state which one accounts for the highest fraction of fire-related fatalities in process industries.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four elements of the Fire Pyramid (from base to apex) are:
1. Toxic gases (base) — CO, HCN, HCl, H₂S (1 mark)
2. Heat (1 mark)
3. Smoke (0.5 mark)
4. Flame (0.5 mark)

**Toxic gases account for the highest fraction (~65–75%) of fire-related fatalities** in process industries, primarily because they are odorless, colorless, and incapacitate victims before they can self-rescue (1 mark for stating toxic gases as the answer with reasoning).

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)**

> Choose **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the Fire Pyramid in detail. Compare it with the **Fire Triangle** and **Fire Tetrahedron**, highlighting the distinct stage of fire development that each model addresses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refinery storage tank containing hexane catches fire. The hexane (molecular formula **C₆H₁₄**, molecular weight ≈ 86 g/mol) burns incompletely due to limited air supply. Estimate the **mass of carbon monoxide (CO) generated per kilogram of hexane burned**, and discuss why this makes the Fire Pyramid's base tier the dominant hazard in such a scenario. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The Fire Pyramid is a **post-ignition consequence model** used in process safety engineering. It ranks the four major products of a fire in order of lethality contribution: **toxic gases (base), heat, smoke, and flame (apex)**. The pyramid structure communicates that the most invisible hazard is also the most lethal, which has direct implications for **fire and gas (F&G) detection system design** in chemical process industries (1.5 marks).

**Comparison with the Fire Triangle:** The Fire Triangle (Fuel + Oxygen + Heat) describes the *pre-ignition* conditions — i.e., the three necessary elements that must coexist for a fire to initiate. It is primarily used in **fire prevention** and **ignition source control** (e.g., hot-work permits, bonding/grounding) (1.5 marks).

**Comparison with the Fire Tetrahedron:** The Fire Tetrahedron adds a fourth element — the **uninhibited chemical chain reaction** — to the triangle. It describes the *sustained combustion* phase, where free radicals (H•, OH•) propagate the reaction. Removing any one vertex (e.g., via a chemical suppressant like dry chemical powder) extinguishes the fire. The Tetrahedron is the conceptual basis of **fire suppression agent selection** (1.5 marks).

**Distinct stage addressed:**

| Model | Stage | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Fire Triangle | Pre-ignition | Fire prevention |
| Fire Tetrahedron | Sustained combustion | Suppression |
| **Fire Pyramid** | **Post-ignition** | **Life safety, PPE, F&G mapping** (1.5 marks for tabular comparison + conclusion) |

The three models are **complementary** — each addresses a different temporal stage of a fire event.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric equation for incomplete combustion.**

For hexane burning with limited oxygen:

$$
2\,C_6H_{14} + 13\,O_2 \rightarrow 12\,CO + 14\,H_2O
$$

(1.5 marks for correct balanced equation)

**Step 2 — Convert to a per-kg-of-hexane basis.**

Moles of hexane per kg:

$$
n_{\text{hexane}} = \frac{1000 \; \text{g}}{86 \; \text{g/mol}} = 11.628 \; \text{mol}
$$

Moles of CO produced per mole of hexane = $12/2 = 6$ (1 mark).

$$
n_{\text{CO}} = 11.628 \times 6 = 69.77 \; \text{mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute mass of CO.**

$$
m_{\text{CO}} = 69.77 \; \text{mol} \times 28 \; \text{g/mol} = 1953.5 \; \text{g} \approx 1.95 \; \text{kg CO per kg hexane}
$$

(1.5 marks for numerical computation)

**Step 4 — Discussion (3 marks).**

This result shows that **~1.95 kg of carbon monoxide** is produced per kilogram of hexane under *incomplete* combustion. Since CO is:

- **Colorless and odorless** — undetectable by human senses
- **Lethal at ~1500 ppm · 30 min** — easily achieved in a confined refinery space
- **Faster-acting** than heat or smoke due to hemoglobin binding (240× affinity vs. O₂)

The Fire Pyramid's base tier (toxic gases) becomes the **dominant fatality driver** in this scenario. The CO will fill low-lying areas (CO is slightly denser than air at high concentrations), travel along the wind direction, and incapacitate operators in the **nearest control room or adjacent unit** — *even without any direct exposure to flame*. This is why **fixed toxic gas detectors with cascading ESD logic** are mandatory under API RP 2510 and NFPA 30.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **Fire Pyramid** and explain why **toxic gases form its base**. Discuss the implications for the **design philosophy of Fire and Gas (F&G) detection systems** in a process plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process operator in a chemical plant is exposed to a release of **hydrogen cyanide (HCN)** at a concentration of **180 ppm** during an emergency. Using **Haber's rule**, calculate the **toxic dose received** in 20 minutes of exposure and comment on whether the dose is potentially lethal. Given: **LCt₅₀ of HCN ≈ 150 ppm·min** for short-duration exposure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Diagram description (2 marks):** Draw a four-tiered pyramid. From apex to base: Flame → Smoke → Heat → Toxic Gases. Annotate each tier with its visibility status and primary hazard mechanism.

**Why toxic gases form the base (2 marks):** Toxic gases (CO, HCN, HCl, NOx) are the most abundant combustion products, are invisible and odorless, and statistically account for ~65–75% of fire fatalities. The geometric base of the pyramid represents this *cumulative large-magnitude risk*. Haber's rule and clinical data confirm their high lethality per unit exposure.

**Implications for F&G design (3 marks):**
- **Detection philosophy must be weighted toward the base tier** — electrochemical and IR toxic gas detectors must be placed at *high-occupancy* and *low-lying* locations (since many combustion gases are denser than air).
- **Detector placement density** for toxic gases should be *higher* than for flame detectors (per API RP 1169 and ISA 84.00.07).
- **Logic solver hierarchy** in F&G systems should treat toxic gas trips as **higher-priority ESD triggers** than flame trips in many SIL-rated configurations.
- **PPE provisioning** (SCBA) for personnel is mandated based on this risk weighting.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — State Haber's rule (1 mark).**

$$
D = C \times t
$$

where $C$ = concentration (ppm), $t$ = exposure time (min), and $D$ = toxic dose (ppm·min).

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark).**

$$
D = 180 \; \text{ppm} \times 20 \; \text{min} = 3600 \; \text{ppm·min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with LCt₅₀ (2 marks).**

$$
\frac{D}{\text{LCt}_{50}} = \frac{3600}{150} = 24
$$

The dose received is **24 times** the LCt₅₀ threshold.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (3 marks).**

The exposure is **catastrophically lethal**. The operator will experience:
- Rapid respiratory failure (within 1–3 minutes)
- Cellular asphyxiation (cytochrome oxidase inhibition)
- Cardiac arrest
- Death before any medical intervention can be effective

This numerical example reinforces the **Fire Pyramid's base tier** as the dominant fatality driver — and underscores the necessity of **immediate area isolation, SCBA deployment, and toxic-gas-triggered ESD logic** in process plant safety instrumented systems (SIS).

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Fire Pyramid Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the Fire Triangle with the Fire Pyramid** — many students mix them up. Triangle = *pre-ignition*; Pyramid = *post-ignition hazards*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Writing the pyramid upside down** in the diagram — the **base must be the broadest tier** (toxic gases), and the apex the narrowest (flame). Drawing it inverted costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting units in dose calculations** — Haber's rule $D = C \times t$ requires **ppm and minutes**. Writing $D$ without units loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the engineering implication** — merely listing the four tiers is worth only 1–2 of the 7 marks. Examiners expect the *design or operational implication* (e.g., F&G mapping, PPE).
> 5. **Skipping the complementary nature** of Triangle → Tetrahedron → Pyramid. Always state that they cover *different temporal stages* of a fire event.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Fire Pyramid** is a **post-ignition** consequence model with four tiers: **Toxic Gases (base) → Heat → Smoke → Flame (apex)**.
- ✅ The pyramid shape encodes a **statistical reality**: ~65–75% of fire fatalities in process industries are due to **toxic gas inhalation** (CO, HCN, HCl).
- ✅ Toxic gases are **invisible, odorless, and fast-acting** — the body has no chemoreceptor to detect them.
- ✅ The Fire Pyramid is **complementary to** (not a replacement for) the **Fire Triangle** (pre-ignition) and **Fire Tetrahedron** (sustained combustion).
- ✅ Haber's rule for toxic dose: $D = C \times t$ (ppm·min). The **LCt₅₀ of CO ≈ 1500 ppm · 30 min**; **LCt₅₀ of HCN ≈ 100–200 ppm · 30 min**.
- ✅ Engineering implications: **F&G detector placement, ESD logic priority, SCBA provisioning, PHA/HAZOP consequence modeling**.
- ✅ For KTU ESE: always draw a **neat pyramid** with clear labels, and conclude with the **dominant hazard (toxic gases)** and its **design consequence**.
- ✅ Numerical examples (CO from hexane, HCN dose calculation) are **favourite 7-mark sub-questions** in Part B.
- ✅ Standard references (per KTU syllabus): **Crowl & Louvar (Chemical Process Safety), Mannan (Lees' Loss Prevention), NFPA 921, API RP 2510**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fire Extinguishers and Their Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Fire Extinguishers and Their Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Fire Extinguisher?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A *fire extinguisher* is a portable, pressurized, active fire-protection device containing an extinguishing agent (water, foam, dry chemical, CO₂, wet chemical, clean agent, or metal powder) that is manually discharged to extinguish or control incipient-stage fires by removing one or more elements of the **Fire Tetrahedron** (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, Chemical Chain Reaction).

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
Imagine a pressure cooker with a narrow nozzle. Inside, instead of rice, you store a chemical that can "suffocate" or "cool down" a fire. When you press the handle, the stored gas pushes the agent out through a hose and nozzle directly onto the fire — just like squeezing a giant water pistol, but the "water" is engineered specifically to break the fire triangle. That portable pressure vessel is your fire extinguisher.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fire Tetrahedron** (modern extension of the Fire Triangle): For extinguishment, we must disrupt at least one of:
> 1. **Fuel** (combustible material)
> 2. **Oxygen** (oxidizer, normally air, ~21 % O₂)
> 3. **Heat** (energy above ignition temperature)
> 4. **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** (free radicals sustaining combustion)

### 1.2 Classes of Fire (IS 2190 / NFPA 10 / BS EN 3)

| Class | Fuel Type | Common Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids & gases | Petrol, diesel, LPG, solvents, oils |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases (under pressure) | Methane, propane, hydrogen |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, titanium, zirconium |
| **Class E / K** | Electrical / Cooking oils | Live electrical equipment, kitchen oils |

> [!NOTE]
> In **IS 2190 (India)** and **BS EN 3 (UK)**, "Class E" denotes *Electrical fires*, while **NFPA (USA)** designates electrical fires simply under their original class with a "Class C" electrical symbol. **Class K** is specifically for *cooking oil/fat fires* (kitchen).

### 1.3 Why Understanding Extinguisher Types Matters in Process Safety

In process industries (refineries, petrochemical plants, pharma), an inappropriate extinguisher can:
- **Spread** the fire (e.g., water on oil → boilover / BLEVE risk)
- **Cause explosion** (e.g., water on Class C gas fires)
- **Conduct electricity** back to the operator (water on live electrical gear)
- **React violently** with reactive metals (water on burning magnesium → hydrogen gas + flame)

Hence the **right selection** of extinguisher is a *first-line engineering control* under the **Hierarchy of Controls** (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of Each Major Extinguisher Type

- **Water Extinguisher (Stored Pressure Type)**
  - *Mechanism:* **Cooling** (removes heat) + slight **smothering** (steam displaces O₂).
  - *Application:* Class A only.
  - *Limitation:* ❌ Never on oil, electrical, or metal fires.

- **Foam (AFFF — Aqueous Film Forming Foam)**
  - *Mechanism:* Forms a film that **seals the fuel surface**, blocking oxygen and preventing re-ignition. Also provides some cooling.
  - *Application:* Class A & B (especially hydrocarbon fires).
  - *Limitation:* ❌ Not for live electrical, ❌ not for polar solvents (use AR-AFFF / alcohol-resistant foam).

- **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) — ABC Type (Monoammonium Phosphate, MAP)**
  - *Mechanism:* Interrupts the **chemical chain reaction** of combustion; MAP melts and forms a glassy barrier that also excludes oxygen.
  - *Application:* Class A, B, C, and *near* electrical fires.
  - *Most versatile* extinguisher for process plants.

- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**
  - *Mechanism:* **Smothering** — discharges cold (−78 °C) gaseous CO₂ that displaces oxygen and cools the fire.
  - *Application:* Class B & Electrical (preferred for switchgear rooms, server rooms).
  - *Limitation:* No post-fire residual protection (fire can re-ignite).

- **Wet Chemical (Potassium Acetate / Citrate)**
  - *Mechanism:* **Saponification** — reacts with hot cooking oil to form a soapy foam layer that seals and cools.
  - *Application:* Class K (F in BS EN 3) — commercial kitchens.

- **Clean Agent (HFC-227ea, FK-5-1-12 / Novec 1230)**
  - *Mechanism:* **Chemical inhibition** + cooling, leaves no residue, electrically non-conductive.
  - *Application:* Server rooms, control rooms, museums, aviation.

- **Metal / Special Dry Powder (Class D)**
  - *Mechanism:* Smothers and forms an oxygen-excluding crust specific to the burning metal (e.g., **sodium chloride (NaCl)** for Na/Mg fires; **copper powder** for lithium fires).
  - *Application:* Class D only.

### 2.2 Colour-Coding Standard (IS 2190 / BIS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under **IS 2190 : 1992 (India)**, the entire **bottle is painted a single signal colour** corresponding to the agent. (This differs from the older BS / European convention which uses a *red body* with a *colour band*.)

| Extinguisher Type | Body Colour (IS 2190) | Agent |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Water | **Signal Red** | Water |
| Foam | **Cream** | AFFF |
| Dry Powder (ABC) | **Red** (with white band historically) | Monoammonium Phosphate |
| Dry Powder (BC) | **Red** | Sodium Bicarbonate / Potassium Bicarbonate |
| CO₂ | **Signal Red** (black band historically) | Carbon Dioxide |
| Wet Chemical | **Red** (with yellow band) | Potassium Acetate |
| Class D Powder | **Red** (with blue band) | Metal-specific powders |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern **BS EN 3 / IS 15683** convention: All extinguisher bodies are **fire-engine red**, with **5–10 %** of the surface in a *colour-coded band* identifying the agent. This is now the universally accepted standard.

### 2.3 Extinguisher Markings & Rating

Each extinguisher carries a **rating** that numerically indicates its effectiveness:
- **Class A rating:** e.g., 3A = equivalent to 2.5 gallons of water (UL 711 test).
- **Class B rating:** e.g., 80B = extinguishable square-foot area of a standard heptane pan fire.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, remember the *minimum* portable extinguisher rating: at least **2A** for ordinary hazards and **4A** for severe hazards per **NFPA 10**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas and engineering parameters relevant to fire-extinguisher sizing and discharge.

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Discharge Time | $t_{d} = \dfrac{V_{agent}}{Q_{flow}}$ | $t_{d}$ = discharge duration [s], $V_{agent}$ = agent volume [m³], $Q_{flow}$ = mass flow rate [kg/s] |
| Agent Mass for Water | $m = \rho_{w} \cdot V_{w}$ where $\rho_{w} = 1000 \;\mathrm{kg/m^{3}}$ | Density of water **≈ 1000 kg/m³** at 4 °C |
| CO₂ Mass Required | $m_{CO_2} = \dfrac{n \cdot M_{CO_2}}{V_{m}}$ | $M_{CO_2} = 44 \;\mathrm{g/mol}$, $V_{m} = 22.4 \;\mathrm{L/mol}$ at STP |
| Minimum O₂ to Sustain Combustion | $C_{O_2,min} \approx 14\;\text{to}\;16\;\mathrm{\%}$ v/v | Below this, most hydrocarbon fires self-extinguish |
| Foam Expansion Ratio | $ER = \dfrac{V_{foam}}{V_{liquid}}$ | AFFF typically **6:1 to 10:1**; High-expansion foams **>200:1** |
| Cooling Capacity (Water) | $Q = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | $c_{p,water} = 4.18 \;\mathrm{kJ/kg\cdot K}$ |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization (Water) | $L_{v} = 2260 \;\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ | At 100 °C, **1 kg water absorbs ~2260 kJ** upon vaporization |
| Discharge Range (Water Jet) | $R = k \cdot \sqrt{P_{gauge}}$ | $k$ ≈ nozzle constant; typical **6–10 m** for 9 L extinguisher |
| Hydrostatic Test Pressure | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{working}$ | As per **IS 2190 / TPED** for cylinder safety |

> [!WARNING]
> Never use the vertical bar `|` in markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation (e.g., $c_{p,water} = 4.18 \;\mathrm{kJ/kg\cdot K}$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Plant Layout (PDIL / OISD 118):** Extinguisher placement every **15 m** along escape routes; **1 extinguisher per 200 m²** of process area.
- **Offshore Platforms (API RP 14G):** CO₂ flooding systems for machinery spaces; dry powder for well-head.
- **Hazardous Area Classification (IS 5571 / IEC 60079):** Extinguishers in Zone 1/2 must be **anti-static, spark-resistant**, and certified for the gas group.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Discharge Duration and Effective Coverage

**Problem Context:** A 9 L stored-pressure water extinguisher is pressurized to **10 bar** (gauge) with N₂ at 20 °C. The nozzle has a discharge coefficient $C_{d} = 0.85$ and an orifice area of **2.5 cm²**. Determine:
1. The **mass flow rate** at the instant of discharge.
2. The **total discharge time**.
3. The **cooling energy** delivered to the fire assuming all water is vaporized.

> [!NOTE]
> Apply the **Bernoulli equation with discharge coefficient** for a pressurized tank discharging to atmosphere.

**Step 1 — Convert gauge pressure to absolute and find driving pressure.**

$$
P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 101.325 \;\mathrm{kPa} + (10 \times 100) \;\mathrm{kPa} = 1101.325 \;\mathrm{kPa}
$$

$$
\Delta P = P_{gauge} = 1000 \;\mathrm{kPa} = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \;\mathrm{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the orifice flow equation.**

The ideal volumetric flow rate through a sharp-edged orifice is:

$$
Q_{ideal} = C_{d} \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

Substituting values (water density $\rho = 1000 \;\mathrm{kg/m^{3}}$, $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \;\mathrm{m^{2}}$):

$$
Q_{ideal} = 0.85 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{6}}{1000}}
$$

$$
Q_{ideal} = 0.85 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2000}
$$

$$
Q_{ideal} = 0.85 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 44.721
$$

$$
Q_{ideal} \approx 9.50 \times 10^{-3} \;\mathrm{m^{3}/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_{ideal} = 1000 \times 9.50 \times 10^{-3} = 9.50 \;\mathrm{kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Total discharge time for 9 L (= 9 kg) of water.**

$$
t_{d} = \dfrac{V_{w}}{Q_{ideal}} = \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{-3}}{9.50 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.95 \;\mathrm{s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> In practice, the discharge lasts longer (≈ 30–60 s for a 9 L extinguisher) because the propellant gas pressure decays and flow rate decreases. The above is the **initial** flow rate; the integrated average gives a longer practical duration.

**Step 5 — Cooling energy delivered (water heated from 20 °C → 100 °C → fully vaporized).**

Sensible heat (raising water from 20 °C to 100 °C):

$$
Q_{sens} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T = 9 \times 4.18 \times (100 - 20) = 3009.6 \;\mathrm{kJ}
$$

Latent heat of vaporization:

$$
Q_{lat} = m \cdot L_{v} = 9 \times 2260 = 20340 \;\mathrm{kJ}
$$

Total cooling energy:

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{sens} + Q_{lat} = 3009.6 + 20340 = 23349.6 \;\mathrm{kJ} \approx 23.35 \;\mathrm{MJ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 9 L water extinguisher therefore delivers ~**23 MJ** of cooling — enough to absorb the heat release rate of ~1 kg of burning wood for several seconds (heat of combustion of cellulose ≈ **17 MJ/kg**).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Extinguisher by Fire Class

The matrix below is the **decision aid** examiners frequently use to test extinguisher selection logic.

| ↓ Fuel / Hazard → | Class A (Solid) | Class B (Liquid) | Class C (Gas) | Class D (Metal) | Class E (Electrical) | Class K (Cooking Oil) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Water** | ✅ Best | ❌ (Spreads) | ❌ | ❌ (Reacts) | ❌ (Conducts) | ❌ (Splash) |
| **Foam (AFFF)** | ✅ | ✅ Best | ⚠️ Limited | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Dry Powder (ABC)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (Use Class D) | ✅ | ⚠️ |
| **CO₂** | ❌ | ✅ | ⚠️ | ❌ | ✅ Best | ❌ |
| **Wet Chemical** | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Best |
| **Class D Powder** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Best | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Clean Agent** | ⚠️ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ⚠️ |

**Legend:** ✅ = Recommended    ⚠️ = Use with caution    ❌ = Do NOT use

### 3.3 Derivation: CO₂ Quantity Required for Flooding a Switchgear Room

**Given:** A switchgear room of volume $V = 60 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}$ requires inertion to suppress a fire by reducing O₂ from 21 % to **14 %**.

**Step 1 — Required O₂ displacement (volumetric).**

$$
\Delta C_{O_2} = (21 - 14)\% = 7\% = 0.07
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of CO₂ needed (assuming ideal mixing and 100 % displacement efficiency).**

$$
V_{CO_2} = \Delta C_{O_2} \cdot V_{room} = 0.07 \times 60 = 4.2 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass using ideal gas law at standard conditions.**

At STP, $V_{m} = 22.4 \;\mathrm{L/mol}$ and $M_{CO_2} = 44 \;\mathrm{g/mol}$.

$$
n = \dfrac{V_{CO_2}}{V_{m}} = \dfrac{4200 \;\mathrm{L}}{22.4 \;\mathrm{L/mol}} \approx 187.5 \;\mathrm{mol}
$$

$$
m_{CO_2} = n \cdot M = 187.5 \times 44 = 8250 \;\mathrm{g} = 8.25 \;\mathrm{kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, **NFPA 12** specifies designing for a minimum **34 % v/v CO₂ concentration** for total flooding in spaces — giving a higher real-world mass requirement (~**3× the stoichiometric minimum**) to account for leakage and incomplete mixing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Extinguisher Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fire Detected] --> B{Identify Fire Class}
    B --> C[Class A: Ordinary Combustibles]
    B --> D[Class B: Flammable Liquids]
    B --> E[Class C: Flammable Gases]
    B --> F[Class D: Combustible Metals]
    B --> G[Class E: Electrical]
    B --> H[Class K: Cooking Oils]

    C --> C1[Water Extinguisher - Cooling]
    C --> C2[ABC Dry Powder]
    C --> C3[Foam AFFF]

    D --> D1[CO2 Extinguisher - Smothering]
    D --> D2[ABC Dry Powder]
    D --> D3[Foam AFFF]

    E --> E1[ABC Dry Powder]
    E --> E2[CO2 - Limited]

    F --> F1[Metal-Specific Dry Powder]
    F --> F2[Special Class D Agent]

    G --> G1[CO2 Extinguisher]
    G --> G2[Clean Agent HFC]
    G --> G3[Dry Powder ABC]

    H --> H1[Wet Chemical Potassium Acetate]

    C1 --> X[Discharge via PASS Technique]
    C2 --> X
    C3 --> X
    D1 --> X
    D2 --> X
    D3 --> X
    E1 --> X
    E2 --> X
    F1 --> X
    F2 --> X
    G1 --> X
    G2 --> X
    G3 --> X
    H1 --> X

    X --> Y[Fire Suppressed - Ventilate and Inspect]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Internal Components of a Stored-Pressure Dry Powder Extinguisher

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CYL[Cylinder Body - Seamless Steel or Aluminium] --> P1[Pressurizing Gas - N2 at 10 to 15 bar]
    CYL --> P2[MAP Monoammonium Phosphate - ABC Powder]
    CYL --> P3[Diptube - Polypropylene Riser Pipe]
    P3 --> V[Valve Assembly - Forged Brass]
    V --> PR[Pressure Relief Valve]
    V --> GA[Pressure Gauge]
    V --> LE[Level Indicator]
    V --> SH[Safety Pin and Tamper Seal]
    V --> HS[Handle and Trigger Lever]
    HS --> H[Discharge Hose - Reinforced EPDM]
    H --> NZ[Nozzle - Anti-Static Plastic or Brass]

    subgraph SafetyFeatures [Safety Subsystem]
        PR
        GA
        SH
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: The PASS Operating Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: P - Pull the Safety Pin] --> S2[Step 2: A - Aim Low at the Base of the Fire]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: S - Squeeze the Handle to Discharge]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: S - Sweep Side to Side Across the Fire Base]
    S4 --> S5{Effective Suppression?}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Stand By - Watch for Re-ignition]
    S5 -->|No| S7[Evacuate and Call Fire Brigade]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **PASS** acronym is universally tested in KTU Process Safety papers — memorize it with this cause-effect chain.

### 4.4 Process Flow: Extinguisher Inspection & Maintenance Cycle (OISD / IS 2190)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Monthly Visual Inspection] --> B[Check Pressure Gauge in Green Zone]
    B --> C[Verify Pin and Tamper Seal Intact]
    C --> D[Inspect Hose and Nozzle for Blockage]
    D --> E[Annual Maintenance by Trained Person]
    E --> F[Hydrostatic Test Every 3 to 5 Years]
    F --> G[Re-certify or Condemn Based on Test Result]
    G --> H[Record in Fire Log Book]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List the various classes of fire as per IS 2190 and give one example fuel for each class.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 2190 : 1992**, fires are classified into five categories:

| Class | Fuel Type | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles (solid) | Wood, paper, cloth |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids | Petrol, diesel, acetone |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases (under pressure) | LPG, methane, hydrogen |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, aluminium |
| **Class E** | Electrical equipment (energized) | Live switchgear, motors |

> Each example must be cited correctly for full marks.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the working principle of a CO₂ fire extinguisher. Why is it preferred for electrical fires?**

**Model Answer:**

A CO₂ extinguisher stores liquid carbon dioxide under high pressure (~**58 bar at 20 °C**). On discharge, the liquid CO₂ expands rapidly, cooling to approximately **−78 °C**, forming a snow (dry ice) + gas mixture.

- **Mechanism:** Primarily **smothering** (oxygen displacement — O₂ drops below the 14–16 % minimum required for combustion) and secondarily **cooling** (latent heat of vaporization of CO₂ ≈ **571 kJ/kg**).
- **Why preferred for electrical fires:**
  1. The agent is **electrically non-conductive** (resistivity > 10¹⁶ Ω·cm).
  2. It leaves **zero residue**, preventing damage to delicate electronics.
  3. It does not contaminate clean rooms or server environments.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks on extinguisher questions by (1) confusing **Class C** vs **Class E** definitions, (2) failing to mention the *Fire Tetrahedron* mechanism, and (3) omitting the **PASS** technique. Always state the *extinguishing principle* explicitly.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify the different types of portable fire extinguishers used in process industries. Describe the working principle, application, and limitations of **foam (AFFF)** and **dry chemical powder (ABC)** extinguishers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **PASS technique** for operating a fire extinguisher with a neat sketch. List the safety precautions to be observed while handling extinguishers in a hydrocarbon processing unit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — Types, Foam & DCP:**

Portable extinguishers are classified by the extinguishing agent:

1. **Water type** — cooling action
2. **Foam (AFFF / AR-AFFF)** — smothering + cooling
3. **Dry chemical powder (ABC / BC)** — chemical chain interruption
4. **CO₂** — smothering + cooling
5. **Wet chemical** — saponification
6. **Clean agent** — chemical inhibition
7. **Class D metal powder** — crust formation

**Foam (AFFF):**
- *Principle:* When discharged, the foam concentrate (typically **1–6 %**) mixes with water and aerates through a nozzle to form a **fluoro-protein film** that floats on the fuel surface. The film **seals** the surface, preventing O₂ ingress and flammable-vapour release.
- *Application:* Class A and B fires — hydrocarbon pools, bund fires, tank-farm dike fires.
- *Limitations:* Not effective on **polar solvents** (alcohol, acetone) — the foam is destroyed. For these, **Alcohol-Resistant AFFF (AR-AFFF)** is used. Cannot be used on live electrical equipment.

**Dry Chemical Powder (ABC):**
- *Principle:* The agent is **monoammonium phosphate (MAP)**. On discharge, the fine powder **interrupts the chain reaction** of combustion by reacting with free radicals (H•, OH•). The powder melts at ~**190 °C** and forms a glassy blanket that smothers the fire.
- *Application:* Versatile — Class A, B, C, and **electrical** (where CO₂ is unavailable).
- *Limitations:* **No cooling**, so re-ignition risk is high. Powder cloud reduces visibility. Can cause **malfunction of delicate electronics** due to residual ingress.

**Model Solution (b) — PASS Technique & Safety Precautions:**

**PASS Technique (refer to Section 4.3 diagram):**

| Letter | Action | Detail |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **P** | **Pull** the safety pin | Breaks the tamper seal; frees the lever |
| **A** | **Aim** low at the base of the fire | Hit the fuel, not the flame |
| **S** | **Squeeze** the handle | Releases the agent through the nozzle |
| **S** | **Sweep** side to side | Cover the entire burning area evenly |

**Safety Precautions in a Hydrocarbon Unit:**
1. **Maintain safe distance** — typically **2–3 m** for powder, **1–2 m** for CO₂.
2. **Approach with the wind at your back** to avoid smoke/agent inhalation.
3. **Always have an escape route** behind you.
4. **Never use water** on hydrocarbon pool fires or LPG leaks.
5. Ensure **all electrical isolation** is confirmed only by authorized personnel; do not assume "low voltage" is safe.
6. **Activate the fire alarm** and inform control room **before** attempting extinguishment.
7. Wear full **PPE**: fire proximity suit, BA set (positive pressure SCBA), helmet, gloves, boots.
8. **Standby hose team** must be ready in case of escalation.
9. Do not use extinguishers **beyond their discharge time** (e.g., 30–60 s) — evacuate if not controlled.
10. After use, the extinguisher must be **immediately recharged and tagged** for inspection.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q. B)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and operation of a **CO₂ extinguisher**. Derive an expression for the **mass of CO₂ required to inert a switchgear room** of volume 50 m³ if the O₂ concentration must be reduced from 21 % to 14 % v/v. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Class D** and **wet chemical** extinguishers. Why is water **strictly prohibited** on sodium and magnesium fires? What is the correct extinguishing agent for alkali metal fires? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — CO₂ Extinguisher & Derivation:**

**Construction (refer to Section 4.2 diagram adapted for CO₂):**
- **Seamless steel cylinder** (typically **2 kg, 4.5 kg, 22.5 kg** capacities), painted **signal red**.
- **Syphon (diptube) tube** reaching the bottom to discharge liquid CO₂.
- **Valve head** with **safety pin, lever, and burst-disc pressure relief**.
- **High-pressure hose** (rated **≥ 250 bar**) ending in a **horn-shaped discharge nozzle** (the horn acts as a diffuser, converting liquid to gas and preventing static charge build-up).
- **Pressure gauge** not used (CO₂ pressure depends on temperature, not on contents); weight is the indicator.

**Operation:** Opening the valve releases liquid CO₂; pressure drops below the **5.11 bar triple point** and CO₂ vaporises, expanding **450×** in volume. The horn cools to **−78 °C** due to the Joule–Thomson effect.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Required displacement:

$$
\Delta C_{O_2} = (0.21 - 0.14) = 0.07
$$

Step 2 — CO₂ volume at room conditions:

$$
V_{CO_2} = 0.07 \times 50 = 3.5 \;\mathrm{m^{3}} = 3500 \;\mathrm{L}
$$

Step 3 — Moles (at STP, $V_m = 22.4 \;\mathrm{L/mol}$):

$$
n = \dfrac{3500}{22.4} \approx 156.25 \;\mathrm{mol}
$$

Step 4 — Mass:

$$
m = n \times M_{CO_2} = 156.25 \times 44 = 6875 \;\mathrm{g} \approx 6.88 \;\mathrm{kg}
$$

> Add a **safety factor of 2–3×** as per NFPA 12, giving **~14–21 kg** in practice.

**Model Solution (b) — Class D vs Wet Chemical, Water Prohibition:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Class D (Metal Powder) | Wet Chemical (Class K) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Agent** | NaCl, Cu powder, graphite, Na₂CO₃ | Potassium acetate / citrate |
| **Mechanism** | Smothering + crust formation | Saponification of hot oil |
| **Application** | Burning Mg, Na, K, Al, Ti | Cooking oil / fat fires |
| **Appearance** | Grey to black powder | Pale yellow liquid |
| **Discharge** | Scoop / shovel + extinguisher | Fine mist spray |
| **Colour band** | Blue | Yellow |

**Why Water is Prohibited on Sodium and Magnesium Fires:**

1. **Sodium + Water → Violent exothermic reaction:**

$$
2 \mathrm{Na} + 2 \mathrm{H_2O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH} + \mathrm{H_2}\uparrow + \text{Heat} \;(\Delta H \approx -369 \;\mathrm{kJ/mol})
$$
