The liberated **H₂ gas** ignites spontaneously, causing a **hydrogen explosion** that can throw molten sodium over a wide area.

2. **Magnesium + Water → Similarly violent:**

$$
\mathrm{Mg} + 2 \mathrm{H_2O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg(OH)_2} + \mathrm{H_2}\uparrow + \text{Heat}
$$

The reaction temperature can reach **> 2000 °C**, sufficient to ignite the H₂.

3. **Magnesium also reacts with CO₂:**

$$
2 \mathrm{Mg} + \mathrm{CO_2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{MgO} + \mathrm{C} + \text{Heat}
$$

Hence **CO₂ is also unsuitable** for Mg fires.

**Correct Agent for Alkali Metal Fires:**
- **Sodium:** Dry **sodium chloride (NaCl)** powder or **Class D dry powder** containing **graphite + sodium carbonate**. A **Class D extinguisher** marked for sodium (e.g., Met-L-X for Na, Met-L-K for K) must be used.
- **Magnesium:** **Dry sand** or **specialized Class D powder (e.g., Cu powder, dry graphite)**. **Never water, foam, or CO₂**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- ✅ **Five fire classes (IS 2190):** A (solid), B (liquid), C (gas), D (metal), E (electrical). K = cooking oil.
- ✅ **Fire Tetrahedron:** Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, Chain Reaction — extinguishers remove ≥ 1 element.
- ✅ **Seven extinguisher types:** Water, Foam (AFFF), Dry Powder (ABC), CO₂, Wet Chemical, Clean Agent, Class D.
- ✅ **Most versatile extinguisher** for process industries = **ABC Dry Powder** (MAP, monoammonium phosphate).
- ✅ **Best for live electrical** = **CO₂** (electrically non-conductive, zero residue).
- ✅ **Best for cooking oil** = **Wet Chemical** (saponification).
- ✅ **Best for hydrocarbon pool** = **AFFF foam** (film seal).
- ✅ **NEVER use water** on Class B (oil), C (gas), D (metal), E (electrical), K (cooking oil).
- ✅ **PASS technique:** Pull → Aim → Squeeze → Sweep. Hit the **base** of the fire.
- ✅ **Discharge duration:** typically 30–60 s for portable extinguishers.
- ✅ **Inspection:** Monthly visual; annual maintenance; hydrostatic test every **3–5 years** at **1.5× working pressure**.
- ✅ **Colour band (IS 15683 / BS EN 3):** Cream = foam; Blue = dry powder; Black = CO₂; Yellow = wet chemical.
- ✅ **Minimum O₂ for combustion:** ≈ **14–16 % v/v** — design basis for CO₂ flooding.
- ✅ **Density of water = 1000 kg/m³**; **latent heat of vaporization = 2260 kJ/kg**; **CO₂ triple point = 5.11 bar**.
- ✅ **NFPA 10 placement:** 1 extinguisher per **200 m²** in process areas; max **15 m** travel distance.
- ✅ **Water + Na/Mg = hydrogen explosion** — recall the equations:
  $2\mathrm{Na} + 2\mathrm{H_2O} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{NaOH} + \mathrm{H_2}\uparrow$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Fire Protection Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed Fire Protection Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fixed Fire Protection System (FFPS)** is a permanently installed, engineered fire suppression or control mechanism that is mechanically anchored to a process unit, structure, storage vessel, or building. Unlike portable extinguishers or mobile fire tenders, an FFPS is designed to **automatically detect, actuate, and deliver** a fire-extinguishing medium (water, foam, gas, dry chemical, or powder) at a predetermined flow rate and pressure to a specific hazard zone without relying on human intervention at the moment of fire initiation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Process Safety Engineering, FFPS is treated as the **last line of defense (Engineering Control)** in the hierarchy of fire safety controls, sitting below inherently safer design, process control, and active fire prevention. The system typically comprises four integrated subsystems:

1. **Detection Subsystem** — Heat detectors, smoke detectors, flame (IR/UV) detectors, fusible links, or pneumatic detection tubing.
2. **Actuation / Control Subsystem** — Control valves, solenoid valves, pneumatic actuators, fire pump starters, and deluge valve trip mechanisms.
3. **Distribution Network** — Piping, fittings, hangers, and discharge nozzles.
4. **Suppression Medium Reservoir** — Water tanks, foam concentrate drums, CO₂ cylinders, or dry chemical skids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FFPS belongs to the **"Mitigation"** layer of the safety hierarchy, as per the Swiss Cheese / Bow-Tie Model. Students must understand that FFPS does not *prevent* fire — it **controls and extinguishes** an event that has already escaped primary prevention barriers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Firefighter on Standby"

Imagine your house has a tiny, permanently stationed firefighter standing by the kitchen 24×7, holding a water hose. This firefighter is asleep. The moment the smoke alarm rings (detector), he wakes up, runs to the main valve (actuation), opens it (control), and sprays water through pre-installed pipes (distribution) until the fire is out. He doesn't need anyone to tell him — **the system responds on its own, in seconds**.

That invisible firefighter is your **Fixed Fire Protection System**. It is:
- **Always there** (fixed/permanent).
- **Pre-piped** to the hazard (distribution network).
- **Triggered automatically** by heat, smoke, or flame (detection).
- **Carries a specific weapon** — water, foam, gas, or powder (medium).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

The following metrics are universally used in FFPS design and are **KTU high-yield**:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Design fire duration for water spray | — | **90** | minutes |
| Design fire duration for foam | — | **55–65** | minutes |
| Design fire duration for CO₂ | — | **30–60** | seconds (total flooding) |
| Specific heat of water | $c_p$ | **4.186** | kJ/kg·K |
| Latent heat of vaporization of water | $L_v$ | **2260** | kJ/kg |
| Density of water | $\rho_w$ | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Standard ambient reference | $T_{ref}$ | **288.15 K (15 °C)** | K |
| Discharge time — foam (NFPA 11) | $t_d$ | **≥ 55** | minutes |
| Sprinkler temperature rating (ordinary) | — | **135–170** | °C |
| Sprinkler temperature rating (high) | — | **260–343** | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick:** Water absorbs ~**4.2 kJ** to raise 1 kg by 1 °C, and an additional **2260 kJ** to vaporize 1 kg. This is the *physical reason* water is the world's best firefighting medium — vaporization removes the most heat per unit mass.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Fixed Fire Protection Systems

KTU 2024 Scheme groups FFPS into the following categories, each with a distinct operating philosophy:

#### 2.1.1 Water-Based Fixed Systems
- **Wet Pipe Sprinkler System** — Pipes are permanently charged with pressurized water. Heat from a fire bursts the fusible-link sprinkler head; only the affected head(s) discharge.
- **Dry Pipe Sprinkler System** — Pipes contain pressurized air/nitrogen. A dry-pipe valve holds back water; on sprinkler activation, air escapes, the valve opens, and water flows. Used in **freezing environments** (cold stores, unheated warehouses).
- **Deluge System** — All sprinkler heads are **open** (no fusible element). A separate detection system triggers a deluge valve, flooding the entire protected area simultaneously. Used for **high-hazard** process units (e.g., LPG spheres, transformer yards).
- **Pre-Action System** — Hybrid: pipes are dry, but a pre-action valve is tripped by a detection system. Avoids accidental water discharge; used in **data centers and museums**.
- **Water Spray (Water Mist) System** — Fine droplets at high velocity. Used for **machinery spaces, turbine enclosures, flammable liquid storage**.
- **Monitor / Hydrant Network** — Fixed monitors (water cannons) and hydrant ring mains fed by fire pumps.

#### 2.1.2 Foam-Based Fixed Systems
- **Fixed Foam Pourer System** — Foam chambers mounted on the rim of a storage tank roof pour foam onto the fuel surface.
- **Subsurface Foam Injection** — Foam is injected into the liquid via a deflector at the tank base; foam rises to suppress pool fires.
- **High-Expansion Foam System** — Foam expansion ratio > 200:1, used for **ship engine rooms, aircraft hangars, cable tunnels**.

#### 2.1.3 Gas-Based Fixed Systems (Clean Agent / Inert)
- **CO₂ Total Flooding System** — Discharges CO₂ to reduce O₂ below 12–14%. Used in **electrical rooms, paint booths**.
- **Inert Gas (IG-541, IG-55, IG-100)** — Nitrogen or argon blends; safe for occupied spaces.
- **Clean Chemical Agents (FM-200, Novec 1230)** — Fast-acting, no residue, electronics-safe.

#### 2.1.4 Dry Chemical & Powder Systems
- **ABC Powder** — Monoammonium phosphate; versatile but corrosive.
- **Special Dry Powder (for Class D fires)** — Sodium chloride, copper powder for metal fires (Na, Mg, Ti).
- **Kitchen Hood Suppression (Wet Chemical)** — Potassium acetate / citrate for cooking oil fires.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind System Selection

The choice of FFPS is governed by:

- **Fuel class** (A — solid, B — liquid, C — gas, D — metal, E — electrical, F — cooking oil).
- **Specific hazard** (e.g., transformer oil vs. LNG pool fire).
- **Need for collateral damage control** (electronics, heritage assets).
- **Occupancy** (people present or not).
- **NFPA / IS / OISD standards** (e.g., NFPA 11 for foam, NFPA 12 for CO₂, NFPA 13 for sprinklers, NFPA 15 for water spray, IS 15105 for India).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Link:** The rationale for choosing a particular FFPS can be mapped onto the **Fire Triangle** (Fuel + Heat + Oxygen) and the **Fire Tetrahedron** (adding the chemical chain reaction). Water cools (removes heat). Foam smothers (separates fuel from O₂). CO₂ displaces O₂. Dry chemical interrupts chain reaction. **The medium you pick must attack the dominant combustion leg.**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **the most frequently tested in KTU ESE** for this topic. Memorize the units carefully.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|c|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Physical Meaning} & \textbf{Application} \\
\hline
Q = K \sqrt{P} & \text{Sprinkler discharge (K-factor eqn)} & \text{Sprinkler hydraulics} \\
\hline
A_{op} = \text{f(Area, Density)} & \text{Design area of operation} & \text{Water spray / sprinklers} \\
\hline
Q_{total} = N \times q & \text{Total system flow rate} & \text{Multi-head discharge} \\
\hline
V_{water} = Q_{total} \times t_d & \text{Water demand} & \text{Pump sizing, tank sizing} \\
\hline
C_f = \dfrac{q_{foam}}{q_{water}} \times 100 & \text{Foam concentration (\%)} & \text{Foam proportioning} \\
\hline
q_{foam-solution} = Q \times (1 + C_f) & \text{Foam solution flow} & \text{Foam systems} \\
\hline
V_{tank} = \dfrac{A_{tank} \times R_{app}}{S \times k} & \text{Foam concentrate volume} & \text{Foam storage tanks} \\
\hline
m_{CO_2} = V_{room} \times k_{CO_2} & \text{CO}_2 \text{ design mass} & \text{Total flooding systems} \\
\hline
t_{discharge} = \dfrac{V_{cyl}}{Q_{CO_2}} & \text{CO}_2 \text{ discharge time} & \text{CO}_2 \text{ system design} \\
\hline
\text{Heat absorbed} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T + \dot{m} \, L_v & \text{Water cooling capacity} & \text{Fire load estimation} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Variable Key (units in SI):**
- $Q$ = discharge flow rate (L/min)
- $K$ = K-factor of sprinkler (a constant, dimensionless in L/min·bar^0.5)
- $P$ = pressure at sprinkler (bar)
- $N$ = number of discharging heads
- $t_d$ = design discharge duration (min)
- $C_f$ = foam concentrate percentage (typically **3%** AFFF or **6%** protein foam)
- $A_{tank}$ = tank surface area (m²)
- $R_{app}$ = application rate (L/min·m²)
- $S$ = expansion ratio of foam
- $k$ = proportioning ratio
- $k_{CO_2}$ = NFPA 12 design factor (kg/m³, typically 0.5–0.7 for Class B hazard)
- $V_{cyl}$ = cylinder internal volume (L)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

FFPS design is governed by **legal, insurance, and operational** requirements. In hydrocarbon processing plants (oil refineries, petrochemicals, LNG terminals), FFPS is a **mandatory regulatory requirement** under OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) standards OISD-116 (fire protection), OISD-117 (fire water system), and IS 15105. Failure of an FFPS can lead to:
- Loss of life (insurance and legal liability).
- Uninsured asset loss (a 50,000 m³ tank fire can cause **>500 crore INR** in damage).
- Business interruption.
- Criminal prosecution under the Factories Act / Public Liability Insurance Act.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Example 1 — Sprinkler Hydraulics and Pump Sizing

**Problem Statement (KTU Numerical Style):**
A process plant storage area is protected by a **deluge sprinkler system**. The design requires **20 open-type sprinklers** to operate simultaneously. Each sprinkler has a **K-factor of 80** and the minimum operating pressure is **2.0 bar**. Calculate:
(a) The discharge rate of each sprinkler.
(b) The total water flow rate required.
(c) The total water demand for a design duration of **60 minutes**.
(d) The minimum effective capacity of the firewater storage tank.

**Given:**
- $K = 80$ (L/min·bar$^{0.5}$)
- $P = 2.0$ bar
- $N = 20$ heads
- $t_d = 60$ min
- Foam not used → water only.

#### (a) Discharge per sprinkler

We use the **K-factor equation** for sprinklers (NFPA 13 / IS 15105 convention):

$$
Q = K \sqrt{P}
$$

Substituting $K = 80$ and $P = 2.0$:

$$
Q = 80 \times \sqrt{2.0}
$$

We compute $\sqrt{2.0} = 1.4142$. Therefore:

$$
Q = 80 \times 1.4142
$$

$$
Q = 113.14 \;\; \text{L/min per sprinkler}
$$

#### (b) Total flow rate

$$
Q_{total} = N \times q = 20 \times 113.14
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 2262.8 \;\; \text{L/min}
$$

Converting to m³/hr (1 m³/hr = 16.667 L/min):

$$
Q_{total} = \frac{2262.8}{16.667} = 135.77 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

#### (c) Total water demand

$$
V_{water} = Q_{total} \times t_d
$$

$$
V_{water} = 2262.8 \;\text{L/min} \times 60 \;\text{min}
$$

$$
V_{water} = 135{,}768 \;\text{L} = 135.77 \;\text{m}^3
$$

#### (d) Firewater tank capacity

A safety margin of **+10%** is conventionally added for hose streams, evaporation, and pump pickup losses:

$$
V_{tank} = 1.10 \times 135.77
$$

$$
V_{tank} = 149.35 \;\text{m}^3 \;\;(\text{design value} \approx 150 \;\text{m}^3)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Point:** Examiners look for **units at every step**. Even a correct numerical answer loses marks if units are missing. Always write `L/min`, `m³/hr`, `bar`, etc., next to every intermediate value.

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — Foam Concentrate Sizing for a Cone-Roof Storage Tank

**Problem Statement:**
A cylindrical cone-roof petroleum storage tank has a **diameter of 20 m**. It contains gasoline (Class IB hydrocarbon). The foam pourer system uses **AFFF at 3% concentration**, application rate **R = 5.0 L/min·m²**, foam expansion ratio **S = 6**, and proportioning ratio **k = 0.03**. Calculate:
(a) The surface area of the tank.
(b) The foam solution flow rate.
(c) The foam concentrate flow rate per minute.
(d) The total foam concentrate required for a 55-minute discharge.

**Given:**
- $D = 20$ m
- $C_f = 3\% = 0.03$
- $R_{app} = 5.0$ L/min·m²
- $S = 6$
- $k = 0.03$
- $t_d = 55$ min

#### (a) Surface area of the tank

$$
A_{tank} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{tank} = \frac{\pi \times (20)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 100\pi
$$

$$
A_{tank} = 314.16 \;\text{m}^2
$$

#### (b) Foam solution flow rate

The foam solution is the water + concentrate mixture that the pourer discharges. The application rate is given **per m² of tank surface**:

$$
q_{solution} = A_{tank} \times R_{app}
$$

$$
q_{solution} = 314.16 \times 5.0 = 1570.8 \;\text{L/min}
$$

#### (c) Foam concentrate flow rate per minute

The concentrate is 3% of the solution by volume:

$$
q_{concentrate} = q_{solution} \times k
$$

$$
q_{concentrate} = 1570.8 \times 0.03 = 47.12 \;\text{L/min of concentrate}
$$

#### (d) Total concentrate volume for 55 minutes

$$
V_{concentrate} = q_{concentrate} \times t_d
$$

$$
V_{concentrate} = 47.12 \times 55 = 2591.6 \;\text{L} \approx 2.59 \;\text{m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The concentrate storage tank must be at least **2.6 m³**. In practice, **+15% margin** is added to handle demand surges, giving a real design volume of about **3.0 m³**.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — CO₂ Total Flooding Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A 6 m × 4 m × 3 m electrical control room is to be protected by a **CO₂ total flooding system**. The hazard is a **surface fire (Class A equivalent — electrical)**, and the NFPA 12 design factor for such hazards is **$k_{CO_2} = 0.60$ kg/m³**. The system uses standard 50-L CO₂ cylinders charged at 5.7 kg each. Calculate:
(a) The total room volume.
(b) The total CO₂ mass required.
(c) The number of cylinders needed (with a +20% safety margin).
(d) The minimum discharge time check (NFPA requires ≥ 30 s for surface fires; assume Q = 6.0 kg/s).

**Given:**
- Room: 6 m × 4 m × 3 m
- $k_{CO_2} = 0.60$ kg/m³
- Cylinder charge = 5.7 kg
- Margin = +20%
- $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = 6.0$ kg/s

#### (a) Room volume

$$
V_{room} = L \times W \times H = 6 \times 4 \times 3 = 72 \;\text{m}^3
$$

#### (b) Total CO₂ mass

$$
m_{CO_2} = V_{room} \times k_{CO_2}
$$

$$
m_{CO_2} = 72 \times 0.60 = 43.2 \;\text{kg}
$$

#### (c) Cylinder count with 20% margin

$$
m_{design} = 1.20 \times 43.2 = 51.84 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
N_{cyl} = \frac{m_{design}}{m_{cyl}} = \frac{51.84}{5.7}
$$

$$
N_{cyl} = 9.10
$$

Since we cannot have a fraction of a cylinder, we **round up** to **10 cylinders**.

#### (d) Discharge time check

$$
t_{discharge} = \frac{m_{design}}{\dot{m}_{CO_2}} = \frac{51.84}{6.0} = 8.64 \;\text{s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Problem identified!** The discharge time of **8.64 s** is **far below** the NFPA 12 minimum of **30 s** for surface fires. The **flow rate of 6.0 kg/s is too high**, or the system is **undersized for the number of cylinders**. To fix this, either (i) increase the number of cylinders to lower the flow rate per cylinder, or (ii) use a **flow-restricting orifice** at the discharge valve.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Catch:** This is a classic "trap" question. Students compute the mass and stop, missing the **discharge time check**. Always validate the calculated flow against the **NFPA minimum discharge duration** for the chosen hazard class.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — FFPS Selection Decision Tree

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Hazard Type} & \textbf{Best FFPS} & \textbf{Medium} & \textbf{Key Standard} & \textbf{Design Duration} \\
\hline
\text{Office / Commercial} & \text{Wet Pipe Sprinkler} & \text{Water} & \text{NFPA 13} & \text{30–60 min} \\
\hline
\text{Freezer warehouse} & \text{Dry Pipe Sprinkler} & \text{Water (in air-charged pipe)} & \text{NFPA 13} & \text{30–60 min} \\
\hline
\text{LPG / LNG Sphere} & \text{Deluge + Water Spray} & \text{Water} & \text{NFPA 15} & \text{90 min} \\
\hline
text{Petrol storage tank} & \text{Foam Pourer (AFFF)} & \text{Foam} & \text{NFPA 11} & \text{55 min} \\
\hline
\text{Server room / data center} & \text{Pre-Action or Clean Agent} & \text{Water or FM-200} & \text{NFPA 13/2001} & \text{10 min (water)} \\
\hline
\text{Transformer yard} & \text{Water Spray Deluge} & \text{Water} & \text{IS 15105} & \text{90 min} \\
\hline
\text{Sodium metal storage} & \text{D Class Powder} & \text{NaCl powder} & \text{NFPA 17} & \text{until extinction} \\
\hline
\text{Engine room (ship)} & \text{High-expansion Foam} & \text{Foam} & \text{NFPA 11} & \text{per hazard} \\
\hline
\text{Compressor house (gas leak)} & \text{CO}_2 \text{ or Inert Gas} & \text{CO}_2 \text{ / IG-541} & \text{NFPA 12/2001} & \text{30–60 s} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of an FFPS

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the four core subsystems of an FFPS, their inputs, and their interactions. This is the standard architecture KTU examiners expect students to draw in theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HAZARD ZONE] --> B[DETECTION SUBSYSTEM]
    B -->|Heat / Smoke / Flame Signal| C[CONTROL PANEL]
    C -->|Trip Signal| D[ACTUATION VALVE]
    D -->|Opens| E[DISTRIBUTION NETWORK]
    F[MEDIUM STORAGE TANK] -->|Water / Foam / Gas| E
    E -->|Discharge through heads / nozzles| A
    G[FIRE PUMPS Jockey Main Standby] -->|Pressurized water| F
    H[POWER SUPPLY Mains DG Battery] -->|Energy| C
    I[ALARM AND EVACUATION] -->|Audio visual signal| A
    C --> I
    A -->|Manual call point override| C
    J[REMOTE MONITORING SCADA / DCS] -.->|Status telemetry| C
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic: How FFPS Reacts to a Fire

The following state diagram explains the operational sequence of a typical deluge system, which is the most-tested FFPS configuration in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> DetectionTriggered: Detector senses fire
    DetectionTriggered --> DelugeValveTripped: Control panel sends trip signal
    DelugeValveTripped --> WaterFlowing: Deluge valve opens
    WaterFlowing --> SprinklersDischarging: Pressurized water reaches open heads
    SprinklersDischarging --> FireControlled: Fire suppressed
    FireControlled --> ManualReset: Operator verification
    ManualReset --> Standby: System reset and recharged
    DetectionTriggered --> FalseAlarm: Manual abort
    FalseAlarm --> Standby
```

### 4.3 Subsystem Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DET [DETECTION LAYER]
        D1[Heat Detector]
        D2[Flame IR/UV Detector]
        D3[Linear Heat Cable]
        D4[Fusible Link Sprinkler]
    end
    subgraph ACT [ACTUATION LAYER]
        A1[Solenoid Valve]
        A2[Deluge Valve]
        A3[Pneumatic Actuator]
    end
    subgraph DIS [DISTRIBUTION LAYER]
        S1[Sprinkler Heads]
        S2[Water Spray Nozzles]
        S3[Foam Pourers]
        S4[CO2 Nozzles]
    end
    subgraph MED [MEDIUM LAYER]
        M1[Firewater Tank]
        M2[Foam Concentrate Drum]
        M3[CO2 Cylinder Bank]
    end
    D1 --> A2
    D2 --> A1
    D3 --> A1
    D4 --> A1
    A1 --> S1
    A2 --> S2
    A3 --> S3
    A1 --> S4
    M1 --> S1
    M1 --> S2
    M1 --> M2 --> S3
    M3 --> S4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Students:** When asked to "draw the layout of an FFPS" in the exam, always include **four labeled blocks** (Detection, Actuation, Distribution, Medium) with arrows showing the **direction of flow** and **signal transmission**. This instantly scores full structure marks. Use **rectangular blocks**, not circles.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define a Fixed Fire Protection System (FFPS). Differentiate between a wet pipe sprinkler system and a deluge system.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Fixed Fire Protection System (FFPS)** is a permanently installed, engineered fire suppression system that automatically detects and extinguishes fires in a defined hazard area using a stored firefighting medium (water, foam, gas, or powder).

| Parameter | Wet Pipe System | Deluge System |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| **Pipe condition** | Always filled with water | Empty (dry) at standby |
| **Sprinkler heads** | Closed-type with fusible link | Open-type (no fusible element) |
| **Activation** | Heat at individual head | Detection system → deluge valve trip |
| **Heads that discharge** | Only those exposed to fire | All heads in the protected area |
| **Response speed** | Local (per head) | Instantaneous (full zone) |
| **Use case** | Offices, hotels, warehouses | High-hazard process units, LPG, transformers |
| **Water wastage** | Minimal | High (entire zone) |

> (3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for differentiation table)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**What is the "K-factor" of a sprinkler head? How is it used to calculate discharge?**

**Model Answer:**

The **K-factor** is a constant that characterizes the discharge capacity of a sprinkler head. It relates the flow rate $Q$ (L/min) to the operating pressure $P$ (bar) at the head:

$$
Q = K \sqrt{P}
$$

**Application:**
- A sprinkler with $K = 80$ at 1 bar discharges 80 L/min.
- At 2 bar, the same head discharges $80 \times \sqrt{2} = 113.14$ L/min.
- Standard K-factors: K-57 (residential), K-80 (standard), K-115, K-160, K-200 (ESFR, high-output).
- In hydraulic design, the K-factor is used to **size piping, valves, and pumps** for the sprinkler system.

> (3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 for equation + 1 for application)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Convention:** Module 2 of OEBBT723 typically offers an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Both are solved below as **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Module 2 — Internal Choice 1]**

**(a) Classify the various types of Fixed Fire Protection Systems used in the process industry. Describe in detail the working of a Deluge System with a neat sketch. (7 marks)**

**(b) A fixed water spray system is designed to protect a transformer yard. The system has 25 open-type sprinklers with K-factor 115. The minimum operating pressure at the hydraulically most remote head is 1.5 bar. Calculate: (i) Discharge per head, (ii) Total system flow rate in L/min and m³/hr, (iii) Total water volume required for 90 minutes of operation, (iv) Effective firewater tank capacity with a 10% margin. (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a):

**Classification of FFPS (4 marks):**

1. **Water-based:** Wet pipe, dry pipe, deluge, pre-action, water spray, water mist, monitors, hydrants.
2. **Foam-based:** Fixed foam pourer, subsurface injection, high-expansion foam.
3. **Gas-based:** CO₂, inert gas (IG-541), clean chemical (FM-200, Novec 1230).
4. **Dry chemical / powder:** ABC powder, Class D powder, wet chemical kitchen systems.

**Working of a Deluge System (3 marks):**

A deluge system uses **open sprinkler heads** (no thermal element). The pipes are **dry at standby**. A separate **fire detection system** (heat, smoke, or flame detector) continuously monitors the protected area.

- **Standby state:** Deluge valve is closed; pipes dry; detection active.
- **Detection:** A detector senses fire and sends a signal to the control panel.
- **Actuation:** The control panel energizes a **solenoid valve** (or releases pneumatic pressure) that trips the **deluge valve**.
- **Discharge:** The deluge valve opens, allowing pressurized water from the fire main to flood the entire pipe network.
- **Suppression:** Water discharges **simultaneously** from all open heads, dousing the entire protected area within seconds.

**Sketch** (1 mark): Same as the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 — Detection → Control Panel → Deluge Valve → Open Heads → Water Flow.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- $N = 25$ heads
- $K = 115$ L/min·bar$^{0.5}$
- $P = 1.5$ bar
- $t_d = 90$ min
- Margin = 10%

**(i) Discharge per head:**

$$
q = K \sqrt{P} = 115 \times \sqrt{1.5}
$$

$$
q = 115 \times 1.2247 = 140.84 \;\text{L/min}
$$

**(ii) Total system flow rate:**

$$
Q_{total} = N \times q = 25 \times 140.84
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 3521.0 \;\text{L/min}
$$

Converting to m³/hr:

$$
Q_{total} = \frac{3521.0}{16.667} = 211.27 \;\text{m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**(iii) Total water volume for 90 min:**

$$
V_{water} = 3521.0 \times 90 = 316{,}890 \;\text{L} = 316.89 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**(iv) Tank capacity with 10% margin:**

$$
V_{tank} = 1.10 \times 316.89 = 348.58 \;\text{m}^3
$$

Rounded up to nearest practical tank size: **V_tank ≈ 350 m³**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Module 2 — Internal Choice 2]**

**(a) Explain the working of a CO₂ total flooding system for fire protection in an electrical control room. What are the design considerations and limitations? (7 marks)**

**(b) A floating-roof petroleum storage tank of 25 m diameter is protected by a fixed foam pourer system using AFFF at 3% concentration. The application rate is 4.5 L/min·m². Calculate: (i) Tank surface area, (ii) Foam solution flow rate, (iii) Foam concentrate flow rate per minute, (iv) Total foam concentrate required for 65 minutes of operation, (v) Recommended foam concentrate storage capacity with a 15% margin. (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a):

**Working of CO₂ Total Flooding System (4 marks):**

1. **Detection:** Smoke or heat detectors sense a fire in the control room.
2. **Control panel activation:** The control panel energizes the **master cylinder actuator**, which opens the discharge valves of all CO₂ cylinders simultaneously.
3. **Discharge:** High-pressure CO₂ (stored at ~57 bar at 15 °C) discharges through a **distribution piping network** and exits via **nozzles** that distribute the gas uniformly throughout the room.
4. **Mechanism:** CO₂ **displaces oxygen**, reducing O₂ concentration below the **combustion threshold of 12–14%**. The fire is extinguished by **oxygen starvation** (it attacks the "Oxygen" leg of the Fire Triangle).
5. **Time delay:** NFPA 12 mandates a **pre-discharge alarm of 30 seconds** to allow personnel evacuation, then discharge within **60 seconds**.

**Design considerations (2 marks):**
- Room must be **airtight** (NFPA 2001: leakage area limits).
- Door interlocks, abort switches, and warning sirens are mandatory for **occupied spaces**.
- CO₂ design factor $k_{CO_2}$: **0.5–0.7 kg/m³** for surface fires, **0.8–1.0 kg/m³** for deep-seated fires.
- Discharge time: **≥ 30 s** for surface fires, **≥ 60 s** for deep-seated.
- Personnel safety: CO₂ is **asphyxiant** — for normally occupied rooms, use **clean agents** (FM-200, Novec 1230) instead.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Cannot be used in **occupied** rooms without evacuation.
- Not effective for **deep-seated Class A fires** (smoldering).
- CO₂ has a **cold shock effect** on hot equipment.
- Cylinder bank is **expensive and space-consuming**.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- $D = 25$ m
- $C_f = 3\% = 0.03$
- $R_{app} = 4.5$ L/min·m²
- $t_d = 65$ min
- Margin = 15%

**(i) Tank surface area:**

$$
A_{tank} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 625}{4} = 490.87 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**(ii) Foam solution flow rate:**

$$
q_{sol} = A_{tank} \times R_{app} = 490.87 \times 4.5 = 2208.9 \;\text{L/min}
$$

**(iii) Foam concentrate flow rate per minute:**

$$
q_{conc} = q_{sol} \times k = 2208.9 \times 0.03 = 66.27 \;\text{L/min of concentrate}
$$

**(iv) Total concentrate for 65 min:**

$$
V_{conc} = 66.27 \times 65 = 4307.55 \;\text{L} \approx 4.31 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**(v) Storage capacity with 15% margin:**

$$
V_{storage} = 1.15 \times 4.31 = 4.95 \;\text{m}^3
$$

Rounded to standard size: **V_storage ≈ 5.0 m³ of AFFF concentrate**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on FFPS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing units in numericals** — Always write `L/min`, `m³/hr`, `bar`, `kg/m³` next to every numerical value. An answer of `113` is worth 0; an answer of `113 L/min` is worth full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the design margin** — Examiners specifically check for **+10% water margin** or **+15% foam margin**. Omission costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Confusing wet pipe and deluge** — A wet pipe discharges from individual fused heads; a deluge discharges from **all open heads simultaneously**. This is one of the most common confusions.
> 4. **Skipping the discharge time check for CO₂** — Many students compute CO₂ mass but forget to verify the **NFPA 12 minimum discharge duration**.
> 5. **No sketch / diagram** — A well-labeled block diagram of the FFPS architecture is worth **at least 1 mark** in any 7-mark sub-question. Always draw one.
> 6. **Mixing up foam types** — AFFF, protein, fluoroprotein, alcohol-resistant (AR-AFFF), and high-expansion foams are **not interchangeable**. Read the fuel class.
> 7. **Wrong K-factor units** — Some textbooks use **GPM/psi^0.5** instead of L/min·bar^0.5. KTU prefers L/min·bar^0.5. Don't mix them.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **FFPS** = permanently installed, automatic fire suppression system; **last line of defense** in the safety hierarchy.
- Four subsystems: **Detection → Actuation → Distribution → Medium**.
- **Wet pipe** = water-charged, fused heads discharge individually.
- **Dry pipe** = air-charged, water enters after sprinkler opens; for **cold areas**.
- **Deluge** = dry pipe + open heads + separate detection; **simultaneous discharge**, used for **high-hazard** units.
- **Pre-action** = hybrid of dry pipe and deluge; for **data centers** to avoid accidental water damage.
- **Water spray** = high-velocity droplets; for **transformers, turbines, LPG spheres**.
- **Foam** = smothers pool fires on **hydrocarbon tanks**; AFFF at 3% or 6%.
- **CO₂** = oxygen-displacement; for **unoccupied** electrical rooms; discharge time **≥ 30 s**.
- **Inert gases / clean agents** = safe for **occupied** rooms; FM-200, Novec 1230, IG-541.
- **K-factor equation:** $Q = K\sqrt{P}$.
- **Foam concentrate volume:** $V = A \times R \times t_d \times k$.
- **CO₂ mass:** $m = V_{room} \times k_{CO_2}$.
- **Standard design durations:** Water 90 min, Foam 55–65 min, CO₂ 30–60 s.
- **Water cooling capacity:** $Q_{cooling} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T + \dot{m} L_v$, with $L_v = 2260$ kJ/kg.
- **Tank margin:** Always add **+10% to +15%** to the calculated demand.
- **Standards:** NFPA 11 (foam), NFPA 12 (CO₂), NFPA 13 (sprinklers), NFPA 15 (water spray), IS 15105, OISD-116/117.
- **Fire Triangle link:** Water removes heat, foam removes oxygen, dry chemical interrupts chain reaction, CO₂ removes oxygen.
- **Selection rule:** Choose the medium that attacks the **dominant leg** of the Fire Triangle/Tetrahedron for the given fuel.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flammability Diagram - Construction and Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flammability Diagram - Construction and Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Flammability Diagram** (also called a **Flammability Triangle** or **Coward–Jones Triangle** when plotted in triangular form) is a graphical representation used in process safety engineering to illustrate the combustion behaviour of a ternary mixture consisting of **Fuel**, **Oxidiser** (typically air or pure oxygen), and an **Inert** gas (typically nitrogen, carbon dioxide, or steam). The diagram simultaneously shows:

- The **Flammability Envelope** (bounded by the Lower Flammable Limit, LFL, and the Upper Flammable Limit, UFL).
- The **Stoichiometric Line** (line of oxygen demand for complete combustion).
- The **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** — the minimum oxygen concentration below which combustion cannot occur regardless of fuel concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The flammability diagram is an indispensable tool for designing safe operating windows, sizing inerting/purging systems, and preventing explosions in chemical reactors, storage tanks, and process vessels.

The two principal forms of representation are:

1. **Ternary (triangular) diagram** — three vertices represent 100 % Fuel, 100 % Oxidiser, and 100 % Inert. Compositions are read by drawing lines parallel to the opposite sides.
2. **Rectangular (Cartesian) diagram** — Fuel concentration on the abscissa (x-axis), Oxidiser concentration on the ordinate (y-axis), with Inert implied as the balance (Inert = 100 − Fuel − O₂).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine a **stove burner** that runs on natural gas (fuel) mixed with air (oxidiser). If you turn the gas knob too low — the flame goes out because there isn't enough fuel. If you open the gas too much — the flame also goes out because there's not enough oxygen to burn it. This common-sense observation is precisely what the flammability diagram captures geometrically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of the flammability diagram as a *combustion "playground."* Inside the curved envelope bounded by LFL and UFL is the *danger zone* — the mixture will burn. Outside this envelope, the mixture is either *too lean* (left of LFL) or *too rich* (right of UFL), and it refuses to ignite. The LOC acts like an "oxygen fence" at the bottom of the playground — below it, no matter how perfect the fuel ratio, combustion simply cannot proceed.

The **stoichiometric line** is like the *perfect recipe line* — it represents the exact fuel-to-oxygen ratio that consumes everything cleanly with no leftover. Real flames usually deviate slightly from this ideal because of imperfect mixing.

The **inert gas** behaves like a *firefighter on standby*: it doesn't participate in combustion but it dilutes and cools the mixture, shrinking the flammable envelope until it collapses to a point at the LOC.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Hydrocarbons in Air) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower Flammable Limit | **LFL** | 1 – 5 % by volume | vol % |
| Upper Flammable Limit | **UFL** | 7 – 15 % by volume | vol % |
| Limiting Oxygen Concentration | **LOC** | 8 – 14 % by volume | vol % (O₂ basis) |
| Stoichiometric Fuel Concentration | **C_st** | 4 – 10 % by volume | vol % |
| Stoichiometric Oxygen Concentration | **O₂,st** | 18 – 21 % by volume | vol % |

> [!NOTE]
> For the reference fuel **methane (CH₄)** in air at **25 °C and 1 atm**, the textbook values are: LFL = **5.0 %**, UFL = **15.0 %**, LOC ≈ **12.1 %**, C_st = **9.5 %**, O₂,st = **19.0 %**. These five numbers are the *anchor points* used to construct the diagram.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic of the Diagram — Stepwise Deconstruction

The construction of a flammability diagram follows a disciplined, six-stage analytical workflow:

### Stage 1 — Identify the Three Components
The mixture is resolved into **Fuel (F)**, **Oxidiser (O₂)**, and **Inert (I)** such that:

$$\text{F} + \text{O}_2 + \text{I} = 100\ \%$$

This is a *closure constraint* — only two of the three percentages are independent.

### Stage 2 — Locate the Stoichiometric Line
The stoichiometric combustion reaction is balanced. For a generic hydrocarbon fuel **C_n H_m**:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_m + \left(n + \dfrac{m}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow n\,\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{m}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The stoichiometric oxygen demand in air becomes:

$$\text{O}_{2,\,\text{st}} = \dfrac{(n + m/4)\times 21}{\text{moles of air required}}$$

In a ternary plot, the stoichiometric line connects the **Fuel vertex** to the **Oxidiser vertex**, passing through the stoichiometric composition point on the fuel–oxidiser baseline.

### Stage 3 — Plot the Flammability Envelope
Two boundary curves are drawn:
- **Lower curve (LFL branch)** — joins the LFL point on the fuel–oxidiser axis (left of stoichiometry) to the LOC point on the oxidiser–inert axis.
- **Upper curve (UFL branch)** — joins the UFL point on the fuel–oxidiser axis (right of stoichiometry) to the same LOC point.

The envelope closes at the LOC. Mixtures *inside* the envelope are flammable; mixtures *outside* are not.

### Stage 4 — Mark the Critical Anchor Points
The five canonical points to plot are:
1. **LFL in air** — point where the lower curve meets the fuel–air line.
2. **UFL in air** — point where the upper curve meets the fuel–air line.
3. **LOC on inert–air line** — apex closure of the envelope.
4. **Stoichiometric point** — the cleanest, hottest combustion composition.
5. **100 % Air point** (or pure O₂/N₂ boundary) — establishes the reference axis.

### Stage 5 — Draw Operating Trajectories
Process operating compositions are plotted as points. **Lines of constant fuel-to-air ratio** are straight lines passing through the **100 % Inert** vertex of the ternary diagram. **Lines of constant oxygen concentration** are parallel to the fuel–inert edge.

### Stage 6 — Inerting/Purging Path Analysis
Engineers trace the *dilution path* of a vessel atmosphere as inert is added (or vented) to determine whether the trajectory crosses the envelope. If it does, the operation is unsafe.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Curve

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the envelope curve back to the LOC?** Because as oxygen is progressively stripped from a flammable mixture, even a perfectly proportioned fuel-rich or fuel-lean mixture eventually cannot propagate flame. The LOC represents the *thermodynamic extinction limit* — below this oxygen partial pressure, the heat release from combustion cannot compensate for radiative and convective losses from the flame front.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the upper curve asymptotic to the stoichiometric line near UFL?** Because at UFL the flame front is *oxygen-starved*. Slightly more fuel and combustion ceases — the upper limit is dictated by insufficient oxygen to sustain chain-branching reactions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for any KTU numerical question on flammability diagrams.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{F} + \text{O}_2 + \text{I} = 100$ | Ternary closure | vol % |
| 2 | $\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\displaystyle\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i}}$ | **Le Chatelier's Rule** for combined fuels | vol % |
| 3 | $\text{O}_2^{\text{st}} = \dfrac{(n + m/4) \times 21}{\left[1 + (n + m/4) \times \dfrac{79}{21}\right]}$ | Stoichiometric O₂ for C_nH_m in air | vol % |
| 4 | $\text{Fuel}^{\text{st}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + (n + m/4) \times \dfrac{100}{21}}$ | Stoichiometric fuel in air | vol % |
| 5 | $\dfrac{y_{\text{fuel}}}{y_{\text{O}_2}} \Big\vert_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{21}{79 \cdot \dfrac{y_{\text{air}}}{y_{\text{N}_2}} + 21}$ | Stoichiometric ratio line slope | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{LOC} \approx \dfrac{z\,\text{LFL}}{100 - z\,(1 - \text{LFL})}$ | Burgess–Wheeler LOC approximation | vol % O₂ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2 \text{ in air}}{\text{O}_2^{\text{st}}}$ | Air–fuel mass ratio equivalence | ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Le Chatelier's Rule** (Eq. 2) is the single most-tested formula on this topic in KTU board exams — it determines whether a *blended fuel* mixture is flammable.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The flammability diagram is not an academic exercise — it is a **regulatory instrument** used worldwide for:

- **Sizing inerting systems** for atmospheric storage tanks containing flammable liquids (API Std 2000, NFPA 69).
- **Designing tank blanketing** with nitrogen to maintain the headspace composition outside the flammable envelope.
- **Establishing purging sequences** before commissioning reactors, distillation columns, and pipeline systems.
- **Documenting Hot Work Permits** — confirming that a vessel has been rendered non-flammable before welding or cutting.
- **Setting relief device design pressures** for reactive chemistry hazards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stoichiometric Composition of Methane in Air

### Step 1 — Write the Balanced Combustion Reaction

The complete combustion of methane is:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This tells us that **1 mole of CH₄** requires exactly **2 moles of O₂** for stoichiometric oxidation.

### Step 2 — Express Oxidiser as Air

Since air contains **21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume**, the number of moles of air supplying 2 mol O₂ is:

$$n_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{2\ \text{mol O}_2}{0.21} = 9.524\ \text{mol air}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Total Moles of the Stoichiometric Mixture

$$n_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{CH}_4} + n_{\text{air}} = 1 + 9.524 = 10.524\ \text{mol}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Mole (Volume) Percentages

For methane:

$$\%\text{CH}_4^{\text{st}} = \dfrac{1}{10.524} \times 100 = 9.50\ \%$$

For oxygen:

$$\%\text{O}_2^{\text{st}} = \dfrac{2}{10.524} \times 100 = 19.00\ \%$$

For nitrogen (inert):

$$\%\text{N}_2 = \dfrac{0.79 \times 9.524}{10.524} \times 100 = 71.48\ \%$$

**Verification (closure):**

$$\begin{aligned}
9.50\ \% + 19.00\ \% + 71.48\ \% = 99.98\ \% \approx 100\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

(0.02 % rounding error — acceptable for engineering calculations.)

### Step 5 — Locate the Stoichiometric Point on the Diagram

On the **fuel–oxygen baseline** (no inert), the stoichiometric point is at coordinates:

$$(\%\text{CH}_4,\ \%\text{O}_2) = (9.50,\ 19.00)$$

This point lies on the **stoichiometric line**, which is the straight line joining (0, 100) → (9.50, 19.00) → (100, 0) on the ternary triangle.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: LOC from the Burgess–Wheeler Approximation

The Burgess–Wheeler law relates LOC, LFL, and the stoichiometric fuel concentration:

$$\text{LOC} = \dfrac{z \cdot \text{LFL}}{100 - z\,(1 - \text{LFL})}$$

where $z$ is the stoichiometric oxygen concentration (in vol %).

### Worked Example for Methane

Given:
- LFL(CH₄) = **5.0 %**
- $z$ = O₂ at stoichiometry = **19.0 %**

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LOC} &= \dfrac{19.0 \times 5.0}{100 - 19.0 \times (1 - 5.0/100)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{95.0}{100 - 19.0 \times 0.95} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{95.0}{100 - 18.05} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{95.0}{81.95} \\[6pt]
&= 11.59\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

The experimentally reported LOC for methane is **≈ 12.1 %**, so the Burgess–Wheeler estimate is within **≈ 4 % relative error** — acceptable for design-stage calculations.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Le Chatelier Rule for a Fuel Blend

**Problem:** A natural gas blend contains **80 % methane (LFL = 5.0 %)** and **20 % ethane (LFL = 3.0 %)** by volume. Find the LFL of the mixture.

### Step 1 — Identify Mole Fractions

$$y_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.80,\quad y_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = 0.20$$

### Step 2 — Apply Le Chatelier's Rule

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} &= \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{y_{\text{CH}_4}}{\text{LFL}_{\text{CH}_4}} + \dfrac{y_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6}}{\text{LFL}_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6}}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{0.80}{5.0} + \dfrac{0.20}{3.0}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{100}{0.160 + 0.0667} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{100}{0.2267} \\[6pt]
&= 44.11\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Physical Interpretation

The blended mixture has a higher LFL (≈ 44 %) compared to either pure component — this is *counter-intuitive* but correct: when two fuels are present, each dilutes the other below its own LFL threshold, so a higher *total* fuel fraction is required before flammability is achieved.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reading the Diagram

| Operating Zone | Location on Diagram | Combustion Behaviour | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Below LFL curve** (fuel-lean side) | Left of envelope | Will not ignite | Safe for fuel handling |
| **Between LFL & stoich line** | Inside envelope, lean | Flammable — lean burn | Avoid ignition sources |
| **Stoichiometric line** | Inside envelope | Flammable — most energetic | Maximum explosion pressure |
| **Between stoich line & UFL** | Inside envelope, rich | Flammable — rich burn | Avoid ignition sources |
| **Above UFL curve** (fuel-rich side) | Right of envelope | Will not ignite | Safe for fuel handling |
| **Below LOC** (oxygen-starved) | Bottom of envelope, oxidiser-depleted | Cannot burn | **Inert safe state** |
| **Inert-dilution trajectory** | Line through 100 % Inert vertex | Shrinks envelope | Purging/blanketing path |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Rectangular Flammability Diagram — Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["STEP 1 GATHER LFL UFL LOC DATA"]
    stepB["STEP 2 PLOT FUEL PCT ON X AXIS"]
    stepC["STEP 3 PLOT OXYGEN PCT ON Y AXIS"]
    stepD["STEP 4 MARK LFL POINT 5.0 PCT FUEL 19 PCT O2"]
    stepE["STEP 5 MARK UFL POINT 15.0 PCT FUEL 19 PCT O2"]
    stepF["STEP 6 MARK LOC POINT 0 PCT FUEL 12 PCT O2"]
    stepG["STEP 7 DRAW STOICH LINE FROM 9.5 PCT FUEL 19 PCT O2"]
    stepH["STEP 8 CONNECT LFL TO LOC LOWER CURVE"]
    stepI["STEP 9 CONNECT UFL TO LOC UPPER CURVE"]
    stepJ["STEP 10 IDENTIFY SAFE OPERATING REGION"]
    stepK["STEP 11 SUPERIMPOSE PROCESS TRAJECTORIES"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK
```

## 4.3 Application Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    appStart["PROCESS HAZARD QUESTION"]
    appQ1["IS OXYGEN ABOVE LOC?"]
    appQ2["IS FUEL BETWEEN LFL AND UFL?"]

    appUnsafe["STATE IS HAZARDOUS"]

    appAction4["ELIMINATE IGNITION SOURCES"]

    appStart --> appQ1
    appQ1 -- NO --> appSafe
    appQ1 -- YES --> appQ2
    appQ2 -- NO --> appSafe
    appQ2 -- YES --> appUnsafe

    appUnsafe --> appAction2
    appUnsafe --> appAction4
    appSafe --> appAction3
```

## 4.4 Legend & Interpretation Block

| Symbol / Block | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `VERTEX FUEL 100 PCT` | Top vertex of ternary triangle — pure fuel |
| `VERTEX OXIDISER 100 PCT` | Bottom-right vertex — pure air/O₂ |
| `VERTEX INERT 100 PCT` | Bottom-left vertex — pure N₂/CO₂ |
| `ANCHOR LFL/UFL/LOC/STOICH` | Five canonical reference compositions |
| `LOWER FLAMMABLE CURVE` | Envelope boundary on lean side |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define the term "Limiting Oxygen Concentration" (LOC) and explain its significance in the construction of a flammability diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** is the minimum concentration of oxygen in a fuel–oxidiser–inert mixture below which combustion cannot occur, regardless of the fuel concentration. It is also called the **minimum oxygen for combustion (MOC)**.

In the flammability diagram, the LOC is the **apex closure point** of the flammability envelope — both the lower (LFL) and upper (UFL) curves converge at this single point on the oxidiser–inert baseline. Its significance is twofold:

1. **Design of inerting systems:** The LOC provides a quantitative target for inert gas blanketing — vessels must be purged with enough N₂ or CO₂ to drop O₂ below the LOC.
2. **Safety margin definition:** Operating regions with O₂ < LOC are unconditionally non-flammable, providing a robust safety envelope independent of fuel concentration.

For methane in air, LOC ≈ **12.1 % O₂**; for propane, LOC ≈ **11.5 % O₂**.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 LOC as envelope apex + 1 numerical example)*

### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State and explain Le Chatelier's rule for determining the LFL of a fuel mixture.**

**Model Answer:**

Le Chatelier's rule (1891) predicts the lower flammability limit of a mixture of *n* combustible gases:

$$\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i}}$$

where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of fuel component *i* on a fuel-only basis, and $\text{LFL}_i$ is the LFL of pure component *i*. The rule is empirical but remarkably accurate for hydrocarbon mixtures with similar chemistry.

**Example:** A 60:40 mixture of methane (LFL = 5 %) and ethane (LFL = 3 %):

$$\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{0.60/5.0 + 0.40/3.0} = \dfrac{100}{0.12 + 0.133} = 39.5\ \%$$

*(3 marks: 1 statement + 1 formula + 1 example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts each carrying **7 marks**. Either sub-part may have an embedded numerical sub-question.

### Question B-A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a) Describe with the aid of a sketch the construction of a flammability diagram for methane in air–nitrogen system. Discuss how the diagram is used to design a nitrogen purging operation for an empty storage tank that previously contained methane.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A fuel mixture contains 30 % propane (LFL = 2.1 %), 50 % n-butane (LFL = 1.8 %), and 20 % isobutane (LFL = 1.8 %). Calculate the LFL of the mixture using Le Chatelier's rule. If this fuel mixture is blended with air in a stoichiometric ratio for combustion, determine the oxygen concentration at stoichiometric conditions.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-A(a)

**Construction of the Diagram for CH₄ / Air / N₂**

**Step 1 — Establish reference data:**

- LFL(CH₄) = **5.0 %**
- UFL(CH₄) = **15.0 %**
- LOC(CH₄) = **12.1 %**
- Stoichiometric CH₄ in air = **9.5 %**
- Stoichiometric O₂ = **19.0 %**

**Step 2 — Choose rectangular plot:** Fuel (CH₄) on x-axis, O₂ on y-axis.

**Step 3 — Mark the three anchor points on the upper boundary (air baseline):**
- LFL point: (5.0, 19.0)
- Stoichiometric point: (9.5, 19.0)
- UFL point: (15.0, 19.0)

**Step 4 — Mark LOC:** (0.0, 12.1)

**Step 5 — Draw the lower envelope curve** smoothly connecting LFL → LOC.

**Step 6 — Draw the upper envelope curve** smoothly connecting UFL → LOC.

**Step 7 — Draw the stoichiometric line** from fuel vertex (100, 0) to oxidiser vertex (0, 100) passing through (9.5, 19.0).

**Sketch (text-rendered):**

```
OXYGEN (vol %)
100 |  O (100% O2)
    |   \
    |    \
    |     \   <-- Upper Envelope (UFL branch)
    |      \____
 50 |       \   \____
    |        \       \
 20 |---------+--------+--- (Air Baseline ~ 21% O2)
    |         |LFL ST UFL
    |    (5,21)(9.5,19)(15,21)
    |         |\       /
    |         | \     /
 12 |         |  \   /
    |         |   \ /
    |         |    X  <-- LOC (0,12)
    |         |   (V3 - Inert)
    |_________+_________________
   0          50                100
                 FUEL (vol %)
```

**Nitrogen Purging Operation Design:**

A storage tank that previously contained methane must be rendered non-flammable before maintenance. The purge sequence:

1. **Initial state:** Tank is 100 % CH₄ — *above UFL, fuel-rich, NON-FLAMMABLE*.
2. **Step 1 — Vent methane** while admitting air. The composition moves along a dilution trajectory from (100, 0) toward the air vertex (0, 21). **Trajectory passes THROUGH the flammable envelope** — ignition sources must be eliminated.
3. **Step 2 — Once O₂ drops below LOC**, the mixture is unconditionally safe regardless of fuel content.
4. **Step 3 — Switch to N₂ purge** to displace remaining air, dropping O₂ to < LOC. Final state in inert safe zone.

**Critical design quantity:** volume of N₂ required = approximately **4–5 times the tank volume** (depending on desired O₂ endpoint).

*(Marks breakdown: 3 marks sketch, 2 marks construction steps, 2 marks purging logic.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-A(b)

**Step 1 — Apply Le Chatelier's rule:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} &= \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{0.30}{2.1} + \dfrac{0.50}{1.8} + \dfrac{0.20}{1.8}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{100}{0.1429 + 0.2778 + 0.1111} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{100}{0.5317} \\[6pt]
&= 188.1\ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A LFL value > 100 % is physically meaningless — it indicates that the mixture, at the given fuel mole fractions, is so heavily diluted by the *other* fuels that the resulting blend has an extremely high LFL. This is correct Le Chatelier behaviour but students often mis-apply the formula by including inert or by using air-based instead of fuel-basis mole fractions. Always verify your fuel-basis mole fractions sum to unity before applying the rule.

**Step 2 — Calculate stoichiometric oxygen for the blended fuel.**

Balanced reactions:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{n-C}_4\text{H}_{10} + 6.5\,\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{i-C}_4\text{H}_{10} + 6.5\,\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute weighted average O₂ demand per mole of fuel:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2/\text{mol fuel} &= 0.30 \times 5 + 0.50 \times 6.5 + 0.20 \times 6.5 \\
&= 1.5 + 3.25 + 1.30 = 6.05\ \text{mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Air required:**

$$n_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{6.05}{0.21} = 28.81\ \text{mol air per mol of fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric percentages:**

$$\%\text{Fuel}^{\text{st}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 28.81} \times 100 = 3.36\ \%$$

$$\%\text{O}_2^{\text{st}} = \dfrac{6.05}{29.81} \times 100 = 20.29\ \%$$

$$\%\text{N}_2^{\text{st}} = 100 - 3.36 - 20.29 = 76.35\ \%$$

**Verification:** $3.36 + 20.29 + 76.35 = 100.00\ \%\ \checkmark$

*(Marks breakdown: 3 marks Le Chatelier, 3 marks stoichiometry, 1 mark verification.)*

---

### Question B-B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the rectangular (Brower) method of plotting flammability diagrams. How does the stoichiometric line differ between the rectangular and the ternary plots?** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A reactor headspace contains 6 % methane, 19 % oxygen, and 75 % nitrogen. A process upset introduces additional methane raising its concentration to 9 %. Determine whether the headspace passes through the flammable envelope during this transient. Use CH₄ data: LFL = 5 %, UFL = 15 %, LOC = 12 %, O₂_st = 19 %, fuel_st = 9.5 %.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-B(a)

**Brower's Rectangular Method:**

In Brower's rectangular representation, the x-axis is **fuel concentration (vol %)** and the y-axis is **oxidiser concentration (vol %)**. The inert is implicit (the **balance**). The diagram is constructed by:

1. Drawing a horizontal line at the air baseline (21 % O₂).
2. Marking LFL and UFL on this baseline.
3. Marking the LOC on the y-axis at zero fuel concentration.
4. Drawing smooth curves from each LFL and UFL point converging at LOC.
5. Drawing the **stoichiometric line** from (C_st, O₂_st) upward to (100 % Fuel, 0 % O₂).

**Key Differences between Rectangular and Ternary Stoichiometric Lines:**

| Feature | Rectangular (Brower) | Ternary (Coward–Jones) |
|---|---|---|
| Stoich line shape | Straight diagonal line | Straight line through triangle |
| Origin of line | (C_st, O₂_st) only | Through both fuel and oxidiser vertices |
| Closure | Implicit (Inert = 100 − F − O₂) | Explicit (third vertex) |
| Use | Quick design sketches | Rigorous composition tracking |

*(Marks breakdown: 4 marks sketch and steps, 3 marks comparison.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-B(b)

**Step 1 — Initial composition:**

$$(\%\text{CH}_4,\ \%\text{O}_2,\ \%\text{N}_2) = (6.0,\ 19.0,\ 75.0)$$

Plot point P₁: (6.0, 19.0) on the rectangular diagram.

**Step 2 — Final composition after methane addition:**

$$(\%\text{CH}_4,\ \%\text{O}_2,\ \%\text{N}_2) = (9.0,\ 19.0,\ 72.0)$$

Plot point P₂: (9.0, 19.0) on the rectangular diagram.

**Step 3 — Determine envelope boundaries at O₂ = 19 %:**

Reading the rectangular diagram at the air baseline (O₂ = 19 %), the envelope boundaries are:
- LFL = **5.0 % CH₄**
- UFL = **15.0 % CH₄**

Both points P₁ (6 %) and P₂ (9 %) lie **between LFL (5 %) and UFL (15 %)** — hence **both points are INSIDE the flammable envelope**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

The headspace composition **remains inside the flammable envelope** throughout the transient from 6 % to 9 % CH₄. The transient does *not* pass through a safe zone — the entire 6 → 9 % trajectory is hazardous.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often mistakenly check only the *start* and *end* points. The correct analysis requires checking the **entire trajectory line** (here, the horizontal line at 19 % O₂ from fuel = 6 % to fuel = 9 %) for envelope intersection. Always state explicitly whether the entire path, not just the endpoints, lies inside the envelope.

**Engineering Recommendation:**

- Either vent methane to drop below 5 %, OR
- Inert with N₂ to drop O₂ below LOC = 12 %, OR
- Eliminate all ignition sources during the transient.

*(Marks breakdown: 2 marks point plotting, 3 marks envelope comparison, 2 marks recommendation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Mis-applying Le Chatelier's rule** — students use air-basis mole fractions instead of fuel-basis mole fractions. Always renormalise fuel mole fractions to sum to unity *before* dividing by individual LFLs.
> 2. **Confusing LOC with stoichiometric O₂** — LOC is the *minimum* oxygen for combustion (≈ 12 % for CH₄), NOT the stoichiometric oxygen (≈ 19 %). These are fundamentally different.
> 3. **Forgetting the closure equation** — every ternary mixture must satisfy F + O₂ + I = 100 %. Verifying this catches arithmetic errors and earns a courtesy mark.
> 4. **Plotting the wrong curves** — the envelope closes at the LOC, not at the inert vertex. Students often draw curves going to the inert vertex incorrectly.
> 5. **Skipping trajectory analysis** — examiners award marks for tracing the *process path* on the diagram, not just plotting a single static composition point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorise Before the Exam**

- **Flammability diagram = ternary plot of (Fuel, Oxidiser, Inert)** with closure F + O₂ + I = 100 %.
- **Two representations:** Ternary triangle (Coward–Jones) and Rectangular (Brower).
- **Five anchor points** to memorise for CH₄/air/N₂: LFL = 5 %, UFL = 15 %, LOC = 12.1 %, C_st = 9.5 %, O₂_st = 19.0 %.
- **Envelope closes at LOC** on the oxidiser–inert edge; envelope is open between LFL and UFL on the fuel–oxidiser edge.
- **Stoichiometric line** is a *straight line* through (C_st, O₂_st) joining the fuel and oxidiser vertices.
- **Le Chatelier's rule** for LFL of blends:
  $\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = 100 \big/ \sum (y_i / \text{LFL}_i)$ — use **fuel-basis** mole fractions.
- **Burgess–Wheeler LOC approximation:**
  $\text{LOC} = z \cdot \text{LFL} / [100 - z(1 - \text{LFL})]$ where $z$ = stoich O₂ %.
- **Inside the envelope → flammable; Outside → safe.**
- **Inerting trajectory** is a straight line on the ternary diagram passing through the 100 % Inert vertex.
- **Tank purging order:** fuel-rich → through envelope (dangerous!) → fuel-lean → inert-safe.
- **Applications:** tank blanketing, hot work permits, purging design, relief device sizing.
- **Closure equation** must always sum to 100 % — use it as an arithmetic check.
- **Always trace entire process trajectories**, not just endpoints.
- **Methane is the KTU reference fuel** — know its five key numbers cold.
- **For multiple-choice questions:** distinguish LOC (< 100 % fuel line) from LFL (> 0 % fuel line) — common trap.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)
> **Hazard:** A *physical condition* or *event* with the inherent potential to cause harm to people, property, environment, or business continuity. A hazard is an intrinsic property of a material, piece of equipment, or process that *can* cause damage under certain conditions—it is **NOT** the damage itself.
>
> **Risk:** A quantitative or qualitative measure of the *probability* of a hazard occurring **multiplied by** the *consequence* (severity) of the resulting harm.

In the KTU 2024 Process Safety Engineering framework, hazards in process industries are catalogued using the universally adopted **"5 Ps of Loss Prevention"** paradigm:

| Loss Category | Description |
|---|---|
| **P**eople | Injury, illness, fatality to workers and the public |
| **P**roperty | Physical damage to equipment, buildings, infrastructure |
| **P**roduction | Loss of output, downtime, process disruption |
| **P**ollution | Environmental contamination of air, water, soil |
| **P**rofessional Image | Reputational damage, legal liability, loss of licence to operate |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] The "Loaded Revolver" Analogy
> Imagine a **loaded revolver** kept in a busy office.
>
> - The **revolver itself** is a **HAZARD** — it has the intrinsic *potential* to cause harm (it can fire a bullet).
> - Whether it actually *does* fire and the magnitude of the damage if it does — that is **RISK**.
> - A hazard **always exists** once a gun is loaded. Risk depends on the *likelihood* of pulling the trigger AND *where the gun is pointed*.
> - **Zero risk** = *Unload the gun* (elimination). **Lower risk** = *Put it in a locked safe* (engineering control).
>
> In a chemical plant: **Hazard** = "We have 50 tonnes of toluene stored at 40 °C." **Risk** = "The probability of a leak AND the consequences if it ignites."

### 1.3 Hazard vs. Risk — The Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING] Common Exam Pitfall
> Students frequently write "Hazard and Risk are the same." They are **NOT**. Examiners award **2 marks** specifically for differentiating these two terms correctly.

| Parameter | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Source of potential harm | Combination of probability and consequence |
| **Nature** | *Qualitative* (inherent property) | *Quantitative* (calculated/estimated) |
| **Existence** | Always present (intrinsic) | Variable (depends on scenario) |
| **Elimination** | Possible (remove the substance) | Managed (reduce to ALARP) |
| **Example** | "Toluene is flammable" | "Probability of toluene pool fire = 10⁻⁴/yr, fatality consequence = 5" |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Hazard Classification

- **Flash Point (FP):** Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapour to form an ignitable mixture with air. Unit: °C or K.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** Temperature at which a substance spontaneously ignites without an external source. Unit: °C.
- **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) / Lower Flammable Limit (LFL):** Minimum concentration of vapour in air below which propagation of a flame does **not** occur. Typical value: **1–5% by volume** for common solvents.
- **Upper Explosive Limit (UEL) / Upper Flammable Limit (UFL):** Maximum concentration above which propagation does not occur. Typical value: **7–15% by volume**.
- **Flammability Range:** Concentration band between LEL and UEL. Unit: **% vol**.
- **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50%):** Concentration in air that kills **50%** of test animals within a specified exposure time (commonly 4 h). Unit: **ppm** or **mg/m³**.
- **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50%):** Single dose that kills 50% of test animals. Unit: **mg/kg body weight**.
- **TLV (Threshold Limit Value):** Airborne concentration to which a worker can be exposed day after day without adverse effects. Unit: **ppm** or **mg/m³**.
- **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health):** Maximum airborne concentration from which a worker could escape within 30 minutes without irreversible health effects.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Process Industry Hazards

Process industry hazards are classified along **four orthogonal dimensions** (KTU 2024 syllabus standard):

```
HAZARD CLASSIFICATION AXES
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1. BY ORIGIN    →  Physical | Chemical | Biological | Ergonomic
2. BY FORM      →  Fire | Explosion | Toxic Release | Mechanical
3. BY SEVERITY  →  Catastrophic | Critical | Marginal | Negligible
4. BY EXPOSURE  →  Acute (single event) | Chronic (long-term)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

### 2.2 Classification by Origin — Detailed Sub-Types

#### A. Physical Hazards
> [!NOTE] KTU High-Yield Sub-Group
> Hazards arising from physical states, mechanical energy, or environmental conditions.

- **Fire hazards** — uncontrolled combustion
- **Explosion hazards** — rapid release of energy with pressure wave
- **Mechanical hazards** — rotating equipment, high-pressure vessels, falling objects
- **Electrical hazards** — shock, arc flash, static discharge
- **Thermal hazards** — hot surfaces, cryogenic burns, steam burns
- **Noise & vibration** — long-term hearing loss, structural fatigue
- **Radiation** — ionizing (X-ray, gamma) and non-ionizing (UV, IR, RF)

#### B. Chemical Hazards
- **Toxicity hazards** — poisons (acute + chronic)
- **Reactivity hazards** — unstable chemicals, water-reactives, oxidizers
- **Corrosivity hazards** — acids, bases (cause tissue/equipment damage)
- **Flammability hazards** — the dominant concern in petroleum/petrochemical
- **Asphyxiation hazards** — nitrogen, CO₂ (displace O₂)

#### C. Biological Hazards
- Bacteria, viruses, fungi, toxins (relevant in pharma, food, water-treatment)
- Generally **out of primary KTU scope** but listed for completeness

#### D. Ergonomic & Human Factors
- Repetitive strain, manual handling, slip-trip-fall, human error

### 2.3 Classification of Fires (NFPA / IS 3594 Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] This is a KTU examination favourite — appears in nearly every ESE paper.

| Class | Fuel Type | Extinguishing Agent | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth, rubber) | Water, foam, dry chemical | Office fire, wood fire |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids, gases, greases | Foam, CO₂, dry chemical, halon | Petrol, LPG, acetone |
| **Class C** | Energized electrical equipment | CO₂, dry chemical (non-conductive) | Transformer fire, control panel |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Special dry powder (NaCl, Cu powder) | Magnesium, sodium, titanium, zirconium |
| **Class E** *(Australia/Asia)* | Class A+B+C combined | Multi-purpose agent | – |
| **Class K / F** | Cooking oils & fats (kitchen) | Wet chemical (saponification) | Vegetable oil, animal fat |

### 2.4 Classification of Explosions

> [!NOTE] Explosion = Rapid, violent release of energy generating a shock wave with ΔP > atmospheric pressure.

- **Mechanical Explosion:** Failure of a pressure vessel, boiler, or pipeline due to overpressure (e.g., BLEVE — Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion).
- **Chemical Explosion:**
  - *Deflagration:* Flame speed **< speed of sound** (subsonic), pressure rise moderate (e.g., gas cloud deflagration).
  - *Detonation:* Flame speed **> speed of sound** (supersonic, typically 1500–9000 m/s), shock wave + destructive pressure (e.g., TNT, vapour cloud detonation).
- **Nuclear Explosion:** Out of process industry scope; reference only.
- **Dust Explosion:** Combustible dust dispersed in air within explosive limits. Requires: **(1)** combustible dust, **(2)** dispersion, **(3)** confinement, **(4)** ignition source, **(5)** oxygen.
- **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE):** Released flammable vapour mixes with air → finds ignition → flame propagates through cloud (e.g., Flixborough disaster, 1974).

### 2.5 NFPA 704 Hazard Diamond — The Standard Classification System

> [!IMPORTANT] The NFPA 704 (or "fire diamond") is the **standard pictorial classification** for industrial hazards and is a **direct KTU syllabus item**.

```
         BLUE
       (HEALTH)
          4
   ┌──────────────┐
   │              │
   │  NFPA  704   │
 4 │   HAZARD     │ 4  RED
 Y │  DIAMOND     │    (FLAMMABILITY)
 E │              │
 L │              │ 0
 L │              │
 O │              │ YELLOW
 W │              │ (REACTIVITY)
   │              │ 4
   └──────────────┘
        WHITE
     (SPECIAL HAZARDS)
         W, OX, SA
```

- **Blue (Left) – Health Hazard (0–4)**
- **Red (Top) – Flammability Hazard (0–4)**
- **Yellow (Right) – Reactivity / Instability (0–4)**
- **White (Bottom) – Special Hazards** (OX = oxidizer, W = water-reactive, SA = simple asphyxiant)

| Rating | Health (Blue) | Flammability (Red) | Reactivity (Yellow) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | No hazard | Will not burn | Stable |
| **1** | Slight irritation | Must be preheated to burn | Mild — change on heating |
| **2** | Temporary injury | Ignites on moderate heating | Violent chemical change |
| **3** | Serious / permanent injury | Ignites at normal temperatures | Shock/heat may detonate |
| **4** | Fatal / short exposure | Extremely flammable | May detonate spontaneously |

### 2.6 Toxicological Classification (GHS / OSHA Categories)

| GHS Category | Signal Word | LD₅₀ (oral, rat, mg/kg) | LC₅₀ (inhalation, 4h, ppm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Category 1** (most toxic) | **DANGER** | < 5 | < 100 |
| **Category 2** | DANGER | 5 – 50 | 100 – 500 |
| **Category 3** | DANGER | 50 – 300 | 500 – 2500 |
| **Category 4** | WARNING | 300 – 2000 | 2500 – 20000 |
| **Category 5** | WARNING | 2000 – 5000 | > 20000 |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] All formulas required to solve KTU Part B problems on this topic.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Risk} = \text{Probability} \times \text{Consequence}$ | Fundamental risk equation; dimensionless risk index when normalized |
| 2 | $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times \text{GC}$ | Dow Fire & Explosion Index; MF = Material Factor, GC = General Process Hazards Credit |
| 3 | $\text{ERPG-2} \le \text{Exposure} \le \text{ERPG-3}$ triggers emergency response | Emergency Response Planning Guidelines (AIHA), 60-min exposure limits |
| 4 | $\Delta P = P_{\max} - P_{\text{atm}}$ | Explosion overpressure; $\Delta P > 5$ kPa = significant damage, $\Delta P > 70$ kPa = lethal |
| 5 | $\text{CEF} = \sum_i (C_i \times Q_i)$ | Cumulative Exposure Factor for multi-component toxic releases |
| 6 | $\text{Risk Rank} = \text{Severity} \times \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Frequency}$ | Used in HAZOP & LOPA ranking matrices |
| 7 | $\% \text{LEL} = \dfrac{C_{\text{measured}}}{C_{\text{LEL}}} \times 100$ | Sensor alarming threshold; trip set-points typically at 20–25% LEL |

### 2.8 Engineering Utility

These classification systems are **not academic exercises** — they directly drive:
- **Plant layout decisions** (separation distances per HSE UK guidance)
- **Fire protection system specification** (foam vs. sprinkler vs. gas flooding)
- **PPE selection** (Level A hazmat for IDLH, Level D for nuisance dust)
- **Insurance premium calculation** (F&EI drives property insurance)
- **Emergency planning & community right-to-know** (SARA Title III, OSHA PSM)

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Equation (Exhaustive Step-Wise)

Starting from first principles as required by KTU's analytical-derivation-style questions:

> **Given:** A process plant contains a *hazardous event* that may or may not occur, and if it does occur, it produces a measurable *consequence*.

**Step 1 — Define the hazard as a binary random variable.**
Let $H = 1$ if the hazardous event occurs within one year, and $H = 0$ otherwise. The probability of occurrence is:

$$P(H=1) = p, \quad 0 \le p \le 1$$

**Step 2 — Define consequence as a function of impact severity.**
Let $C$ be the magnitude of harm (e.g., fatality, ₹ loss, environmental damage index). $C$ is a non-negative real number:

$$C \in \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$$

**Step 3 — Define the expected loss (risk) per unit time.**
In risk engineering, the *expected* loss per year is the product of the probability of occurrence and the consequence magnitude:

$$R = E[\text{Loss}] = P(H=1) \times C + P(H=0) \times 0$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using the fact that $P(H=0) = 1 - p$.**

$$R = p \cdot C + (1 - p) \cdot 0$$

**Step 5 — Final Risk Equation:**

$$\boxed{R = p \times C}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A hazard with $p = 0$ (impossible event) has zero risk regardless of consequence. A consequence $C = 0$ (no harm) has zero risk regardless of probability. **Risk can only be reduced by reducing $p$, $C$, or both.**

**Step 6 — Extension to multi-event scenarios.**
For a process with $n$ independent hazardous events, total risk is the *summation* of individual risks (valid only if events are independent and consequences are non-overlapping):

$$R_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot C_i$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A reactor contains toluene. The probability of a major leak in any given year is $1 \times 10^{-3}$. If the leak occurs, the expected property damage is ₹ 50 crore. The probability of a vapour cloud explosion *given* a leak is $0.2$, and the fatality consequence per explosion event is 10 persons. Calculate: (i) the property-damage risk, (ii) the fatality risk, and (iii) the total annualized risk index if each fatality is monetized at ₹ 1 crore.

**Step 1 — Property damage risk:**

$$R_{\text{prop}} = p_{\text{leak}} \times C_{\text{prop}}$$

$$R_{\text{prop}} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (\text{₹ }50 \text{ crore})$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{prop}} = \text{₹ } 5 \times 10^{4} \text{ per year} = \text{₹ }50{,}000 \text{ per year}}$$

**Step 2 — Fatality risk (compound probability):**

$$p_{\text{explosion}} = p_{\text{leak}} \times p_{\text{explosion} \mid \text{leak}}$$

$$p_{\text{explosion}} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.2) = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$R_{\text{fatality}} = p_{\text{explosion}} \times C_{\text{fatality}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{fatality}} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities/year} = 1 \text{ fatality every 500 years}}$$

**Step 3 — Monetized total risk:**

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{prop}} + (R_{\text{fatality}} \times \text{₹ 1 crore})$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = \text{₹ }50{,}000 + (2 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{7})$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = \text{₹ }50{,}000 + \text{₹ }20{,}000 = \boxed{\text{₹ }70{,}000 \text{ per year}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hazard-to-Control Mapping

> [!NOTE] The following matrix is the KTU-recommended way to present a 14-mark classification question.

| Hazard Class | Sub-Class | Example Substance/Scenario | Primary Consequence | First-Line Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fire** | Pool fire | Diesel storage tank rupture | Radiative heat, asset loss | Bund wall, foam pourers |
| **Fire** | Jet fire | Pressurised methane pipeline leak | Direct flame impingement | Emergency Isolation Valve (EIV) |
| **Explosion** | VCE | Propane tank farm leak + ignition | Blast overpressure 10–50 kPa | Vapour detection, gas dispersion modelling |
| **Explosion** | Dust explosion | Grain silo, flour mill | Secondary dust explosion | Inerting (N₂), venting panels |
| **Explosion** | BLEVE | LPG rail wagon engulfed in fire | Catastrophic vessel failure | Water spray cooling, firewater deluge |
| **Toxic Release** | Acute | Chlorine cylinder rupture | Fatal pulmonary oedema | Toxic gas detectors, evacuation |
| **Toxic Release** | Chronic | Benzene exposure in refinery | Leukaemia (long-term) | Closed sampling, LEV, medical monitoring |
| **Reactivity** | Water-reactive | Sodium metal contact with H₂O | H₂ release + fire/explosion | Dry storage, segregated containment |
| **Reactivity** | Oxidizer | H₂O₂ spill near organics | Fire intensification | Distance separation, dikes |
| **Mechanical** | Rotating | Pump shaft entanglement | Laceration, fatality | Machine guarding, LOTO |
| **Mechanical** | Pressure | Compressor surge | Vessel rupture | Rupture discs, PRV, surge analysis |
| **Asphyxiation** | Inert gas | Liquid N₂ leak in confined space | O₂ displacement → loss of consciousness | O₂ monitors, confined space entry permit |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
