> **Differentiate between Hazard and Risk with two industrial examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Limit: 60–80 words):**

A **Hazard** is an intrinsic property of a material, equipment, or process that has the *potential* to cause harm. It is qualitative and always exists once the hazardous source is present. For example, *toluene is flammable* is a hazard.

**Risk** is the quantitative combination of the *probability* of the hazardous event occurring and the *consequence* (severity) of the resulting harm. Risk = Probability × Consequence. For example, the *annual probability of toluene pool fire = 10⁻⁴* with a consequence of ₹10 crore is a risk statement.

A hazard can exist with zero risk if the probability of release is negligible; conversely, a high consequence with low probability may still have low risk. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **List and briefly explain the five classes of fire as per NFPA / IS 3594 classification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Class | Fuel | Extinguishing Agent |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Ordinary combustibles — wood, paper, cloth, rubber | Water, foam, dry chemical |
| **B** | Flammable liquids & gases — petrol, LPG, acetone | Foam, CO₂, dry chemical |
| **C** | Energized electrical equipment — transformers, panels | CO₂, dry chemical (non-conductive) |
| **D** | Combustible metals — magnesium, sodium, titanium | Special dry powder (NaCl, Cu) |
| **K/F** | Cooking oils & fats — vegetable oil, animal fat | Wet chemical (saponification) |

Selecting the wrong extinguishing agent (e.g., water on Class D metal fires) *intensifies* the fire by liberating hydrogen. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 2 Choice 1]`

> **(a)** Classify the various **types of hazards** encountered in a process industry. Give at least **two examples** for each major class. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the **NFPA 704 hazard identification diamond**. Explain the significance of the 0–4 rating scale for each coloured quadrant. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Classification of Process Industry Hazards (7 Marks):**

Process industry hazards are broadly classified under **four major classes** based on the nature of the source of harm:

**1. Physical Hazards (2 marks):**
- **Fire:** Pool fire, jet fire, flash fire arising from flammable liquids/gases.
- **Explosion:** Vapour cloud explosion (VCE), dust explosion, BLEVE, mechanical explosion from overpressure vessels.
- *Examples:* LPG rail wagon fire (Bhopal 1984-style), grain silo dust explosion.

**2. Chemical Hazards (2 marks):**
- **Toxicity:** Acute poisons (Cl₂, HCN, phosgene) and chronic carcinogens (benzene, vinyl chloride).
- **Reactivity:** Water-reactives (sodium), oxidizers (H₂O₂, KMnO₄), unstable peroxides.
- **Corrosivity:** Strong acids (H₂SO₄, HCl), strong bases (NaOH, KOH).
- *Examples:* Chlorine cylinder leak in a water-treatment plant; benzene exposure in a refinery.

**3. Mechanical / Mechanical-Energy Hazards (1.5 marks):**
- Rotating equipment entanglement, high-pressure vessel rupture, crane drop, conveyor nip points.
- *Examples:* Pump shaft entanglement; compressor surge causing pipe whip.

**4. Biological & Ergonomic Hazards (1.5 marks):**
- Biological: bacterial contamination in pharma/food plants.
- Ergonomic: manual lifting injuries, repetitive strain, slip-trip-fall.
- *Examples:* Legionella in cooling towers; back injury from manual material handling.

> The classification can also be cross-cut by **severity** (catastrophic / critical / marginal / negligible) and **exposure duration** (acute vs. chronic).

---

**Part (b) — NFPA 704 Hazard Identification Diamond (7 Marks):**

The **NFPA 704 (Standard System for the Identification of the Hazards of Materials for Emergency Response)** is a diamond-shaped placard that conveys, at a glance, the health, flammability, reactivity, and special hazards of a material. The diamond is divided into **four coloured quadrants** (see Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2):

| Quadrant | Position | Colour | Hazard Type | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Left** | 9 o'clock | **BLUE** | Health | No hazard | Slight irritation | Temporary injury | Serious or permanent | Fatal or short exposure |
| **Top** | 12 o'clock | **RED** | Flammability | Will not burn | Must be preheated | Ignites on moderate heat | Ignites at normal temp | Extremely flammable |
| **Right** | 3 o'clock | **YELLOW** | Reactivity | Stable | Mild — change on heating | Violent chemical change | Shock/heat may detonate | May detonate spontaneously |
| **Bottom** | 6 o'clock | **WHITE** | Special | (No rating) | (No rating) | (No rating) | (No rating) | Uses symbols like **OX** (oxidizer), **W** (water-reactive), **SA** (simple asphyxiant) |

**Significance of the 0–4 Rating (3 marks):**
- The scale is **logarithmic in consequence severity**, not linear.
- Rating **0** = minimal residual hazard; Rating **4** = severe, life-threatening, requiring full Level-A hazmat response.
- The placement at the **centre of the diamond** is a quick reference; the numbers are read by emergency responders (fire brigade, paramedics) from a distance of up to **15 metres**.
- **Example:** Chlorine is rated as **Health 3, Flammability 0, Reactivity 0, Special OX** indicating that the primary concern is acute inhalation toxicity with negligible flammability.

> **Engineering Utility:** NFPA 704 ratings drive the specification of firewater systems, PPE levels for emergency response, the choice of extinguishing media, and the labelling of storage tanks in plant layout drawings.

**(Total: 7 marks)** — *Marks breakdown: diagram 3 marks + rating scale 3 marks + example 1 mark.*

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 2 Choice 2]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of explosions** in process industries, highlighting the difference between **deflagration and detonation** with a clear **P-t (pressure–time) sketch** description. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A chemical reactor stores **10,000 kg of acetone**. The probability of catastrophic rupture in a year is **5 × 10⁻⁴**. If rupture occurs, the expected fatality is **3 persons**. Each fatality is monetized at **₹ 50 lakh**. Calculate (i) the **fatality risk** per year, and (ii) the **monetized risk** in ₹/year. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Classification of Explosions & Deflagration vs. Detonation (7 Marks):**

An **explosion** is a sudden, violent release of energy accompanied by a shock wave and rapid expansion of gases. Explosions in process industries are classified as:

**1. Mechanical Explosion (1 mark):** Caused by overpressure failure of vessels, boilers, pipelines. E.g., boiler explosion, pipeline rupture.

**2. Chemical Explosion (1 mark):** Caused by rapid oxidation / decomposition reactions. Sub-divided into:
- **Deflagration (1.5 marks):** Flame speed **subsonic** (typically < 300 m/s). Pressure rises **moderately** to a peak of ~0.5–1 MPa. Pressure-time curve is *smooth* and *gradual* (long rise time, ~50–500 ms).
- **Detonation (1.5 marks):** Flame speed **supersonic** (1500–9000 m/s). Pressure rises **sharply** to a peak of 1.5–2 MPa with a near-vertical front. Pressure-time curve is *near-instantaneous* (rise time < 1 ms).

**P-t (Pressure–Time) Diagram — Verbal Sketch Description:**
> *For deflagration, the pressure ramp is a gentle, parabolic curve rising from atmospheric to peak pressure over hundreds of milliseconds. For detonation, the curve is an almost vertical jump from atmospheric to peak pressure within microseconds, followed by an equally sharp decay — resembling the letter "spike" rather than a "hump".*

**3. Other Sub-Classes (2 marks):**
- **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE):** Flammable vapour released, mixes with air, then ignites → flame propagates through cloud. *Example:* Flixborough 1974.
- **Dust Explosion:** Combustible dust dispersed in air + confined + ignited. Often has a **primary** (small) followed by **secondary** (larger, devastating) explosion. *Example:* Imperial Sugar 2008.
- **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion):** Fire impinges on a liquid-filled pressure vessel → wall weakens → catastrophic failure → fireball. *Example:* San Juanico 1984.
- **Physical Explosion (e.g., steam boiler):** Rapid phase change or overpressure, no chemical reaction.

---

**Part (b) — Risk Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Mass of acetone = 10,000 kg (not directly needed for risk calculation)
- Probability of rupture, $p = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per year
- Consequence in fatalities, $C = 3$ persons
- Monetization rate, $V = \text{₹ 50 lakh per fatality} = \text{₹ } 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ per fatality}$

**Step 1 — Fatality risk per year (3 marks):**

Using the fundamental risk equation:

$$R_{\text{fatality}} = p \times C$$

$$R_{\text{fatality}} = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 3$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{fatality}} = 15 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities per year}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This corresponds to **1 fatality every ~667 years** — within the broadly acceptable individual risk band of 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ per year for public fatality risk.

**Step 2 — Monetized risk per year (4 marks):**

$$R_{\text{monetary}} = R_{\text{fatality}} \times V$$

$$R_{\text{monetary}} = (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (\text{₹ } 5 \times 10^{6})$$

$$R_{\text{monetary}} = 7.5 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{monetary}} = \text{₹ } 7{,}500 \text{ per year}}$$

**Step 3 — Decision (for full marks, include a brief comment):**
> Since monetized risk is only ₹ 7,500/year and fatality risk is 1.5 × 10⁻³/yr (within tolerable limits), no additional risk-reduction measures are immediately mandated — but a cost-benefit analysis against engineering controls (e.g., rupture disc at ₹ 1 lakh) is recommended.

**(Total: 7 marks)** — *Marks breakdown: 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for numerical substitution & final fatality risk, 1 mark for monetization formula, 2 marks for numerical substitution & final monetary risk, 1 mark for engineering interpretation.*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Conflating Hazard with Risk** — 2 marks lost in nearly 60% of answer scripts. Always explicitly state: *"Hazard is intrinsic, Risk is probabilistic."*
> 2. **Forgetting units in risk calculations** — Writing "1.5" instead of "1.5 × 10⁻³ fatalities/year" costs 1 mark.
> 3. **NFPA 704 ratings confused with NFPA 13 / 30** — The 704 diamond is for **emergency response**; it is NOT the same as fire-protection codes (13) or flammable-liquid codes (30).
> 4. **Class D fire extinguished with water** — Wrong. Water + burning magnesium releases **H₂** → intensifies fire. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 5. **Deflagration vs. Detonation** — Students often write *"detonation is faster"* but miss the key difference: **detonation has a coupled shock wave** that preheats and pre-compresses the unreacted mixture. The shock wave *is* the distinguishing feature.
> 6. **Risk = Consequence** — A common error. Risk is **Probability × Consequence**, not consequence alone.
> 7. **GHS vs. NFPA** — GHS (Globally Harmonized System) uses **1–5** for toxicity categories; NFPA uses **0–4** for ALL quadrants. Mixing up the numbering scheme loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Hazard** = intrinsic potential to harm. **Risk** = Probability × Consequence. They are **NOT** the same.
- [ ] **5 Ps of Loss** = People, Property, Production, Pollution, Professional Image.
- [ ] **Fire classes:** A (wood), B (liquids), C (electrical), D (metals — **NEVER use water**), K (kitchen oils).
- [ ] **Explosion classes:** Mechanical, Chemical (Deflagration < sound / Detonation > sound), Dust, VCE, BLEVE.
- [ ] **Deflagration:** subsonic, smooth P-t curve. **Detonation:** supersonic, near-vertical P-t spike with shock wave.
- [ ] **NFPA 704 Diamond:** Blue = Health, Red = Flammability, Yellow = Reactivity, White = Special (OX, W, SA).
- [ ] **Risk equation:** $R = p \times C$. For multi-event plants: $R_{\text{total}} = \sum_i p_i \cdot C_i$.
- [ ] **Toxic metrics:** LC₅₀, LD₅₀, TLV, IDLH, ERPG-1/2/3. Always quote **units** (ppm, mg/m³, mg/kg).
- [ ] **GHS toxicity scale:** Category 1 (most toxic, signal word DANGER) to Category 5 (least toxic, WARNING).
- [ ] **LEL/UEL range** is the *flammability envelope*. Sensor trips are usually at **20–25% LEL**.
- [ ] **Hazard identification precedes risk assessment** — you cannot evaluate risk without first classifying the hazard.
- [ ] Standard tools: **NFPA 704, OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119, Dow F&EI, CEI, HAZOP, LOPA, Bow-Tie.**
- [ ] KTU-2024 typical paper: **1 Part-A (3 marks)** on hazard vs. risk OR fire classes; **1 Part-B (14 marks)** on classification + numerical risk.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consequences of Chemical Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Consequences of Chemical Hazards # SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Consequences of Chemical Hazards** are the quantified physical, physiological, and environmental outcomes that occur when a hazardous chemical substance is unintentionally released from its containment due to loss of process control, equipment failure, or operational error. In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, consequence analysis is defined as the systematic determination of the magnitude and spatial distribution of damaging effects (toxic exposure, thermal radiation, overpressure) following an unwanted chemical release. The analysis proceeds through four sequential stages: 1. **Source Model** – Quantifies the rate, quantity, and phase of the discharged material. 2. **Dispersion Model** – Calculates the transport of the released material through air, water, or soil. 3. **Effect (Consequence) Model** – Converts the dispersed concentration or energy into a measurable damage intensity (thermal flux, overpressure, toxic dose). 4. **Damage Model (Probit Analysis)** – Translates the damage intensity into a probabilistic outcome for human life, structures, and the environment. Mathematically, consequence severity is expressed through the **Probit Variable ($Y$)**, which links physical dose ($V$) to percentage probability of injury or fatality: $$Y = a + b \ln(V)$$ where $a$ and $b$ are empirical constants derived from experimental accident data. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Dam Break Scenario** > > Imagine a large water reservoir held by a dam. If the dam cracks: > - **Source** = The hole in the dam (release rate of water). > - **Dispersion** = How the water flows downhill and spreads. > - **Consequence** = How deep the water gets at each village downstream and how much damage it does. > - **Damage Probit** = How many houses collapse based on water depth (e.g., 1 m depth = 10% damage probability; 3 m = 90%). > > In chemical hazards, the "water" is replaced by toxic vapor, fire, or blast energy, and the "villages" are people, equipment, and the environment. The deeper the chemical "flood" or the hotter the "heat wave," the more severe the consequence. ## 1.3 Classification of Chemical Hazard Consequences | Consequence Type | Physical Phenomenon | Damage Mechanism | Typical Outcome | |---|---|---|---| | **Toxic Exposure** | Dispersal of poisonous gas/vapor | Inhalation, skin absorption | Lethality, chronic illness | | **Fire (Flash/Pool/Jet/Fireball)** | Combustion of released flammable material | Thermal radiation | Burns, ignition of secondary materials | | **Explosion (VCE/BLEVE/Confined)** | Rapid exothermic reaction | Overpressure shock wave | Lung rupture, structural collapse | | **Environmental Release** | Spill to soil/water | Bioaccumulation, ecosystem toxicity | Long-term ecological damage | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Metric:** The **Emergency Response Planning Guidelines (ERPG)** and **Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health (IDLH)** values set by **AIHA (American Industrial Hygiene Association)** and **OSHA** respectively are the industry-standard benchmarks used worldwide. ERPG-2 = **10 ppm** airborne concentration for 60 minutes for most reactive chemicals. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Toxic Consequence Analysis The physiological response to a toxic gas is governed by **Haber's Law** for dose-response: $$D = C^n \cdot t$$ where: - $D$ = toxic dose (dimensionless or in ppm·min) - $C$ = airborne concentration (ppm) - $t$ = exposure duration (min) - $n$ = substance-specific exponent (commonly $n = 1$ for many gases) ### Probit Equation for Toxic Lethality (General Form) $$Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$$ For specific substances, KTU-prescribed standard probit correlations are: | Substance | Probit Equation | Reference | |---|---|---| | **Chlorine ($\text{Cl}_2$)** | $Y = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(C \cdot t)$ | Withers & Lees | | **Ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$)** | $Y = -30.57 + 4.685 \ln(C \cdot t)$ | Withers & Lees | | **Hydrogen Sulfide ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$)** | $Y = -31.42 + 4.246 \ln(C \cdot t)$ | Withers & Lees | | **Sulfur Dioxide ($\text{SO}_2$)** | $Y = -22.32 + 4.103 \ln(C \cdot t)$ | Withers & Lees | > [!NOTE] > The percentage probability of fatality is obtained from the probit $Y$ using the standard normal cumulative distribution: > $$\text{Percent} = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$ ## 2.2 Fire Consequence Analysis Three principal fire scenarios are analyzed in process safety: ### (a) Pool Fire A pool fire results when a liquid spills and ignites, forming a burning pool. **Thermal radiation intensity** at a distance $x$ is computed using: $$q'' = \frac{\dot{m} \, \Delta H_c \, \tau}{4 \pi x^2}$$ where $\dot{m}$ = mass burning rate (kg/s), $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg), $\tau$ = atmospheric transmissivity, and $x$ = distance from flame center. ### (b) Jet Fire A turbulent diffusion flame from a pressurized gas leak. Thermal flux at a target distance $r$: $$q'' = \frac{\dot{m} \, \Delta H_c}{4 \pi r^2} \cdot f_{\text{rad}}$$ where $f_{\text{rad}}$ is the **fraction of heat radiated** (typically $0.15$–$0.30$ for hydrocarbon jet fires). ### (c) Fireball A near-instantaneous combustion of a vapor cloud. Diameter and duration: $$D_{\text{fireball}} = 5.8 \, M^{1/3}, \quad t_{\text{duration}} = 0.45 \, M^{1/3}$$ where $M$ is the mass of fuel (kg). Thermal dose received by a target at distance $x$: $$q'' = \frac{\Delta H_c \cdot M \cdot f_{\text{rad}}}{4 \pi x^2 \cdot t_{\text{duration}}}$$ ### Probit Equation for Thermal Lethality (Burn Death) $$Y = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln\!\left(t \cdot q''^{4/3}\right)$$ where $q''$ is in $\text{W/m}^2$ and $t$ in seconds. This is the **Eisenberg probit** for skin burn lethality due to thermal radiation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Thermal Radiation Thresholds (Crowl & Louvar):** > - $q'' = 1.0 \text{ kW/m}^2$ = Solar radiation equivalent (no harm) > - $q'' = 5.0 \text{ kW/m}^2$ = Pain threshold for 20 s exposure > - $q'' = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ = First-degree burn, wooden structures ignite > - $q'' = 25.0 \text{ kW/m}^2$ = Significant injury in 10 s > - $q'' = 37.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ = 100% fatality in less than 10 s ## 2.3 Explosion Consequence Analysis ### Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion (UVCE) The TNT-equivalent method is the KTU-prescribed standard: $$M_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{\eta \cdot M_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \Delta H_{c,\text{fuel}}}{\Delta H_{text{TNT}}}$$ where $\eta$ = explosion efficiency (typically $0.01$–$0.10$ for vapor cloud explosions), and $\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}} = 4686 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Scaled distance and peak overpressure: $$Z = \frac{r}{M_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}}, \quad P_s = f(Z)$$ ### Probit Equation for Overpressure Lethality (Lung Rupture) $$Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_s$$ where $P_s$ is the peak side-on overpressure in **psi** (pounds per square inch). At $P_s = 5 \text{ psi}$, structural damage to houses begins; at $P_s = 15 \text{ psi}$, reinforced concrete is damaged. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Save for KTU Exam Day** | # | Formula | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D = C^n \cdot t$ | Toxic dose (Haber's Law) | ppm·min | | 2 | $Y = a + b\ln(C^n t)$ | Toxic probit (lethality) | dimensionless | | 3 | $D_{\text{FB}} = 5.8 M^{1/3}$ | Fireball diameter | m | | 4 | $t_{\text{FB}} = 0.45 M^{1/3}$ | Fireball duration | s | | 5 | $q'' = \dot{m}\Delta H_c \tau / (4\pi x^2)$ | Thermal flux from pool/jet fire | $\text{kW/m}^2$ | | 6 | $Y = -36.38 + 2.56\ln(t \cdot q''^{4/3})$ | Thermal death probit | dimensionless | | 7 | $M_{\text{TNT}} = \eta M_f \Delta H_c / \Delta H_{\text{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalent mass | kg TNT | | 8 | $Z = r / M_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}$ | Scaled distance | $\text{m/kg}^{1/3}$ | | 9 | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91\ln P_s$ | Overpressure lethality probit | dimensionless | | 10 | $\% = 50[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$ | Probit-to-percent conversion | % | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This framework is the cornerstone of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** mandated by the **Major Accidents Hazard (MAH) Rules** under the Indian Factories Act and the international **CCPS / AIChE Guidelines**. Industries where consequence analysis is legally required include LNG terminals, refineries (IOCL, BPCL), petrochemical complexes (Reliance Jamnagar), and pharmaceutical bulk-drug plants. The output of consequence analysis feeds into **Land Use Planning (LUP)** and **Emergency Shutdown System (ESD)** logic. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Toxic Consequence (Chlorine Release) **Problem:** A rail tank car releases **1000 kg of chlorine** in an open rural area. Atmospheric stability class is **F (very stable)**, wind speed $u = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$. The maximum ground-level concentration at the receptor downwind is measured at $C = 50 \text{ ppm}$ for an exposure duration of $t = 30 \text{ min}$. Determine the percentage probability of fatality using the Withers-Lees chlorine probit. ### Step 1: Write the Probit Equation for Chlorine From the formula sheet: $$Y_{\text{Cl}_2} = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(C \cdot t)$$ ### Step 2: Substitute the Numerical Values $$C \cdot t = 50 \text{ ppm} \times 30 \text{ min} = 1500 \text{ ppm}\cdot\text{min}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Natural Logarithm $$\ln(1500) = 7.3132$$ ### Step 4: Plug into the Probit Equation $$ \begin{aligned} Y &= -19.27 + 3.686 \times 7.3132 \\ Y &= -19.27 + 26.957 \\ Y &= 7.687 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Convert Probit to Percentage $$ \begin{aligned} P(\%) &= 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] \\ P(\%) &= 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{7.687 - 5}{1.414}\right)\right] \\ P(\%) &= 50\left[1 + \text{erf}(1.900)\right] \\ P(\%) &= 50\left[1 + 0.9928\right] \\ P(\%) &= 99.64\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A worker exposed at 50 ppm chlorine for 30 minutes under F-stability conditions has a **99.64% probability of fatality**. This justifies evacuation at concentrations as low as **ERPG-1 = 1 ppm** for the public. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pool Fire Thermal Consequence **Problem:** A leaking gasoline storage tank forms a circular pool of diameter $D_p = 20 \text{ m}$. The mass burning rate is $\dot{m} = 0.05 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$, heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 43{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$, and atmospheric transmissivity $\tau = 0.7$. Find the thermal radiation flux received at a distance of $x = 100 \text{ m}$ from the flame center. ### Step 1: Compute Pool Area $$A_p = \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (20)^2}{4} = 314.16 \text{ m}^2$$ ### Step 2: Compute Total Mass Burning Rate $$\dot{m}_{\text{total}} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p = 0.05 \times 314.16 = 15.708 \text{ kg/s}$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Point Source Thermal Radiation Model $$ \begin{aligned} q'' &= \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{total}} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \tau}{4 \pi x^2} \\ q'' &= \frac{15.708 \times 43{,}000 \times 0.7}{4 \pi (100)^2} \\ q'' &= \frac{472{,}803}{125{,}664} \\ q'' &= 3.76 \text{ kW/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Interpret Damage Potential Comparing to the critical thresholds: - $3.76 \text{ kW/m}^2$ is **below** the pain threshold ($5 \text{ kW/m}^2$) → minimal harm at 100 m for short exposures. - However, at 50 m, $q'' \propto 1/x^2$ would yield $q'' \approx 15.05 \text{ kW/m}^2$ → **first-degree burns in seconds**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) Overpressure **Problem:** A propane cloud of mass $M_f = 10{,}000 \text{ kg}$ undergoes an unconfined vapor cloud explosion with efficiency $\eta = 0.05$. Heat of combustion of propane = $\Delta H_c = 46{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Calculate the equivalent TNT mass and the peak side-on overpressure at a distance $r = 200 \text{ m}$. ### Step 1: TNT Equivalent Mass $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{TNT}} &= \frac{\eta \cdot M_f \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{\text{TNT}}} \\ M_{\text{TNT}} &= \frac{0.05 \times 10{,}000 \times 46{,}000}{4686} \\ M_{\text{TNT}} &= \frac{23{,}000{,}000}{4686} \\ M_{\text{TNT}} &= 4908.7 \text{ kg TNT} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Compute Scaled Distance $$ \begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{r}{M_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}} \\ Z &= \frac{200}{(4908.7)^{1/3}} \\ Z &= \frac{200}{17.01} \\ Z &= 11.76 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Use the Baker-Strehlow-Tang (BST) Overpressure Curve For a vapor cloud in open terrain (Mach reflection assumed), the empirical BST correlation gives: $$P_s \approx \frac{8 \times 10^5}{Z^2.75} \quad \text{(in Pa, for } Z > 3\text{)}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_s &= \frac{8 \times 10^5}{(11.76)^{2.75}} \\ P_s &= \frac{8 \times 10^5}{3826} \\ P_s &= 209.1 \text{ Pa} \\ P_s &\approx 0.030 \text{ psi} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > At 200 m, the overpressure is negligible (no lung damage), but **window glass breakage** begins at $P_s \approx 0.15 \text{ psi}$, which would occur at approximately $r \approx 95 \text{ m}$. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Probit-to-Percent Conversion Reference Table | Probit ($Y$) | Percent Probability (%) | |---|---| | 2.67 | 1 | | 3.72 | 5 | | 4.16 | 10 | | 5.00 | 50 | | 5.84 | 90 | | 6.28 | 95 | | 7.33 | 99 | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Consequence Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hazard Identification
HAZOP / What-If] --> B[Source Model
Release Rate & Phase] B --> C[Dispersion Model
Gaussian Plume / Heavy Gas] C --> D{Consequence Type} D -->|Toxic| E1[Toxic Dose Calculation
C x t] D -->|Flammable| E2[Fire Model
Pool / Jet / Fireball] D -->|Reactive| E3[Explosion Model
TNT / TNO Multi-Energy] E1 --> F1[Toxic Probit Equation
Y = a + b ln Cnt] E2 --> F2[Thermal Probit Equation
Y = -36.38 + 2.56 ln t q4/3] E3 --> F3[Blast Probit Equation
Y = -77.1 + 6.91 ln Ps] F1 --> G[Probit to Percent Conversion
Normal Distribution] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> H[Damage Outcome
Fatality / Injury / Property Loss] H --> I[Risk Evaluation
f x C Matrix] I --> J[Safety Recommendations
ESD / Layout / PPE] stepA[Hazard Inventory] --> A stepB[Process Conditions] --> B stepC[Meteorological Data] --> C stepD[Substance Properties] --> D ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Damage Escalation Tree ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Chemical Release Event] --> B0{Loss of Containment} B0 -->|Gas/Vapor Leak| C1[Dispersion] B0 -->|Liquid Spill| C2[Pool Formation] B0 -->|Pressurized Release| C3[Jet Discharge] C1 --> D1{Atmospheric Conditions} D1 -->|Stable F| E1[High Concentration Plume] D1 -->|Neutral D| E2[Moderate Dispersion] C2 --> F1[Ignition Source Check] F1 -->|Ignited| G1[Pool Fire] F1 -->|Delayed| G2[Vapor Cloud Formation] G2 -->|Confined Ignition| H1[VCE Explosion] G2 -->|Confined Obstacles| H2[Blast Wave] C3 --> I1[Jet Fire Scenario] E1 --> J1[Toxic Exposure] E2 --> J1 J1 --> K1[Fatality / Health Damage] G1 --> K2[Thermal Damage] I1 --> K2 H1 --> K3[Overpressure Damage] H2 --> K3 stepX[Wind Speed and Direction] --> D1 stepY[Topography] --> D1 stepZ[Population Density] --> K1 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Probit Damage Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Physical Intensity
Concentration / Radiation / Pressure] --> P2[Dose Accumulation
Time Integrated] P2 --> P3[Probit Variable Y
Empirical Correlation] P3 --> P4[Normal Distribution
Cumulative Function] P4 --> P5[Damage Probability %] P5 --> P6[Risk = f x C
Frequency x Consequence] P6 --> P7[Safety Decision
ALARP Justification] stepQ[Threshold ERPG / IDLH] --> P1 stepR[Population Vulnerability Factor] --> P5 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **probit analysis** as used in chemical consequence estimation. Why is it preferred over a deterministic threshold approach? **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > Probit analysis is a statistical dose-response model that converts a physical intensity of a hazard (toxic concentration, thermal radiation, overpressure) into a **probability of injury or fatality** expressed as a percentage. > > It is preferred because: > 1. **Probabilistic realism** — accounts for biological variability among exposed populations. > 2. **Continuous risk curve** — instead of a binary safe/unsafe threshold, it gives a smooth probability distribution. > 3. **Regulatory acceptance** — AIChE/CCPS and Indian MAH rules mandate probit-based QRA documentation. The probit variable $Y$ is related to percentage probability by: $$Y = 5 + \frac{P - 50}{P(100-P)} \quad \text{(approximate inversion)}$$ > **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between a **pool fire** and a **jet fire** with respect to ignition source, geometry, and the dominant damage mechanism in process plants. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Pool Fire | Jet Fire | |---|---|---| | **Origin** | Liquid spill forms pool; vapor ignites | Pressurized gas leak ignites at release point | | **Geometry** | Flat, circular burning area | Long, turbulent flame jet | | **Direction** | Vertical/buoyant plume | Along the leak trajectory | | **Damage Mode** | Pool-edge thermal radiation | Direct flame impingement + radiation | | **Typical Fuel** | LPG, gasoline, diesel | Natural gas, hydrogen, ethylene | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]** With a neat sketch, explain the four-step methodology of chemical consequence analysis. A storage tank containing **5000 kg of chlorine** develops a leak releasing the entire contents over **30 minutes** in an open, flat area with D-stability, wind speed $u = 4 \text{ m/s}$. The maximum ground-level concentration at the nearest residential receptor located **500 m downwind** is **30 ppm**. Using the Withers-Lees chlorine probit, estimate the probability of fatality. Comment on whether evacuation of the locality is justified. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Outline the Four-Step Methodology **Step 1: Source Model** The source model quantifies how much material escapes. For a pressurized liquefied gas like chlorine, two-phase flashing release calculations use the Bernoulli equation: $$Q = C_d A_p \sqrt{2 \rho \, \Delta P}$$ where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient ($\approx 0.62$ for sharp-edged orifice), $A_p$ is the hole area, $\rho$ is the fluid density, and $\Delta P$ is the pressure difference. **Step 2: Dispersion Model** The Gaussian Plume equation for continuous ground-level release from an elevated source is: $$C(x,y,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$ where $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are Pasquill-Gifford dispersion coefficients depending on stability class and downwind distance $x$. **Step 3: Effect (Damage Intensity) Model** Converts dispersed concentration into dose using Haber's law: $D = C \cdot t$ (for $n=1$). **Step 4: Damage (Probit) Model** Converts dose to probability using the probit equation for chlorine and the probit-to-percent conversion. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Computation **Given:** - $C = 30 \text{ ppm}$ - $t = 30 \text{ min}$ - Withers-Lees chlorine probit: $Y = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(C \cdot t)$ **Step 1: Compute Dose** $$C \cdot t = 30 \times 30 = 900 \text{ ppm}\cdot\text{min}$$ **Step 2: Compute Natural Logarithm** $$\ln(900) = 6.802$$ **Step 3: Compute Probit** $$ \begin{aligned} Y &= -19.27 + 3.686 \times 6.802 \\ Y &= -19.27 + 25.072 \\ Y &= 5.802 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Convert to Percent** $$ \begin{aligned} P(\%) &= 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{5.802 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] \\ P(\%) &= 50\left[1 + \text{erf}(0.567)\right] \\ P(\%) &= 50\left[1 + 0.569\right] \\ P(\%) &= 78.45\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The probability of fatality is **78.45%**, which is **unacceptable** under any risk standard. **Evacuation is unequivocally justified**, and immediate shelter-in-place orders should be issued within a **2.5 km radius** following the AIHA ERPG-3 guideline of 20 ppm for chlorine. --- ### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2]** A **20 kg LPG cylinder** undergoes BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion) after external fire impingement. Assume: > - Combustion efficiency $\eta_c = 0.30$ > - Radiation fraction $f_{\text{rad}} = 0.25$ > - $\Delta H_c = 46{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$ > - Atmospheric transmissivity $\tau = 0.7$ > > Compute: > (a) The fireball diameter and duration, and thermal radiation flux at 50 m. > (b) Using the Eisenberg thermal probit, find the percentage probability of fatality for an exposed person for **15 seconds**. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Fireball Geometry and Thermal Flux **Step 1: Fireball Diameter** $$ D_{\text{FB}} = 5.8 \, M^{1/3} = 5.8 \times (20)^{1/3} $$ $$ (20)^{1/3} = 2.714 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_{\text{FB}} = 5.8 \times 2.714 = 15.74 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2: Fireball Duration** $$ t_{\text{FB}} = 0.45 \, M^{1/3} = 0.45 \times 2.714 = 1.22 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3: Total Radiative Energy** $$ E_{\text{rad}} = \eta_c \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot f_{\text{rad}} $$ $$ E_{\text{rad}} = 0.30 \times 20 \times 46{,}000 \times 0.25 = 69{,}000 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 4: Thermal Flux at 50 m (using point source)** $$ q'' = \frac{E_{\text{rad}} / t_{\text{FB}}}{4 \pi x^2} \cdot \tau $$ $$ q'' = \frac{69{,}000 \times 1000 / 1.22}{4 \pi (50)^2} \times 0.7 $$ $$ q'' = \frac{5.66 \times 10^7}{31{,}416} \times 0.7 = 1261 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 1.26 \text{ kW/m}^2 $$ > [!NOTE] > Note: $q'' = 1.26 \text{ kW/m}^2$ is below pain threshold for 15 s but remember peak fireball flux is much higher within the flame envelope. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Thermal Probit Calculation **Eisenberg Probit Equation:** $$Y = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln(t \cdot q''^{4/3})$$ **Step 1: Compute the Argument** $$ q''^{4/3} = (1261)^{4/3} = (1261)^1 \times (1261)^{1/3} = 1261 \times 10.81 = 13{,}632 $$ **Step 2: Multiply by Exposure Time** $$ t \cdot q''^{4/3} = 15 \times 13{,}632 = 204{,}480 $$ **Step 3: Compute the Probit** $$ \begin{aligned} \ln(204{,}480) &= 12.228 \\ Y &= -36.38 + 2.56 \times 12.228 \\ Y &= -36.38 + 31.30 \\ Y &= -5.08 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Convert to Percentage** $$ \begin{aligned} P(\%) &= 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{-5.08 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] \\ P(\%) &= 50\left[1 + \text{erf}(-7.12)\right] \\ P(\%) &= 50\left[1 - 1.0\right] \approx 0\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** At 50 m, probability of fatality from a 20 kg LPG BLEVE is **negligible**. However, this masks the danger at closer ranges. At 10 m, $q''$ would be $25\times$ higher (by inverse square), giving $Y \approx +5$ and **near 50% fatality**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Probit equations for thermal use **W/m²** and **seconds**, while toxic probits use **ppm·min**. Unit mismatch is the #1 reason for zero marks in numericals. > 2. **Using TNT overpressure probit for thermal damage** or vice-versa — examiners explicitly look for the correct probit equation matching the hazard type. > 3. **Skipping the probit-to-percent conversion** — students often stop at the probit value $Y$ without converting to percentage. Always show the erf-based conversion step. > 4. **Ignoring the ERPG/IDLH context** — even with a correct probit, missing the regulatory threshold discussion costs 1–2 marks. > 5. **Wrong natural-log base** — KTU strictly uses $\ln$ (natural log). Using $\log_{10}$ will give a wrong answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 2 / Chemical Hazard Consequences** - [ ] Consequence analysis follows 4 sequential steps: **Source → Dispersion → Effect → Damage (Probit)**. - [ ] **Haber's Law:** $D = C^n t$ (with $n=1$ for most gases). - [ ] **Toxic probit (chlorine):** $Y = -19.27 + 3.686 \ln(Ct)$. - [ ] **Thermal probit (Eisenberg):** $Y = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln(t q''^{4/3})$. - [ ] **Blast probit:** $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_s$ ($P_s$ in psi). - [ ] **Fireball diameter:** $D = 5.8 M^{1/3}$, **duration:** $t = 0.45 M^{1/3}$. - [ ] **TNT equivalent:** $M_{\text{TNT}} = \eta M_f \Delta H_c / 4686$ ($\Delta H_c$ in kJ/kg). - [ ] **Critical thermal flux:** $5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ = pain, $12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ = first-degree burn, $37.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ = 100% fatal. - [ ] **Probit 5 → 50% probability** (this is the median mortality point). - [ ] Always **convert probit $Y$ to percentage** via the error function or normal distribution table. - [ ] Distinguish between **probit (statistical)** vs **threshold (deterministic)** approaches. - [ ] Three fire modes: **Pool / Jet / Fireball** — each with different geometry and damage pattern. - [ ] Three explosion modes: **UVCE / BLEVE / Confined Explosion**. - [ ] Indian regulatory framework: **Factories Act 1948**, **MSIHC Rules 1989**, **MAH Rules**, **OISD standards**. - [ ] International standards: **AIHA ERPG**, **OSHA IDLH**, **API 752/753** for building layout, **CCPS Guidelines**. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** Remember the magic numbers — **5.8** (fireball diameter coeff.), **0.45** (fireball duration coeff.), **4686** (TNT heat of combustion in kJ/kg), **2.56** (Eisenberg slope), **6.91** (blast probit slope), **5.00** (median probit). These constants appear in nearly every KTU numerical. ---

topics.title: Physical Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Physical Hazards

In the context of Process Safety Engineering (PSE), a **Physical Hazard** is defined as any environmental factor, energy form, or process condition within an industrial facility that can cause bodily injury, property damage, or loss of material without necessarily involving a toxic chemical reaction. These hazards arise from the physical properties of materials, equipment, energy sources, and the work environment itself.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OEBBT723, Module 2 categorizes physical hazards as a distinct class of process risks that originate from mechanical, thermal, electrical, radiative, and acoustic energy transfers. The **OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration)** classifies physical hazards into categories including fire, explosion, pressure release, electrical shock, noise, vibration, and temperature extremes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 Focus):**
> Physical hazards in process industries primarily revolve around **Fire, Explosion, Pressure, Electrical, Thermal, and Mechanical** energy mismanagement. Mastery of flammability parameters (LFL, UFL, Flash Point) is mandatory for university examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Think of a process plant as a **human body**. Chemicals are the "blood" flowing through pipes, and energy is the "metabolism" driving reactions. Physical hazards are like **fevers, shocks, and fractures** — they occur when the body's energy is not properly regulated. A pressure vessel rupture is analogous to a burst blood vessel; an electrical arc flash is like a sudden nerve shock; a fire is a fever that consumes the body itself.

**Real-World Analogy — The Kitchen Stove:**
A domestic LPG stove perfectly illustrates physical hazard management:
- **Fuel (LPG) + Oxygen + Ignition Source** → Fire Triangle
- A small leak without ignition → No hazard realized
- A spark with the right mixture → Deflagration (rapid combustion)
- A blocked vent with overpressure → BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion)

This demonstrates that hazards remain **dormant** until the right combination of energy, material, and conditions align. The role of a Process Safety Engineer is to **break the hazard triangle** before ignition occurs.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following standard engineering units and constants are fundamental to physical hazard analysis:

- **LFL (Lower Flammable Limit)** — measured in **% volume** or **mg/L**
- **UFL (Upper Flammable Limit)** — measured in **% volume** or **mg/L**
- **Flash Point (FP)** — measured in **°C** or **K**
- **Fire Point** — measured in **°C** or **K**
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — measured in **°C** or **K**
- **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** — measured in **mJ (millijoules)**
- **MOC (Minimum Oxygen Concentration)** — typically **< 10% v/v**
- **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)** — measured in **dB(A)** for noise
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure** = **101.325 kPa** or **1 atm**
- **Stoichiometric Combustion Ratio** depends on hydrocarbon family

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** All physical hazards in process plants obey the **Conservation of Energy**. Energy is never destroyed — it is merely converted into forms that cause damage (heat, pressure waves, kinetic fragments, sound).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Physical Hazards (Logical Breakdown)

Physical hazards in process industries are systematically classified into the following energy-based categories:

### 2.1.1 Fire Hazards
- **Definition:** Uncontrolled combustion reaction that releases heat, light, and combustion products.
- **Fire Triangle:** Fuel + Oxidizer (O₂) + Ignition Source + (Uninhibited Chain Reaction → forms the modern **Fire Tetrahedron**)
- **Classes of Fire:**
  - **Class A:** Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth) → Cool with water
  - **Class B:** Flammable liquids/gases (oil, LPG, solvents) → Smother with foam/CO₂
  - **Class C:** Energized electrical equipment → Non-conductive agents
  - **Class D:** Combustible metals (Mg, Na, K, Ti) → Special dry powder
  - **Class K:** Cooking oils/fats → Wet chemical extinguishers

### 2.1.2 Explosion Hazards
- **Deflagration:** Flame speed **< Mach 1** (subsonic), pressure rise **< 8 bar**
- **Detonation:** Flame speed **> Mach 1** (supersonic), pressure rise **> 10 bar**
- **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion):** Catastrophic failure of a pressure vessel containing a superheated liquid.
- **Dust Explosion:** Combustion of finely divided solid particles (grain, flour, coal, metal powders).
- **Confined Space Explosion:** Occurs in vessels, ducts, or enclosed equipment.

### 2.1.3 Pressure Hazards
- **Overpressure:** Internal pressure exceeds MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure).
- **Vacuum Collapse:** External pressure exceeds internal pressure.
- **Pressure Relief Devices:** PSV (Pressure Safety Valve), rupture discs, vent panels.

### 2.1.4 Electrical Hazards
- **Arc Flash:** Release of energy due to a fault in electrical systems.
- **Static Electricity:** Charge accumulation on non-conductive surfaces → ESD (Electrostatic Discharge).
- **Lightning:** Direct strike on process equipment.

### 2.1.5 Thermal Hazards
- **Burns:** From hot surfaces, steam, cryogenic liquids (LNG, LN₂).
- **Heat Stress:** Ambient temperature + humidity + radiant heat → Heat stroke.

### 2.1.6 Mechanical Hazards
- **Rotating Equipment:** Pumps, compressors, mixers → Entanglement risks.
- **Falling Objects:** Crane failures, dropped tools.
- **Confined Space Entry:** Oxygen deficiency, engulfment.

### 2.1.7 Noise & Vibration Hazards
- **Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL):** Sustained exposure **> 85 dB(A)** for 8 hours.
- **Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS):** From prolonged tool use.

## 2.2 The Fire Triangle and Fire Tetrahedron (Operational Concept)

The **Fire Triangle** is the foundational model for understanding combustion. Removing any one element extinguishes the fire.

**Modern Fire Tetrahedron** adds a fourth element — the **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** — to account for chemical flame suppression mechanisms (e.g., Halon, dry chemical powders).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every fire prevention strategy in a process plant targets at least one leg of the fire tetrahedron. Foam systems remove fuel, inert gas blanketing removes oxygen, explosion-proof enclosures eliminate ignition, and chemical suppression breaks the chain reaction.

## 2.3 Flammability Characteristics — Detailed Analysis

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Measurement Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flash Point | FP | Lowest temperature at which vapors ignite momentarily | °C | Storage temperature design |
| Fire Point | — | Temperature at which sustained combustion occurs | °C | Fire protection system trigger |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | AIT | Spontaneous ignition without external source | °C | Equipment surface temperature limits |
| Lower Flammable Limit | LFL | Minimum vapor concentration for combustion | % v/v | Ventilation design, LEL meters |
| Upper Flammable Limit | UFL | Maximum vapor concentration for combustion | % v/v | Inertion system design |
| Minimum Ignition Energy | MIE | Lowest spark energy to ignite the mixture | mJ | Electrostatic hazard assessment |
| Minimum Oxygen Concentration | MOC | O₂ level below which combustion cannot occur | % v/v | Blanketing/purging calculations |
| Vapor Density | — | Ratio of vapor density to air | dimensionless | Ventilation layout decisions |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FP \leq T_{storage} \leq AIT - 10°C$ | Safe liquid storage rule | °C | T storage must be below AIT |
| 2 | $\text{Flammable Range} = UFL - LFL$ | Breadth of flammable envelope | % v/v | LFL < Stoichiometric < UFL |
| 3 | $\text{Oxygen Limit} = \frac{LFL \times 21}{C_{stoich}}$ | Stoichiometric O₂ requirement | % v/v | Used in inertion |
| 4 | $E_{arc} = 4.184 \times C_f \times V \times I \times t$ | Arc flash incident energy (empirical) | cal/cm² | IEEE 1584 model |
| 5 | $P_{relief} = \frac{W \cdot R \cdot T}{V \cdot MW}$ | Pressure rise rate (ideal gas) | Pa/s | Isothermal assumption |
| 6 | $TNT_{eq} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | Explosion yield in TNT equivalent | kg | η = yield factor (1–10%) |
| 7 | $P_{max} = P_{initial} \times \left(\frac{T_{final}}{T_{initial}}\right)$ | Constant volume explosion pressure | kPa | Isochoric process |
| 8 | $L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | Sound pressure level | dB | P₀ = 20 µPa (reference) |
| 9 | $t_{safe} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$ | OSHA permissible noise exposure | hours | L > 85 dB(A) |
| 10 | $V_{critical} = \frac{Q}{A \cdot \rho_{vapor}}$ | Required ventilation flow | m³/s | For vapor dilution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The KTU university exam frequently tests the **Fire Triangle elimination strategies** and **Flammability parameter interpretation**. A single parameter misread (e.g., LFL vs UFL) costs full marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of physical hazard analysis are applied in:
- **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) Studies** — Identifying deviation causes
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** — Mechanical/electrical failure modes
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** — Calculating individual and societal risk
- **PSM (Process Safety Management)** — OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 compliance
- **EHS Audits** — Verifying compliance with IS 14489, NFPA standards

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Oxygen Concentration (MOC) for Inertion

The **Minimum Oxygen Concentration (MOC)** is the oxygen level below which a flammable mixture cannot be ignited, even if fuel and ignition energy are present. This is critical for designing **inert blanketing systems** in storage tanks.

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Combustion follows the stoichiometric equation: $\text{Fuel} + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O$
- LFL of the fuel is known
- The mixture behaves ideally (valid for low-pressure process tanks)

**Step 1: Write the general stoichiometric combustion**

$$ \text{Fuel} + n \cdot O_2 \rightarrow \text{Products} $$

where $n$ is the number of moles of $O_2$ required per mole of fuel.

**Step 2: Express the LFL stoichiometric relationship**

At the lower flammable limit, the fuel-to-air ratio equals the LFL. In air, oxygen is approximately **21% by volume**.

$$ \text{LFL}_{\text{air}} = \frac{n}{n + 3.76n + 1} \times 100 = \frac{n \times 100}{4.76n + 1} $$

This simplifies (for $n \gg 1$):

$$ \text{LFL}_{\text{air}} \approx \frac{100}{4.76} = 21\% \text{ (for oxygen)} $$

**Step 3: Reformulate for an oxygen-limited atmosphere**

When nitrogen is added (inertion), oxygen concentration decreases. The LFL expressed in terms of oxygen partial pressure is:

$$ \text{LFL}_{O_2} = \text{LFL}_{\text{air}} \times 21\% $$

**Step 4: Define the safety margin**

The MOC is computed as the oxygen concentration at which a stoichiometric mixture can no longer support combustion. The empirical formula used in industry (Le Chatelier's Rule) is:

$$ \text{MOC} = \text{LFL} \times \frac{21}{C_{stoich}} $$

**Step 5: Numerical example — Methane (CH₄)**

For methane:
- LFL = **5% v/v**
- Stoichiometric concentration: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2O$, so stoichiometric O₂ ≈ **10% v/v** in mixture

$$ \text{MOC}_{CH_4} = 5 \times \frac{21}{10} = 10.5\% $$

Thus, if oxygen in a methane storage tank is reduced below **10.5% v/v**, the mixture becomes non-flammable regardless of methane concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Industry standard for MOC is **8% to 10% O₂** for most hydrocarbon vapors. The **NFPA 69** standard mandates design oxygen concentrations at least **2% below the MOC** as a safety margin.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fire Triangle Energy Balance

The combustion process releases energy that must be dissipated. The **Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c)** is the primary energy source of fire hazards.

**Step 1: Standard enthalpy of combustion**

For a generic hydrocarbon $C_x H_y$:

$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \Delta H_c $$

**Step 2: Heat release rate**

$$ \dot{Q} = \dot{m} \times \Delta H_c $$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass burning rate (kg/s).

**Step 3: Pool fire heat flux (Thomas/Heskestad models)**

$$ \dot{Q}_{pool} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_{pool} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta_{rad} $$

where $\dot{m}''$ is the mass loss flux per unit area, $A_{pool}$ is the pool area, and $\eta_{rad}$ is the radiative fraction.

**Step 4: Numerical example — Hexane pool fire**

Given: Pool diameter = 5 m, $\dot{m}''$ = 0.08 kg/m²/s, $\Delta H_c$ = 45,000 kJ/kg, $\eta_{rad}$ = 0.20

$$ A_{pool} = \pi \times (2.5)^2 = 19.63 \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ \dot{Q}_{pool} = 0.08 \times 19.63 \times 45000 \times 0.20 = 14,134 \text{ kW} \approx 14.1 \text{ MW} $$

This is the heat release rate, enough to ignite surrounding equipment within seconds.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Explosion Pressure Rise (Constant Volume)

For a confined explosion, the pressure rise follows the ideal gas law under adiabatic conditions.

**Step 1: Initial state**

$$ P_1 V = n R T_1 $$

**Step 2: Final state after combustion**

$$ P_2 V = n R T_2 $$

**Step 3: Divide the two equations**

$$ \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} $$

**Step 4: Express $T_2$ using energy balance**

$$ T_2 = T_1 + \frac{\Delta H_c \cdot Y_f}{C_p \cdot (1-Y_f)} $$

where $Y_f$ is the fuel mass fraction and $C_p$ is the specific heat capacity of products.

**Step 5: Numerical example — Methane-air explosion in a closed vessel**

Given: $P_1$ = 101.325 kPa, $T_1$ = 298 K, $\Delta H_c$ = 55,500 kJ/kg, $Y_f$ = 0.055 (stoichiometric), $C_p$ = 1.7 kJ/kg·K

$$ T_2 = 298 + \frac{55500 \times 0.055}{1.7 \times (1-0.055)} = 298 + 1986 = 2284 \text{ K} $$

$$ P_2 = 101.325 \times \frac{2284}{298} = 776.6 \text{ kPa} \approx 7.66 \text{ atm} $$

This is the **fundamental reason** why confined vapor cloud explosions are devastating — pressures can rise by a factor of 7 to 10 in milliseconds.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hazard Type vs. Control Strategy

| Hazard Type | Primary Energy Form | Elimination Strategy 1 | Elimination Strategy 2 | Control Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fire | Chemical (combustion) | Remove fuel | Remove O₂ (inertion) | Foam, sprinklers, ESD |
| Explosion | Pressure wave | Vapor recovery | Inertion | Blast walls, relief panels |
| Pressure rupture | Mechanical (P·V energy) | PSV sizing | Rupture disc | Block valves, flare |
| Arc flash | Electrical | De-energize | Arc-resistant MCC | PPE, distance, training |
| Static discharge | Electrical (low energy) | Grounding/bonding | Humidity control | Conductive flooring |
| BLEVE | Thermal + pressure | Water cooling sprays | Limit filling | Mounded vessels, dykes |
| Noise | Acoustic | Enclose source | Damping materials | Ear protection, isolation |
| Heat stress | Thermal | Ventilation | Cooling systems | PPE, work-rest cycles |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fire Tetrahedron — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fuel Source] --> D[Fire Tetrahedron Core]
    B[Oxygen Source] --> D
    C[Ignition Energy] --> D
    E[Uninhibited Chain Reaction] --> D
    D --> F[Fire Incident]
    F --> G[Heat Damage]
    F --> H[Smoke Inhalation]
    F --> I[Property Loss]
    F --> J[Environmental Release]
    D --> K[Prevention Strategy 1: Remove Fuel]
    D --> L[Prevention Strategy 2: Eliminate O2]
    D --> M[Prevention Strategy 3: Control Ignition]
    D --> N[Prevention Strategy 4: Chemical Suppression]
```

## 4.2 Process Safety Barrier Model (Swiss Cheese Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hazard Source: Hydrocarbon Leak] --> B1[Inherent Safe Design Layer 1]
    B1 --> B2[Engineering Control Layer 2]
    B2 --> B3[Procedural Control Layer 3]
    B3 --> B4[Active Protection Layer 4]
    B4 --> B5[Mitigation Layer 5]
    B5 --> C{Incident Occurred?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Fire/Explosion Event]
    C -->|No| E[Safe Operation]
    B1 -.-> F1[Process Design Pressure]
    B2 -.-> F2[PSV and Alarms]
    B3 -.-> F3[SOPs and Permits]
    B4 -.-> F4[Fire Water System]
    B5 -.-> F5[Emergency Response]
```

## 4.3 Flammability Parameter Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Liquid Hydrocarbon] --> Q1{Is temperature > Flash Point?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is concentration within LFL to UFL?}
    Q1 -->|No| Safe[Safe Storage Condition]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is O2 above MOC?}
    Q2 -->|No| Safe2[Too Rich or Too Lean]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Is ignition energy > MIE?}
    Q3 -->|No| Safe3[Inerted Environment]
    Q4 -->|Yes| FireHazard[FIRE / EXPLOSION HAZARD]
    Q4 -->|No| Safe4[No Ignition Source]
    FireHazard --> Mitigation[Apply Active Protection]
    Mitigation --> Foam[Foam System]
    Mitigation --> CO2[CO2 Flooding]
    Mitigation --> DryChem[Dry Chemical]
    Mitigation --> Water[Water Spray Deluge]
```

## 4.4 Pressure Hazard Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Process Vessel] --> B[Pressure Transmitter]
    A --> C[Level Transmitter]
    A --> D[Temperature Transmitter]
    B --> E[SIS Safety Instrumented System]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{SIL Level Trip Active?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Emergency Shutdown ESD]
    F -->|No| H[Normal Operation]
    A --> I[Pressure Safety Valve PSV]
    I --> J[Flare Header]
    I --> K[Atmospheric Vent]
    G --> L[Block Valves Close]
    L --> M[Safe State Achieved]
    H --> N[Continue Production]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Physical Hazards with examples. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A **Physical Hazard** is an environmental or energy-based condition in a process plant that can cause injury, property damage, or loss without involving a chemical toxic reaction. They arise from uncontrolled energy transfer.

**Three examples:**
1. **Fire hazard** — Uncontrolled combustion of hydrocarbons in a refinery.
2. **Pressure hazard** — Overpressure rupture of an LPG storage sphere.
3. **Electrical hazard** — Arc flash during maintenance of a motor control center.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse physical hazards with **chemical hazards** (toxicity, reactivity). Always clarify that physical hazards involve **energy transfer**, not chemical poisoning.

---

### Question 2: Explain the Fire Triangle with elimination strategies. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **Fire Triangle** consists of three essential elements for combustion:
- **Fuel** (vapor, liquid, or solid combustible)
- **Oxygen** (typically from air, ~21% v/v)
- **Ignition Source** (spark, heat, static electricity)

The modern **Fire Tetrahedron** adds a fourth element — the **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** — which explains chemical flame suppression.

**Elimination Strategies:**
1. **Remove Fuel** — Drain pools, shut off gas valves, vapor recovery
2. **Remove Oxygen** — Nitrogen inertion, foam blanketing, CO₂ flooding
3. **Eliminate Ignition** — Explosion-proof fittings, bonding/grounding, hot work permits
4. **Break Chain Reaction** — Dry chemical powder, Halon replacements (FM-200)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The fire triangle is the conceptual basis for every fire protection system in a process plant. Always link your answer to a **real engineering control** (e.g., fire water network, foam pourers).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Detailed Analysis of Flammability Parameters

**(a)** Define the following flammability parameters: Flash Point, Fire Point, Auto-Ignition Temperature, LFL, UFL, MIE, and MOC. Explain the engineering significance of each in process safety. **(7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(b)** A process vessel contains a hexane-air mixture. The LFL of hexane is 1.1% v/v and the UFL is 7.5% v/v. The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio requires 10% O₂ in the mixture. Calculate the Minimum Oxygen Concentration (MOC) and recommend an inertion strategy. **(7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Flash Point** | Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off vapor in sufficient quantity to form an ignitable mixture with air near the surface | Determines safe storage temperature and handling protocols |
| **Fire Point** | Temperature at which the vapor continues to burn for at least 5 seconds after ignition | Defines the boundary of sustained combustion for fire protection |
| **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** | Minimum temperature at which a substance spontaneously ignites without an external source | Limits maximum equipment surface temperature (T-class equipment) |
| **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** | Minimum concentration of vapor in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur | LEL meter alarm settings; ventilation design |
| **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL)** | Maximum concentration of vapor in air above which propagation does not occur | Critical for transfer line purging; rich-side safety |
| **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** | Smallest electrical spark energy capable of igniting the mixture at optimal concentration | Determines electrostatic hazard severity; container material selection |
| **Minimum Oxygen Concentration (MOC)** | O₂ concentration below which combustion cannot occur regardless of fuel concentration | Basis for nitrogen inertion systems in storage tanks |

**Engineering Significance Summary:**
- These parameters collectively define the **flammability envelope** of a substance.
- They guide the selection of **equipment temperature class (T1–T6)**, **electrical area classification (Zone 0/1/2)**, and **fire suppression medium**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- LFL (hexane) = 1.1% v/v
- UFL (hexane) = 7.5% v/v
- Stoichiometric O₂ concentration in mixture = 10% v/v

**Step 1: Apply MOC formula**

The MOC is calculated using Le Chatelier's empirical relation:

$$ \text{MOC} = \text{LFL} \times \frac{\text{O}_2 \text{ in air (\%)}}{\text{Stoichiometric O}_2 \text{ required (\%)}} $$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$ \text{MOC} = 1.1 \times \frac{21}{10} = 2.31\% \text{ v/v} $$

**Step 3: Verify with safety margin**

Apply **NFPA 69** safety factor — design O₂ concentration must be at least **2% below MOC**:

$$ \text{Design O}_2 = 2.31 - 2.0 = 0.31\% \text{ v/v} $$

However, in practice, the lower limit of measurement reliability for oxygen analyzers is ~1% v/v, so a more practical design value is **5% v/v O₂**.

**Step 4: Recommended Inertion Strategy**

1. **Primary:** Nitrogen blanketing with continuous O₂ analyzer (set alarm at 5% v/v, trip at 6% v/v).
2. **Secondary:** Pressure swing adsorption (PSA) for high-purity N₂ supply.
3. **Verification:** Perform monthly LEL measurements and verify O₂ at multiple sample points.
4. **Documentation:** Maintain inertion logbook as per OSHA PSM 1910.119.

**Final Answer:** MOC for hexane = **2.31% v/v**; design O₂ target = **5% v/v** with NFPA 69 margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply the **2% safety margin** from NFPA 69. Always state the design O₂ as MOC minus safety margin. Also, distinguish between the **theoretical MOC** and the **operational target**.

---

### Question B: Explosion Hazards and BLEVE Analysis

**(a)** Differentiate between Deflagration and Detonation. Explain BLEVE with a real-world case study. Describe the mechanism of dust explosions and the conditions required. **(7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(b)** A 50 m³ storage sphere contains propane at 8 bar gauge and 25°C. Due to external fire impingement, the vessel wall temperature rises to 600°C. Calculate the new pressure using the ideal gas law, and comment on the structural integrity if the design pressure is 12 bar gauge. **(7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparison: Deflagration vs Detonation**

| Feature | Deflagration | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Flame speed | Subsonic (< 340 m/s) | Supersonic (1800–9000 m/s) |
| Pressure rise | 7–8 bar | 15–20 bar |
| Mechanism | Heat transfer | Shock wave compression |
| Damage pattern | Pressure pulse | Shattering effect |
| Examples | Vapor cloud fire | Gas-phase detonation, dust detonation |

**BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion):**

A BLEVE occurs when a pressure vessel containing a **superheated liquid** fails catastrophically. The mechanism:
1. External fire heats the vapor space of a partially filled vessel.
2. The metal wall weakens (creep failure) above design temperature.
3. The vessel ruptures, and the superheated liquid **flashes instantly** to vapor.
4. A massive fireball forms, projecting fragments at high velocity.

**Real-World Case Study — San Juanico, Mexico (1984):**
A BLEVE of an LPG sphere at a Pemex facility killed **500+ people** and injured 7000. The vessel failed due to fire impingement, leading to a massive unconfined vapor cloud explosion.

**Dust Explosion Mechanism — Pentagon Model:**
A dust explosion requires:
1. **Combustible dust** (particle size < 500 µm)
2. **Confinement** (vessel, duct, building)
3. **Dispersion** in air
4. **Oxidizer** (O₂ > MOC)
5. **Ignition source** (spark, hot surface)

Famous example: **2008 Georgia Imperial Sugar Refinery dust explosion** — 14 fatalities.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Vessel volume, $V$ = 50 m³
- Initial pressure, $P_1$ = 8 bar gauge = **9.01325 bar absolute** (≈ 901.325 kPa)
- Initial temperature, $T_1$ = 25°C = **298.15 K**
- Final temperature, $T_2$ = 600°C = **873.15 K**
- Design pressure = 12 bar gauge = **13.01325 bar absolute**

**Step 1: Apply ideal gas law (constant volume process)**

$$ \frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2} $$

**Step 2: Solve for $P_2$**

$$ P_2 = P_1 \times \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 9.01325 \times \frac{873.15}{298.15} $$

**Step 3: Numerical evaluation**

$$ P_2 = 9.01325 \times 2.9285 = 26.39 \text{ bar absolute} $$

**Step 4: Convert to gauge pressure**

$$ P_{2, gauge} = 26.39 - 1.01325 = 25.38 \text{ bar gauge} $$

**Step 5: Compare with design pressure**

$$ \frac{P_2}{P_{design}} = \frac{25.38}{12.0} = 2.115 $$

The vessel pressure **exceeds the design pressure by 2.115 times**. This is a **catastrophic overpressure condition** that will cause immediate vessel rupture — i.e., a **BLEVE** is imminent.

**Step 6: Engineering Recommendations**

1. **Immediate action:** Evacuate the area; activate emergency water spray deluge.
2. **Long-term fix:** Install a **rupture disc** sized for fire case scenarios (API 521).
3. **Relief device:** PSV set at 12 bar gauge with sufficient capacity for fire exposure.
4. **Fire case sizing:** API 521 mandates wetted area calculation for fire relief.

**Final Answer:** $P_2$ = **26.39 bar absolute** (25.38 bar gauge), which is **2.1× the design pressure**, confirming vessel rupture and BLEVE risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. **Gauge vs Absolute pressure** — Always convert to absolute before applying the gas law. Mixing units is the #1 cause of full mark deductions.
> 2. **Constant volume assumption** — State this assumption explicitly; otherwise marks are cut.
> 3. **Compare with design pressure** in the same unit system; do not directly mix gauge and absolute.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Physical Hazards**

- **Fire Triangle:** Fuel + Oxygen + Ignition → **Fire Tetrahedron** adds Chain Reaction
- **Class B fires** (flammable liquids) — use foam or CO₂, never direct water jet
- **Flash Point < 25°C** = Highly flammable liquid (Class IA per NFPA)
- **LFL of Methane = 5%, Hexane = 1.1%, Propane = 2.1%, Gasoline = 1.4%**
- **LFL + UFL** = Flammable Range; outside this range, no ignition
- **MOC (Methane) ≈ 10.5%, Hexane ≈ 2.3%, Propane ≈ 11%**
- **MIE of hydrogen = 0.02 mJ** (extremely easy to ignite) — highest electrostatic hazard
- **Deflagration: P rise = 7–8 bar; Detonation: P rise = 15–20 bar**
- **BLEVE prevention:** Water spray cooling of vessel, limit liquid filling < 80%
- **API 521** mandates PSV sizing for **fire case** scenarios
- **NFPA 69** requires design O₂ < MOC - 2% safety margin
- **T-class equipment** (T1–T6): surface temp < AIT
- **Zone 0**: continuous hazard; **Zone 1**: occasional; **Zone 2**: rare
- **Permissible Noise Exposure:** 8 hrs at 85 dB(A); halves every 3 dB increase
- **Always convert gauge → absolute** before ideal gas law calculations
- **Fire water demand:** Q = 0.002 × Total Wetted Area (m³/hr) per API 521
- **OSHA PSM 1910.119** mandates process hazard analysis for highly hazardous chemicals
- **TNT equivalent** for vapor cloud explosions: $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot H_c / E_{TNT}$
- **Key Indian Standards:** IS 14489 (HAZOP), IS 15092 (PSV), IS 15138 (Risk Assessment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **Definition → Diagram → Numerical Solution → Engineering Recommendation**. KTU examiners award 2–3 marks for the final recommendation/discussion section. Never skip the conclusion paragraph.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Hazard (KTU Definition):** An electrical hazard is any unsafe condition involving electrical energy that has the potential to cause harm to personnel, damage to equipment, fire, or explosion. In process industries, electrical hazards are categorized as primary ignition sources under API RP 2210, NFPA 77, and IEC 60079, and they encompass **electrical shock, arc flash, arc blast, static electricity accumulation, lightning strikes, and stray/ground currents**.

In the context of **Process Safety Engineering (OEBBT723)**, electrical hazards are studied because they are leading causes of:
- **Fires** (40% of industrial fires have an electrical origin as per NFPA data)
- **Explosions** (when ignited in flammable atmospheres — Zones 0, 1, 2 for gases; Zones 20, 21, 22 for dusts)
- **Personnel injury** (electrocution, burns from arc flash, secondary falls)
- **Equipment damage and process downtime**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tired Water Hose"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of electricity like water flowing through a hose. **Voltage** is the water pressure, **Current (Amperage)** is the actual flow rate, and **Resistance** is the narrowness of the hose. A high-pressure hose with the nozzle shut (high voltage, low current) is annoying but not deadly. Open the nozzle (allow current to flow through your body) and a small amount of high-pressure water can still knock you down. Now imagine a burst hose spraying in all directions (an **arc flash** or **explosion**) — that is the destructive, multi-hazard event. Your body is the hose that wasn't designed to carry this flow.

This analogy is useful because in process safety, we always think in terms of **current through the human body** (mA range is dangerous; 100 mA through the heart is typically fatal within 1 second), not voltage alone.

---

## 1.3 Categories of Electrical Hazards (KTU Board Classification)

| S.No | Hazard Category | Primary Concern | Governing Standard |
|:----:|:----------------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **Static Electricity** | Sudden discharge igniting flammables | NFPA 77, IEC 60079-32-1 |
| 2 | **Lightning** | Direct strike & induced surges | IEC 62305, NFPA 780 |
| 3 | **Stray Currents** | Corrosion, sparking, heating | API RP 651, NACE standards |
| 4 | **Electrical Shock** | Current through human body | IEEE C2, NFPA 70E, IEC 60479 |
| 5 | **Arc Flash & Arc Blast** | Thermal & pressure energy release | NFPA 70E, IEEE 1584 |
| 6 | **Short Circuits / Overloads** | Fire, equipment failure | NEC (NFPA 70), IEC 60364 |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Human body resistance (dry skin):** $\mathbf{100{,}000\ \Omega}$ (≈ 100 kΩ)
- **Human body resistance (wet/broken skin):** $\mathbf{1{,}000\ \Omega}$ (≈ 1 kΩ)
- **Let-go threshold current (men):** $\mathbf{9\ mA}$ AC / $\mathbf{20\ mA}$ DC
- **Ventricular fibrillation threshold:** $\mathbf{100\ mA}$ for > 0.2 s
- **Minimum ignition energy (typical hydrocarbon):** $\mathbf{0.25\ mJ}$
- **Static charge capacitance of human body:** $\mathbf{100\ to\ 300\ pF}$
- **Standard body weight for arc blast calculations:** $\mathbf{70\ kg}$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote both the **voltage limit (50 V AC / 120 V DC – IEC 60950)** and the **current threshold (5 mA nominal let-go)**. Examiners love numbers!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sub-Topic A — Static Electricity

### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Charge Generation

Static electricity is generated when two materials come into contact and then separate. The process involves:

1. **Contact** — Electrons transfer from one material to the other (becoming positively/negatively charged based on the **triboelectric series**).
2. **Separation** — If the separation is rapid, charges do not have time to recombine → accumulated charge.
3. **Accumulation** — Charge builds up on insulated conductors (e.g., a worker on a rubber-soled shoe, a fluid flowing in a non-conductive pipe).
4. **Discharge** — When the electric field exceeds the dielectric strength of the surrounding medium, a spark occurs.

### 2.1.2 Why This Matters in Process Industries

In petroleum, pharmaceutical, and chemical plants, common static-generating operations include:
- Fluid flow through pipes, filters, or fine orifices
- Powder handling, sieving, pneumatic transfer
- Steam, mist, or spray operations
- Movement of non-conductive sheets (plastic liners)
- Worker walking on insulating floors

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Point:** A static discharge of just **0.25 mJ** can ignite many hydrocarbon vapors. Yet a human body accumulating charge can store **up to 20 mJ** — about **80 times** the minimum ignition energy of gasoline vapor!

---

## 2.2 Sub-Topic B — Electrical Shock

### 2.2.1 Effect of Current on the Human Body (IEC 60479)

| Current Range (AC 50/60 Hz) | Physiological Effect |
|:-----------------------------|:---------------------|
| 0.5 – 5 mA | Threshold of perception, mild tingle |
| 5 – 15 mA | Muscle control loss ("let-go" threshold ~9 mA) |
| 15 – 50 mA | Painful shock, muscle contraction, breathing difficulty |
| 50 – 100 mA | Severe pain, possible ventricular fibrillation onset |
| 100 mA – 5 A | **Ventricular fibrillation**, death within seconds |
| > 5 A | Severe burns, cardiac arrest, tissue destruction |

### 2.2.2 Key Variables in Shock Severity

The current through the body is governed by **Ohm's Law** applied to the human circuit:

$$
I_{body} = \frac{V_{source}}{R_{body} + R_{contact}}
$$

where:
- $V_{source}$ = Touch voltage (V)
- $R_{body}$ = Internal body resistance (≈ 300–1000 Ω from hand to foot)
- $R_{contact}$ = Skin/contact resistance (varies from 1000 Ω wet to 100,000 Ω dry)

---

## 2.3 Sub-Topic C — Arc Flash and Arc Blast

### 2.3.1 What is an Arc Flash?

An **arc flash** is a sustained electric arc between conductors or between a conductor and ground. It produces:
- **Intense heat** (up to **35,000 °F / 19,400 °C** — about 4× the temperature of the sun's surface)
- **Intense light** (ultraviolet & infrared causing permanent eye damage)
- **Pressure wave (arc blast)** — copper expands 67,000× in volume when vaporized
- **Toxic plasma gases** (copper vapor, decomposed air → NOx, ozone)

### 2.3.2 Arc Flash Energy (IEEE 1584 Empirical Model)

$$
E = 10^{K_1 + K_2 + 1.081 \log_{10}(I_a) + 0.0011\, G}
$$

where:
- $E$ = Incident energy (cal/cm²)
- $I_a$ = Arcing current (kA)
- $G$ = Gap between conductors (mm)
- $K_1, K_2$ = Empirical constants for equipment type

The **arc flash boundary** is the distance at which the incident energy equals **1.2 cal/cm²** (the threshold for a curable burn).

---

## 2.4 Sub-Topic D — Lightning

Lightning is a high-current electrostatic discharge between a charged cloud and ground (or another cloud). Key parameters:

- Peak current: $\mathbf{30{,}000\ A}$ (typical), up to $\mathbf{200{,}000\ A}$
- Voltage: $\mathbf{10^8\ to\ 10^9\ V}$
- Duration: $\mathbf{100\ to\ 300\ \mu s}$
- Energy dissipated: $\mathbf{250\ kWh}$ per strike

Lightning can ignite storage tanks, vapor clouds, and damage control systems. The **cone of protection** of a Franklin rod is determined by a **45° protective angle**.

---

## 2.5 Sub-Topic E — Stray Currents

Stray currents are unintended electrical currents flowing through paths other than the intended circuit. Sources include:
- DC traction systems (railways, cathodic protection)
- Fault currents seeking a return path
- Telluric currents (Earth's magnetic field-induced)

In refineries and tank farms, stray currents cause:
- **Stray current corrosion** of buried tanks
- **Sparking** at bonds, gaskets, or supports
- **Hazardous area ignition**

---

## 2.6 Sub-Topic F — Hazardous Area Classification & Equipment Protection

| Zone | Gas/Dust Type | Equipment Protection Level |
|:----:|:--------------|:---------------------------|
| Zone 0 / 20 | Continuous hazard | **Ex ia** (intrinsically safe) |
| Zone 1 / 21 | Likely hazard | Ex d (flameproof), Ex e, Ex p |
| Zone 2 / 22 | Occasional hazard | Ex n, non-sparking acceptable |

The **Maximum Experimental Safe Gap (MESG)** and **Minimum Igniting Current (MIC)** ratio determine grouping (IIA, IIB, IIC).

---

## 2.7 🔥 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:------|:------|
| 1 | Ohm's Law (Body) | $I = V / R$ | A, V, Ω | Use total body+contact resistance |
| 2 | Static charge stored | $Q = C \cdot V$ | C, F, V | C = body capacitance (~200 pF) |
| 3 | Discharge energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J, F, V | The critical ignition energy parameter |
| 4 | Body current (touch) | $I_{b} = V_{touch} / (R_b + R_c)$ | A | Skin resistance varies by sweat/wet |
| 5 | Step potential current | $I_s = V_{step} / R_{body \to feet}$ | A | Earth gradient hazard |
| 6 | Arc flash energy | $E = 4.184 \cdot C_f \cdot E_n \cdot (t/0.2) \cdot (610^x / D^x)$ | cal/cm² | IEEE 1584 simplified form |
| 7 | Heat flux at distance D | $q = E / 4\pi D^2$ | W/m² | Inverse-square law |
| 8 | Pressure wave (arc blast) | $P = \rho\, c\, v$ | Pa | Acoustic velocity model |
| 9 | Resistive heating (P) | $P = I^2 R = V^2/R$ | W | Cause of ignition in resistors |
| 10 | Capacitor discharge current | $i(t) = (V_0/R)\, e^{-t/RC}$ | A | Exponential decay |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize formulas #1, #2, #3, and #4 — these are the most frequently asked in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions on Electrical Hazards.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical plants:** Bonding & grounding of transfer lines, tank trucks, railcars to prevent static ignition during loading.
- **Pharmaceutical plants:** Controlling dust clouds (MIE ~ 3–10 mJ for many organic powders).
- **Offshore platforms:** Lightning protection combined with cathodic protection and explosion-proof enclosures.
- **Battery manufacturing:** Static-controlled workstations to prevent Li-ion cell fires.
- **Food processing:** Avoiding dust explosions via IP-rated (Ingress Protection) enclosures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Static Discharge Energy from a Worker

**Problem Statement:** A worker wearing rubber-soled shoes walks across an insulating floor and accumulates a charge of **4 μC** at a body capacitance of **200 pF**. Determine:
(a) The potential of the worker relative to ground.
(b) The energy stored in the body.
