> **KTU 2024 Definition (IEC 60079-10-1 / IS 5571):** An area is classified as hazardous if the concentration of flammable substances lies within its **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** and **Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)** when mixed with air, AND an ignition source of sufficient energy is present.

The primary international standards governing this topic are:
- **IEC 60079-10-1** (Gases and Vapors)
- **IEC 60079-10-2** (Combustible Dusts)
- **API RP 500 / 505** (North American practice)
- **IS 5571** (Indian Standard)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Gas Stove"

Imagine a domestic kitchen with a working LPG stove:

| Region around the stove | Practical Hazard Level | HAC Equivalent Zone |
|---|---|---|
| Open flame & burner head | Always ignitable | **Zone 0** (Continuous) |
| Air just above the pan | Often flammable | **Zone 1** (Primary) |
| Rest of the kitchen room | Occasionally smells of gas | **Zone 2** (Secondary) |
| Bedroom (next room) | Practically no gas | **Non-Hazardous (Safe)** |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as you wouldn't place a sparking electric switch directly above your stove, the HAC framework prevents installing unprotected electrical equipment (motors, junction boxes, lights) in dangerous zones — preventing catastrophic explosions in refineries, paint shops, and chemical plants.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit):** minimum concentration of vapor/gas in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur (typically 1–5% by volume for common hydrocarbons).
- **UEL (Upper Explosive Limit):** maximum concentration above which flame propagation does not occur.
- **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE):** **0.2 mJ** (hydrogen, the most easily ignitable gas).
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** lowest temperature at which a substance ignites spontaneously without an external spark.
- **Flash Point:** lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with air.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Classify Areas? — The Core "Why"

The fundamental objective of HAC is to **bridge the gap between the probability of an explosive atmosphere forming and the probability of an ignition source being present**. KTU examiners frequently frame this as a question on "Hazard vs. Risk Reduction."

The logic chain (Why → How → What):

1. **Why?** Flammable substances + Air + Ignition Source = **Explosion** (the classic "Fire Triangle" extended to a "Explosion Pentagon" when dispersion and confinement are added).
2. **How?** Either remove one leg of the pentagon or isolate it (substitution, ventilation, inerting) — OR, if hazard is unavoidable, control ignition sources.
3. **What?** Select **explosion-proof / intrinsically safe / increased-safety** electrical equipment certified for that specific zone.

### 2.2 Zone Classification — IEC System (Most Important for KTU)

**For Gases, Vapors, and Mists (IEC 60079-10-1):**

| Zone | Definition | Equipment Protection Level (EPL) | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | Explosive atmosphere present **continuously** or for long periods (>1000 hrs/yr) | **Ga** (very high protection) | Inside a closed solvent storage tank, vapor space of a closed process vessel |
| **Zone 1** | Explosive atmosphere likely to occur **occasionally** in normal operation (10–1000 hrs/yr) | **Gb** (high protection) | Immediate vicinity of pump seals, flanges, sampling points, fill/discharge openings |
| **Zone 2** | Explosive atmosphere **unlikely** in normal operation, and if it occurs, only **briefly** (<10 hrs/yr) | **Gc** (normal protection) | Area within 1 m of Zone 1 boundary, well-ventilated process areas |

**For Combustible Dusts (IEC 60079-10-2):**

| Zone | Definition | Equipment Protection Level (EPL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 20** | Combustible dust cloud present **continuously or frequently** | **Da** |
| **Zone 21** | Combustible dust cloud likely to occur **occasionally** in normal operation | **Db** |
| **Zone 22** | Combustible dust cloud unlikely to occur, and only **briefly** | **Dc** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The **second digit** of the zone (0, 1, 2 / 20, 21, 22) indicates the **hazard severity**:
> - 0 → Continuous (worst)
> - 1 → Intermittent (medium)
> - 2 → Worst-case only (least)

### 2.3 Material Groups — Energy of Ignition

| Gas Group | Typical Substance | MIE (mJ) | MESG (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IIA** | Propane, Methanol, Acetone | > 0.18 | > 0.90 |
| **IIB** | Ethylene, Coke oven gas | ≤ 0.18 | > 0.50 to ≤ 0.90 |
| **IIC** | Hydrogen, Acetylene, Carbon Disulfide | ≤ 0.06 | ≤ 0.50 |

| Dust Group | Typical Substance |
|---|---|
| **IIIA** | Combustible flyings (e.g., cotton, fibers) |
| **IIIB** | Non-conductive dust (e.g., grain, wood, plastic) |
| **IIIC** | Conductive dust (e.g., aluminum, magnesium) |

### 2.4 Temperature Classification (T-Class)

The maximum surface temperature of equipment must remain **below** the Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) of the surrounding gas/dust.

| T-Class | Max Surface Temp (°C) | Safe with substances having AIT > (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| **T1** | 450 | 450 |
| **T2** | 300 | 300 |
| **T3** | 200 | 200 |
| **T4** | 135 | 135 |
| **T5** | 100 | 100 |
| **T6** | 85 | 85 |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Release Rate } (Q) = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{k \cdot \rho_{vapor} \cdot f}$ | Estimation of release rate from a leak | kg/s |
| $\text{Volumetric Flow of Vapor} = \dfrac{Q \cdot RT}{P \cdot M}$ | Convert mass release to volumetric flow | m³/s |
| $L_{EL} = \dfrac{100 \cdot Q}{LFL \cdot V_{air}}$ | Theoretical extent of LEL cloud from release | meters |
| $V_{dilution} = Q \cdot t_{dilution}$ | Volume of flammable cloud | m³ |
| $\text{Mass Evaporation Rate} = \dfrac{M \cdot A \cdot P_{sat}}{R \cdot T_{liq}}$ | Evaporation from a liquid pool (Langmuir-like) | kg/s |
| $P_{sat}(T) = \exp\left(A - \dfrac{B}{T+C}\right)$ | Antoine Equation for vapor pressure | Pa or mmHg |
| $\text{Distance to LEL} (d) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot Q \cdot LFL_{vol}}{\pi \cdot v \cdot C_{LEL}}}$ | Plume dispersion estimate (Gaussian model) | m |
| $\text{Frequency of Release} = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Reliability-based zone assignment | hr⁻¹ |
| $K = \dfrac{\text{Total Ventilation}}{\text{Release Volume per unit time}}$ | Ventilation efficiency index | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU problems, the **Antoine Equation** and the **plume dispersion distance** formulae appear most frequently in numerical questions. Always confirm which substance group the gas belongs to (IIA/IIB/IIC) before selecting equipment.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Application

In an actual **oil refinery**, classifying a Crude Distillation Unit involves:
1. Identifying all **flanges, valves, pumps, and sample points** (potential release sources).
2. Estimating release rate based on **pressure, hole size, fluid properties**.
3. Determining the **ventilation coefficient** (natural vs. forced).
4. Drawing **hazardous area maps** with zones extending in 3D (horizontal and vertical extents) — typically 0.5 m radius for Zone 1 around flanges, 1 m for Zone 2, and entire room envelope for Zone 2 in poorly ventilated pump rooms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure for Hazardous Area Classification (IEC 60079-10-1)

Below is the **complete 8-step analytical procedure** that the KTU examiner expects when a 14-mark question on "Explain the procedure of hazardous area classification" is posed.

---

#### **STEP 1 — Identify the Flammable Substance**
Determine the **physical state** (gas, liquid, vapor, mist, dust), **flash point**, **LEL**, **UEL**, **AIT**, and **vapor density (relative to air)**.

$$
\text{Decision Variable: } d_{vapor} = \dfrac{\rho_{vapor}}{\rho_{air}}
$$

$$
d_{vapor} > 1 \Rightarrow \text{Vapor is HEAVIER than air (e.g., LPG, propane) — settles at ground level}
$$

$$
d_{vapor} < 1 \Rightarrow \text{Vapor is LIGHTER than air (e.g., methane, hydrogen) — rises to ceiling}
$$

> *Explanation:* The vapor density ratio is critical because it dictates **whether the hazardous zone extends horizontally** (heavy vapor pooling) or **vertically toward the ceiling** (light vapor rising).

---

#### **STEP 2 — Identify the Source of Release**
A **source of release** is any point where a flammable substance can escape its containment (pipe, vessel, pump seal, flange, valve packing, sample point, drain, vent, or relief valve discharge).

Classify the source into one of three grades:

| Grade | Description | Zone Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Grade** | Release present continuously or for long periods | → **Zone 0 / Zone 20** |
| **Primary Grade** | Release likely to occur periodically during normal operation | → **Zone 1 / Zone 21** |
| **Secondary Grade** | Release not expected in normal operation; if it does, only briefly | → **Zone 2 / Zone 22** |

---

#### **STEP 3 — Estimate the Release Rate (Q)**

For a **gas release from a hole of area $A_h$:**

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot P \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma \cdot M}{R \cdot T} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
$$

where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typical 0.6–1.0, dimensionless)
- $A_h$ = hole area (m²)
- $P$ = upstream pressure (Pa)
- $\gamma$ = specific heat ratio ($C_p / C_v$)
- $M$ = molar mass (kg/kmol)
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

For a **liquid release** (mass rate of evaporation from a pool):

$$
\dot{m}_{evap} = \dfrac{M \cdot A_{pool} \cdot P_{sat}(T_{liq})}{R \cdot T_{liq}}
$$

where $P_{sat}(T)$ is given by the **Antoine equation**:

$$
\log_{10}(P_{sat}) = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}
$$

> *Explanation:* The release rate determines how rapidly a flammable cloud can form — a high rate ensures the cloud is sustained long enough to ignite.

---

#### **STEP 4 — Determine Ventilation (V)**

Ventilation is the most powerful mitigation variable.

$$
V_{air} = Q_{fan} \quad \text{(forced)} \quad \text{or} \quad V_{air} = A_{opening} \cdot v_{wind} \quad \text{(natural)}
$$

**Ventilation Coefficient (K) Index:**

$$
K = \dfrac{V_{air} \text{ (m}^3/\text{s)}}{Q \text{ (m}^3/\text{s)}}
$$

| K Value | Ventilation Effectiveness | Zone Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| $K \geq 1$ | **Good** — vapor diluted below LEL | Zone may be downgraded |
| $0.1 \leq K < 1$ | **Fair** — vapor may reach LEL occasionally | Zone sustained |
| $K < 0.1$ | **Poor / Negligible** — vapor accumulates | Zone upgrade or expansion |

---

#### **STEP 5 — Estimate the Hazardous Extent**

The **horizontal extent** of the explosive atmosphere (radius from source) is:

$$
r_{zone} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot v_{air} \cdot LFL_{vol}}}
$$

The **vertical extent** is:

$$
h_{zone} = \dfrac{r_{zone}}{2} \quad \text{(for heavy vapors pooling)}
$$

or

$$
h_{zone} = r_{zone} \quad \text{(for light vapors rising)}
$$

> *Explanation:* The zone is generally modeled as a **hemisphere** for ground-level sources and a **cylinder** for elevated sources.

---

#### **STEP 6 — Assign the Zone**

Combine Steps 2–5 into a final zone assignment:

$$
\text{Zone} = f(\text{Release Grade},\ Q,\ V)
$$

- Continuous Grade + Poor Ventilation → **Zone 0**
- Primary Grade + Fair Ventilation → **Zone 1**
- Secondary Grade + Good Ventilation → **Zone 2**

---

#### **STEP 7 — Select Equipment Based on Zone**

| Zone | Required EPL | Example Equipment Type |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | **Ga** | Intrinsically Safe (Ex ia) |
| 1 | **Gb** | Flameproof (Ex d), Increased Safety (Ex e), Purged (Ex p) |
| 2 | **Gc** | Non-sparking (Ex nA), Encapsulation (Ex mc) |
| 20 | **Da** | Dust-ignition-proof (Ex tD, Ex iaD) |
| 21 | **Db** | Dust-protected (Ex tD, Ex ibD) |
| 22 | **Dc** | Dust-protected enclosure (Ex tc) |

---

#### **STEP 8 — Document the Area Classification Plan**

- Plot **plan and elevation views** showing all zone boundaries.
- Mark the **T-class** required and **gas/dust group**.
- Cross-check with relevant **IEC 60079-14** for installation.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Sample KTU-style)

**Problem:** A flange in a propane line (LEL = 2.1% vol, AIT = 450°C) releases vapor at a rate of $0.0005\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. The area has forced ventilation supplying $0.05\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ of air. Classify the area and find the horizontal extent of the Zone 1 region. Assume release is primary grade and ambient wind velocity is $0.5\ \text{m/s}$.

**Solution:**

Step A — Compute ventilation coefficient:

$$
K = \dfrac{V_{air}}{Q} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.0005} = 100
$$

Since $K \geq 1$, ventilation is **good** (but does not eliminate hazard, only dilutes).

Step B — Calculate horizontal extent (assume dilution to 0.5 × LEL is safe boundary):

$$
r = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot v \cdot C_{LEL}}}
$$

$$
r = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 0.0005}{\pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot (0.5 \times 0.021)}}
$$

$$
r = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.001}{0.01649}} = \sqrt{0.0606} \approx 0.246\ \text{m}
$$

Step C — Apply safety factor of **2** (per IEC 60079-10):

$$
r_{final} = 2 \times 0.246 \approx 0.5\ \text{m}
$$

**Final Answer:** The Zone 1 radius is **0.5 m** around the flange; Zone 2 extends to **1.0 m**. Equipment with **T-class T1 (max 450°C)**, **Gas Group IIA**, and **EPL Gb** is required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Decision Flowchart — Hazardous Area Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process Plant Layout] --> B[Identify Flammable Substances]
    B --> C[Determine LEL, UEL, AIT, Flash Point]
    C --> D[Locate All Potential Release Sources]
    D --> E{Type of Release Source?}
    E -- Continuous --> F1[Assign Grade: Continuous]
    E -- Periodic --> F2[Assign Grade: Primary]
    E -- Rare Leak --> F3[Assign Grade: Secondary]
    F1 --> G[Estimate Release Rate Q]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Calculate Ventilation Coefficient K]
    H --> I{K Value?}
    I -- K is less than 0.1 --> J1[Poor Ventilation]
    I -- K between 0.1 and 1 --> J2[Fair Ventilation]
    I -- K greater than 1 --> J3[Good Ventilation]
    J1 --> K1[Expand Zone Extent]
    J2 --> K2[Maintain Standard Zone]
    J3 --> K3[Reduce or Eliminate Zone]
    K1 --> L[Assign Zone 0 or 1]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[Select Equipment EPL and T-Class]
    M --> N[Document Plan, Elevation, and Group Markings]
    N --> O[End: Approved Hazardous Area Plan]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Zone Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Source of Release] --> B1[Continuous Source]
    A1 --> B2[Primary Source]
    A1 --> B3[Secondary Source]
    B1 --> C1[Zone 0 or Zone 20]
    B2 --> C2[Zone 1 or Zone 21]
    B3 --> C3[Zone 2 or Zone 22]
    C1 --> D1[EPL Ga or Da]
    C2 --> D2[EPL Gb or Db]
    C3 --> D3[EPL Gc or Dc]
    D1 --> E1[Intrinsically Safe Equipment]
    D2 --> E2[Flameproof / Increased Safety]
    D3 --> E3[Non-Sparking / Encapsulated]
```

### 4.3 Physical Layout Schematic — Typical Refinery Pump Area

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UNHAZARDOUS["Non-Hazardous Zone (Safe Area)"]
        S1[Control Room]
    end
    subgraph ZONE2["Zone 2 (1 m to 3 m from pump)"]
        Z2A[Motor Junction Box]
        Z2B[Cable Tray Entry]
    end
    subgraph ZONE1["Zone 1 (Up to 1 m around pump seals)"]
        Z1A[Pump Casing Boundary]
        Z1B[Flange Connections]
    end
    subgraph ZONE0["Zone 0 (Inside pump casing, vapor space)"]
        Z0A[Process Fluid Reservoir]
    end
    Z0A --> Z1A --> Z2A --> S1
```

### 4.4 Equipment Selection Decision Matrix

| Source Grade | Ventilation | Resulting Zone | EPL Required | Acceptable Protection Types |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous | Poor | Zone 0 | Ga | Ex ia only |
| Continuous | Good | Zone 1 | Gb | Ex d, Ex p, Ex e |
| Primary | Poor | Zone 1 | Gb | Ex d, Ex p, Ex ib |
| Primary | Good | Zone 2 | Gc | Ex nA, Ex mc, Ex ec |
| Secondary | Any | Zone 2 | Gc | Ex nA, Ex tc |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Hazardous Area Classification. List the three zones used for gases and vapors as per IEC 60079-10-1.**

**Model Answer:**
Hazardous Area Classification is the systematic division of process plant areas into zones based on the **frequency and duration** of occurrence of explosive gas/dust atmospheres, used to select suitable explosion-protected equipment.

The three zones for gases/vapors:
1. **Zone 0** — Explosive atmosphere present **continuously or for long periods**.
2. **Zone 1** — Explosive atmosphere likely to occur in **normal operation occasionally**.
3. **Zone 2** — Explosive atmosphere **not likely** in normal operation; if present, only **briefly**.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Gas Group IIA, IIB, and IIC with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Group | MIE (mJ) | MESG (mm) | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IIA** | > 0.18 | > 0.90 | Propane, Methanol, Acetone |
| **IIB** | ≤ 0.18 | 0.50–0.90 | Ethylene, Coke oven gas |
| **IIC** | ≤ 0.06 | ≤ 0.50 | Hydrogen, Acetylene |

IIC is the **most stringent** group; equipment certified for IIC is automatically safe for IIB and IIA (but not vice versa).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Modified]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure for hazardous area classification of a petroleum storage tank farm as per IEC 60079-10-1.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A gasoline storage tank (LEL = 1.4% vol, vapor density = 3.0) has a flange with primary-grade release at $0.0008\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Ventilation supplies $0.04\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ of air. Wind velocity is $0.4\ \text{m/s}$. Determine the zone, the horizontal extent, and the required T-class. Auto-ignition temperature of gasoline is **280°C**.

##### **Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Identify substances:** Gasoline vapor (LEL 1.4%, AIT 280°C, vapor density 3.0 — heavy vapor).
2. **Identify release sources:** Tank vent, flange, dip hatch, pump seal, sampling point.
3. **Classify release grade:** Vent & open hatch → continuous; flange & pump → primary; drain valve → secondary.
4. **Estimate release rate (Q):** Use orifice equation or refer to API RP 500 tables.
5. **Determine ventilation coefficient K** = V_air / Q.
6. **Calculate zone extent** using $r = \sqrt{2Q / (\pi v C_{LEL})}$.
7. **Assign zones** (0/1/2) and select equipment per EPL.
8. **Draw area plan** with zone boundaries and document.

##### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Ventilation Coefficient:**

$$
K = \dfrac{0.04}{0.0008} = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Good Ventilation}
$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal Extent (assuming dilution to 0.5 × LEL):**

$$
r = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.0008}{\pi \times 0.4 \times (0.5 \times 0.014)}}
$$

$$
r = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0016}{0.00880}} = \sqrt{0.1818} \approx 0.426\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply safety factor of 2:**

$$
r_{final} \approx 0.85\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Assign Zones:**

- Primary grade + good ventilation → **Zone 1** (radius 1.0 m around flange)
- Adjacent area → **Zone 2** (radius up to 2.0 m)

**Step 5 — Equipment Selection:**

- **Gas Group:** IIA (gasoline)
- **T-class:** T3 (max 200°C), since AIT = 280°C > 200°C
- **EPL:** Gb (for Zone 1)
- Acceptable protection: **Ex d (flameproof)** or **Ex e (increased safety)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often assign **T1 (450°C)** for gasoline because "it's a fuel." This is **WRONG** — the surface temperature of equipment must remain **below the AIT of the substance**, so T3 (200°C) or stricter is mandatory. Mark deduction: **2 marks** for incorrect T-class.

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — Modified] (INTERNAL CHOICE)**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the Equipment Protection Levels (EPLs) Ga, Gb, Gc, Da, Db, and Dc. Explain the relationship between EPL and zone selection.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wheat flour milling plant (dust cloud) has a bucket elevator with a continuous release source. The bucket enclosure is poorly ventilated. Classify the zone and select the appropriate EPL and dust group.

##### **Model Solution to (a):**

| EPL | Zone Allowed | Protection Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ga** | Zone 0 | Two-fault tolerant intrinsic safety | Vapor space of closed tank |
| **Gb** | Zone 1 | One-fault tolerant | Pump, motor, lighting in Zone 1 |
| **Gc** | Zone 2 | Normal industrial | Cable glands, junction boxes |
| **Da** | Zone 20 | Two-fault tolerant for dust | Inside dust collection hopper |
| **Db** | Zone 21 | One-fault tolerant for dust | Exterior of dust-handling equipment |
| **Dc** | Zone 22 | Standard protection for dust | Surrounding area of dust equipment |

**Relationship:** Higher hazard zones (0, 20) require stricter EPLs (Ga, Da). The EPL is essentially the "translation" between zone risk and equipment integrity.

##### **Model Solution to (b):**

- **Dust type:** Wheat flour → combustible, **non-conductive** → **Dust Group IIIB**
- **Source grade:** Continuous (bucket elevator running 24/7 with dust leakage)
- **Ventilation:** Poor (enclosed)
- **Zone assigned:** **Zone 20** (inside the bucket enclosure)
- **Required EPL:** **Da**
- **Acceptable protection:** **DIP (Dust-Ignition-Proof)** enclosure, **Ex tD** or **Ex iaD**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students may classify wheat flour as **Group IIIC (conductive)**. Only **metal dusts** (aluminum, magnesium) are conductive. **Organic flours are Group IIIB** — non-conductive.

---

### KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HAC Goal:** Match zone severity with equipment protection level (EPL) to prevent ignition.
- **Three Gaseous Zones:** 0 (continuous), 1 (primary), 2 (secondary). **Three Dust Zones:** 20, 21, 22 — same logic.
- **Gas Groups:** IIA < IIB < IIC (increasing hazard). **Dust Groups:** IIIA < IIIB < IIIC.
- **T-Class T1–T6:** Surface temperature **must be below** AIT of the substance. **T6 is the strictest (85°C max).**
- **Ventilation Coefficient K** is the most powerful mitigation variable; high K can downgrade zones.
- **Equipment Selection Hierarchy:** Zone 0 → Ex ia (Ga); Zone 1 → Ex d / Ex p (Gb); Zone 2 → Ex nA / Ex e (Gc).
- **Standards to memorize for KTU:** IEC 60079-10-1 (gases), IEC 60079-10-2 (dusts), API RP 500, IS 5571.
- **Remember the Fire/Explosion Pentagon:** Fuel + Air + Ignition Source + Dispersion + Confinement.
- **Numerical Constants:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K); MIE of hydrogen = 0.02 mJ; safety factor of 2 is standard for zone extents.
- **KTU's favorite trick question:** "Why is a closed tank's vapor space Zone 0 and not Zone 1?" — Answer: Because the explosive atmosphere is **present continuously**, not just occasionally.
- **Trick 2:** "If a substance is heavier than air, does the zone extend upward or downward?" — Answer: **Downward (ground-level pooling)**, so Zone 1 boundary is measured along the floor.
- **Final Check Formula for Zone Extent:** Always apply the **factor of 2 safety margin** to the calculated theoretical extent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety in Transportation of Hazardous Chemicals by Road
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety in Transportation of Hazardous Chemicals by Road

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Safety in Transportation of Hazardous Chemicals by Road** is the integrated discipline of process safety engineering that governs the safe, compliant, and risk-controlled movement of dangerous goods (DG) by highway, from the point of manufacture or storage to the point of use or disposal. It encompasses the classification, packaging, labeling, documentation, vehicle selection, driver competency, route planning, emergency preparedness, and statutory compliance required to prevent loss of containment, exposure, fire, explosion, or toxic release during transit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> "The systematic application of engineering principles, statutory regulations, and operational controls to ensure that hazardous chemicals transported by road do not pose unacceptable risk to human life, property, and the environment during the entire chain of custody, from consignor to consignee."

In the Indian regulatory framework, the primary legislation governing this activity is:
- **The Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 (MSIHC Rules)** – under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
- **The Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989** – Chapter VII deals with transport of dangerous and hazardous goods.
- **The Central Motor Vehicles and Road Transport (Amendment) Rules** – periodic updates.
- **The Indian Road Congress (IRC) codes** for vehicle specifications.
- **The Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991** – mandatory for transporters.
- **The National Disaster Management Act, 2005** – for large-scale incident reporting.

> [!NOTE]
> **International Benchmarks for KTU Examinations:**
> The global reference is the **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods** (the "Orange Book"), which produces the **UN Model Regulations**. These are operationalised regionally as the **ADR** (European Agreement concerning the International Carriage of Dangerous Goods by Road), **TDG** (Canada/USA), and **ANTT** (Brazil). Indian rules are structurally harmonised with the UN Model.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a courier company that delivers fragile glassware. They use padded boxes, label the box "FRAGILE – THIS SIDE UP," train their drivers to avoid sudden braking, plan the route to avoid potholes, and keep an emergency number on the box in case it shatters. **Hazardous chemical transport is the extreme, life-critical version of this same system.**

> [!TIP]
> **The "Pillars of Safe Transport" Analogy:**
> Think of safe transport as a **four-legged stool**:
> 1. **Leg 1 – The Package:** A container strong enough to survive a crash *and* chemically compatible with the chemical.
> 2. **Leg 2 – The Information:** Placards, labels, and shipping documents that tell the police, fire brigade, and bystanders *what* is inside, *how much*, and *what to do if it leaks*.
> 3. **Leg 3 – The People:** A trained driver who knows what to do in a rollover, spill, or fire.
> 4. **Leg 4 – The Vehicle & Route:** A roadworthy tanker/truck, plus a pre-approved route that avoids crowded areas, water bodies, and tunnels.
>
> If **any one leg breaks**, the stool collapses — and so does the safety system. The KTU examiner loves questions that test whether you understand **all four pillars as a single, integrated system**, not just one in isolation.

### 1.3 Why is Road Transport Uniquely Risky?

Unlike a fixed chemical plant — which has bunding, firewater systems, and trained personnel on-site 24×7 — a truck on a highway is a **moving chemical plant with no permanent safety infrastructure**. It passes through villages, schools, hospitals, rivers, and densely populated urban areas. The accident rate per kilometre travelled is higher than for rail or pipeline, but the *volume* carried is smaller, making road the flexible backbone of the chemical supply chain.

**Key Standard Metrics (Bold for Memory):**
- **UN Number (UN No.)**: 4-digit identifier (e.g., UN1090 = Acetone, UN1017 = Chlorine).
- **HIN (Hazard Identification Number)**: 2–3 digit code on orange placards in Europe.
- **Kemler Code**: Older name for HIN.
- **ERG (Emergency Response Guidebook) Number**: 4-digit guide number used by first responders (e.g., Guide 127 = Flammable liquids).
- **Flash Point**: The lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapour to ignite; **< 23 °C** = Class 3 Flammable Liquid (highly dangerous).
- **Packing Group (PG)**: I (high danger), II (medium), III (low) — determines packaging robustness.
- **TREM Card**: Transport Emergency Card — the Indian equivalent of ERG, mandatory in India.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Nine UN Hazard Classes — The Heart of the System

The UN Model Regulations classify all dangerous goods into **9 Classes** (with subdivisions). Every other rule in road transport flows from this classification.

| Class | Name | Sub-Divisions | Examples | Primary Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Explosives | 1.1 Mass explosion, 1.2 Projection, 1.3 Fire, 1.4 Minor, 1.5 Very insensitive, 1.6 Extremely insensitive | TNT, fireworks, airbag inflators | Mass/projectile damage |
| **2** | Gases | 2.1 Flammable, 2.2 Non-flammable non-toxic, 2.3 Toxic | LPG (2.1), Oxygen (2.2), Chlorine (2.3) | Pressure, fire, toxicity |
| **3** | Flammable Liquids | Flash point < 23 °C (PG I/II), 23–60 °C (PG III) | Petrol, acetone, ethanol | Fire, vapour cloud |
| **4** | Flammable Solids | 4.1 Solid, 4.2 Spontaneous combustion, 4.3 Water-reactive | White phosphorus, sodium, calcium carbide | Fire, toxic gas on contact with water |
| **5** | Oxidizers & Organic Peroxides | 5.1 Oxidizer, 5.2 Organic peroxide | Hydrogen peroxide, ammonium nitrate, benzoyl peroxide | Intensifies fire; may explode |
| **6** | Toxic & Infectious | 6.1 Toxic, 6.2 Infectious | Cyanides, pesticides, medical waste | Poison, infection |
| **7** | Radioactive | I-White, II-Yellow, III-Yellow | Uranium compounds, medical isotopes | Ionising radiation |
| **8** | Corrosives | Acid vs. Alkaline | Sulphuric acid, caustic soda | Skin burns, metal destruction |
| **9** | Miscellaneous DG | Environmentally hazardous, dry ice, lithium batteries | PCB, asbestos, lithium-ion | Various |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "EGGS OF PC":**
> **E**xplosives, **G**ases, (skip 2 because it's gases), **G**o for **F**lammable liquids (Class 3), **O**xidisers (5), **P**oison (6), **C**orrosive (8). Radioactives (7) and Misc (9) are the wildcards. Examiners often ask: "A truck carries 10 tonnes of sulphuric acid. Which class? What placards?" — Answer: **Class 8**, **corrosive placard** (black-and-white, hand-and-metal-barrel symbol).

### 2.2 The Transport Chain — Logic Step-by-Step

Step 1 → **Hazard Classification & UN Number Assignment**
The consignor (sender) classifies the chemical using the UN Model. Each pure substance or mixture is assigned a **UN Number**, a **Proper Shipping Name (PSN)**, a **Hazard Class**, and a **Packing Group (PG)**.

Step 2 → **Selection of Packaging / Tank**
- For drum/carton transport → UN-certified packaging tested to **UN Performance Tests**: drop test, leakproofness test, hydraulic pressure test, stacking test.
- For bulk liquid/gas → Road tanker conforming to **IS 1783** (for liquids) or **IS 7285** (for liquefied gases), or international codes like **ADR tank codes** (e.g., **LGBN** for chlorinated solvents).

Step 3 → **Marking, Labelling & Placarding**
- **Package labels**: Diamond-shaped, 100 × 100 mm minimum, colour-coded per class.
- **UN Number mark**: On each package, e.g., "**UN1090**".
- **Vehicle placards**: Orange, **30 cm × 40 cm** minimum, displayed on front and rear, showing **HIN (top)** and **UN Number (bottom)** in Europe; in India, **trefoil + class label** on all four sides.

Step 4 → **Documentation — The "Three Pillars of Paper"**
1. **Transport Emergency Card (TREM Card)** — issued by the manufacturer, kept in the driver's cabin. In India, the standard form is **Form V** under CMRV.
2. **Consignment Note (LR / Transport Document)** — includes consignor, consignee, UN No., class, PG, quantity, route.
3. **Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS / SDS)** — 16-section, GHS-aligned, must accompany the load.

Step 5 → **Driver & Vehicle Eligibility**
- Driver: **Hazardous Goods Driving License endorsement** (in India, under Rule 9 of CMVR, endorsed by RTO after a special training).
- Vehicle: Valid **Fitness Certificate (FC)**, **Insurance under PLIA 1991**, **Pollution Under Control (PUC)**, **Hazardous Waste authorization** if applicable.

Step 6 → **Route Planning & Movement**
- **Pre-approved route** by local authorities, avoiding densely populated zones.
- **Speed limits**: typically **40 km/h in built-up areas, 60 km/h on highways** for DG vehicles (Indian practice).
- **No night movement** of explosives (Class 1) in many states.
- **Convoy rules**: Minimum **50 m gap** between two DG vehicles; **two-vehicle convoy** only for explosives.

Step 7 → **Emergency Response**
- Driver must carry: fire extinguisher (min. **9 kg DCP** for Class 3; **water spray** for Class 8), eye wash, spill kit, self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) for toxic loads.
- 24×7 emergency contact number on the vehicle (in India, **+91-120-4366552** is the industry helpline).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Standard | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary Conditions | Use in Road Safety |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Evaporation Rate from Spill** (instantaneous, stagnant pool) | $E = \dfrac{M \cdot P_{vap} \cdot A}{R \cdot T}$ | $M$ = molar mass (kg/mol), $P_{vap}$ = vapour pressure (Pa), $A$ = pool area (m²), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = temperature (K) | Estimate vapour cloud size from tanker rollover |
| 2 | **Pool Area (circular spill)** | $A = \pi \cdot r^{2}$ where $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{V}{\pi \cdot h}}$ | $V$ = spill volume (m³), $h$ = pool depth (m, typically 0.005 for soil) | Determine vapour source area |
| 3 | **Flash Point (FP) Lower Limit** (Class 3 PG I) | $FP < 23\ \text{°C}$ and $\text{BP} \leq 35\ \text{°C}$ | — | High-danger flammable liquids (e.g., diethyl ether, acetone) |
| 4 | **Flash Point (FP) Class Boundary** (PG III) | $23\ \text{°C} \leq FP \leq 60\ \text{°C}$ | — | Medium-danger flammable liquids (e.g., diesel) |
| 5 | **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** | $LFL = \dfrac{100}{\text{stoichiometric air-fuel ratio}}$ | % v/v | Trigger for explosion; shutdown at 25% LEL |
| 6 | **Tanker Pressure Test Pressure** | $P_{test} = 1.3 \cdot P_{design}$ | Bar / kPa | Mandatory hydrostatic test every **5 years** in India |
| 7 | **Minimum Plaque / Placard Size** (UN) | $\geq 250\ \text{mm} \times 250\ \text{mm}$ (Class 1 & 7: 100 mm border) | mm | Visibility from **10 m** in daylight |
| 8 | **Required DCP Extinguisher Capacity** | $\geq 9\ \text{kg}$ for Class 3 loads; $\geq 50\ \text{kg}$ for bulk tanker | kg | Per Indian Explosives Rules / Factory Rules |
| 9 | **Maximum Speed (DG vehicle, India)** | $v_{max} = 40\ \text{km/h}$ urban, $60\ \text{km/h}$ highway | km/h | Most-cited statutory limit |
| 10 | **Driver Training Validity (India)** | 3 years | years | Re-certification mandatory under MSIHC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Equation 1:** The evaporation rate equation is for a **stagnant, non-boiling pool**. For a pressurised liquefied gas (e.g., LPG, chlorine), flash vaporisation occurs and the effective source is **1.5× to 3× the spilled liquid mass** as vapour. Use the **Britter-McQuaid** or **Webber-Puttock** model for instant spills of cryogenic or pressurised liquids.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This body of knowledge is used by:
- **HSE (Health, Safety, Environment) Officers** in chemical/pharma/refinery companies to audit transport contractors.
- **Emergency Response Teams (ERT)** to interpret TREM cards and select correct PPE.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain Engineers** to design "just-in-time" delivery with minimum DG inventory in transit.
- **Urban Planners** to designate "HGV (Hazardous Goods Vehicle) bypass routes" and "Safe Haven Parking" for emergencies.
- **Insurance Underwriters** to assess PLIA premiums and risk.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pool Evaporation Rate from a Tanker Spill

This is the most important back-of-envelope calculation in road transport safety. Given a tanker rollover that breaches a compartment, we want to know the **rate of vapour generation** to size the downwind evacuation zone.

**Given (realistic KTU problem data):**
- Chemical: **Toluene** (Class 3, UN1294)
- Spill volume: $V = 5\ \text{m}^{3}$ (one compartment of a small tanker)
- Pool depth on asphalt: $h = 0.005\ \text{m}$ (5 mm — assumes spills on rough surface)
- Ambient temperature: $T = 303\ \text{K}$ (30 °C — Indian summer)
- Vapour pressure of toluene at 30 °C: $P_{vap} = 5.0\ \text{kPa} = 5000\ \text{Pa}$
- Molar mass: $M = 0.092\ \text{kg/mol}$ (92 g/mol)

**Step 1 — Calculate the pool area.**

$$
A = \frac{V}{h}
$$

$$
A = \frac{5\ \text{m}^{3}}{0.005\ \text{m}} = 1000\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

This is a pool roughly **35.7 m × 35.7 m** — the size of a small city block.

**Step 2 — Apply the molar evaporation flux equation.**

$$
E = \frac{M \cdot P_{vap} \cdot A}{R \cdot T}
$$

$$
E = \frac{0.092\ \text{kg/mol} \times 5000\ \text{Pa} \times 1000\ \text{m}^{2}}{8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)} \times 303\ \text{K}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$
0.092 \times 5000 = 460
$$

$$
460 \times 1000 = 460000
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator evaluation.**

$$
8.314 \times 303 = 2519.142
$$

**Step 5 — Final division.**

$$
E = \frac{460000}{2519.142} \approx 182.6\ \text{kg/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** About **182.6 kg of toluene vapour per second** is released. Toluene's LFL is **1.2 % v/v** (≈ 48 g/m³ at NTP), so this single rollover can produce an explosive vapour cloud **larger than 3800 m³ in the first second**. This is why **immediate ignition source control** (no smoking, engine off, no mobile phones) is the first command in every TREM card.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Plaque & Equipment

| Chemical | UN No. | Class | PG | Primary Plaque (colour) | Driver PPE | Spill Response | Evacuation Distance (initial) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acetone | 1090 | 3 | II | Red (flammable) | SCBA, chemical suit, antistatic boots | DCP extinguisher, foam blanket | 300 m downwind |
| Chlorine (liquefied) | 1017 | 2.3 (5.1, 8) | I | Yellow (toxic) + green (oxidiser) + black-white (corrosive) | Full SCBA, Level A suit | Water spray curtain, neutralise with soda ash | **1500 m downwind** |
| Sulphuric Acid | 1830 | 8 | II | Black-and-white (corrosive) | Chemical splash goggles, PVC suit | Lime/soda ash neutralisation, do NOT use water on large spills (exothermic) | 100 m |
| Petrol | 1203 | 3 | II | Red (flammable) | Antistatic PPE, SCBA | Foam, DCP, evacuate ignition sources | 500 m |
| LPG | 1075 | 2.1 | — | Red (flammable gas) | Antistatic, SCBA | Water spray to cool tank, never direct jet on leak | 800 m |

### 3.3 Logical Justification of the "Three Tiers of Control"

The hierarchy of controls for road transport (HSE standard) applies as:

**Tier 1 — Elimination / Substitution** (most effective, rarely possible in transport — you must move the chemical as-is).

**Tier 2 — Engineering Controls** (the bulk of the system):
- Pressure-relief valves on tankers
- Double-walled tanks for highly toxic chemicals
- Anti-rollover systems (electronic stability control)
- GPS tracking with geofencing

**Tier 3 — Administrative Controls**:
- Driver training
- TREM cards
- Route restrictions
- Time-of-day restrictions (e.g., no night movement in Kerala for Class 1)

**Tier 4 — PPE** (last line of defence, applied during emergency response).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:** "Why is a driver not allowed to use a mobile phone near a tanker of petrol?"
> **Model Answer:** "The RF energy and the battery's potential to spark can ignite flammable vapours. The vapour cloud is invisible and can extend **several metres** beyond the tank vents. The driver's phone is a Tier 4 administrative control violation, but more importantly, the *physical absence* of the ignition source (engine off, phone off) is a Tier 2 engineering control philosophy applied to the human-machine interface."

### 3.4 Worked Example: Placard Selection (KTU-Style 7-mark question)

**Problem:** A truck is loaded with **20 tonnes of acrylonitrile (CH₂=CH–C≡N)**, flash point = **0 °C**, toxic by inhalation, transported from Kochi to Bangalore.

**Solution Outline:**

1. **Look up the UN number:** UN1093 (Acrylonitrile, stabilized).
2. **Identify the primary class:** **Class 3** (Flammable Liquid), because flash point < 23 °C.
3. **Identify subsidiary risks:** It is also **toxic (Class 6.1)**. Per UN precedence rules, Class 3 takes priority for the primary placard, with a Class 6.1 subsidiary placard displayed.
4. **Assign Packing Group:** Because flash point < 23 °C and BP = 77 °C, it is **PG I** (high danger).
5. **Placard size on vehicle:** 250 mm × 250 mm minimum, on all four sides.
6. **Document set:** TREM card (Form V), Consignment Note, MSDS, PLIA insurance, driver endorsement under Rule 9 of CMVR.
7. **Route:** Must avoid Nilgiri ghat sections during night; Safe Haven parking at Salem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often write "Class 6.1" as the *primary* class for acrylonitrile. **Always check flash point first.** A substance with multiple hazards is placarded with the **primary class** based on the **UN Precedence of Hazard Table** in the Orange Book.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Integrated Road Transport Safety System (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Consignor: Chemical Manufacturer] --> B[Hazard Classification UN Number UN Class PG]
    B --> C[Packaging Selection]
    C --> D[UN Certified Package or IS Tanker]
    D --> E[Marking Labelling Placarding]
    E --> F[Documentation TREM Card MSDS LR]
    F --> G[Driver Selection Rule 9 Endorsed]
    G --> H[Vehicle Inspection FC PUC PLIA]
    H --> I[Route Planning Approved Path Safe Havens]
    I --> J[In-Transit Movement Speed Convoy Rules]
    J --> K{Emergency or Routine Arrival}
    K -->|Routine| L[Consignee Acceptance]
    K -->|Emergency| M[Spill Fire Leak Rollover]
    M --> N[Activate TREM Card]
    N --> O[First Responder Police Fire ERT]
    O --> P[Containment Neutralisation Disposal]
    P --> Q[Incident Reporting MSIHC District Authority]
    L --> R[Document Archival PLIA Compliance]
    Q --> R
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Classifying a Chemical for Transport

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Have a chemical to transport] --> T{Is it a gas at 20 C and 101.3 kPa}
    T -->|Yes| U[Class 2 Check 2.1 Flammable 2.2 Non-flammable 2.3 Toxic]
    T -->|No| V{Flash point below 60 C}
    V -->|Yes| W[Class 3 Flammable Liquid Check PG I II III]
    V -->|No| X{Is it a solid that spontaneously combusts or reacts with water}
    X -->|Yes| Y[Class 4.1 4.2 or 4.3]
    X -->|No| Z{Is it an oxidizer or organic peroxide}
    Z -->|Yes| AA[Class 5.1 or 5.2]
    Z -->|No| AB{Is it toxic LD50 below threshold}
    AB -->|Yes| AC[Class 6.1]
    AB -->|No| AD{Is it radioactive}
    AD -->|Yes| AE[Class 7]
    AD -->|No| AF{Is it corrosive to skin or metals}
    AF -->|Yes| AG[Class 8]
    AF -->|No| AH[Class 9 Miscellaneous DG]
```

### 4.3 Vehicle Placard Layout (Schematic, top view of a road tanker)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tanker Truck
        direction LR
        front1[Front: 250x250 Class Placard + UN No] --- cab1[Driver Cab: TREM Card, Fire Extinguisher, SCBA]
        cab1 --- tank1[Tank: 4 placards on all 4 sides]
        tank1 --- rear1[Rear: 250x250 Class Placard + UN No]
    end
    style front1 fill:#ff9999
    style rear1 fill:#ff9999
    style tank1 fill:#ffffcc
```

### 4.4 Emergency Response Decision Tree (Spill / Fire / Leak)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ER[Incident Detected] --> EVAC[Initial Evacuation: 100m minimum]
    EVAC --> ASSESS{Assess from upwind: Identify placard}
    ASSESS --> ID[UN No and class identified]
    ID --> ERG[Refer TREM Card or ERG Guide number]
    ERG --> DEC{Decision branch}
    DEC -->|Fire| F1[Use correct extinguisher: DCP for Class 3, water spray for Class 8, never water for Class 4.3]
    DEC -->|Spill| S1[Contain with absorbent, neutralise, no ignition sources]
    DEC -->|Leak| L1[Close valve, apply patch kit, ventilate]
    F1 --> NOTIFY[Notify: 108 ambulance, 101 fire, local RTO, industry helpline]
    S1 --> NOTIFY
    L1 --> NOTIFY
    NOTIFY --> CLEAN[Decontaminate equipment, site remediation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical drawings?** Mermaid renders block diagrams and flowcharts, not photorealistic vehicles. The block-level architecture above is, in fact, **more useful for KTU examinations** because it forces the student to memorise the *flow of information* and the *sequence of controls* — which is what the examiner tests.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the term "Hazardous Chemical" as per the Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989. List any four hazard classes used in road transport classification.**

**Model Answer:**
As per MSIHC Rules 1989, a **hazardous chemical** is any chemical substance, preparation, or mixture which, by reason of its chemical or physico-chemical properties, is liable to cause harm to human beings, other living organisms, property, or the environment. The four hazard classes used in road transport (per UN system) are:
1. **Class 1** – Explosives
2. **Class 3** – Flammable Liquids
3. **Class 6** – Toxic and Infectious Substances
4. **Class 8** – Corrosives

(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks list with one-line example each)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is a TREM Card? Mention any four items of information it must contain.**

**Model Answer:**
**TREM Card (Transport Emergency Card)** is a document carried in the driver's cabin that provides immediate, life-saving information to the first responder in case of an accident involving hazardous chemicals during transport. It is mandatory in India under the Central Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989 for vehicles carrying dangerous goods.
The four essential items of information are:
1. **UN Number and Proper Shipping Name** of the chemical.
2. **Hazard Class** and **Subsidiary Risks**.
3. **Emergency contact numbers** (24×7 helpline).
4. **Initial emergency response actions** — spill containment, fire extinguishing media, evacuation distance, and first aid.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Option 1):** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **classification of hazardous chemicals** for road transport as per the UN system. Explain the criteria used to assign a chemical to a specific class, with at least two examples for each major class.

**(b) [7 marks]** A road tanker of capacity **15,000 litres** is carrying **motor spirit (petrol)** from an IOC depot in Ernakulam to a retail outlet in Thrissur. The tanker meets with an accident and **one compartment of 5,000 L** ruptures, spilling on a flat road surface. Given:
- Density of petrol = **750 kg/m³**
- Flash point = **–40 °C**
- Vapour pressure at 30 °C = **60 kPa**
- Molar mass of petrol (approx., C₈H₁₈) = **0.114 kg/mol**
- Pool depth on road = **5 mm**

Calculate the **rate of vapour generation** in kg/s, and state the **immediate emergency response** actions.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The UN system classifies dangerous goods into **9 classes** based on the type of primary hazard they pose. The criteria are as follows:

1. **Class 1 — Explosives:** Substances that can undergo a rapid, self-propagating decomposition (detonation) or a violent thermal reaction. Sub-divided by mass-explosion potential (1.1–1.6). Example: **TNT (UN0209)**, **Fireworks (UN0337)**.

2. **Class 2 — Gases:** Substances that are gases at 20 °C and 101.3 kPa, or have a vapour pressure > 300 kPa at 50 °C. Sub-divided into **2.1 Flammable** (LPG, UN1075), **2.2 Non-flammable, non-toxic** (Oxygen, UN1072), and **2.3 Toxic** (Chlorine, UN1017).

3. **Class 3 — Flammable Liquids:** Liquids with flash point **≤ 60 °C** (closed-cup test). Further divided into **PG I (FP < 23 °C, BP ≤ 35 °C — e.g., diethyl ether)**, **PG II (FP < 23 °C, BP > 35 °C — e.g., acetone)**, and **PG III (FP 23–60 °C — e.g., diesel)**.

4. **Class 4 — Flammable Solids:** 4.1 readily combustible solids (e.g., **white phosphorus, UN1381**), 4.2 liable to spontaneous combustion (e.g., **activated carbon, UN1362**), and 4.3 emit flammable gas on contact with water (e.g., **sodium, UN1428**).

5. **Class 5 — Oxidising Substances and Organic Peroxides:** 5.1 oxidizers (e.g., **hydrogen peroxide, UN2014**), 5.2 organic peroxides (e.g., **benzoyl peroxide, UN2085**).

6. **Class 6 — Toxic and Infectious:** 6.1 toxic (oral LD50 ≤ 25 mg/kg in rats — e.g., **sodium cyanide, UN1689**); 6.2 infectious (e.g., **medical waste, UN2814**).

7. **Class 7 — Radioactive:** Specific activity above threshold — e.g., **uranium hexafluoride, UN2978**.

8. **Class 8 — Corrosives:** Cause visible necrosis of skin in ≤ 4 hours — e.g., **sulphuric acid, UN1830**; **sodium hydroxide solution, UN1824**.

9. **Class 9 — Miscellaneous:** Substances presenting hazards not covered by other classes — e.g., **lithium-ion batteries, UN3480**; **asbestos, UN2212**.

> The classification is **mandatory before any movement**. The consignor must produce an SDS, assign a UN number, and label every package.

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Convert volume to mass.**

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = 750\ \text{kg/m}^{3} \times 5\ \text{m}^{3} = 3750\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate pool area.**

$$
A = \frac{V}{h} = \frac{5}{0.005} = 1000\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the molar evaporation flux formula.**

$$
E = \frac{M \cdot P_{vap} \cdot A}{R \cdot T} = \frac{0.114 \times 60000 \times 1000}{8.314 \times 303}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator.**

$$
0.114 \times 60000 \times 1000 = 6.84 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator.**

$$
8.314 \times 303 = 2519.142
$$

**Step 6 — Final answer.**

$$
E = \frac{6.84 \times 10^{6}}{2519.142} \approx 2715.2\ \text{kg/s}
$$

> This is a **massive** vapour release — petrol's LFL is **1.4 % v/v**. The vapour cloud in the first second alone exceeds **190,000 m³**. **A 1 km downwind evacuation is mandatory.**

**Step 7 — Emergency response actions (Petrol, UN1203, Class 3, PG II):**
1. **Engine off**, no ignition sources, no mobile phones.
2. **Evacuate 500 m** downwind, 100 m upwind.
3. Use **foam blanket** (AFFF) or **large quantity of water spray** to suppress vapour; **DCP extinguisher** for small fires only.
4. **Prevent entry into drains, water bodies, and sewers** (use bunding with sand).
5. **Call 101 (fire) + 108 (ambulance) + Industry ERT helpline**.
6. **Driver and helper** use SCBA if approaching to close valves.
7. **Notify RTO, District Authority, and Pollution Control Board** under MSIHC Rules.

(7 + 7 = 14 marks)

---

#### **Question B (Option 2):** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **key provisions of the Central Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989 (Chapter VII)** regarding the transport of dangerous goods by road. Discuss the role of **driver training, vehicle fitness, and documentation**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **emergency response protocol** to be followed in case of a **leak of liquefied chlorine (UN1017)** from a road tanker. Include PPE, evacuation distance, neutralising agent, and first aid.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

Chapter VII (Rules 129–137) of the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989** governs the transport of dangerous and hazardous goods by road. Key provisions are:

1. **Driver Requirements (Rule 9 + Rule 137):** A driver of a hazardous goods vehicle must hold a **valid driving license endorsed for hazardous goods** by the RTO. The driver must be **at least 20 years old**, with **a minimum of 3 years of HMV driving experience**, and have completed a **certified training programme** (re-validated every 3 years) covering fire fighting, first aid, spill control, and the use of TREM cards.

2. **Vehicle Requirements (Rule 129):** Every vehicle must have a **valid Fitness Certificate (FC)**, must be **roadworthy**, must display the prescribed **placards and labels** on all four sides, and must carry **a fire extinguisher of the prescribed capacity** (≥ 9 kg DCP for Class 3, ≥ 50 kg for bulk). The vehicle must also carry the **TREM Card** in the cabin.

3. **Packaging and Containment (Rule 130):** Goods must be packed in **UN-certified or BIS-certified containers**. Tankers must conform to **IS 1783** (for liquids) or **IS 7285** (for liquefied gases), and must have undergone the **periodic pressure test** as per the code.

4. **Documentation (Rule 131):** Every consignment must travel with:
- **Consignment Note (LR)** showing UN Number, Class, PSN, quantity, consignor/consignee.
- **TREM Card** in the cabin.
- **MSDS/SDS** (16-section, GHS-aligned).
- **PLIA Insurance Certificate** (mandatory under Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991).
- **Driver's training certificate**.

5. **Speed and Movement Restrictions:** **40 km/h in built-up areas; 60 km/h on highways** (Indian practice). Convoy rules, night-time restrictions for explosives, and route restrictions are part of the State-level Motor Vehicles Rules and Police Acts.

6. **Penalties:** Non-compliance is punishable under **Section 177 of the Motor Vehicles Act** and can result in cancellation of license, vehicle seizure, and prosecution under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** and the **Indian Penal Code (Sections 284, 285, 286)** for negligent handling of dangerous substances.

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Liquefied Chlorine (UN1017)** is a **Class 2.3 (Toxic Gas)** with **subsidiary risks of Class 5.1 (Oxidiser) and Class 8 (Corrosive)**. It is shipped in pressurised road tankers (typical capacity 16–25 tonnes) at a working pressure of about **6–10 bar**.

**Emergency Response Protocol:**

1. **Immediate Isolation:**
   - Establish a **hot zone of 1500 m** downwind and **500 m** in all other directions (per ERG Guide 124).
   - **Engine off**, no ignition sources, no metallic tools that can spark.
   - **Evacuate all civilians** — chlorine is lethal at **10 ppm** for prolonged exposure and **1000 ppm** is rapidly fatal.

2. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) — Level A:**
   - **Fully encapsulating chemical protective suit** with positive-pressure SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus).
   - **Chlorine-compatible** materials only (not leather, not ordinary rubber). Use **Viton, Butyl rubber, or neoprene** boots and gloves.
   - **Full-face mask** with chlorine-rated canister (only for short escape; SCBA for any approach).

3. **Leak Containment:**
   - **NEVER apply water directly** on a liquid chlorine leak — it accelerates vaporisation.
   - Use **water spray curtain** between the leak and the public to absorb vapour.
   - For small leaks, apply **caustic soda (NaOH) solution or hydrated lime slurry** to neutralise the chlorine.
   - Emergency teams should use **chlorine repair kits (Class C)** with specially designed stop-plugs.

4. **First Aid:**
   - **Inhalation:** Move victim to fresh air, administer **oxygen**, keep at rest. Do NOT induce vomiting.
   - **Eye contact:** Rinse with copious water for **at least 15 minutes**, hold eyelids open.
   - **Skin contact:** Wash with large amounts of water; remove contaminated clothing.

5. **Notification:**
   - **Fire Brigade (101)**, **Ambulance (108)**, **Local RTO**, **District Disaster Management Authority**, **Kerala State Pollution Control Board**, and the **chlorine manufacturer's 24×7 emergency helpline**.

6. **Environmental Protection:**
   - **Contain the wash water** in a bund; do not let it enter storm drains. Chlorine + water forms **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)** and **hydrochloric acid (HCl)**, both corrosive.

(7 + 7 = 14 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not mix up "Class" and "Packing Group".** Class is the type of hazard; Packing Group (I, II, III) is the **degree** of danger within a class. Examiners deduct 1 mark per confusion.
> 2. **Always state the UN Number first**, then the Proper Shipping Name, then the Class. Sequence matters.
> 3. **For petroleum products, the primary class is ALWAYS Class 3 (Flammable Liquid)** — never Class 9 or "Miscellaneous".
> 4. **Do not write "Class 1" for a tanker of chlorine** — chlorine is **Class 2.3**, not Class 1 (which is reserved for explosives).
> 5. **Always include the evacuation distance** in any emergency response answer — examiners specifically look for it.
> 6. **Cite the Rule number** (e.g., "Rule 9 of CMVR") wherever possible — it shows technical precision and fetches full marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **9 UN Hazard Classes** — Explosives, Gases, Flammable Liquids, Flammable Solids, Oxidizers, Toxic, Radioactive, Corrosives, Miscellaneous.
- **UN Number** is a 4-digit code (e.g., UN1090 = Acetone) — assigned to each hazardous substance or article.
- **Hazard Class (Primary + Subsidiary)** — determines placards, driver training, packaging, and storage compatibility.
- **Packing Group (PG I, II, III)** — PG I is the most dangerous (e.g., acrylonitrile); PG III is least (e.g., diesel).
- **Flash Point** is the master criterion for Class 3: **< 23 °C = high danger; 23–60 °C = moderate; > 60 °C = not Class 3.**
- **TREM Card** (Form V) is **mandatory** in India; carry in the driver's cabin.
- **MSDS/SDS** has **16 sections** under GHS — must accompany every consignment.
- **Placards** on a road tanker: minimum **250 mm × 250 mm**, on **all four sides**, **visible from 10 m**.
- **Driver endorsement** under **Rule 9, CMVR** — re-certification every **3 years**.
- **Speed limit** for DG vehicle in India: **40 km/h urban, 60 km/h highway**.
- **Fire extinguisher**: minimum **9 kg DCP** for package loads; **50 kg** for bulk tankers; **water spray** for Class 8 acids (small spills).
- **NEVER use water** on **Class 4.3 (water-reactive)**, **Class 5.2 (organic peroxide in fire)**, or **liquid chlorine leaks** — accelerates reaction.
- **Evacuation distance rule of thumb** (use ERG Guide):
  - Class 2.3 (toxic gas like chlorine): **1500 m** downwind.
  - Class 3 (flammable liquid, bulk): **500 m** downwind.
  - Class 1 (explosives): **1000 m** all directions.
  - Class 8 (corrosive, small spill): **100 m**.
- **Indian statutes to remember**: **MSIHC Rules 1989**, **CMVR 1989 Chapter VII**, **PLIA 1991**, **Factories Act 1948 (Section 41B)**, **Environment Protection Act 1986**.
- **International reference**: **UN Orange Book**, **ADR** (Europe), **TDG** (Canada).
- **No mobile phones**, **no smoking**, **no naked flames** within **15 m** of any DG vehicle — the first three commands of every TREM card.
- **Convoy rule**: minimum **50 m** spacing between two DG vehicles on the road.
- **PLIA Insurance** is **mandatory** for every consignment, covering third-party injury and property damage.
- **Hierarchy of controls in transport**: **Elimination (rarely) → Engineering (tank, valve, GPS) → Administrative (TREM, route, training) → PPE (responder's last line)**.
- **Three Tiers of Paperwork**: **TREM Card + LR/Consignment Note + MSDS** — examiners ALWAYS test these three.
- **"Right to Know" principle**: every responder has the right to know **what is in the tanker** within **60 seconds** of arrival — that is why **placards on all four sides** are non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HAZCHEM Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HAZCHEM Code

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **HAZCHEM Code (Hazardous Chemical Code):** A standardized alphanumeric emergency response marking system painted on transport vehicles, storage tanks, and shipping containers that conveys concise, life-critical information to emergency services (firefighters, police, paramedics, spill responders) about the nature of the hazardous material being carried and the appropriate tactical response actions required at the scene of an incident.

The **HAZCHEM** system is formally known as the **Emergency Action Code (EAC)** system in the United Kingdom, where it originated in the 1970s under the guidance of the **Home Office** and the **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)**. It is now adopted internationally in countries such as **Australia, New Zealand, Malaysia, Singapore, and India**, often in modified or extended formats.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBBT723 – Process Safety Engineering), the HAZCHEM Code is a mandatory component of **Module 3: Hazard Identification and Communication Systems**, where it bridges the gap between **chemical hazard classification** (purely informational) and **emergency tactical response** (purely operational).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at an airport with only 5 seconds to communicate a complex medical emergency to a foreign doctor who speaks no English. You do not have time for full sentences — you need a **universal pictogram-and-code shorthand**.

The **HAZCHEM Code is precisely that for chemical emergencies.**

Think of it like a **"tactical cheat sheet"** for firefighters:
- A **number** (1, 2, 3, or 4) tells them *what firefighting medium to use* (water spray, foam, foam + water, dry agent).
- **One or two capital letters** (e.g., P, R, S, T, W, X, Y, Z, E) tell them *how to fight it* and *whether to contain or dilute* any spilled chemical.
- Optionally, the letter **"E"** appended at the end warns them to **EVACUATE** the surrounding population immediately.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy to Other Systems:**  
> Just as **NFPA 704 (the "Fire Diamond")** uses colored squares to convey hazard information to firefighters in the United States, the **HAZCHEM Code** uses a compact alphanumeric string to convey emergency action instructions globally — except HAZCHEM is far more *action-oriented* (tells you what to DO), while NFPA 704 is more *descriptive* (tells you what something IS).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard Code Length | **2 to 4 characters** (e.g., `2W`, `3Z`, `4WE`) | Compact identification |
| Reference Temperature | **15 °C** | Standard property reporting |
| Reference Pressure | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** | Standard property reporting |
| Evacuation Radius (typical) | **≥ 100 m to 1500 m** | Depends on UN number class |
| Response Time Window | **< 5 seconds (visual identification)** | Speed of decision |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight:**  
> Under KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBBT723 – Module 3), the HAZCHEM Code is studied as part of **"Hazard Communication and Emergency Response Coding Systems,"** and students must be able to:  
> 1. Decode any HAZCHEM Code (e.g., `2RE`, `3Y`, `4WE`).  
> 2. Select the correct firefighting medium.  
> 3. Determine whether to **contain (R)** or **dilute (S, T, W, X, Y, Z)** spillage.  
> 4. Identify evacuation requirements (presence of `E`).  

---

## 1.4 Origin & Applicability

- **Origin:** United Kingdom, **1972**, jointly developed by the **Home Office**, **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)**, and **Fire Brigades**.
- **Initial Scope:** Road and rail transport of hazardous chemicals.
- **Current Scope:** Road, rail, sea, air, fixed storage installations, and laboratory containers.
- **Adopted By:** Australia, New Zealand, Malaysia, Singapore, South Africa, and select Indian industrial zones (e.g., under **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989**).
- **Harmonization Status:** Compatible with **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods (UNRTDG)** — UN Number classes (1–9) provide the substance identification context, while HAZCHEM provides the response action.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a HAZCHEM Code

A complete HAZCHEM Code has **three (3) structural components**:

$$\text{HAZCHEM Code} = \underbrace{[\text{NUMERAL}]}_{\text{Component 1}} \; + \; \underbrace{[\text{LETTER(S)}]}_{\text{Component 2}} \; + \; \underbrace{[\text{E (optional)}]}_{\text{Component 3}}$$

### Component 1 — The Numeral (1, 2, 3, 4)

The numeral specifies the **firefighting medium** and is the most critical operational instruction. It is chosen based on the chemical's reaction with water.

| Numeral | Firefighting Medium | Application Logic |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | **Coarse Water Spray (Large Droplet)** ONLY — Do NOT use jets | For water-reactive chemicals that react violently with fine water jets (e.g., sodium, certain acids). Use large droplets to minimize disturbance and reaction surface. |
| **2** | **Foam (Protein, AFFF, or Alcohol-Resistant AFFF)** — Apply via coarse water spray | For flammable liquid fires (Class B) — e.g., gasoline, ethanol, kerosene. Foam blankets the liquid and smothers the fire by excluding oxygen. |
| **3** | **Foam + Water (Coarse Spray)** — Use both agents together | For polar solvents (water-miscible) such as methanol, acetone. Pure foam dissolves; coarse water dilutes. |
| **4** | **Dry Chemical Agent (Powder)** — e.g., Monoammonium Phosphate, CO₂, BCF | For chemical fires where water is *absolutely contraindicated* (e.g., sodium fires, magnesium fires, white phosphorus). |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Critical Distinction Between 1 and Others:**  
> Numeral `1` (coarse water spray only) is for chemicals that **react violently with water** but where water is still the only practical extinguishing medium in a flood (e.g., calcium carbide releasing acetylene). Numeral `4` means **water is forbidden entirely.**

---

### Component 2 — The Letter(s) (P, R, S, T, W, X, Y, Z)

The letter specifies two combined tactical actions:

- **(A)** Whether the **spilled material should be CONTAINED (prevented from entering drains/waterways)** or **DILUTED (flushed away with copious water).**
- **(B)** The **level of personal protective equipment (PPE)** required by responders.

#### Sub-Table A: Personal Protection Codes

| Letter | Personal Protection Requirement |
| :---: | :--- |
| **P** | **Full Protective Clothing (Gas-Tight Chemical Suit)** + Breathing Apparatus (BA) |
| **R** | **Liquid-Tight Chemical Protective Clothing** + BA |
| **S** | **Normal Firefighter's Clothing (BA required, but standard kit)** |
| **T** | **Normal Firefighter's Clothing + BA (chemical splash goggles/face shield)** |
| **W** | **Normal Firefighter's Clothing + BA** |
| **X** | **Normal Firefighter's Clothing only (no BA — minor hazard)** |
| **Y** | **Normal Firefighter's Clothing + BA** |
| **Z** | **Normal Firefighter's Clothing only (low hazard)** |

#### Sub-Table B: Tactical Spill Action Codes

The same letters also encode the **dilute-or-contain** action:

| Action Code | Tactical Spill Response |
| :---: | :--- |
| **R** | **CONTAIN** — Prevent spillage from entering drains, sewers, or watercourses. Do NOT dilute. Dam up. |
| **S** | **DILUTE with copious water** — Flush spilled material away. Suitable for water-soluble, low-toxicity materials. |
| **T** | **DILUTE with copious water** — But substance is mildly toxic. |
| **W** | **DILUTE with copious water** — Substance is environmentally hazardous but low-toxicity to humans. |
| **X** | **CONTAIN** — Substance is highly toxic AND water-reactive. |
| **Y** | **CONTAIN** — Highly toxic. Do not dilute. May be incompatible with water. |
| **Z** | **CONTAIN** — Highly toxic, water-reactive, AND requires full gas-tight PPE. |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Mnemonic for Students:**  
> **R = Retain / Restrict (CONTAIN)**  
> **S, T, W = Splash, Top-up, Wash (DILUTE)**  
> **X, Y, Z = eXclude, seYal off, Zero-leakage (HARD CONTAIN)**  
> The progression P→Z represents a **decrease in PPE level** but an **increase in toxicity/confinement severity.**

---

### Component 3 — The Optional "E"

The letter **"E"** is **appended after the action letters** when there is a **significant risk to the surrounding public** from toxic vapors, thermal radiation, or explosion overpressure. It mandates:

- **Public EVACUATION** within the marked radius on the placard.
- **Cordoning off** of the area.
- **Deployment of breathing apparatus by all personnel**, even outside the immediate hot zone.

**Example:** `4YE` means — Use dry chemical powder; CONTAIN (highly toxic); **EVACUATE public**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol / Format | Value / Range | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Numeral (1) | $N_{1}$ | Coarse spray | Volumetric flow: 10–20 L/min per m² |
| Numeral (2) | $N_{2}$ | Foam AFFF | Application rate: 4–10 L/min·m² |
| Numeral (3) | $N_{3}$ | Foam + Coarse spray | Foam: 5 L/min·m²; Spray: 10 L/min·m² |
| Numeral (4) | $N_{4}$ | Dry powder | 1–2 kg/m² coverage |
| Evacuation Radius (HCDG) | $R_{evac}$ | 100 to 1500 | metres |
| Spill Containment Volume | $V_{contain}$ | $\geq 110\%$ of $V_{tank}$ | cubic metres (m³) |
| Spill Dilution Factor | $D_f$ | $\geq 100 : 1$ (water : chemical) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]  
> **No strict "formula" exists for HAZCHEM Code selection** — it is a *lookup-based* system tied to the **UN Number / Substance Identification**. However, the *logical decision tree* used to assign a code follows the IF–THEN rules in §2.3 below.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The HAZCHEM Code is deployed in:

1. **Road Tanker Placards** — Mandatory on tankers carrying ≥ 4500 L of hazardous substances (India) / > 500 kg (UK).
2. **Rail Tank Wagons** — International rail (RID) regulations.
3. **Fixed Storage Tanks** — Above-ground and underground bulk chemical storage.
4. **Marine IMO Placards** — Container ships and chemical tankers.
5. **Hospitals / Laboratories** — Small-quantity compressed-gas cylinders (e.g., chlorine, ammonia).

In a real engineering context, the **HAZCHEM Code is the FIRST thing a firefighter reads** upon arrival, before opening the Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS/SDS). It dictates the **first 60 seconds of tactical response**, which statistically determines whether casualties occur.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The HAZCHEM Assignment Decision Matrix (Exhaustive)

The following decision matrix illustrates the *exhaustive* step-by-step logic used by hazardous substances regulators to ASSIGN a HAZCHEM Code to a new chemical. This is the operational logic examiners love to test.

### **Step 1 — Identify the UN Number Class (1–9)**

The UN classification provides the foundational hazard category:

| UN Class | Hazard Type |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Explosives |
| 2 | Gases (compressed, liquefied, dissolved under pressure) |
| 3 | Flammable Liquids |
| 4 | Flammable Solids |
| 5 | Oxidizing Substances & Organic Peroxides |
| 6 | Toxic & Infectious Substances |
| 7 | Radioactive Material |
| 8 | Corrosive Substances |
| 9 | Miscellaneous Dangerous Goods |

### **Step 2 — Determine the Numeral (Firefighting Medium)**

This is governed by the **water-reactivity** of the substance and its **fire class**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numeral Selection Rule:} \quad N &= \mathbf{1} \;\; \text{if } \Delta H_{rxn} \text{(water)} \gg 0 \text{ and coarse spray safe} \\
N &= \mathbf{2} \;\; \text{if } \text{Flammable Liquid (Class 3) and immiscible with water} \\
N &= \mathbf{3} \;\; \text{if } \text{Flammable Liquid (Class 3) and miscible with water} \\
N &= \mathbf{4} \;\; \text{if } \text{Water is FORBIDDEN (e.g., alkali metals, white phosphorus)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3 — Determine the Action Letter (Contain vs. Dilute)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Letter} &= \mathbf{R} \;\; \text{if } \text{water-reactive AND toxic} \\
&= \mathbf{S} \;\; \text{if } \text{water-soluble AND low-toxicity} \\
&= \mathbf{T} \;\; \text{if } \text{water-soluble AND moderately toxic} \\
&= \mathbf{W} \;\; \text{if } \text{water-soluble AND environmental hazard only} \\
&= \mathbf{X} \;\; \text{if } \text{water-reactive AND highly toxic} \\
&= \mathbf{Y} \;\; \text{if } \text{highly toxic, do NOT dilute} \\
&= \mathbf{Z} \;\; \text{if } \text{highly toxic AND water-reactive AND requires gas-tight PPE}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4 — Determine PPE Level (P/R/S/T/W/X/Y/Z)**

This is **already encoded within the letter** selected above (Sub-Table A in §2.1).

### **Step 5 — Determine the "E" (Evacuation) Suffix**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Append } E &= \text{true if } LC_{50} < 200 \text{ ppm (vapor hazard) OR} \\
&\quad \text{explosive overpressure radius} > 100 \text{ m} \\
\text{Append } E &= \text{false otherwise}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Examples (Step-by-Step Decoding)

### **Example 1: Decode HAZCHEM `2RE`**

A road tanker is labeled `2RE` carrying UN 1170 (Ethanol).

**Step 1 — Decode the Numeral `2`:**
- Numeral 2 → **AFFF Foam** (coarse spray applied via foam branches).

**Step 2 — Decode the Letter `R`:**
- Letter R → **CONTAIN** the spillage (do NOT dilute with water).
- PPE → **Liquid-Tight Chemical Protective Clothing** + Breathing Apparatus.

**Step 3 — Decode the Suffix `E`:**
- Letter E → **EVACUATE** the public within the marked radius.

> **Operational Translation:**  
> "Fight fire with AFFF foam only. If ethanol spills, **dam it up** — do not wash it into drains because it is miscible with water and will contaminate watercourses. Wear liquid-tight chemical suit and BA. **Evacuate nearby residents** because ethanol vapor can form explosive clouds with air."

---

### **Example 2: Decode HAZCHEM `4WE`**

A tanker carries **Sodium Metal (UN 1428)** labeled `4WE`.

**Step 1 — Numeral `4`:**  
- Use **Dry Chemical Powder ONLY**. Water is absolutely forbidden (sodium + water → NaOH + H₂, which ignites).

**Step 2 — Letter `W`:**  
- **DILUTE** any residue with copious water (sodium's *reaction products* NaOH are water-soluble and dilute safely once the metal is consumed).
- PPE → Normal firefighter kit + BA.

**Step 3 — Suffix `E`:**  
- **EVACUATE** — Hydrogen gas generated from sodium-water reaction creates explosion risk.

---

### **Example 3: Decode HAZCHEM `3Y`**

A tanker carries **Anhydrous Hydrogen Fluoride (UN 1052)** labeled `3Y`.

**Step 1 — Numeral `3`:**  
- Foam + coarse water spray (HF is miscible with water).

**Step 2 — Letter `Y`:**  
- **CONTAIN** — HF is so toxic that dilution is unsafe (creates a large contaminated runoff).
- PPE → Normal firefighter clothing + BA.

**Step 3 — No `E`:**  
- (HF is toxic but its vapor is dense and stays low; cordon radius handled via UN class rules.)

> **Why "Y" instead of "P"?**  
> HF has a "contact-burn" toxicity rather than inhalation toxicity, so liquid-tight suits are adequate — no gas-tight suit required. Hence `Y` (less PPE than `R` or `P`).

---

### **Example 4: HAZCHEM `1Z` (Chlorine Cylinders, UN 1017)**

**Step 1 — Numeral `1`:**  
- **Coarse water spray only** — fine jets would aerosolize Cl₂ gas and spread contamination.

**Step 2 — Letter `Z`:**  
- **CONTAIN** — Chlorine is water-reactive (forms HCl + HOCl) and is highly toxic.
- PPE → Normal firefighter clothing only (chlorine has a strong odor warning at sub-lethal levels).

**Step 3 — No `E`:**  
- (Smaller quantity typical.)

---

## 3.3 Detailed Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Substance | UN No. | HAZCHEM | Numeral (Medium) | Letter (Action) | PPE | Suffix |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Gasoline (Petrol) | 1203 | `2YE` | Foam | Contain | Normal + BA | Evacuate |
| Ethanol | 1170 | `2RE` | Foam | Contain (water-miscible toxic) | Liquid-tight + BA | Evacuate |
| Methanol | 1230 | `2WE` | Foam | Dilute (miscible) | Normal + BA | Evacuate |
| Acetone | 1090 | `2YE` | Foam | Contain (volatile) | Normal + BA | Evacuate |
| Sulfuric Acid | 1830 | `2P` | Foam | Contain | Gas-tight + BA | — |
| Sodium Metal | 1428 | `4WE` | Dry powder | Dilute residue | Normal + BA | Evacuate |
| Hydrogen Fluoride | 1052 | `3Y` | Foam + spray | Contain | Normal + BA | — |
| Chlorine (gas) | 1017 | `1Z` | Coarse spray | Contain | Normal | — |
| LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) | 1075 | `2YE` | Foam | Contain | Normal + BA | Evacuate |
| Ammonia (anhydrous) | 1005 | `2XE` | Foam | Contain | Normal + BA | Evacuate |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Pattern Recognition for Exams:**  
> - **Water-miscible flammable liquids** → `2W` or `3Y`  
> - **Water-immiscible flammable liquids** → `2R` or `2Y`  
> - **Alkali metals** → `4W` or `4X`  
> - **Compressed toxic gases** → `1X` or `1Z`  
> - **Strong acids** → `2P`  

---

## 3.4 Quantitative Comparison — Containment vs. Dilution

The choice of **R (contain)** vs. **S/T/W (dilute)** is governed by a critical mass-balance threshold:

$$
\boxed{
\text{IF} \;\; C_{\text{spill}} \cdot V_{\text{spill}} > C_{\text{safe}} \cdot V_{\text{dilution available}}
\;\; \text{THEN} \;\; \text{CONTAIN (R/X/Y/Z)}
}
$$

$$
\boxed{
\text{IF} \;\; C_{\text{spill}} \cdot V_{\text{spill}} \leq C_{\text{safe}} \cdot V_{\text{dilution available}}
\;\; \text{THEN} \;\; \text{DILUTE (S/T/W)}
}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{spill}}$ = concentration of hazardous substance in spilled material (mass/volume).
- $V_{\text{spill}}$ = volume spilled (m³).
- $C_{\text{safe}}$ = maximum safe concentration in receiving water (typically mg/L).
- $V_{\text{dilution available}}$ = volume of dilution water available (m³).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why Dilute vs. Contain Matters:**  
> For instance, **methanol (HAZCHEM 2WE)** is miscible with water, and methanol's aquatic toxicity threshold ($LC_{50, fish} \approx 100 \text{ mg/L}$) is high enough that dilution is feasible. In contrast, **chlorine gas (HAZCHEM 1Z)** is toxic at < 1 ppm — any amount that escapes would vastly exceed safe levels in the receiving water, so **containment is mandatory**.
