# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAZCHEM Code Structural Anatomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HAZCHEM Code: e.g. 2RE] --> B[Component 1: Numeral]
    A --> C[Component 2: Letter]
    A --> D[Component 3: Suffix E optional]
    B --> B1[Numeral 1: Coarse Water Spray Only]
    B --> B2[Numeral 2: AFFF Foam]
    B --> B3[Numeral 3: Foam plus Coarse Spray]
    B --> B4[Numeral 4: Dry Chemical Powder]
    C --> C1[P: Gas-Tight PPE plus BA]
    C --> C2[R: Liquid-Tight PPE plus BA]
    C --> C3[S: Normal Kit plus BA]
    C --> C4[T: Normal Kit plus BA plus Goggles]
    C --> C5[W: Normal Kit plus BA]
    C --> C6[X: Normal Kit No BA]
    C --> C7[Y: Normal Kit plus BA]
    C --> C8[Z: Normal Kit No BA Low Hazard]
    D --> E[Action Logic]
    E --> E1[R: CONTAIN spillage]
    E --> E2[S or T or W: DILUTE with water]
    E --> E3[X or Y or Z: HARD CONTAIN]
    D --> F[Suffix E]
    F --> F1[E present: EVACUATE public]
    F --> F2[E absent: No evacuation]
```

## 4.2 HAZCHEM Emergency Response Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Emergency Scene Arrival]) --> ID[Read HAZCHEM Code on Tanker]
    ID --> Num{Identify Numeral}
    Num --> N1[1: Coarse Water Spray Only]
    Num --> N2[2: AFFF Foam]
    Num --> N3[3: Foam plus Coarse Spray]
    Num --> N4[4: Dry Powder No Water]
    N1 --> Ltr{Identify Letter}
    N2 --> Ltr
    N3 --> Ltr
    N4 --> Ltr
    Ltr --> R[CONTAIN Spillage]
    Ltr --> STW[DILUTE Spillage with Water]
    Ltr --> XYZ[HARD CONTAIN No Water]
    R --> PPE{Select PPE based on Letter}
    STW --> PPE
    XYZ --> PPE
    PPE --> Evac{E Suffix Present?}
    Evac --> Yes[EVACUATE Public within Radius]
    Evac --> No[No Evacuation Cordoning Only]
    Yes --> Act[Execute Tactical Action]
    No --> Act
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    XZ_PPE --> LowPPE[Lowest Protection]

    R_PPE -.-> Note2[Liquid-Tight Chemical Suit]
    STW_PPE -.-> Note3[Standard Firefighter Kit plus BA]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Chemical reacts violently with water?}
    Q1 -->|Yes but coarse spray tolerable| One[Numeral 1: Coarse Water Spray]
    Q1 -->|No water reaction but flammable liquid immiscible| Two[Numeral 2: AFFF Foam]
    Q1 -->|Flammable liquid miscible with water| Three[Numeral 3: Foam plus Coarse Spray]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Define HAZCHEM Code. Explain the significance of the letter "E" in the code.**

#### Model Answer:

**HAZCHEM Code Definition (2 marks):**  
HAZCHEM (Hazardous Chemical) Code is a standardized emergency response marking system displayed on transport vehicles, storage tanks, and shipping containers carrying dangerous goods. It consists of a numeral (1–4) indicating the firefighting medium, one or two letters (P, R, S, T, W, X, Y, Z) indicating personal protective equipment level and whether to contain or dilute spillage, and an optional suffix "E."

**Significance of "E" (1 mark):**  
The letter "E" when appended at the end of the HAZCHEM Code (e.g., `2YE`) is a warning to emergency responders to **EVACUATE** the surrounding public within the marked radius on the placard. It indicates that the substance poses a significant risk to civilians from toxic vapors, thermal radiation, or explosion overpressure, requiring public evacuation in addition to on-site containment.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]

**Distinguish between HAZCHEM numerals "2" and "3" with suitable examples.**

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Numeral 2 | Numeral 3 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Firefighting Medium** | AFFF Foam (Alcohol-resistant foam as needed) applied via coarse spray | Foam + Coarse Water Spray (both agents simultaneously) |
| **Application** | For flammable liquids IMMISCIBLE with water (e.g., gasoline, kerosene, diesel — UN 1203, 1223) | For flammable liquids MISCIBLE with water (e.g., methanol UN 1230, ethanol UN 1170, acetone UN 1090) |
| **Reason** | Pure foam would be washed away by water if jet-applied; coarse spray carries foam to surface | Water alone dissolves polar solvents; combined application maximizes fire suppression |

**Example:** Gasoline (UN 1203) → HAZCHEM `2YE`; Methanol (UN 1230) → HAZCHEM `2WE`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question B (Choice A).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the structural components of a HAZCHEM Code in detail with a neat labeled diagram. Describe the significance of each numeral (1, 2, 3, 4). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A road tanker carries UN 1170 (Ethanol) with HAZCHEM Code `2RE`. Decode this code step-by-step and describe the appropriate firefighting and spill-control response. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

The **HAZCHEM Code** consists of three structural components:

1. **Numeral (1–4):** Specifies the firefighting medium.
2. **Letter(s) (P, R, S, T, W, X, Y, Z):** Specifies PPE level and whether to contain or dilute spillage.
3. **Suffix "E" (optional):** Indicates public evacuation requirement.

**Significance of Each Numeral:**

- **Numeral 1 — Coarse Water Spray Only:** Used for water-reactive chemicals where fine water jets would cause violent reactions. Apply large droplets to minimize surface disturbance. Example: Sodium metal spills (residual, after initial dry powder).
- **Numeral 2 — AFFF Foam:** Applied via coarse water spray for flammable liquid fires (immiscible with water). Foam blankets the surface, excludes oxygen, and smothers the fire. Example: Gasoline (UN 1203), Kerosene (UN 1223).
- **Numeral 3 — Foam + Coarse Water Spray:** Used for polar solvents miscible with water where pure foam is ineffective. Both agents applied simultaneously. Example: Methanol (UN 1230), Acetone (UN 1090).
- **Numeral 4 — Dry Chemical Powder:** Used when water is absolutely contraindicated. Examples include sodium fires, magnesium fires, white phosphorus, and certain metal hydrides.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of notes — Mermaid structural anatomy block diagram.)*

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Given:** UN 1170 (Ethanol), HAZCHEM `2RE`.

**Step 1 — Identify the substance:**  
Ethanol is a flammable liquid, miscible with water, with UN Class 3.

**Step 2 — Decode Numeral `2`:**  
Numeral 2 → Use **AFFF Foam** as firefighting medium. Apply foam via coarse water spray branches. Direct jet application of water is to be avoided.

**Step 3 — Decode Letter `R`:**  
- Action: **CONTAIN** the spillage. Do NOT dilute. Dam up the spill area and prevent entry into drains, sewers, or watercourses.
- PPE: **Liquid-Tight Chemical Protective Clothing** + Breathing Apparatus (BA).

**Step 4 — Decode Suffix `E`:**  
The "E" indicates **public EVACUATION** within the marked radius on the placard (typically ≥ 100 m). Cordoning off is mandatory.

**Step 5 — Integrated Tactical Response:**

1. **Approach:** Approach from upwind. Don liquid-tight chemical suit + BA.
2. **Fire Suppression:** Apply AFFF foam via coarse spray to extinguish any pool fires.
3. **Spill Control:** Construct containment dikes using sand, earth, or chemical-resistant absorbent booms. Do NOT flush ethanol into drains because:
   - Ethanol is miscible with water → contamination spreads rapidly.
   - Aquatic toxicity is moderate but cumulative oxygen demand in receiving waters is high.
4. **Evacuation:** Evacuate all non-essential personnel within ≥ 100 m radius.
5. **Ventilation:** Vapors are heavier than air → ventilate low-lying areas.
6. **Disposal:** Recover contained ethanol using explosion-proof pumps; transfer to a labelled, sealed container for licensed disposal.

---

### **Question B (Choice B — Alternative to Question A).** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a flowchart, explain the decision logic used to ASSIGN a HAZCHEM Code to a chemical substance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast HAZCHEM Code `2RE` (Ethanol), `2WE` (Methanol), and `4WE` (Sodium Metal). Prepare a tabular analysis of their firefighting media, PPE, spill action, and evacuation requirements. Why is water strictly forbidden for sodium metal but permitted (in diluted form) for methanol and ethanol? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

The HAZCHEM Code assignment decision logic follows a hierarchical five-step procedure:

**Step 1 — UN Number Classification:**  
Identify the UN Class (1–9) of the substance to establish the foundational hazard type.

**Step 2 — Numeral Selection (Firefighting Medium):**  
Evaluate water-reactivity and fire class:
- If water reacts violently → Numeral 4 (dry powder).
- If water reaction tolerable with coarse spray → Numeral 1.
- If flammable liquid immiscible with water → Numeral 2.
- If flammable liquid miscible with water → Numeral 3.

**Step 3 — Letter Selection (PPE + Action):**  
Cross-reference toxicity, water-reactivity, and aquatic hazard:
- Highly toxic + water-reactive → X, Y, or Z.
- Toxic + immiscible with water → R.
- Miscible + low toxicity → W or T.
- Miscible + low aquatic hazard → S.

**Step 4 — Suffix "E" Decision:**  
If $LC_{50} < 200$ ppm (inhalation hazard) OR explosion overpressure radius > 100 m, append "E."

**Step 5 — Validation:**  
Cross-check with regulatory database (e.g., Hazchem Scale issued by **Chemical Industries Association** or **AEP (Australian Emergency Procedures)**).

**Flowchart:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of notes — Emergency Response Decision Flowchart.)*

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Comparative Tabular Analysis:**

| Parameter | HAZCHEM `2RE` (Ethanol) | HAZCHEM `2WE` (Methanol) | HAZCHEM `4WE` (Sodium Metal) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **UN Number** | 1170 | 1230 | 1428 |
| **UN Class** | 3 (Flammable Liquid) | 3 (Flammable Liquid) | 4.3 (Water-Reactive Solid) |
| **Firefighting Medium** | AFFF Foam (coarse spray) | AFFF Foam (coarse spray) | **Dry Chemical Powder ONLY** |
| **Spill Action** | **CONTAIN** (R) — prevent entry into drains | **DILUTE** with water (W) — flush away | **DILUTE residue with water** (W) after metal consumed |
| **PPE Level** | Liquid-Tight Chemical Suit + BA | Normal Firefighter Kit + BA | Normal Firefighter Kit + BA |
| **Evacuation (E)** | YES — public evacuation required | YES — public evacuation required | YES — public evacuation required |
| **Water Miscibility** | Fully miscible | Fully miscible | **REACTS VIOLENTLY** with water |

**Why is water forbidden for sodium but permitted (in diluted form) for ethanol and methanol?**

Sodium metal reacts violently with water according to the following reaction:

$$
2 \, \text{Na}_{(s)} + 2 \, \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \longrightarrow 2 \, \text{NaOH}_{(aq)} + \text{H}_{2(g)} \uparrow + \Delta H \; (\text{highly exothermic})
$$

The reaction is **highly exothermic**, generates **flammable hydrogen gas**, and the heat released is sufficient to **ignite the hydrogen**, producing a **fireball or explosion**. Therefore, **water is absolutely contraindicated** for active sodium metal — hence Numeral 4 (dry powder).

In contrast, ethanol and methanol undergo **simple dissolution** in water without violent reaction. Their fire suppression using foam (Numeral 2) is preferred, but if the *liquid* (not burning) spills, water can be used to dilute (W) for methanol because its aquatic toxicity, though real, can be managed at 100:1 dilution ratios. For ethanol, dilution is avoided (R) because ethanol's miscibility allows it to spread rapidly through water systems before dilution is complete.

**Conclusion:** The difference arises from the fundamental **chemical reactivity** of the substance with water — sodium undergoes a **redox reaction** that releases hydrogen and heat, while alcohols undergo only a **physical dissolution** (no chemical reaction).

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Confusing Numeral 1 with Numeral 4:** Students often write that Numeral 1 means "no water" — **INCORRECT**. Numeral 1 means **coarse water spray** (large droplets). Numeral 4 means **no water at all**. This is a 2-mark penalty.  
> 2. **Misinterpreting "R" as "Rescue":** "R" stands for **RETAIN/CONTAIN**, not Rescue. Many students confuse it.  
> 3. **Forgetting the "E" suffix:** When asked to decode `2RE` or `4WE`, students often describe only the numeral and letter, omitting the **evacuation requirement** marked by "E." Lose 1–2 marks.  
> 4. **Conflating HAZCHEM with NFPA 704:** HAZCHEM is an **action code**; NFPA 704 is a **hazard diamond**. They serve different purposes. Examiners penalize confusion.  
> 5. **PPE hierarchy mistakes:** The PPE scale runs **P (highest) → R → S/T/W/Y → X/Z (lowest).** Mixing this order costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for HAZCHEM Code:**

- ☐ **HAZCHEM = Hazardous Chemical Emergency Code** — Action-oriented emergency marking system.
- ☐ **Three components:** Numeral (1–4) + Letter(s) (P–Z) + Optional "E".
- ☐ **Numeral 1:** Coarse water spray only (water-reactive but tolerable).
- ☐ **Numeral 2:** AFFF Foam — for **immiscible** flammable liquids (gasoline, kerosene).
- ☐ **Numeral 3:** Foam + Coarse Spray — for **miscible** flammable liquids (methanol, ethanol, acetone).
- ☐ **Numeral 4:** Dry Chemical Powder — water **absolutely forbidden** (sodium, magnesium, white phosphorus).
- ☐ **Letter R, X, Y, Z:** CONTAIN the spillage (do NOT dilute).
- ☐ **Letter S, T, W:** DILUTE the spillage with copious water.
- ☐ **PPE Hierarchy:** P > R > S/T/W/Y > X/Z (decreasing protection level).
- ☐ **Suffix "E":** EVACUATE the public within the placard radius.
- ☐ **Origin:** United Kingdom (1972), Chemical Industries Association.
- ☐ **Adopted in:** Australia, New Zealand, Malaysia, Singapore, India (partially).
- ☐ **Compatible with:** UN Recommendations on Transport of Dangerous Goods (UNRTDG).
- ☐ **Common Examples:**  
  - Gasoline (UN 1203) → `2YE`  
  - Ethanol (UN 1170) → `2RE`  
  - Methanol (UN 1230) → `2WE`  
  - Sodium (UN 1428) → `4WE`  
  - Chlorine (UN 1017) → `1Z`  
  - Sulfuric Acid (UN 1830) → `2P`
- ☐ **Decision Tree:** UN Class → Numeral → Letter → Suffix.
- ☐ **Why Sodium + Water = Forbidden:** $2\text{Na} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2 \uparrow$ (explosive).
- ☐ **HAZCHEM ≠ NFPA 704:** Action Code vs. Hazard Diamond.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TREM Card
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TREM Card

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **TREM Card (Transport Emergency Card)** is a standardized, vehicle-mounted or transport-document emergency information card issued in accordance with **CEFIC (Conseil Européen de l'Industrie Chimique / European Chemical Industry Council)** guidelines, designed to provide first responders (firefighters, police, paramedics, civil defense) with immediate, life-saving information about the **hazards, protective equipment, and emergency response actions** required when a transport vehicle carrying dangerous goods is involved in an accident (spill, fire, explosion, or leak).

It functions as the **"first 30-minute survival manual"** for emergency personnel arriving at the scene of a chemical transport incident. The card is printed on **fluorescent yellow-orange paper** (high visibility) and is placed in a **clear plastic envelope** on the driver's door, on the side of the tank/vehicle, and a copy accompanies the transport documents (consignment note / e-Way bill).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Medical Alert Bracelet" for a Chemical Tanker:**
> Just as a diabetic person wears a medical alert bracelet telling paramedics *"I have diabetes — give me sugar, not insulin"*, a chemical tanker carrying **Acrylonitrile** carries a TREM Card telling the firefighter:
> *"Highly flammable + Highly toxic — Use SCBA + chemical-resistant suit — Evacuate 100 m downwind — DO NOT spray water directly on the tank."*
>
> Without the TREM Card, the firefighter is **blind to the chemical**; with it, the first responder can act in **under 60 seconds** instead of wasting precious time identifying the substance and its hazards.

### 1.3 Core Identifying Features (Mandatory Physical Traits)

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| Paper Colour | Fluorescent **yellow-orange** (RAL 2003 equivalent) |
| Standard Size | **A4 landscape** orientation (297 mm × 210 mm) |
| Issuing Body | **CEFIC** (Europe) / National Chemical Associations (India: **ICC – Indian Chemical Council**) |
| Format Language | Multilingual (typically English + local language + UN pictograms) |
| Placement | Driver's cab door pocket + Tank side placard holder + Consignment note attachment |
| Number of Copies | Minimum **3 copies** (driver + vehicle + consignment) |

### 1.4 Governing Standards & Legal Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Key References (Verifiable):**
> - **CEFIC TREM Card Guidelines** (current edition)
> - **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods** (UN Orange Book, 21st revised edition)
> - **ADR (European Agreement concerning the International Carriage of Dangerous Goods by Road)**
> - **RID** (Rail), **IMDG Code** (Sea), **IATA DGR** (Air)
> - **Indian Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989** — Rule 137 (Hazardous goods transport)
> - **ISO 7000 / ISO 7010** — Graphical symbols for safety signs

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of TREM Card — Why Does It Exist?

The **golden hour** in chemical emergency response dictates that the first 15–30 minutes determine whether the incident becomes a **contained event** or a **major industrial disaster** (Bhopal 1984, Seveso 1976, Flixborough 1974). The TREM Card addresses three critical questions in this window:

1. **WHAT** is leaking? (Substance identification + UN Number)
2. **HOW DANGEROUS** is it? (Hazard classification + toxicity + flammability)
3. **WHAT TO DO** in the first 30 minutes? (Evacuation, PPE, firefighting media, first aid)

### 2.2 Structural Anatomy of a TREM Card — The 9 Logical Blocks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A standard TREM Card contains **9 structured information blocks**. Examiners frequently ask students to list these blocks — memorize them in the order shown below.

**Block 1 — Substance Identification:**
- Trade name, chemical name, IUPAC name
- UN Number (4-digit)
- Hazchem / Kemler / HIN code
- CAS Number

**Block 2 — Hazard Classification:**
- Primary hazard (e.g., toxic, flammable, corrosive, oxidizer)
- Secondary hazards
- UN Class labels (Class 1 to Class 9 pictograms)

**Block 3 — Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) for Responders:**
- Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (**SCBA**)
- Chemical-resistant suit (Level A / B / C as per **OSHA HAZWOPER 29 CFR 1910.120**)
- Gloves (material specified: nitrile, butyl, Viton)
- Boots, goggles, face shield

**Block 4 — Immediate Public Safety Measures:**
- Evacuation distance (**Primary isolation radius**)
- Downwind evacuation distance (weather-dependent)
- Shelter-in-place vs. evacuate decision

**Block 5 — Firefighting Measures:**
- Suitable extinguishing media (water spray, foam, dry chemical, CO₂)
- **DO NOT USE** water jets (for water-reactive chemicals)
- Special firefighting procedures (cool tanks with water spray from maximum distance)

**Block 6 — Spill or Leak Procedures:**
- Containment (dikes, absorbent materials — vermiculite, sand, activated carbon)
- Neutralization chemicals (for acids/alkalis)
- Decontamination

**Block 7 — First Aid Measures:**
- Inhalation: Move to fresh air, administer oxygen
- Skin contact: Flush with water for ≥ 15 minutes
- Eye contact: Rinse for ≥ 15 minutes
- Ingestion: Do NOT induce vomiting (for hydrocarbons/alkalis)

**Block 8 — Environmental Precautions:**
- Prevent entry into drains, surface water, groundwater
- Report to environmental authorities (Pollution Control Board in India)

**Block 9 — Emergency Contact Information:**
- Manufacturer 24-hr emergency phone
- Local fire brigade / disaster management authority
- Poison control center
- Transport company emergency desk

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> TREM Card content is **qualitative** (information-based), but quantitative emergency response uses the following KTU-tested formulas for **evacuation distance, fire-water flow, and spill volume estimation**. These often appear in numerical sub-parts of 14-mark questions.

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Isolation Distance** (D) | $D = k \cdot \sqrt{M \cdot V_{vapour}}$ | metres | Small spill radius, first 30 min |
| **Downwind Evacuation Distance** | $D_{down} = D \cdot (1 + 2.5 \cdot \log_{10} V_{wind})$ | metres | Day-time / night-time differential |
| **Spill Volume (cylindrical tank)** | $V_{spill} = \pi r^{2} \cdot L \cdot \phi$ | m³ | Hole-area fraction method |
| **Fire Water Application Rate** | $Q_{w} = K \cdot A_{tank}$ (NFPA 15) | L/min | Cooling of adjacent tanks |
| **Vapour Cloud Dispersion (Pasquill-Gifford)** | $C(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_{y} \sigma_{z}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^{2}}{2\sigma_{y}^{2}} - \dfrac{H^{2}}{2\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)$ | ppm | Ground-level concentration |
| **ERPG-2 Threshold Dose** | $D_{tox} = C \cdot t$ (Haber's Law) | ppm·min | Acute toxicity reference |

> **Boundary / Standard Values:**
> - **Wind speed (Pasquill Stability D — neutral):** $u = 4$ to $6$ m/s
> - **Initial isolation distance (small spill, toxic gas):** **30 m to 100 m** (ERG 2024 default)
> - **TREM Card validity / revision cycle:** **Every 3 years** or upon regulatory update

> **Notation Guide:**
> - $M$ = mass of released substance (kg), $V_{vapour}$ = vapour volume fraction
> - $k$ = empirical dispersion constant (typically $k = 0.4$ to $0.8$)
> - $\sigma_{y}, \sigma_{z}$ = horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients (m)
> - $u$ = mean wind speed at release height (m/s), $H$ = effective release height (m)
> - $Q$ = continuous emission rate (g/s), $A_{tank}$ = tank wetted surface area (m²)
> - $\phi$ = hole area fraction (typically $0.05$ for minor, $0.5$ for catastrophic)
> - $D_{tox}$ = toxic load, $C$ = concentration (ppm), $t$ = exposure time (min)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Initial Isolation Distance from a TREM Card Perspective

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical 7-mark sub-part):**
> A road tanker carrying **2,000 kg of liquid chlorine** (UN 1017) overturns and a small leak develops. The wind speed is **5 m/s** under Pasquill class D stability. Using the CEFIC TREM Card evacuation model, calculate the **initial isolation distance** and the **downwind evacuation distance**.

**Given Data:**
- Released mass, $M = 2{,}000$ kg
- Vapour volume fraction (chlorine is 100% vaporizable under ambient), $V_{vapour} = 1.0$
- Empirical constant, $k = 0.6$ (for toxic gases, Pasquill D)
- Wind speed, $u = 5$ m/s
- Daytime dispersion factor, $V_{wind} = 5$ m/s

#### Step 1 — Write the governing equation
The initial isolation distance $D$ is governed by the empirical CEFIC formula:

$$
D \;=\; k \cdot \sqrt{M \cdot V_{vapour}}
$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values
Substitute $k = 0.6$, $M = 2{,}000$ kg, $V_{vapour} = 1.0$:

$$
D \;=\; 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{2000 \cdot 1.0}
$$

$$
D \;=\; 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{2000}
$$

#### Step 3 — Evaluate the square-root
Compute the square-root of 2000:

$$
\sqrt{2000} \;=\; \sqrt{2 \cdot 10^{3}} \;=\; \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1000} \;\approx\; 1.4142 \cdot 31.6228
$$

$$
\sqrt{2000} \;\approx\; 44.7214 \text{ m}
$$

#### Step 4 — Final multiplication
Multiply by the empirical constant $k = 0.6$:

$$
D \;=\; 0.6 \times 44.7214
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D \;\approx\; 26.83 \text{ m} \, \approx \, 30 \text{ m (rounded to standard TREM Card value)}\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** All persons within **30 m** of the leak must be **immediately isolated** (no entry except with full PPE).

#### Step 5 — Downwind Evacuation Distance
The downwind distance $D_{down}$ accounts for vapour cloud drift:

$$
D_{down} \;=\; D \cdot \left(1 + 2.5 \cdot \log_{10} V_{wind}\right)
$$

Substitute $D = 30$ m, $V_{wind} = 5$ m/s:

$$
D_{down} \;=\; 30 \cdot \left(1 + 2.5 \cdot \log_{10} 5\right)
$$

$$
\log_{10} 5 \;\approx\; 0.6990
$$

$$
2.5 \cdot 0.6990 \;\approx\; 1.7475
$$

$$
D_{down} \;=\; 30 \cdot (1 + 1.7475)
$$

$$
D_{down} \;=\; 30 \cdot 2.7475
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D_{down} \;\approx\; 82.4 \text{ m} \approx 100 \text{ m (rounded to standard TREM Card value)}\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Persons in the downwind sector must **evacuate up to 100 m** from the spill. This value is **directly written on the TREM Card** as the "Public Safety Distance."

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: TREM Card vs. Similar Emergency Documents

| Feature | TREM Card | Hazchem Code (UK/India) | MSDS / SDS (16-section) | ERG (Emergency Response Guidebook) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary User | Driver + First Responder | Fire Brigade | Industrial worker | First Responder (NA, used globally) |
| Information Depth | Medium (1 page) | Code only (compact) | Very high (16 sections, 8–12 pages) | High (264 pages guidebook) |
| Colour | Yellow-orange fluorescent | Plate on vehicle | White (paper) | Orange cover book |
| Legal Mandate | CEFIC guideline (mandatory in EU) | Statutory (Indian MV Rules) | OSHA / REACH mandatory | Voluntary in India |
| Transport Specific | **Yes** (road/rail focus) | Yes (road tankers) | No (general chemical) | Yes (multi-modal) |
| Numeric Quantities | Evacuation distances, UN No. | Emergency Action Code (EAC) | All physical/chemical/toxicological data | IDLH, ERPG values |
| Languages | Multilingual | Numeric/alphanumeric | Single (translated) | English (primary) |
| Cost | Free (chemical association) | Plate cost (₹500–1500) | Free from supplier | Free (PDF) |

> **Key takeaway for KTU exam:** TREM Card is **transport-specific** and **driver-focused**; SDS is **handler-focused**; ERG is **firefighter-focused**. Mixing these up is a common 3-mark error.

### 3.3 Numerical Example: Spill Volume from a Tanker (Worked Out)

**Problem:** A horizontal cylindrical tanker (radius 1.5 m, length 8 m) is punctured. The hole area fraction is $\phi = 0.05$. Find the spill volume.

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$
V_{spill} \;=\; \pi r^{2} \cdot L \cdot \phi
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
V_{spill} \;=\; \pi \cdot (1.5)^{2} \cdot 8 \cdot 0.05
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
V_{spill} \;=\; 3.1416 \cdot 2.25 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.05
$$

$$
V_{spill} \;\approx\; 2.83 \text{ m}^{3} \;\approx\; 2{,}830 \text{ litres}
$$

**Step 4 — TREM Card implication:**
Since the spill exceeds **1,000 L** of a hazardous substance, the **"Major Spill"** procedure of the TREM Card applies: full evacuation + foam blanket + diking with sand.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Information Flow on a TREM Card

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TREM Card Activated on Accident Site] --> B[Step 1: Identify Substance]
    B --> C[Step 2: Read UN Number and Class Label]
    C --> D[Step 3: Identify Primary and Secondary Hazards]
    D --> E[Step 4: Don Appropriate PPE]
    E --> F[Step 5: Establish Initial Isolation Distance]
    F --> G{Is Spill Contained?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Step 6: Apply Containment and Neutralization]
    G -- No --> I[Step 7: Trigger Downwind Evacuation]
    H --> J[Step 8: Notify Emergency Contacts]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step 9: Handover to Fire Brigade with TREM Card Copy]
    K --> L[Step 10: First Aid to Victims per TREM Block 7]
    L --> M[Step 11: Environmental Reporting per TREM Block 8]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of a TREM Card

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Block 1: Substance ID]
        A1[Trade Name]
        A2[UN Number]
        A3[CAS Number]
        A4[Hazchem Code]
    end
    subgraph B[Block 2: Hazard Classification]
        B1[UN Class Pictogram]
        B2[Primary Hazard]
        B3[Secondary Hazard]
    end
    subgraph C[Block 3: PPE]
        C1[SCBA]
        C2[Chemical Suit]
        C3[Gloves and Boots]
    end
    subgraph D[Block 4: Public Safety]
        D1[Isolation Distance]
        D2[Evacuation Distance]
    end
    subgraph E[Block 5: Fire Response]
        E1[Suitable Extinguishing Media]
        E2[Do Not Use Media]
    end
    subgraph F[Block 6: Spill Response]
        F1[Containment]
        F2[Neutralization]
    end
    subgraph G[Block 7: First Aid]
        G1[Inhalation]
        G2[Skin Contact]
        G3[Eye Contact]
    end
    subgraph H[Block 8: Environmental]
        H1[Drainage Prevention]
        H2[Pollution Control Board]
    end
    subgraph I[Block 9: Emergency Contacts]
        I1[Manufacturer 24h Line]
        I2[Fire Brigade]
        I3[Poison Control]
    end
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Driver Action Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PreTrip: TREM Card Issued by Supplier
    PreTrip --> InTransit: Mounted on Driver Door and Tank Side
    InTransit --> Accident: Collision or Leak Detected
    Accident --> Assess: Driver Picks Up TREM Card
    Assess --> CallEmergency: Dial Emergency Contact Block 9
    CallEmergency --> IsolateArea: Follow Block 4 Distances
    IsolateArea --> FirstAid: Apply Block 7 If Victims
    FirstAid --> Handover: Hand TREM Card to Responder
    Handover --> [*]: Incident Closed
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — PPE Selection from TREM Card

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify UN Class from TREM Block 2] --> Q1{Is Substance Toxic or Unknown?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> LevelA[Level A: Fully Encapsulated SCBA + Chemical Suit]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is Substance Corrosive or Skin Harmful?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> LevelB[Level B: SCBA + Splash Suit]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is Substance Flammable Liquid Only?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> LevelC[Level C: Air Purifier + Flame Retardant Suit]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is Substance Solid or Low Hazard?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> LevelD[Level D: Standard Work Uniform]
    Q4 -- No --> Expert[Call Hazmat Expert via Block 9]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define a TREM Card. List any four pieces of information it contains.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **TREM (Transport Emergency) Card** is a CEFIC-standardized, fluorescent yellow-orange information card placed on a transport vehicle carrying dangerous goods, providing first responders with immediate instructions on hazards, protective equipment, and emergency actions in the event of a transport incident. **(1 mark for definition)**
>
> **Four pieces of information (any 4, ½ mark each = 2 marks):**
> 1. Substance identification — UN Number, trade name, CAS Number
> 2. Hazard classification — UN Class pictograms and primary/secondary hazards
> 3. Initial isolation and downwind evacuation distances
> 4. Suitable extinguishing media and prohibited media (e.g., water jets)
> 5. First aid measures (inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion)
> 6. 24-hour emergency contact telephone numbers

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between a TREM Card and a Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS/SDS).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | TREM Card (1½ marks) | MSDS / SDS (1½ marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Quick reference for **transport emergency** response | Comprehensive **handling, storage, and toxicology** data |
| Format | Single A4 yellow-orange card | 16-section document, 8–12 pages |
| User | Driver + Fire Brigade + First Responder | Plant operator, chemist, safety officer |
| Content depth | Action-oriented, qualitative | Quantitative (LD50, LC50, PEL, TLV) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — OR — Question B (14 Marks)**

Choose **ONE** of the following questions.

---

#### **Question A: TREM Card Detailed Analysis** [14 Marks]

**(a) Explain the structure and contents of a TREM Card in detail with reference to CEFIC guidelines. List all 9 standard information blocks.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A TREM Card issued by **CEFIC (European Chemical Industry Council)** is a transport emergency information card designed to assist first responders during the first 30 minutes of a chemical transport incident. It is printed on **fluorescent yellow-orange paper** in **A4 landscape** format and is placed in three locations: driver's cab, vehicle exterior, and consignment note.

**The 9 standard information blocks are:** **(1 mark for listing all 9 = ~0.5 mark per block, plus explanation marks)**

1. **Substance Identification:** Trade name, chemical name, UN Number, CAS Number, Hazchem/Kemler code. *Tells the responder EXACTLY what is in the tanker.* **(0.5 mark)**

2. **Hazard Classification:** UN Class pictograms (Class 1 Explosives to Class 9 Miscellaneous), primary hazard, secondary hazards. *For example, acrylonitrile is Class 3 (flammable) + Class 6.1 (toxic).* **(0.5 mark)**

3. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Specifies the level of protection (A, B, C, or D as per OSHA HAZWOPER) — SCBA, chemical-resistant suit (material type), gloves, boots, face shield. *Prevents responder incapacitation.* **(1 mark)**

4. **Immediate Public Safety Measures:** Primary isolation distance (typically 30–100 m) and downwind evacuation distance (calculated using Pasquill-Gifford dispersion). *Protects the general public.* **(1 mark)**

5. **Firefighting Measures:** Suitable extinguishing media (alcohol-resistant foam, dry chemical, CO₂) and **DO NOT USE** warnings (e.g., water jets for water-reactive chemicals like sodium, magnesium phosphide). *Prevents secondary explosion.* **(1 mark)**

6. **Spill or Leak Procedures:** Containment methods (sand dikes, vermiculite, activated carbon), neutralization (soda ash for acids, dilute acetic acid for alkalis), and decontamination. **(0.5 mark)**

7. **First Aid Measures:** Specific to each route of exposure — inhalation (move to fresh air, oxygen), skin (flush ≥15 min), eye (rinse ≥15 min), ingestion (do NOT induce vomiting for hydrocarbons). *Saved time = saved life.* **(1 mark)**

8. **Environmental Precautions:** Prevent entry into drains, surface water, groundwater; notify the **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** in India. *Prevents ecological disaster.* **(0.5 mark)**

9. **Emergency Contact Information:** Manufacturer 24-hour emergency line, local fire brigade (dial **101** in India), poison control center (**1066** in India — AIIMS), transport company desk. **(0.5 mark)**

> **Mark Distribution Note:** A clear block-wise table with all 9 blocks fetches full 7 marks. Vague one-line answers lose 2–3 marks.

---

**(b) A road tanker carrying 2,000 kg of chlorine (UN 1017, 100% vaporizable) develops a small leak. Using the CEFIC TREM Card evacuation model, calculate the initial isolation distance and the downwind evacuation distance for a wind speed of 5 m/s under Pasquill class D stability. State the standard TREM Card values you would write on the card.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Mass released, $M = 2{,}000$ kg
- Vapour fraction, $V_{vapour} = 1.0$ (chlorine fully vaporizes)
- Empirical constant for toxic gas, Pasquill D: $k = 0.6$
- Wind speed, $u = 5$ m/s
- Daytime dispersion factor, $V_{wind} = 5$ m/s

**Step 1 — Initial Isolation Distance Formula:** **(1 mark)**

$$
D \;=\; k \cdot \sqrt{M \cdot V_{vapour}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitution:** **(1 mark)**

$$
D \;=\; 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{2000 \cdot 1.0} \;=\; 0.6 \cdot 44.72
$$

**Step 3 — Final value:** **(0.5 mark)**

$$
D \;\approx\; 26.83 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — TREM Card rounded value:** **(0.5 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\,D \;\approx\; 30 \text{ m (Initial Isolation Distance)}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Downwind Evacuation Formula:** **(1 mark)**

$$
D_{down} \;=\; D \cdot \left(1 + 2.5 \cdot \log_{10} V_{wind}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitution and evaluation:** **(1 mark)**

$$
D_{down} \;=\; 30 \cdot \left(1 + 2.5 \cdot \log_{10} 5\right) \;=\; 30 \cdot \left(1 + 2.5 \cdot 0.699\right) \;=\; 30 \cdot 2.7475
$$

**Step 7 — Final TREM Card rounded value:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\,D_{down} \;\approx\; 82.4 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 100 \text{ m (Downwind Evacuation Distance)}\,}
$$

> **Answer Summary to be written on TREM Card Block 4:**
> - Initial isolation radius: **30 m** (no entry without full PPE)
> - Downwind evacuation: **100 m** (all unprotected persons must leave)

---

#### **Question B: Comparative Emergency Response** [14 Marks]

**(a) Compare TREM Card with Hazchem Code and Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG) in a tabular form. Highlight the unique advantages of TREM Card for in-transit chemical emergencies.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Parameter | TREM Card (1.5 marks) | Hazchem Code (1.5 marks) | ERG (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Name | Transport Emergency Card | Hazardous Chemical Code | Emergency Response Guidebook |
| Issuing Body | CEFIC (Europe) / ICC (India) | UK Health & Safety Executive | US DOT / Transport Canada |
| Format | A4 fluorescent yellow card | Alphanumeric plate on vehicle | Orange-covered book, 264 pages |
| Information | 9 blocks, action-oriented | 2–4 character code (e.g., 2WE) | IDLH, ERPG, isolation tables |
| User | Driver + first responder | Fire brigade | Fire brigade / hazmat team |
| Legal Mandate | Strong (EU) / Voluntary (India) | Statutory in UK & India | Voluntary reference |
| Cost | Free | Plate cost | Free PDF |
| Transport-Mode Specific | Road + Rail | Road only | Multi-modal (Road/Rail/Sea/Air) |
| Numeric Evacuation Distances | Yes (pre-calculated) | No | Yes (extensive tables) |

**Unique advantages of TREM Card for in-transit emergencies (2 marks):**
1. **Speed:** Single-page A4 card can be read in **< 30 seconds** by a stressed responder, while the ERG requires time to navigate 264 pages. **(1 mark)**
2. **Driver-friendly language:** Multilingual, pictogram-supported, no technical jargon — usable by a driver with limited chemical knowledge. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Multimodal integration:** TREM Card accompanies the **consignment note** and is recognized by road, rail, and customs authorities, ensuring uniform information across the entire logistics chain. **(0.5 mark)**

---

**(b) A horizontal cylindrical tanker of radius 1.5 m and length 8 m is punctured. The hole area fraction is 0.05. Calculate the spill volume and recommend the appropriate TREM Card action level.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Spill volume formula:** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
V_{spill} \;=\; \pi r^{2} \cdot L \cdot \phi
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:** **(1 mark)**

$$
V_{spill} \;=\; \pi \cdot (1.5)^{2} \cdot 8 \cdot 0.05
$$

**Step 3 — Compute intermediate terms:** **(1 mark)**

$$
V_{spill} \;=\; 3.1416 \cdot 2.25 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.05
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:** **(0.5 mark)**

$$
V_{spill} \;\approx\; 2.83 \text{ m}^{3} \;\approx\; 2{,}830 \text{ litres}
$$

**Step 5 — TREM Card action level recommendation:** **(2 marks)**
> Since the spill volume **2,830 L > 1,000 L** (TREM Card's "Major Spill" threshold), the following **Block 6 actions** apply:
> 1. **Full evacuation** within the downwind distance calculated in Block 4.
> 2. **Foam blanket** application if the substance is flammable.
> 3. **Sand/vermiculite diking** to prevent entry into storm drains.
> 4. **Notification** of SPCB and fire brigade via Block 9 contacts.
> 5. **Do NOT use** water jets if the chemical is water-reactive (read Block 5).

**Step 6 — Final answer with units:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{spill} \;\approx\; 2.83 \text{ m}^{3} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Major Spill Protocol of TREM Card Applies}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing "TREM Card = Safety Data Sheet". ❌ They are **different** documents. TREM = transport; SDS = handling. (–2 marks)
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **round** calculated distances to standard TREM Card values (30 m, 100 m, 300 m, 1000 m). Always show the rounding step. (–1 mark)
> - **Mistake 3:** Writing evacuation distance formula as $D_{down} = D \times V_{wind}$ (linear) instead of **logarithmic** with factor 2.5. (–2 marks)
> - **Mistake 4:** Not mentioning the **fluorescent yellow-orange** colour — a CEFIC mandatory specification. (–1 mark)
> - **Mistake 5:** Confusing TREM Card with **HAZCHEM plate** (which is a small metal plate on the tanker, not a paper card). (–2 marks)
> - **Mistake 6:** Writing only UN Class name without the **UN Number** in Block 1. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision — Memorize These 15 Points Before the Exam:**

- **Definition:** TREM Card = **Transport Emergency Card** issued by **CEFIC**, fluorescent yellow-orange, A4 landscape, placed on driver's door + tank side + consignment note.
- **Three copies minimum:** Driver's cab + Vehicle exterior + Consignment note.
- **Nine standard blocks:** Substance ID → Hazard Class → PPE → Public Safety → Fire → Spill → First Aid → Environment → Emergency Contacts.
- **UN Number is mandatory** in Block 1 (4-digit code, e.g., UN 1017 for chlorine, UN 1090 for acetone, UN 1789 for HCl).
- **Initial Isolation Distance formula:** $D = k \cdot \sqrt{M \cdot V_{vapour}}$, with $k = 0.6$ for toxic gases (Pasquill D).
- **Downwind Evacuation formula:** $D_{down} = D \cdot (1 + 2.5 \cdot \log_{10} V_{wind})$.
- **Standard rounded TREM values:** 30 m / 100 m / 300 m / 1000 m (no fractional distances on the printed card).
- **PPE Levels (OSHA HAZWOPER 29 CFR 1910.120):** A = maximum (SCBA + fully encapsulated); B = SCBA + splash suit; C = air-purifier + chemical suit; D = work uniform.
- **Major Spill threshold:** > 1,000 L → full TREM Card Block 6 protocol.
- **DO NOT USE water jets** for water-reactive chemicals: sodium, potassium, calcium carbide, magnesium phosphide, alkali metals, acid + water reactions.
- **Indian emergency numbers:** Fire = **101**, Ambulance = **108**, Poison Control = **1066** (AIIMS New Delhi), Disaster Management = **1078**.
- **TREM Card is transport-specific**, SDS is handling-specific, ERG is multi-modal response — **don't mix them up**.
- **Issuing body in India:** **ICC (Indian Chemical Council)** is the Indian counterpart of CEFIC for TREM Card issuance.
- **Statutory backing:** Indian Motor Vehicles Rules 1989, Rule 137 — hazardous goods transport mandates TREM Card equivalent documentation.
- **Revision cycle:** TREM Card content must be reviewed every **3 years** or upon any regulatory change in transport classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relief Systems and Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relief Systems and Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Relief Systems — Formal Definition

A **Relief System** is a passive (or active) engineered safety installation that automatically protects a pressurized process vessel, piping, or storage tank from exceeding its **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** by deliberately discharging excess fluids (vapors, gases, liquids, or two-phase mixtures) when the process variable (pressure, temperature, or flow) crosses a predetermined safe limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** Relief systems are mandatory "layers of protection" mandated under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** and **ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code Section VIII**. The relief device is the **last line of defense** in the hierarchy of controls (Inherent Safety → Engineering Controls → Active Protective Systems → Passive Protective Systems → Emergency Response).

### 1.2 Detectors — Formal Definition

A **Detector** (or **Sensor / Transducer**) is a field-mounted instrument that continuously monitors a process variable or environmental condition (toxic gas, flammable vapor, flame, smoke, heat, or radiation) and generates an electrical signal — analog (4–20 mA) or digital (Modbus/HART) — that is routed to a logic solver (DCS/PLC/SIS) for alarm annunciation, automatic shutdown (ESD), or fire suppression activation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Per KTU 2024 Module-3 scope:** Detectors are studied as the *front-end perception* of the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** architecture defined by **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**. Each detector is assigned a **Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1, 2, or 3)** based on its **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Intuition

Imagine a household **pressure cooker** on a gas stove. Inside the lid sits a small spring-loaded metallic pin — the **weight valve (pressure regulator)** — and a rubber gasket (**rupture disc equivalent**). If you forget to turn off the stove:

| Domestic Object | Process Plant Equivalent | Safety Function |
|---|---|---|
| Spring pin on lid | Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) | Re-seats after overpressure |
| Rubber safety gasket | Rupture Disc (RD) | One-shot burst relief |
| Lid lock pin | Safety Interlock + Detector | Prevents opening under pressure |
| Pressure gauge | Pressure Transmitter (PT) | Detection of high pressure |
| Whistle on lid | Audible Horn + Annunciator | Alarm/notification |

Now scale this same idea to a **500 m³ naphtha storage sphere** or a **hydrocracker reactor**: the relief device must be **sized for the worst credible overpressure scenario** (fire, runaway reaction, blocked outlet, utility failure).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy for Detectors:** Think of a smoke detector in your hostel room. It does not *fight* the fire — it just *senses* it. The chain is: **Detector → Logic Solver → Final Element (deluge valve / ESD valve)**. In process plants, the same chain protects multi-million dollar assets and human life.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards

| Symbol | Definition | Typical Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **C (API)** | Coefficient for gas/vapor relief | **315 (English)** / **5.14 (SI)** |
| **K_d** | Coefficient of discharge (rated orifice) | **0.975 (R orifice)** |
| **K_b** | Backpressure correction factor | 0.5 – 1.0 (sub-critical flow) |
| **K_c** | Combination factor (RD + PRV) | **0.9 (rupture disc upstream)** |
| **K_v** | Viscosity correction factor | < 1.0 for viscous liquids |
| **R** | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** = **10.73 psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R)** |
| **Z** | Compressibility factor | 0.85 – 1.05 (typical) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pressure Relief Devices

### 2.1.1 By Re-Seating Capability

1. **Reclosing (Re-seating) Devices**
   - **Spring-Loaded Pressure Relief Valves (PRV)** — use a spring opposed by process pressure; opens at set pressure, closes when pressure falls below blowdown.
   - **Balanced Bellows PRV** — isolates backpressure from the spring chamber; used for superimposed backpressure service.
   - **Pilot-Operated Safety Valves (POSV)** — uses a small pilot to modulate main disc; high capacity in compact size.

2. **Non-Reclosing Devices**
   - **Rupture Disc (RD)** — a thin metal/graphite membrane that bursts at a predetermined pressure. No reseating; must be replaced.

3. **Combination Devices** (Rupture Disc + PRV in series) — protects PRV from corrosive media and provides tight isolation during normal operation.

### 2.1.2 By Service Media

| Device Type | Service | Reset Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Safety Valve** | Steam, air, gases | Spring, sudden opening |
| **Relief Valve** | Liquids | Spring, proportional opening |
| **Safety-Relief Valve** | Multi-phase (gas+liquid) | Spring, intermediate opening |
| **Conservative Vent** | Low-pressure storage tanks | Weight-loaded, near-atmospheric |
| **Vacuum Relief Valve** | Prevents tank implosion | Weight or spring-loaded |

---

## 2.2 Operating Principles — The "Why & How"

### 2.2.1 Spring-Loaded PRV — Force Balance

The valve pops open when the upward force from process pressure exceeds the downward spring force:

$$F_{net} = P_{process} \cdot A_{disc} - F_{spring} - W_{disc}$$

- When **P_process > Set Pressure** → net force positive → **stem lifts** → fluid discharges.
- When **P_process < Blowdown Pressure** → net force negative → **disc re-seats**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Blowdown** is the difference between set pressure and reseat pressure, expressed as a percentage (typically **3–7%** for gases, **5–10%** for liquids). Lower blowdown = greater wear but less product loss.

### 2.2.2 Rupture Disc — Burst Mechanism

A rupture disc is a thin pre-scored metal membrane rated for a specific **burst pressure (P_burst)** at a given temperature. It operates on:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t}$$

- When hoop stress exceeds **ultimate tensile strength** of disc material → **instantaneous burst**.
- No gradual opening; full bore release.

### 2.2.3 Sizing Rationale — "Design vs. Operating"

A relief device is sized for the **worst-case relief load (W)**, calculated from credible scenarios:

- **External fire exposure** (most common sizing basis for atmospheric & pressurized storage).
- **Blocked outlet** (pump deadhead, closed block valve).
- **Utility failure** (cooling water, instrument air).
- **Runaway chemical reaction** (exothermic polymerization, decomposition).
- **Thermal expansion** (liquid-filled piping blocked-in).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The set pressure of the relief device **must never exceed MAWP** of the vessel. Per **ASME Sec. VIII UG-125**, accumulation is limited to:
> - **10% MAWP** for fire exposure case (non-fire = **3% MAWP**).
> - Set pressure ≤ **MAWP** for primary relief.

---

## 2.3 Detector Theory — Operating Principles

### 2.3.1 Gas Detectors

| Technology | Detection Principle | Target Gases | Response Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Catalytic Bead (Pellistor)** | Catalytic oxidation → heat → resistance change | Combustible gases (LEL 0–100%) | 20–60 s |
| **Electrochemical (EC)** | Gas reacts at electrode → current proportional to concentration | Toxic (H₂S, CO, Cl₂, NH₃) | 15–60 s |
| **Infrared (NDIR)** | Gas absorbs specific IR wavelength | Hydrocarbons, CO₂, SF₆ | < 5 s |
| **Photoionization Detector (PID)** | UV lamp ionizes VOCs → current | VOCs (benzene, toluene) | < 3 s |
| **Metal Oxide Semiconductor (MOS)** | Gas adsorption changes resistance | H₂S, VOCs | 30–120 s |
| **Acoustic / Ultrasonic** | Detects gas leak sound | Compressed gas leaks | < 1 s |

### 2.3.2 Flame Detectors

- **Ultraviolet (UV) Detectors** — Detect UV radiation (185–260 nm) emitted by flames. Fails on smoky fires; false alarms from arc welding, lightning.
- **Infrared (IR) Detectors** — Detect CO₂ resonance band at **4.3 μm**. Immune to sunlight but blinded by hot surfaces (> 200°C).
- **UV/IR Dual** — AND-logic reduces false alarms; widely used in offshore platforms.
- **IR/IR (Triple IR)** — Multi-spectral IR for maximum discrimination; preferred for **turbine enclosures, FPSOs**.

### 2.3.3 Smoke & Heat Detectors

- **Ionization Smoke** — Detects combustion products via ionization current change (fast for flaming fires).
- **Photoelectric Smoke** — Detects smoke obscuration via light scattering (best for smoldering fires).
- **Rate-of-Rise Heat** — Triggers on rapid ΔT (e.g., **> 8.3°C/min**).
- **Fixed Temperature Heat** — Triggers at preset T (e.g., **57°C, 68°C, 93°C**).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### A. API 520 Sizing for Vapor / Gas Relief

$$A = \frac{W \cdot \sqrt{T \cdot Z / M}}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot K_b}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Required effective discharge area | in² (English) or mm² (SI) |
| **W** | Required mass flow (relief load) | lb/hr (Eng) or kg/hr (SI) |
| **T** | Relieving temperature | °R or K |
| **Z** | Compressibility factor at inlet | dimensionless |
| **M** | Molecular weight | kg/kmol |
| **P₁** | Upstream relieving pressure (= 1.10 × set pressure for fire) | psia or kPa (abs) |
| **C** | Gas coefficient | 315 (Eng), 5.14 (SI) |
| **K_d** | Coefficient of discharge | 0.975 |
| **K_c** | Combination correction | 0.9 (with RD) |
| **K_b** | Backpressure correction | 0.5 – 1.0 |

### B. API 520 Sizing for Liquid Relief

$$A = \frac{Q}{C_v \cdot K_d \cdot K_v \cdot K_b} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{G}{P_1 - P_2}}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Q** | Volumetric relief rate | GPM (Eng) or m³/hr (SI) |
| **G** | Specific gravity at flowing T | dimensionless |
| **P₁ − P₂** | Differential pressure | psid or kPa |
| **K_v** | Viscosity correction | < 1.0 |

### C. API 521 Fire Heat Input (Wetted Surface)

$$Q = 21{,}000 \cdot F \cdot A_{w}^{0.82}$$

Where:
- **Q** = Total heat absorption rate (BTU/hr)
- **F** = Environment factor (**1.0** for bare vessel, **0.5** for insulated, **0.3** for earth-covered)
- **A_w** = Total wetted surface area below 25 ft elevation (ft²)

### D. Vapor Relief Rate from Fire Input

$$W = \frac{Q}{\lambda}$$

Where **λ** = latent heat of vaporization (BTU/lb or kJ/kg).

### E. Sonic / Choked Flow Criterion

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} \leq \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\gamma / (\gamma - 1)}$$

If satisfied → **choked (sonic) flow** at valve outlet → use **isentropic choked-flow formula**.

### F. Detector Coverage Formula (Area Coverage for Flame Detectors)

$$\text{Coverage Area} = \pi \cdot r^2$$

Where **r** is the **detection range** (vendor-specified, typically 5–50 m for IR/UV detectors with 90° cone).

### G. Gas Detector Coverage (Per IEC 60079-29-2)

$$\text{Number of Detectors} = \frac{\text{Area (m²)}}{r^2_{detection}}$$

Typical detection radius for **point gas detector = 5 m** (open area), reduced by barriers/obstructions.

### H. Smoke Detector Spacing (Per NFPA 72)

$$\text{Max Spacing} = 9.1 \text{ m} \quad \text{(smooth ceiling)}$$

Reduced to **6.1 m** for ceilings with deep beams or joists.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Oil & Gas Refining** | PSV sizing for hydrocracker reactors (API RP-521 fire case). |
| **LNG Terminals** | Low-temperature PSVs on LNG storage tanks; ESDV + flame detector integration. |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Rupture disc sizing for batch reactors (DIERS two-phase methodology). |
| **Petrochemical Storage** | Conservation vents on atmospheric tanks (API 2000). |
| **Offshore Platforms** | Triple-IR flame detectors on drilling modules; H₂S gas detectors in mud-handling areas. |
| **Power Plants** | Boiler safety valves (ASME Section I); high-temperature heat detectors. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of API 520 Vapor Relief Sizing Equation

We begin from the **ideal gas orifice equation for choked flow** through a sharp-edged orifice.

### Step 1 — Mass Flow Through an Orifice (Steady State)

The mass flow rate through an orifice is:

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_1 \cdot P_1 \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma + 1)/\gamma}\right]}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Sonic (Choked) Condition

When **P₂/P₁ ≤ [2/(γ+1)]^(γ/(γ−1))**, the bracketed term maximizes to:

$$\left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{1/\gamma} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\gamma}{\gamma + 1} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{1/\gamma - 1}}$$

For air (**γ = 1.4**), this simplifies to:

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot M}{R \cdot T_1}} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{(\gamma + 1)/[2(\gamma-1)]}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Constants and Rearrange for Engineering Use

Solving for **A** (the required discharge area):

$$A = \frac{\dot{m}}{C_d \cdot P_1 \cdot K_b \cdot K_c} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T \cdot Z}{M}} \cdot \frac{1}{C_{constant}}$$

Where **C_constant** is the API-defined coefficient:
- **C = 315** for English (with W in lb/hr).
- **C = 5.14** for SI (with W in kg/hr).

### Step 4 — Apply Unit-Specific Numerical Coefficient

For English units:

$$A \;[in^2] = \frac{W \;[lb/hr] \cdot \sqrt{T \;[°R] \cdot Z / M}}{315 \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \;[psia]}$$

For SI units:

$$A \;[mm^2] = \frac{W \;[kg/hr] \cdot \sqrt{T \;[K] \cdot Z / M}}{5.14 \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \;[kPa_{abs}]}$$

### Step 5 — Select Standard Orifice Size

API 520 / ASME Section VIII defines **standard orifice letters** with specific effective areas:

| Orifice | Area (in²) | Area (mm²) |
|---|---|---|
| D | 0.110 | 71 |
| E | 0.196 | 126 |
| F | 0.307 | 198 |
| G | 0.503 | 324 |
| H | 0.785 | 506 |
| J | 1.287 | 830 |
| K | 1.838 | 1185 |
| L | 2.853 | 1840 |
| M | 3.600 | 2322 |
| N | 4.340 | 2800 |
| P | 6.380 | 4115 |
| Q | 11.05 | 7129 |
| R | 16.00 | 10322 |

Select the orifice **A_actual ≥ A_required**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Fire-Case Relief Sizing

> **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]:**
> An atmospheric naphtha storage tank has **wetted surface area A_w = 2500 ft²**, and is **bare (F = 1.0)**. Naphtha properties: **Latent heat λ = 150 BTU/lb**, **Molecular weight M = 100 kg/kmol**, **Set pressure = 1.0 psig**, **Relieving T = 120°F**. Compute:
> (a) Required fire heat input (Q)
> (b) Required relief rate (W)
> (c) Required orifice area using API 520 gas method (Z = 0.95, γ = 1.2, K_b = 1.0, K_c = 1.0).

### Part (a) — Fire Heat Input (API 521)

$$Q = 21{,}000 \cdot F \cdot A_{w}^{0.82}$$

Substituting:

$$Q = 21{,}000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot (2500)^{0.82}$$

Compute exponent:

$$(2500)^{0.82} = e^{0.82 \cdot \ln(2500)} = e^{0.82 \cdot 7.8240} = e^{6.4157} \approx 611.32$$

Therefore:

$$Q = 21{,}000 \cdot 611.32 \approx 1.284 \times 10^{7} \; BTU/hr$$

### Part (b) — Required Relief Rate

$$W = \frac{Q}{\lambda} = \frac{1.284 \times 10^{7}}{150} \approx 85{,}600 \; lb/hr$$

### Part (c) — Orifice Area (API 520)

Relieving pressure (fire case, 10% accumulation):

$$P_1 = 1.10 \cdot (14.7 + 1.0) = 1.10 \cdot 15.7 = 17.27 \; psia$$

Relieving temperature (absolute):

$$T = 120 + 460 = 580 \; °R$$

Substituting into API 520 formula:

$$A = \frac{W \cdot \sqrt{T \cdot Z / M}}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1}$$

Numerical evaluation:

$$A = \frac{85{,}600 \cdot \sqrt{580 \cdot 0.95 / 100}}{315 \cdot 0.975 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 17.27}$$

Inside the square root:

$$\sqrt{580 \cdot 0.95 / 100} = \sqrt{5.51} \approx 2.347$$

Numerator:

$$85{,}600 \cdot 2.347 \approx 200{,}915$$

Denominator:

$$315 \cdot 0.975 \cdot 17.27 = 5{,}304.5$$

Result:

$$A = \frac{200{,}915}{5{,}304.5} \approx 37.88 \; in^2$$

### Step 6 — Select Standard Orifice

From the API standard orifice table:
- **Orifice P** = 6.380 in² → insufficient (need 37.88).
- **Orifice Q** = 11.05 in² → insufficient.
- **Orifice R** = 16.00 in² → insufficient.

For high-capacity naphtha service, **multiple valves** are used. Required number:

$$n = \left\lceil \frac{37.88}{16.00} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.37 \right\rceil = 3 \; \text{valves}$$

So **three (3) R-orifice PRVs** in parallel are required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** Designers typically choose **two PRVs at 100% capacity each** (N+0 configuration per API 520) for redundancy — one operating, one spare/standby. Three is unusual unless N+1 redundancy is mandated.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Liquid Relief Sizing

> **Problem Statement:** A blocked-outlet liquid propane pump can deliver a flow rate **Q = 200 GPM**. The relief valve set pressure = **300 psig**, vessel MAWP = **350 psig**. Specific gravity **G = 0.51**. Compute the required orifice area (assume K_v = 0.95, K_d = 0.975, no backpressure).

### Step 1 — Determine Relieving Pressure

$$P_1 = 300 + 14.7 = 314.7 \; psia$$
$$P_2 = \text{atmospheric} = 14.7 \; psia$$

### Step 2 — Differential Pressure

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 314.7 - 14.7 = 300 \; psi$$

### Step 3 — Apply API 520 Liquid Sizing Equation

$$A = \frac{Q}{C_v \cdot K_d \cdot K_v \cdot K_b} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{G}{\Delta P}}$$

For liquid water, **C_v = 1.0** (correction factor for water as reference fluid). For propane, C_v ≈ 1.0 at moderate viscosity.

Substituting:

$$A = \frac{200}{1.0 \cdot 0.975 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 1.0} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.51}{300}}$$

Coefficient:

$$\frac{200}{0.9263} \approx 215.9$$

Square root term:

$$\sqrt{0.51 / 300} = \sqrt{0.0017} \approx 0.0412$$

Final:

$$A = 215.9 \cdot 0.0412 \approx 8.89 \; in^2$$

### Step 4 — Select Orifice

- **Orifice P** = 6.38 in² → insufficient.
- **Orifice Q** = 11.05 in² → sufficient ✓

Select **Orifice Q** with margin.

---

## 3.4 Detector Selection — Quantitative Logic Matrix

| Detector | Best For | Worst For | Min Cross-Sensitivity | SIL Achievable | MTBF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catalytic Bead | LEL combustible gases | Inert / non-flammable | Sulfur poisoning | SIL 2 | 5 yr |
| Electrochemical | H₂S, CO, NH₃, Cl₂ | Combustible hydrocarbons | Cross to NO₂ | SIL 2 | 2–3 yr |
| NDIR | Hydrocarbons, CO₂ | Hydrogen, Ammonia | Minimal | SIL 2 / 3 | 10 yr |
| PID | BTEX, VOCs | Non-ionizable gases | Humidity effect | SIL 2 | 2 yr |
| UV Flame | Hydrogen, Methanol fires | Smoky fires, arc welding | Sunlight | SIL 2 | 5 yr |
| IR Flame | Hydrocarbon fires | Hydrogen fires, hot surfaces | Sunlight | SIL 2 | 5 yr |
| UV/IR Dual | Offshore, refineries | Rare false alarms | Sun + welding | SIL 2 / 3 | 7 yr |
| Triple IR | Turbine enclosures | Hydrogen-only | None | SIL 3 | 10 yr |

---

## 3.5 Sizing for Two-Phase Flow (DIERS Methodology) — Conceptual Outline

For **runaway reaction relief**, the fluid may discharge as a **two-phase froth** rather than pure vapor. The **Design Institute for Emergency Relief Systems (DIERS)** developed an integral energy balance:

$$\frac{dm_{total}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{out} - \dot{m}_{in}$$

Coupled with energy balance:

$$\frac{dU}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{rxn} + \dot{Q}_{fire} - \dot{m}_{out} \cdot (h_{out} - h_{in})$$

The required vent area is solved numerically using:

$$\text{Time to maximum rate} \rightarrow A = f(\text{vent rate}, \text{vessel volume}, \text{mixture quality})$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 requires **conceptual familiarity** with DIERS — you are NOT required to solve two-phase flow equations manually in exams. Focus on the **vapor-only API 520 method** as the primary quantitative tool.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    nodeA --> nodeC["BLOCKED OUTLET OR FIRE EVENT"]

```

```mermaid
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeB["USE API 520 LIQUID EQUATION\nA = Q DIVIDED BY Cv Kd Kv Kb\nTIMES sqrt G divided by DP"]
```
