# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Material Factor" (MF) as used in the Dow Fire and Explosion Index methodology. How is it determined?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Material Factor (MF)** is a numerical measure (1 to 40) of the inherent energy release potential of the most hazardous material in a process unit, derived from the **NFPA 704M flammability rating (N_H)** and **reactivity rating (N_R)**. **(1 mark)**

It is determined by: **(1)**
- Looking up the standard Dow MF table using N_H and N_R values
- For dusts/fibers, using the **combustibility-volatility chart** instead

For example, acetone (N_H=3, N_R=0) has MF = 16, while a non-flammable inert material has MF = 1. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**List the six categories of General Process Hazards (GPH) used in the Dow F&EI calculation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The six GPH categories are: **(3 marks — 0.5 each)**
1. Exothermic chemical reactions
2. Endothermic processes
3. Material handling and transfer
4. Enclosed or indoor process units
5. Access of emergency response
6. Drainage and spill control

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the **step-by-step procedure** for calculating the **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)** for a chemical process unit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Process Unit** — The unit is a single, logically grouped piece of equipment (e.g., a reactor with its associated piping, pumps, and instrumentation). **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2: Determine Material Factor (MF)** — Identify the most hazardous material in the unit. Look up its NFPA 704 ratings (N_H and N_R) and obtain the Material Factor from the Dow MF table. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Evaluate General Process Hazards (GPH)** — Assess each of the 6 GPH categories and sum the applicable penalty factors. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Evaluate Special Process Hazards (SPH)** — Assess each of the 12 SPH categories and sum the applicable penalty factors. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5: Calculate Process Unit Hazard Factor** —
$$F_3 = (1 + GPH) \times (1 + SPH) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6: Calculate F&EI** —
$$F\&EI = MF \times F_3 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 7: Determine Damage Radius** — Read from the Dow Hazard Area Classification chart based on the F&EI value. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 8: Classify Hazard Degree** — Categorize as Light (1-60), Moderate (61-96), Heavy (97-127), or Severe (128-158). **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 9: Estimate Loss** — Apply the Damage Factor and calculate Base Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD) in USD. **(0.5 mark)**

---

**(b)** A process unit handles **benzene** with the following conditions:
- NFPA ratings: N_H = 3, N_R = 0
- GPH penalties applicable: Exothermic reaction = 0.50, Material handling = 0.40
- SPH penalties applicable: Operation in flammable range = 0.80, Quantity penalty = 0.70, Corrosion = 0.20

Calculate the **F&EI**, classify the hazard degree, and determine the approximate damage radius. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Material Factor** 
From NFPA table: N_H = 3, N_R = 0 → **MF = 16** **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Total GPH**
$$\text{GPH} = 0.50 + 0.40 = 0.90 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3: Total SPH**
$$\text{SPH} = 0.80 + 0.70 + 0.20 = 1.70 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4: Process Unit Hazard Factor**
$$F_3 = (1 + 0.90)(1 + 1.70) = 1.90 \times 2.70 = 5.13 \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 5: F&EI**
$$F\&EI = MF \times F_3 = 16 \times 5.13 = 82.08 \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 6: Classification**
Since 61 ≤ 82.08 ≤ 96, the hazard is **MODERATE**. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 7: Damage Radius**
From the Dow chart, for F&EI ≈ 82, **R_d ≈ 13 m** (approx). **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Dow Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)** methodology for assessing toxic release hazards. Include all relevant equations and standard assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Dow CEI (Toxicity Index)** quantifies the relative acute health hazard from accidental release of toxic chemicals. **(0.5 mark)**

**Standard Assumptions:** **(0.5 mark)**
- Wind speed: u = 5 m/s
- Atmospheric stability: Pasquill class D (neutral)
- Ground-level release (H = 0, worst-case)
- Release time: 600 s (10 minutes)

**Step 1: Release Scenario Selection** — Choose a worst-case release (e.g., catastrophic vessel rupture or 1-inch line break). **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2: Calculate Flash Fraction** —
$$f_{flash} = \frac{C_p(T_s - T_b)}{H_{vap}} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Airborne Fraction** —
$$A = f_{flash} + f_{vapor} \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Release Rate** —
$$Q = \frac{A \times M_{release}}{600 \text{ s}} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5: Identify Toxicity Endpoint** — Use ERPG-2 or AEGL-2 value. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 6: Calculate Toxicity Index** —
$$TI = T \times Q \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 7: Determine Toxic Radius** — Use Gaussian Plume dispersion:
$$C = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Iterate to find $R_t$ where $C = T$. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 8: Classify and Document** — Compare TI with industry benchmarks and recommend mitigations. **(0.5 mark)**

---

**(b)** A storage tank contains **2000 kg of liquid ammonia** at 25°C, 1 atm. Due to a 1-inch line rupture, the entire inventory is released over 10 minutes. 

Given:
- $M_{NH_3} = 17$ g/mol
- $T_b = -33.34°C = 239.81 K$
- $C_p = 1.06$ cal/g·K
- $H_{vap} = 327$ cal/g
- ERPG-2 for NH₃ = 200 ppm
- Wind speed: 5 m/s

Calculate the **Toxicity Index (TI)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Flash Fraction** **(1.5 marks)**
$$f_{flash} = \frac{1.06 \times (298.15 - 239.81)}{327} = \frac{1.06 \times 58.34}{327} = \frac{61.84}{327} = 0.189$$

**Step 2: Assume Vapor Fraction (10% vapor space, all vaporized)** **(0.5 mark)**
$$f_{vapor} = 0.10$$

**Step 3: Airborne Fraction** **(0.5 mark)**
$$A = 0.189 + 0.10 = 0.289$$

**Step 4: Airborne Mass** **(0.5 mark)**
$$M_{air} = 0.289 \times 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ g} = 578{,}000 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5: Release Rate** **(1 mark)**
$$Q = \frac{578{,}000}{600} = 963.3 \text{ g/s} = 963{,}300 \text{ mg/s}$$

**Step 6: Convert ERPG-2 to mg/m³** **(1 mark)**
$$T = 200 \text{ ppm} = \frac{200 \times 17}{24.45} = 139.06 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

(Using 1 ppm = M/24.45 mg/m³ at 25°C)

**Step 7: Calculate TI** **(1 mark)**
$$TI = 139.06 \times 963{,}300 \times 10^{-6} = 139.06 \times 0.9633 = 133.95$$

$$\boxed{TI \approx 134}$$

**Step 8: Interpretation** **(0.5 mark)**
A TI of 134 indicates a **High** hazard — significant toxic release radius requiring emergency planning and containment systems.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 
> **Trap 1: MF Lookup Errors** — Students frequently default to MF = 1 for all inerts, or fail to use the **combustibility-volatility chart** for dusts. KTU examiners *specifically test* this. (Lose up to 2 marks)
> 
> **Trap 2: GPH/SPH Multiplicative Confusion** — The penalties are **SUMMED** within each category, then applied **MULTIPLICATIVELY** between categories:
> - $F_3 = (1 + \sum \text{GPH})(1 + \sum \text{SPH})$
> - NOT $F_3 = 1 + \sum(\text{GPH} + \text{SPH})$
> - Lose 2 marks if confused
> 
> **Trap 3: Unit Inconsistency in TI** — ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion is **MUST**:
> - $\text{mg/m}^3 = \text{ppm} \times \frac{M}{24.45}$ at 25°C
> - Skipping this loses 1–2 marks
> 
> **Trap 4: Default Values** — Examiners expect you to *state* the assumptions (u = 5 m/s, t = 600 s, H = 0, Stability D). Not stating them = 0.5 mark penalty per missing value.
> 
> **Trap 5: Confusing F&EI with CEI** — F&EI addresses fire/explosion; CEI addresses toxic release. KTU Module 3 specifically tests this distinction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

**Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI):**
- ✅ MF is the foundation — derived from **NFPA 704 ratings**
- ✅ MF range: **1 (inert) to 40 (highly reactive)**
- ✅ GPH: 6 categories, penalties **SUMMED** (typical range 0–6)
- ✅ SPH: 12 categories, penalties **SUMMED** (typical range 0–8)
- ✅ Master formula: $F_3 = (1 + GPH)(1 + SPH)$
- ✅ Master formula: $F\&EI = MF \times F_3$
- ✅ Hazard classification: Light (1-60), Moderate (61-96), Heavy (97-127), Severe (128-158)
- ✅ Damage radius is read from the **Dow Hazard Area Classification Chart**

**Dow Chemical Exposure Index (CEI) / Toxicity Index:**
- ✅ Default assumptions: **u = 5 m/s, t = 600 s, H = 0, Stability D**
- ✅ Flash fraction formula: $f_{flash} = \frac{C_p(T_s - T_b)}{H_{vap}}$
- ✅ Airborne fraction: $A = f_{flash} + f_{vapor}$
- ✅ Release rate: $Q = \frac{A \cdot M}{600}$ (g/s or kg/s)
- ✅ Toxicity endpoint: **ERPG-2** (default) or AEGL-2
- ✅ Unit conversion: 1 ppm = M/24.45 mg/m³ (at 25°C, 1 atm)
- ✅ TI formula: $TI = T(\text{mg/m}^3) \times Q(\text{mg/s}) \times 10^{-6}$
- ✅ Toxic radius solved from **Gaussian Plume inverse**

**Critical Distinctions for KTU Exam:**
- ✅ F&EI → fire/explosion hazard → MF-based
- ✅ CEI → toxic release hazard → ERPG-based + atmospheric dispersion
- ✅ Both are *relative* ranking tools, not absolute risk measures
- ✅ Both were developed by **Dow Chemical Company**
- ✅ Both use multiplicative formulas (not additive) for the final index

**Numerical Problem-Solving Sequence:**
1. **Identify hazard type** → F&EI or CEI?
2. **Gather data** → NFPA ratings OR toxicity endpoints
3. **Apply default assumptions** → State them explicitly
4. **Calculate intermediate quantities** → MF, GPH, SPH, A, Q
5. **Compute final index** → F&EI or TI
6. **Interpret** → Classify hazard degree / radius
7. **Document** → State units, assumptions, and conclusions

**Memory Aid (KTU Exam Hall):**
> **"MF × F₃ = F&EI"** — Think: "**M**y **F**ire and **E**xplosion **I**ndex"
> **"T × Q × 10⁻⁶ = TI"** — Think: "**T**oxicity comes from **Q**uick releases"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emergency Planning - Onsite and Offsite Emergency Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emergency Planning - Onsite and Offsite Emergency Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Emergency Planning — The Foundation of Process Safety

**Emergency Planning** is a systematically documented set of pre-arranged, coordinated, and rehearsed actions designed to protect human life, the environment, and physical assets during an unforeseen catastrophic event such as a fire, explosion, toxic gas release, or natural disaster occurring at a chemical/process plant.

In the context of KTU 2024 OBE framework, Emergency Planning is directly tied to **Course Outcome (CO3)**: *"Develop onsite and offsite emergency preparedness plans for process industries in compliance with national and international safety standards."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize (Examiner's Favorite):**
> Emergency planning is the *anticipatory* and *responsive* mechanism that translates hazard analysis into actionable, time-critical, and resource-backed decisions during a crisis.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Fire Drill of a Chemical Plant"

Imagine you are the captain of a large ship carrying thousands of passengers. Before the ship ever leaves the dock, you conduct a **muster drill** — every passenger knows their lifeboat number, the crew knows their station, the captain knows the communication chain, and the coast guard is on standby.

**Emergency Planning is exactly this — but for a chemical plant.**

- The **ship** = Process Plant
- The **lifeboats** = Evacuation Assembly Points
- The **coast guard** = Offsite Emergency Services
- The **crew stations** = Onsite Emergency Response Team
- The **muster drill** = Mock Drills conducted periodically

Without planning, the moment a crisis hits, *panic takes over and decisions become reactive, late, and often fatal* — exactly what happened in the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)**.

---

## 1.3 Two Pillars of Emergency Planning

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 Focus — KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the strict dichotomy between:**

| Pillar | Scope | Boundary | Authority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Onsite Emergency Plan (OEP)** | Within the plant battery limit | Fence-to-fence | Plant Management |
| **Offsite Emergency Plan (OEP-2 / OEEP)** | Beyond the plant boundary | Community impact zone | District / State Authority |

---

## 1.4 Onsite Emergency Plan (OEP) — Definition

An **Onsite Emergency Plan** is a *plant-specific, internally controlled* document that details the immediate response actions, personnel accountability, fire-fighting mobilization, isolation procedures, and initial medical response to be executed **within the boundary wall** of the industrial facility when a major accident hazard is realized.

**Key Boundaries (Industrial Standard):**
- The OEP typically covers an **inner buffer zone of 1 km to 5 km radius** from the plant's main hazard source.
- The plan is activated by the **Site Incident Controller (SIC)** — usually the Plant Manager.

## 1.5 Offsite Emergency Plan — Definition

An **Offsite Emergency Plan** (also called **External Emergency Plan** in EU/Indian regulations) is a *multi-agency, government-controlled* plan that addresses the consequences of an emergency **beyond the plant boundary**, including public evacuation, shelter-in-place, traffic control, hospital preparedness, environmental decontamination, and post-incident recovery.

**Key Boundaries (Industrial Standard):**
- The Offsite Plan typically covers an **outer zone of 5 km to 10 km radius** (sometimes up to 25 km for highly toxic gas installations as per the **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 — MSIHC Rules**).
- The plan is activated by the **District Magistrate / District Collector**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse OEP and Offsite Plan as the same. They are *complementary but legally distinct* — one is owned by industry, the other is owned by the government. Marks are routinely lost in Part B for missing this distinction.

---

## 1.6 Statutory and Standards Backdrop

For KTU 2024, the following are **mandatory citations** in any emergency planning answer:

1. **Factories Act, 1948** — Section 41B (Emergency Plans in Hazardous Factories)
2. **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC), 1989** — Rules 13 to 15
3. **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991** (amended 1992)
4. **National Disaster Management Act, 2005** — Section 14, 36, 37
5. **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.120(q)** — Emergency Response to Hazardous Substance Releases
6. **API RP 753 / 754** — Management of Hazards Associated with Process Plant Portable Buildings
7. **OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM) — 29 CFR 1910.119(n)** — Emergency Planning & Response
8. **EPA Risk Management Program (RMP) — 40 CFR 68** — Accidental Release Prevention
9. **OECD Working Group on Chemical Accidents (WGCA) Guidelines**
10. **ISO 45001:2018** — Clause 7.4 (Communication) and Clause 8.2 (Emergency Preparedness & Response)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Disaster Management Act, 2005** introduced the *three-tiered structure* — National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA), State DMAs, and District DMAs. KTU frequently asks about this hierarchy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Architecture of an Onsite Emergency Plan (OEP)

The OEP is structured as a **hierarchical, modular document**. Below is the standard architecture mandated under **Rule 14 of MSIHC Rules, 1989** and aligned with KTU 2024 module outcomes.

### 2.1.1 Core Components of OEP

1. **Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment (HIRA) Summary**
   - Inventory of hazardous chemicals with **Threshold Quantities**
   - Maximum Credible Accident Scenarios (MCAS) — **Toxic Release, Fire Pool, Jet Fire, VCE, BLEVE**
   - Consequence analysis outputs — **AEGL, ERPG, IDLH, TLV-TWA values**

2. **Emergency Organization & Command Structure**
   - Incident Command System (ICS) — typically a 5-level hierarchy
   - Site Incident Controller (SIC)
   - Operations Chief, Planning Chief, Logistics Chief, Finance/Admin Chief, Safety Officer

3. **Emergency Response Procedures (ERP)**
   - Stage-wise escalation: **Alert → Standby → Activation → Termination**
   - Plant shutdown and **emergency isolation procedures**
   - **Emergency Depressurization (EDP)** and blowdown sequences
   - Fire-water system activation — **deluge valves, foam systems, monitors**

4. **Communication Plan**
   - Internal: PA system, radio channels (typically **VHF 150–174 MHz**), intercom
   - External: dedicated telephone lines, satellite phones, **Common Alerting Protocol (CAP)** compliant sirens
   - Backup communication: **air-horns, whistles, manual bells** (in case of power failure)

5. **Medical & First Aid Plan**
   - Onsite Occupational Health Centre (OHC) with antidotes
   - Tie-up with **nearest hospital within a stipulated 25 km** for major chemical exposure

6. **Mutual Aid & External Coordination**
   - List of mutual aid partners with their capability matrix
   - Offsite agency contacts — **Fire Service (101), Ambulance (108), Police (100), NDMA helpline (1078)**

7. **Training, Drills & Updating**
   - Onsite mock drills — **twice a year (mandatory under Rule 14)**
   - Tabletop exercises — **quarterly**
   - Plan revision — **annually or after any incident**

8. **Resource Inventory**
   - Firefighting appliances, PPE (Level A/B/C HAZMAT suits), SCBA sets
   - Spill containment booms, neutralizers, absorbents

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Question Pattern:** "List and explain the contents of an Onsite Emergency Plan as per MSIHC Rules 1989" — expect **14-mark weightage** with sub-parts.

---

## 2.2 The Architecture of an Offsite Emergency Plan

The Offsite Plan is the **macro-level response architecture** that protects the community surrounding the plant. It is owned by the **District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA)**.

### 2.2.1 Key Stakeholders in Offsite Plan

| Stakeholder | Primary Role |
|---|---|
| **District Collector / Magistrate** | Overall In-Charge, declared as **Relief Commissioner** during emergency |
| **Local Police** | Traffic control, perimeter security, anti-looting |
| **Fire & Rescue Services** | Offsite firefighting, rescue operations |
| **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** | Air/water sampling, environmental damage assessment |
| **Health Department** | Mass casualty management, hospital coordination |
| **PWD / NHAI** | Road clearance, debris removal |
| **NGO & Red Cross** | Shelters, food, psycho-social support |
| **NDRF / SDRF** | Specialized chemical/biological/radiological response |
| **Indian Army / Air Force** | Activated only for National-Level Disasters |
| **Local Panchayat / Municipality** | Grassroots mobilization, warning dissemination |

### 2.2.2 The Three-Stage Offsite Response Model

**Stage 1 — Notification & Initial Assessment**
- Plant management notifies **District Control Room within 15 minutes** of a major accident.
- The **Crisis Alert Level** is declared (Green, Yellow, Orange, Red).

**Stage 2 — Public Warning & Sheltering/Evacuation**
- **Public Warning Sirens** (multi-tonal, 1 km spacing) are sounded.
- **Reverse 112 (ERSS)**, **Cell Broadcast (CB)**, and **IVRS** calls are triggered.
- **Shelter-in-Place (SIP)** is advised if evacuation is impractical.
- **Mass evacuation** is executed using designated **3 pre-planned routes** (primary, secondary, tertiary) to avoid bottlenecks.

**Stage 3 — Recovery & Rehabilitation**
- **Hazardous Area Re-entry Plan** is activated.
- Long-term environmental monitoring.
- Compensation under **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991**.
- **Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)** counselling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Concept to Know:** The **Public Warning Siren Audibility Radius** is calculated using the inverse-square law of sound propagation. A typical civil defense siren of **125 dB(A)** at source covers approximately **1.5 km to 2 km** in open terrain under standard atmospheric conditions.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Numerical Concepts

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Emergency Planning Distance (EPD) | $\text{EPD} = f(\text{Release Rate, Toxicity, Meteorology})$ | **0.5 km to 25 km** | Defines offsite plan boundary |
| Toxic Load Model (Probit) | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ | $n, a, b$ = chemical constants | Predicts fatality/incapacitation percentage |
| Audibility Radius of Siren | $R = R_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{L_0 - L_{\text{thr}}}{20}}$ | **meters** | Siren placement planning |
| Wind Rose Hazard Frequency | $f(\theta) = \frac{N_{\text{acc}}(\theta)}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | **dimensionless** | Determines downwind hazard sectors |
| Evacuation Clearance Time | $T_{\text{clear}} = \frac{P \cdot L}{v \cdot N \cdot W}$ | **minutes** | Time to evacuate population $P$ over distance $L$ |
| SCBA Air Supply Duration | $t = \frac{(P_0 - P_{\text{alarm}}) \cdot V}{\dot{V}_{\text{consumption}}}$ | **minutes** | Typical: **30 min** at 4500 kPa, 30 L cylinder |
| AEGL Tier | $\text{AEGL-1 / 2 / 3}$ | **ppm (30 min)** | Acute Exposure Guideline Levels |
| ERPG Tier | $\text{ERPG-1 / 2 / 3}$ | **ppm (60 min)** | Emergency Response Planning Guidelines |
| IDLH | — | **ppm** | Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health |
| Pool Fire Heat Flux | $\dot{q}'' = \dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_{\text{rad}}$ | **kW/m²** | Thermal radiation at distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The Probit equation $Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ is the **most-tested** numerical framework. Be ready to apply it to chlorine, ammonia, and LPG release scenarios. The constants ($a, b, n$) are *chemical-specific* and must be quoted correctly.

---

## 2.4 Differences Between Onsite and Offsite Emergency Plan

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU Module 3 of Process Safety Engineering.

| Parameter | Onsite Emergency Plan | Offsite Emergency Plan |
|---|---|---|
| **Authority** | Plant Management | District Administration (DDMA) |
| **Geographic Scope** | Inside plant fence | 5–25 km buffer around plant |
| **Activation Trigger** | Onset of in-plant emergency | Notification from plant / public observation |
| **Primary Objective** | Asset protection, life safety on site, incident mitigation | Public life safety, environmental protection, community welfare |
| **Plan Owner** | Plant Manager / Safety Officer | District Collector / Magistrate |
| **Legal Basis** | Factories Act 1948 Sec 41B; MSIHC Rules 1989 Rule 14 | Disaster Management Act 2005; EPA 1986 Sec 9 |
| **Drill Frequency** | Twice a year (minimum) | Once a year (joint exercise) |
| **Communication** | Internal radio/PA/Intercom | Mass media, sirens, SMS, Cell Broadcast |
| **Resources** | Plant fire brigade, in-house ambulance | Government fire tenders, NDRF, hospitals |
| **Funding** | Plant's operating budget | State Disaster Response Fund (SDRF) |
| **Documentation** | SOPs, HIRA, ERP manuals | District DM Plan, sectoral response plans |
| **Mutual Aid** | Adjacent industries | NGOs, military, civic bodies |
| **Recovery Focus** | Plant restart, investigation | Community rehabilitation, compensation |
| **Statutory Owner Document** | **Onsite Emergency Plan Document** | **Offsite Emergency Plan Document (OEMP)** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Example — Toxic Load (Probit) Analysis for Chlorine Release

This is the **classic KTU numerical problem**. Let us derive the expected fatality fraction in a residential colony **500 m downwind** of a chlorine release.

### Problem Statement

A **10,000 kg chlorine tank** ruptures catastrophically. Atmospheric conditions: **D-Stability, 5 m/s wind speed**. Population at 500 m downwind: **$N = 2{,}000$ people** in open terrain. Determine:
- Toxic concentration at 500 m
- Fatality fraction using Probit equation
- Number of expected casualties

### Step 1 — Source Strength Calculation

The chlorine release is modeled as an **instantaneous puff**. Using the Gaussian Puff Model:

$$
C(x, y, 0, t) = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-ut)^2}{2\sigma_x^2} - \frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = mass released = **10,000 kg**
- $u$ = wind speed = **5 m/s**
- $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = dispersion coefficients (Pasquill-Gifford, D-stability)

For D-stability at downwind distance $x = 500$ m:

$$
\sigma_y = 0.08 \cdot x \cdot (1 + 0.0001 x)^{-0.5} \approx 0.08 \cdot 500 \cdot (1.05)^{-0.5}
$$

$$
\sigma_y \approx 39.05 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\sigma_z = 0.06 \cdot x \cdot (1 + 0.0015 x)^{-0.5} \approx 0.06 \cdot 500 \cdot (1.75)^{-0.5}
$$

$$
\sigma_z \approx 22.68 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 — Centerline Concentration at $t = x/u = 100$ s

$$
C = \frac{10{,}000}{\sqrt{2\pi} \cdot (39.05) \cdot (39.05) \cdot (22.68)}
$$

$$
C = \frac{10{,}000}{2.5066 \cdot 34{,}579.6}
$$

$$
C = \frac{10{,}000}{86{,}672.6}
$$

$$
C \approx 0.1154 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

Converting to ppm (molar mass of Cl₂ = 70.9 g/mol, at 25°C, 1 atm, $V_m$ = 24.45 L/mol):

$$
C_{\text{ppm}} = \frac{C \times 24.45 \times 10^6}{M \times 10^3} = \frac{0.1154 \times 24.45 \times 10^6}{70.9 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
C_{\text{ppm}} = \frac{2{,}821{,}130}{70{,}900} \approx 39.79 \text{ ppm}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Probit Equation for Chlorine Fatality

The WHO/EPA published Probit equation for chlorine lethality is:

$$
Y = -17.1 + 1.69 \ln(C^2 \cdot t)
$$

Where:
- $C$ = concentration in **ppm**
- $t$ = exposure time in **minutes**
- $Y$ = Probit variable

Substituting $C = 39.79$ ppm and $t = 100/60 = 1.667$ min:

$$
C^2 = 1583.2 \text{ ppm}^2
$$

$$
C^2 \cdot t = 1583.2 \times 1.667 = 2639.2
$$

$$
\ln(2639.2) = 7.879
$$

$$
Y = -17.1 + 1.69 \times 7.879
$$

$$
Y = -17.1 + 13.316 = -3.784
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Probit to Fatality Fraction

The relationship between Probit $Y$ and percentage $P$ is:

$$
P = 50 \left[ 1 + \frac{Y - 5}{\vert Y - 5 \vert} \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{\vert Y - 5 \vert}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \right]
$$

Equivalently, using the standard CDF lookup:

$$
P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du
$$

For $Y - 5 = -8.784$, the cumulative probability is essentially **0** (very deep into the left tail).

Using the inverse transformation more carefully via the **Finney Table**:

$$
P \approx 0.00001\% \approx \text{negligible fatality at 500 m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** At 500 m, the *peak* concentration may not cause immediate fatality, but **AEGL-2 (0.4 ppm, 30 min)** and **AEGL-3 (2.0 ppm, 30 min)** thresholds for chlorine are easily breached. This means the public must be **evacuated or sheltered-in-place** even if fatalities are not immediate. This is why **offsite planning extends to kilometers**, not meters.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Siren Placement Calculation

### Problem Statement

A siren has a **source Sound Pressure Level of $L_0 = 125$ dB(A)**. The human hearing threshold is **$L_{\text{thr}} = 70$ dB(A)**. The siren manufacturer specifies a **reference radius $R_0 = 1$ m**. Determine the audibility radius $R$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Inverse-Square Law for Sound

$$
R = R_0 \cdot 10^{(L_0 - L_{\text{thr}})/20}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
R = 1 \cdot 10^{(125 - 70)/20}
$$

$$
R = 10^{55/20} = 10^{2.75}
$$

$$
R \approx 562.3 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 3 — Engineering Adjustment for Terrain

In real engineering, we apply a **terrain attenuation factor** $\eta = 0.5$ to $0.7$ (built-up urban areas have higher attenuation). For an industrial semi-urban area:

$$
R_{\text{effective}} = R \cdot \eta = 562.3 \cdot 0.6 \approx 337.4 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Inference:** Sirens must be placed at **spacing of 300 m to 500 m** in industrial clusters to ensure overlapping coverage. The Bhopal disaster exposed the fatal flaw of inadequate siren coverage in surrounding slums.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The 5-Tier Emergency Alert System

This matrix is the **decision-support backbone** of any KTU-approved Emergency Plan:

| Tier | Alert Level | Trigger | Onsite Action | Offsite Action | Color Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Normal Operations | None | Routine monitoring | Routine | 🟢 Green |
| **1** | Internal Alert | Minor leak, instrument trip | Area wardens activated, area isolation | Inform security | 🟡 Yellow |
| **2** | Plant Emergency | Major fire / uncontrolled release | Plant emergency siren, full mobilization | Inform DDMA control room | 🟠 Orange |
| **3** | Site Emergency | Offsite impact possible | Mutual aid activated, public warning system armed | Public sirens armed, hospitals pre-alerted | 🔴 Red |
| **4** | Community Emergency | Offsite impact realized | Plant isolated, all response focus on-site preservation | Mass evacuation, NDRF deployment | ⚫ Black |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** When the question asks for "explain the emergency alert levels," draw this **5-tier table** — examiners award full marks for a structured, color-coded response.

---

## 3.4 The Eight-Step Emergency Planning Process (Industry Standard)

A universally accepted procedural flow used by **OSHA, EPA, NDMA, and AIChE CCPS** is:

1. **Establish a Planning Team** — Multi-disciplinary, includes operations, safety, security, medical, environment, public relations.
2. **Conduct HIRA (Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment)** — What can go wrong? With what frequency? With what consequence?
3. **Define Goals & Objectives** — Life safety, environmental protection, asset preservation, business continuity.
4. **Develop the Plan** — Assign roles, write SOPs, prepare resource inventory.
5. **Review, Approve & Distribute** — Management sign-off; controlled distribution to all stakeholders.
6. **Implement Training & Communication** — Awareness sessions, structured certification, public outreach.
7. **Conduct Drills & Exercises** — Tabletop, functional, full-scale mock drills.
8. **Review, Revise & Improve** — After-Action Review (AAR), CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Onsite Emergency Response — Incident Command Structure

The following Mermaid block renders the **ICS (Incident Command System)** hierarchy mandated under the National Incident Management System (NIMS) framework:


> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows depict **chain-of-command** reporting. In a real emergency, the SIC has *unified command* and is the single point of decision-making to prevent conflicting orders — a critical lesson from the Bhopal disaster where multiple managers gave contradictory instructions.

---

This Mermaid block maps the **government-controlled offsite response architecture**:

> **KTU Examiner Observation:** Drawing such structured flowcharts in the answer script earns a **minimum of 2 extra marks** even in a 14-mark question, as it demonstrates *organizational clarity* — a core assessment criterion.

---

## 4.3 The Emergency Planning Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["STEP 1 - HIRA AND MCAS IDENTIFICATION"]
```
