# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q1.** *Define the term "Safety Inspection" and list any four objectives of conducting a safety inspection in a process industry.*

**Model Answer:**

Safety Inspection is a systematic, planned, and documented on-site examination of workplace conditions, equipment, and processes conducted by a competent person to identify hazards, unsafe acts, and non-conformances with applicable standards.

**Four Objectives:**
1. **Hazard Identification** – Detect unsafe conditions and acts before they cause incidents.
2. **Compliance Verification** – Ensure adherence to the Factories Act 1948, MSIHC Rules 1989, OISD standards, and ISO 45001.
3. **Early Fault Detection** – Identify incipient failures in equipment (e.g., corrosion, crack, leak) through visual and instrumented inspection.
4. **Continuous Improvement** – Provide a feedback loop to enhance the safety management system and promote safety culture.

> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q2.** *Distinguish between a Safety Inspection, a Safety Audit, and a Hazard Analysis. Mention the typical frequency of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Safety Inspection | Safety Audit | Hazard Analysis |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Focus** | Physical state of equipment and workplace | Safety management system and documentation | Process deviations and consequences |
| **Scope** | Narrow, specific area | Plant-wide, holistic | Process-specific |
| **Method** | Visual + checklist | Document review + interview | HAZOP, QRA, FMEA |
| **Frequency** | Daily / Weekly / Monthly | Annually | During design and major changes |
| **Outcome** | List of findings and corrective actions | Compliance score and recommendations | Risk register and recommendations |
| **Conducted by** | Safety officer / Supervisor | External auditor / Multidisciplinary team | Process engineer / Specialist team |

**Typical Frequencies:**
- Safety Inspection – Daily/Shift/Weekly
- Safety Audit – Annually
- Hazard Analysis – At design stage, before modifications, every 5 years for existing plants

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – With Internal Choice)

> [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]

### 📝 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q.A.** *(a)* *Explain the various types of safety inspections conducted in a process industry. Discuss in detail the **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** methodology as per API 580/581.* **[7 Marks]**

*(b)* *A pressure vessel in a refinery has the following parameters: Probability of Failure P = 4 and Consequence of Failure C = 5. The initial risk score was 20, and after implementing corrective actions, the residual risk is 4. Calculate: (i) The risk score after mitigation, (ii) The percentage reduction in risk, and (iii) Recommend the inspection frequency.* **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Types of Safety Inspections in Process Industries:**

1. **Routine / Patrol Inspection** – Conducted per shift by the area supervisor to identify obvious unsafe conditions.
2. **Periodic Inspection** – Conducted weekly/monthly by the department head with a structured checklist.
3. **Annual / Statutory Inspection** – Conducted by the Chief Inspector of Factories or external agency for pressure vessels, lifts, boilers.
4. **Pre-Startup Safety Inspection (PSSI)** – Conducted before commissioning a new or modified plant as per OSHA 1910.119.
5. **Special Inspection** – Triggered by incidents, near-misses, process modifications, or regulatory orders.
6. **Continuous / Instrumented Inspection** – Performed through DCS/SIS alarm monitoring and continuous emissions monitoring.
7. **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** – Inspection frequency determined by risk score (PoF × CoF) per API 580/581.

**Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) Methodology:**

RBI integrates **Probability of Failure (PoF)** and **Consequence of Failure (CoF)** to determine inspection priorities. The steps are:

- **Step 1:** Identify all equipment and assign to a damage mechanism library.
- **Step 2:** Compute PoF using generic failure frequency data, adjusted for equipment-specific factors (age, service, material).
- **Step 3:** Compute CoF in four categories: Financial, Environmental, Safety, and Health consequences.
- **Step 4:** Combine into the risk score $R = P \times C$.
- **Step 5:** Plot on the 5×5 risk matrix to classify as Low / Medium / High / Very High risk.
- **Step 6:** Determine inspection plan (method, scope, interval) based on the risk classification.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given Data:**
- Initial Probability of Failure, $P_1 = 4$
- Consequence of Failure, $C = 5$ (constant for the vessel)
- Initial Risk Score, $R_1 = 20$
- Residual Risk Score (post-mitigation), $R_2 = 4$ *(Note: problem says residual risk is 4, not 20-P×C; we treat residual as 4)*

**Part (i): Risk Score After Mitigation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2 &= P_2 \times C \\
4 &= P_2 \times 5 \\
P_2 &= \dfrac{4}{5} = 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

Since PoF is now 0.8 ≈ 1 (rounded up to next integer scale), the new risk score is **4 (Low Risk)**.

**Part (ii): Percentage Reduction in Risk (Mitigation Effectiveness)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_m &= \dfrac{R_1 - R_2}{R_1} \times 100\% \\
E_m &= \dfrac{20 - 4}{20} \times 100\% \\
E_m &= \dfrac{16}{20} \times 100\% \\
E_m &= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii): Recommended Inspection Frequency**

Since the residual risk score $R_2 = 4$ falls in the range $R \le 5$ (Low Risk), the recommended inspection frequency is:
- **Visual external inspection every 12 months** (annual)
- **Internal inspection every 4–6 years** (as per OISD-STD-105 for non-corrosive service)
- **No special NDT required** beyond routine UT thickness checks at corroded areas.

---

### 📝 Question B (Choice 2 – Alternative) — 14 Marks

**Q.B.** *(a)* *What is an Inspection Checklist? Discuss its importance, structure, and the major categories it should cover in a process plant.* **[7 Marks]**

*(b)* *The following data is collected during a safety inspection of a chemical plant. Compute the **Plant Safety Index (PSI)** and interpret the result.* **[7 Marks]**

| Sub-system | Score ($s_k$) out of 10 | Weight ($w_k$) |
|------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Fire Protection System | 9 | 0.30 |
| Process Control & Instrumentation | 8 | 0.25 |
| Electrical Safety | 7 | 0.20 |
| Mechanical Integrity | 6 | 0.15 |
| Occupational Health & Hygiene | 8 | 0.10 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Inspection Checklist – Definition:**

An **Inspection Checklist** is a structured, pre-formatted document listing items to be examined during a safety inspection, typically in Yes / No / Not Applicable format with provision for remarks and photographic evidence.

**Importance of Inspection Checklist:**
1. Ensures **completeness** – no critical item is overlooked.
2. Provides **uniformity** – multiple inspectors produce consistent results.
3. Enables **traceability** – a documented record for legal and audit purposes.
4. **Reduces inspector fatigue** – serves as a memory aid.
5. **Facilitates training** – new inspectors can use the checklist as a learning tool.
6. **Enables data analytics** – trend analysis of recurring findings.
7. **Legal evidence** – admissible in courts under Section 114 of the Indian Evidence Act.

**Structure of an Effective Checklist:**
- Header (Plant name, area, date, inspector name, weather)
- Item number and description
- Reference to standard (Factories Act section, OISD, IS)
- Yes / No / NA columns
- Remarks column
- Severity classification (Critical/Serious/Minor)
- Photo evidence reference
- Inspector signature and supervisor counter-signature

**Major Categories in a Process Plant Checklist:**
1. Housekeeping and access/egress
2. Fire protection (extinguishers, hydrants, alarms)
3. Electrical safety (earthing, IP rating, cable management)
4. Pressure vessels and piping (corrosion, leaks, supports)
5. Hazardous chemical storage (bunding, MSDS, labels)
6. PPE availability and usage
7. Emergency preparedness (escape routes, assembly points, lighting)
8. Permit-to-work system (hot work, confined space, height)
9. Process safety interlocks and alarms
10. Training, competency, and safety induction records

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:** 5 sub-systems with scores and weights.

**Formula:**

$$
PSI = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} s_k \cdot w_k
$$

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PSI &= \dfrac{1}{5} \left[ (9 \times 0.30) + (8 \times 0.25) + (7 \times 0.20) + (6 \times 0.15) + (8 \times 0.10) \right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{5} \left[ 2.70 + 2.00 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 0.80 \right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{5} \times 7.80 \\
&= 1.56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $PSI = 1.56$ on a 0–10 scale = **15.6%** (Poor).

The plant's overall safety performance is **POOR**, particularly due to weak **Mechanical Integrity** (score 6) and **Electrical Safety** (score 7). Immediate corrective action is recommended in these sub-systems, followed by a re-inspection within 30 days to verify improvement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 
> 1. **Formula Misuse:** Students often write $PSI = \sum (s_k \times w_k)$ without dividing by $n$. The $1/n$ factor is mandatory to normalize the index to a 0–10 scale. **Lose 1 mark if omitted.**
> 
> 2. **Unit Confusion:** The PSI is **dimensionless**. Do not write units like "%" in the formula step. Convert to % only in the final interpretation.
> 
> 3. **Weight Validation:** Always verify $\sum w_k = 1$. If not, normalize: $w_k^{norm} = w_k / \sum w_k$. In Q.B(b), $0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00$ ✓. If you forget this, **lose 1 mark.**
> 
> 4. **Score Range:** If a student writes scores out of 100, they will get a wrong PSI. Always check that scores are normalized to the same scale.
> 
> 5. **Interpretation Skipped:** Many students stop at the calculation. Always include a **2-3 line interpretation** explaining what the PSI value means in practical terms (e.g., "immediate corrective action required"). **Lose 1 mark if missing.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- **Safety Inspection** = systematic on-site examination for hazard identification.
- **Inspection ≠ Audit ≠ Hazard Analysis** – know the distinction (most common 3-mark question).
- **Types:** Routine, Periodic, Annual/Statutory, Pre-Startup (PSSI), Special, RBI.
- **6 Phases:** Plan → Brief → Execute → Analyze → Report → Follow-up.

### ✅ Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- $R = P \times C$ (Risk Score)
- $PSI = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum s_k \cdot w_k$ (Plant Safety Index)
- $E_m = \dfrac{R_{initial} - R_{residual}}{R_{initial}} \times 100\%$ (Mitigation Effectiveness)
- $f_{inspection} = \dfrac{N_{critical} \times 12}{N_{inspectors} \times H_{effective}}$ (OISD Frequency)

### ✅ Indian Statutory Requirements
- **Factories Act 1948** – Sec 36 (Pre-commissioning), Sec 91 (Pressure vessels).
- **MSIHC Rules 1989** – Annual safety audit for MAH units.
- **OISD-STD-105, 130, 150** – Inspection standards for oil & gas.
- **IS 14489:2018** – Industrial safety audit code.
- **ISO 45001:2018** – OHSMS framework.

### ✅ Inspection Team
- Team Leader, Operations Rep, Maintenance Rep, Safety Officer, Process Engineer, Fire Officer, Medical Officer, Worker Rep, External Expert.

### ✅ Inspection Checklist Categories
- Housekeeping, Fire, Electrical, Pressure vessels, Chemical storage, PPE, Emergency, Permits, Process safety, Training.

### ✅ Severity Classification
- **Critical** → Immediate shutdown, plant director notified.
- **Serious** → Corrective action in 24 hours, HSE manager.
- **Minor** → Corrective action in 7 days, department head.
- **Observation** → Log and track, supervisor.

### ✅ RBI Risk Matrix Thresholds
- $R \le 5$ → Low (Routine)
- $6 \le R \le 12$ → Medium (2 years)
- $13 \le R \le 20$ → High (Annual)
- $R \ge 21$ → Very High (Immediate)

### ✅ Common Examiner Traps
1. Confusing inspection with audit (deducted 2 marks).
2. Forgetting to normalize weights in PSI.
3. Writing `|x|` instead of `\vert x\vert` in tables.
4. Missing the interpretation step.
5. Not citing the relevant Factories Act section.
6. Skipping the re-inspection / closure loop.

### ✅ Real-World Connect (For 7-Mark "Discuss" Questions)
- **Bhopal 1984** → led to MSIHC Rules 1989 and mandatory annual safety inspections.
- **Visakhapatnam Gas Leak 2020** → emphasis on storage inspection and MSDS compliance.
- **Beirut Explosion 2020** → inspection of ammonium nitrate storage.
- **OISD-STD-105** → OPaL, IOCL, BPCL refinery practice.
- **IS 14489:2018** → Most up-to-date Indian audit code.

> **🎯 Final Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, dedicate 2 marks to a diagram (use Mermaid-style or hand-drawn), 2 marks to formulas with units, 4 marks to stepwise calculation, 2 marks to interpretation, and 2 marks to statutory references. This pattern consistently scores **13–14/14** in KTU board exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Audits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Audits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Safety Audits

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Audit (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard Definition):**
> A *Safety Audit* is a **systematic, structured, documented, and objective evaluation** of an organization's safety management systems (SMS), process operations, equipment integrity, and procedural compliance against predetermined criteria such as regulatory standards (e.g., OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, IS 14489, OISD), industry best practices (e.g., CCPS Guidelines), and corporate safety policies. It is conducted by competent, independent auditors to identify non-conformities, hazardous conditions, and opportunities for improvement, culminating in actionable recommendations and verifiable corrective action plans.

In the specific context of **Process Safety Engineering (OEBBT723)**, a safety audit focuses on the integrity of chemical/physical processes, control systems, relief devices, maintenance protocols, and human factors that could lead to catastrophic events such as fires, explosions, and toxic releases.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Annual Physical Check-up for a Factory"**
> Imagine a human body. Even if a person feels healthy, doctors recommend an annual full-body checkup to catch hidden problems — high blood pressure, cholesterol, or early-stage tumors. A **Safety Audit** does exactly the same thing for an industrial plant.
>
> - The **doctor** = the audit team
> - The **patient** = the plant/facility
> - **Medical reports & blood tests** = audit checklists, incident records, P&IDs
> - **Diagnosis** = identification of non-conformities
> - **Prescription** = corrective actions
> - **Follow-up visit** = verification of corrective action implementation
>
> Just as prevention is better than cure, a safety audit prevents **accidents BEFORE they happen**, rather than waiting for an incident to occur.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Standard Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Audit Criteria** | The set of policies, procedures, standards, or regulations used as a reference against which audit evidence is compared. |
| **Audit Evidence** | Verifiable records, statements of fact, or other information which are relevant to the audit criteria and verifiable. |
| **Non-Conformity (NC)** | A deviation from a standard, regulation, or established criterion. Sub-classified as **Major NC** (immediate hazard) or **Minor NC** (documentation gap). |
| **Observation** | A finding that does not constitute a non-conformity but indicates an opportunity for improvement. |
| **Audit Program** | The complete set of audits planned for a specific timeframe, covering all relevant activities of an organization. |
| **Lead Auditor** | The person appointed to manage and lead the audit team. |
| **Auditee** | The organization or department being audited. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the exam, always use the phrase *"systematic, documented, and objective evaluation against predetermined criteria."* This is the gold-standard definition universally accepted by KTU valuation cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Safety Audits

Safety audits are classified along multiple dimensions, each targeting a unique aspect of organizational safety.

#### A. Based on the Audit Performer

1. **Internal Safety Audit**
   - Conducted by the organization's own qualified personnel.
   - **Purpose:** Routine compliance check, housekeeping, procedural adherence.
   - **Frequency:** Typically semi-annual or annual.

2. **External Safety Audit**
   - Conducted by third-party independent auditors (e.g., certified by NEBOSH, IOSH, or statutory bodies like the Factory Inspectorate).
   - **Purpose:** Unbiased evaluation, regulatory compliance verification, certification audits (e.g., ISO 45001, ISO 14001).

#### B. Based on the Audit Scope (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Compliance Audit (Mandatory for KTU 2024 syllabus)**
   - Verifies adherence to statutory regulations (e.g., Factories Act 1948, Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules — MSIHC 1989, OISD standards, EP Act 1986).

2. **Pre-Startup Safety Audit (PSSA / PSSR — Pre-Startup Safety Review)**
   - Conducted **before** commissioning a new process, plant, or after major modification.
   - **Scope:** Equipment integrity, procedural readiness, training, emergency preparedness.

3. **Process Safety Audit**
   - Focuses on the technical integrity of chemical processes.
   - Audits HAZOP, LOPA, SIL assessments, relief valve sizing, instrumented protective systems.

4. **Occupational Health & Hygiene Audit**
   - Examines exposure to noise, dust, fumes, heat stress, ergonomics, ventilation, PPE adequacy.

5. **Environmental Safety Audit**
   - Evaluates emissions, effluent discharge, waste handling, and environmental management system (EMS) compliance.

6. **Management System Audit**
   - Audits the **Safety Management System (SMS)** itself — policy, organization, planning, implementation, checking, and management review (PDCA cycle).

7. **Product Safety Audit**
   - Reviews the safety of the end product during storage, transport, and use.

#### C. Based on Frequency

| Type | Frequency | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Continuous** | Daily/Shift | Supervisor-level safety inspections |
| **Periodic** | Weekly/Monthly | Department-level audits |
| **Annual** | Yearly | Comprehensive plant audit |
| **Special** | On-demand | Post-incident, pre-commissioning, regulatory inspection |

### 2.2 Objectives of a Safety Audit (The 5 Pillars)

A KTU 2024 Scheme student must memorize the **five core objectives**:

1. **Verify Compliance** with statutory and corporate safety standards.
2. **Identify Hazards & Deficiencies** in equipment, procedures, and human behavior.
3. **Evaluate the Effectiveness** of existing safety management systems.
4. **Recommend Corrective Actions** with timelines and responsibilities.
5. **Foster a Safety Culture** by involving management and workers in safety dialogue.

### 2.3 The Audit Process — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** The audit process follows the **PDCA Cycle** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) and has **Three Major Phases**.

#### **Phase 1: Pre-Audit Activities (Planning Stage)**

1. **Define Audit Scope, Objectives, and Criteria**
   - What is being audited? (Entire plant vs. specific unit)
   - Why? (Compliance, incident follow-up, pre-startup)
   - Against what standards? (OISD, IS, ASME, NFPA, internal SOPs)

2. **Select & Train the Audit Team**
   - Lead Auditor + 2-4 Team Auditors
   - Must be competent, independent of the area being audited.

3. **Develop the Audit Plan & Schedule**
   - Timeline, location, personnel to be interviewed.

4. **Prepare Audit Checklists & Questionnaires**
   - These are the auditor's primary tools on-site.

5. **Issue Notification to Auditee**
   - Formal communication, document requests in advance.

#### **Phase 2: On-Site Audit Activities (Execution Stage)**

1. **Opening Meeting** — Confirm scope, introduce team, establish communication protocols.
2. **Site Tour & Physical Inspection** — Visual examination of equipment, housekeeping, signage, PPE usage.
3. **Document Review** — SOPs, logbooks, training records, maintenance records, incident reports, MSDS.
4. **Personnel Interviews** — Talk to operators, supervisors, safety officers, contractors.
5. **Evidence Collection** — Photos, copies of records, witness statements.
6. **Closing Meeting** — Present preliminary findings verbally to management.

#### **Phase 3: Post-Audit Activities (Reporting & Follow-up)**

1. **Audit Report Preparation** — Formal document listing findings, classified by severity.
2. **Corrective Action Plan (CAPA)** — Jointly developed with auditee.
3. **Distribution of Report** — To senior management, EHS committee, regulators (if required).
4. **Verification of Corrective Actions** — Follow-up audit or desktop review.
5. **Closure** — Sign-off once all findings are closed.

### 2.4 Audit Checklists — The Auditor's Toolkit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Checklists?**
> Checklists ensure **consistency, completeness, and objectivity**. A well-designed checklist is the single most important tool for a successful audit. KTU 2024 questions frequently ask for the structure of a safety audit checklist.

**Structure of a Professional Safety Audit Checklist:**

| Column | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| **S. No.** | Unique identifier for each question |
| **Audit Area** | E.g., Mechanical, Electrical, Fire Protection, Housekeeping |
| **Audit Question / Item** | What is being checked? |
| **Reference Standard** | The standard against which compliance is measured |
| **Observation** | Space for auditor to record the finding |
| **Compliance Status** | Compliant / NC Major / NC Minor / Observation / N/A |
| **Auditor Remarks** | Suggestions or risk-level assessment |
| **Photograph Reference** | Link to visual evidence |

### 2.5 Audit Findings — Classification & Severity Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Severity Classification (Universal KTU Pattern):**

| Severity | Definition | Typical Action Timeline |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Major Non-Conformity (NC Major)** | A serious breach that poses immediate danger to life, property, or environment; or systemic failure of a system. | **Immediate closure** (24–72 hours) |
| **Minor Non-Conformity (NC Minor)** | An isolated lapse that does not pose immediate danger but indicates systemic weakness. | Within **30 days** |
| **Observation** | A potential weakness or improvement opportunity. | Within **90 days** |
| **Opportunity for Improvement (OFI)** | Best-practice suggestion. | At management discretion |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although safety audits are primarily procedural, a few quantitative metrics are essential for KTU Board Exams.

| Metric / Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Compliance Percentage** | $\text{CP} = \left( \dfrac{N_c}{N_t} \right) \times 100$ | % | $N_c$ = Number of compliant items, $N_t$ = Total audit items |
| **Risk Score (for a Finding)** | $R = S \times P \times L$ | dimensionless | $S$ = Severity, $P$ = Probability, $L$ = Likelihood (qualitative scale 1–5) |
| **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | dimensionless | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection (FMEA-based auditing) |
| **Audit Effectiveness Index** | $\text{AEI} = \dfrac{\text{NC closed on time}}{\text{Total NCs raised}} \times 100$ | % | Measures robustness of the SMS |
| **Non-Conformity Density** | $\text{NCD} = \dfrac{\text{Total NCs}}{\text{Audit man-hours}}$ | NCs/hour | Benchmarking across plants |

> [!WARNING]
> **Latex Tip for KTU Answer Sheets:** Always use $\mid$ or $\vert$ instead of the vertical bar $\vert$ in written equations, and never use the pipe symbol $\vert$ inside a markdown table row to avoid formatting corruption.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Safety audits are mandated across nearly every high-hazard industry worldwide:

- **Oil & Gas (Upstream, Midstream, Downstream):** OISD audits, offshore platform safety case audits.
- **Petrochemicals & Refineries:** PSM compliance audits under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Process safety audits per FDA and EMA guidelines.
- **Manufacturing:** Machinery safety audits per ISO 12100 and EN 60204-1.
- **Construction:** Site safety audits per IS 4130 and OISD-GDN-192.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** A graduate Process Safety Engineer typically works as a *Plant Safety Officer*, *Lead Auditor*, *EHS Manager*, or *Compliance Specialist*. Strong knowledge of safety audit methodology is a **mandatory employability skill** in the Indian process industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Compliance Percentage Calculation

The **Compliance Percentage (CP)** is a fundamental metric used to quantify how well a plant is performing against an audit standard.

**Starting Point:** The total number of items in an audit checklist is $N_t$, of which $N_c$ items are fully compliant with the standard.

**Step 1:** Define the ratio of compliant items to total items.

$$
R_c = \dfrac{N_c}{N_t}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by 100 to express as a percentage.

$$
\text{CP} = R_c \times 100 = \left( \dfrac{N_c}{N_t} \right) \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Example — A safety audit was conducted on a 200-item checklist for a process unit. The auditor found 176 items compliant. Calculate the Compliance Percentage.

$$
\text{CP} = \left( \dfrac{176}{200} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CP} = 0.88 \times 100 = 88\%
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation. An 88% compliance score means 12% of the audit items had deficiencies, which corresponds to **24 non-conformities**. This score is benchmarked against the corporate target (typically $\geq 95\%$ for mature SMS).

### 3.2 Derivation: Risk Score Using $R = S \times P \times L$

> [!NOTE]
> The Risk Score quantifies the **criticality** of each audit finding, guiding prioritization of corrective actions.

**Definitions:**
- $S$ = Severity of consequence (1 = Negligible, 2 = Minor injury, 3 = Major injury, 4 = Fatality, 5 = Catastrophe)
- $P$ = Probability of occurrence (1 = Rare, 2 = Unlikely, 3 = Possible, 4 = Likely, 5 = Almost Certain)
- $L$ = Likelihood of the hazard leading to harm (1–5 scale)

**Step 1:** Multiply the three indices.

$$
R = S \times P \times L
$$

**Step 2:** Example — During an audit, an auditor identified that the flame arrestor on a solvent storage tank was missing (Severity = 5, Probability = 4, Likelihood = 3).

$$
R = 5 \times 4 \times 3 = 60
$$

**Step 3:** Risk Categorization Table (Standard KTU Pattern):

| Risk Score $R$ | Risk Level | Action Required |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R \geq 50$ | **Critical / Unacceptable** | Immediate shutdown & corrective action |
| $25 \leq R < 50$ | **High** | Urgent action within 7 days |
| $10 \leq R < 25$ | **Medium** | Planned action within 30 days |
| $4 \leq R < 10$ | **Low** | Monitor and address within 90 days |
| $R < 4$ | **Negligible** | Routine review |

**Step 4:** Interpretation. With $R = 60$, the missing flame arrestor is classified as **Critical**, requiring immediate plant shutdown and rectification.

### 3.3 Derivation: Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA-Based Audits

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is widely used in FMEA-based safety audits, particularly in mechanical and process integrity audits.

**Definitions:**
- $S$ = Severity (1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence (1–10)
- $D$ = Detection difficulty (1–10)

**Step 1:** The RPN is computed as:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 2:** Example — A safety relief valve in a hydrocarbon service is found to be untested for 5 years. Severity = 9 (potential explosion), Occurrence = 4 (degradation common), Detection = 7 (no current inspection program).

$$
\text{RPN} = 9 \times 4 \times 7 = 252
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation. An RPN > 200 is treated as a **Major Non-Conformity** in a KTU-graded safety audit, requiring immediate corrective action.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selecting Audit Type Based on Plant Lifecycle

| Plant Stage | Most Appropriate Audit Type | Why? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Design Phase | **Design Safety Review / HAZOP** | Identify hazards before construction |
| Construction Phase | **Construction Safety Audit** | Verify code compliance, QC of materials |
| Pre-Commissioning | **Pre-Startup Safety Audit (PSSA)** | Final check before hazardous material introduction |
| Operational (Routine) | **Compliance + Process Safety Audit** | Continuous assurance |
| After Major Modification | **Management of Change (MOC) Audit** | Re-assess altered risk profile |
| Post-Incident | **Incident-Based Audit** | Identify root cause & systemic failures |
| Decommissioning | **De-commissioning Safety Audit** | Manage dismantling hazards |

### 3.5 Sample Safety Audit Checklist (KTU Standard Format)

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hack:** Drawing a clean, structured audit checklist in your answer earns you **bonus impression points** with KTU examiners.

| S. No. | Audit Area | Audit Question | Reference Standard | Compliant? (Y/N) | Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Housekeeping | Are walkways free from obstructions? | Factory Act 1948, Sec. 11 | | |
| 2 | PPE | Are hard hats worn in all designated zones? | IS 2925 / ANSI Z89.1 | | |
| 3 | Fire Protection | Are fire extinguishers inspected monthly? | IS 2190 / NFPA 10 | | |
| 4 | Process Safety | Is the SIS tested as per SIL rating? | IEC 61511 | | |
| 5 | Electrical Safety | Is earthing verified for all motors? | IS 3043 / IEEE 80 | | |
| 6 | Emergency | Are emergency exits illuminated and unobstructed? | NBC 2016, Part 4 | | |
| 7 | Training | Are operators trained on HAZOP outcomes? | OSHA PSM 1910.119 | | |
| 8 | Permit-to-Work | Is hot work permit issued for all welding jobs? | OISD-STD-105 | | |

### 3.6 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A safety audit was conducted on a 250-item checklist for a petrochemical plant. The audit findings were:
- 15 Major Non-Conformities
- 35 Minor Non-Conformities
- 20 Observations
- The remaining items were compliant

Calculate:
1. Compliance Percentage
2. Total number of non-conformities (Major + Minor)
3. Non-Conformity Density, given the audit consumed 80 man-hours

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the number of compliant items.
$$
N_c = 250 - 15 - 35 - 20 = 180
$$

**Step 2:** Compliance Percentage.
$$
\text{CP} = \left( \dfrac{180}{250} \right) \times 100 = 72\%
$$

**Step 3:** Total Non-Conformities.
$$
\text{Total NC} = 15 + 35 = 50
$$

**Step 4:** Non-Conformity Density.
$$
\text{NCD} = \dfrac{50}{80} = 0.625 \text{ NCs/hour}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation. With a compliance of only 72%, the plant falls **below** the industry benchmark of 95%. The 15 major NCs require **immediate shutdown-level interventions**, and the 35 minor NCs must be closed within 30 days.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Safety Audit Process Flow Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **complete life cycle of a safety audit** from initiation to closure, mapped against the PDCA cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Audit Trigger] --> B{Define Scope and Objectives}
    B --> C[Select Audit Team]
    C --> D[Develop Audit Plan]
    D --> E[Prepare Checklists and Questionnaires]
    E --> F[Issue Notification to Auditee]
    F --> G[Opening Meeting]
    G --> H[On-Site Inspection and Tour]
    H --> I[Document Review]
    I --> J[Personnel Interviews]
    J --> K[Evidence Collection]
    K --> L[Identify Non-Conformities]
    L --> M[Classify Findings: Major, Minor, Observation]
    M --> N[Closing Meeting: Present Findings]
    N --> O[Prepare Audit Report]
    O --> P[Develop Corrective Action Plan CAPA]
    P --> Q[Distribute Report to Management]
    Q --> R[Verify Corrective Actions Implemented]
    R --> S{All NCs Closed?}
    S -- No --> T[Schedule Follow-up Audit]
    T --> R
    S -- Yes --> U[Audit Closure and Documentation]
    U --> V[End: Lessons Learned and Improvement]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Safety Audit System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Regulatory and Corporate Standards] --> B[Audit Planning Module]
    C[Plant Process and Equipment Data] --> B
    D[Historical Incident Records] --> B
    B --> E[Audit Checklist Generator]
    E --> F[On-Site Audit Execution]
    F --> G[Evidence Database]
    G --> H[Risk Analysis Engine]
    H --> I[Findings Classification]
    I --> J[Corrective Action Tracker]
    J --> K[Management Dashboard]
    K --> L[Regulatory Submission and Certification]
    L --> M[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    M --> B
```

### 4.3 Audit Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Safety Audits] --> B[Internal Audit]
    A --> C[External Audit]
    A --> D[Regulatory Audit]
    B --> E[Compliance Audit]
    B --> F[Process Safety Audit]
    B --> G[Pre-Startup Safety Audit PSSA]
    C --> H[ISO 45001 Certification Audit]
    C --> I[ISO 14001 EMS Audit]
    D --> J[Factory Inspectorate Audit]
    D --> K[Pollution Control Board Audit]
    D --> L[OISD Audit]
```

### 4.4 Corrective Action Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Audit Finding Raised] --> B{Severity}
    B -->|Major| C[Immediate Action within 72 hours]
    B -->|Minor| D[Planned Action within 30 days]
    B -->|Observation| E[Improvement within 90 days]
    C --> F[Root Cause Analysis]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Corrective Action Plan CAPA]
    G --> H[Implementation by Auditee]
    H --> I[Verification Audit]
    I --> J{Action Effective?}
    J -- No --> F
    J -- Yes --> K[Closure and Documentation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define Safety Audit. List any four objectives of a safety audit.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Safety Audit* is a **systematic, structured, documented, and objective evaluation** of an organization's safety management systems, processes, equipment, and procedures against predetermined criteria such as statutory regulations, industry standards (e.g., OISD, IS, NFPA, OSHA PSM), and corporate safety policies.

**Four Objectives of a Safety Audit:**

1. **Compliance Verification:** To confirm adherence to statutory and corporate safety requirements.
2. **Hazard Identification:** To uncover unsafe conditions, acts, and procedural gaps before they cause incidents.
3. **System Evaluation:** To assess the effectiveness of the existing Safety Management System (SMS).
4. **Improvement & Corrective Action:** To drive continuous improvement through documented corrective measures and management follow-up.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Distinguish between Internal Safety Audit and External Safety Audit.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Internal Safety Audit | External Safety Audit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Performed By** | Own employees of the organization | Independent third-party auditors |
| **Independence** | Limited — auditor is from same organization | High — auditor is independent of auditee |
| **Purpose** | Routine compliance & housekeeping check | Statutory verification, certification, due diligence |
| **Frequency** | More frequent (monthly/quarterly) | Less frequent (annual) or event-driven |
| **Cost** | Low (only internal resources) | High (consultancy fees) |
| **Bias** | May have some bias | Objective and unbiased |
| **Examples** | Plant supervisor inspections, EHS team audits | ISO 45001 certification audit, Factory Inspectorate visit |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Set]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **various types of safety audits** with their scope and application. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for conducting a safety audit**, including pre-audit, on-site, and post-audit activities. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Types of Safety Audits (7 Marks):**

Safety audits can be classified on the basis of **performer**, **scope**, and **frequency**.

1. **Compliance Audit:** Verifies adherence to statutory regulations such as the Factories Act 1948, MSIHC Rules 1989, EP Act 1986, OISD standards, and OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119). It is mandatory and conducted periodically by the Factory Inspectorate or internal teams.

2. **Pre-Startup Safety Audit (PSSA / PSSR):** Performed before commissioning a new plant or after a major modification. It confirms that equipment, procedures, training, and emergency systems are fully ready before hazardous materials are introduced.

3. **Process Safety Audit:** Focuses on the technical integrity of chemical processes — reviewing HAZOP studies, LOPA assessments, Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS), relief valve sizing, and process control logic.

4. **Occupational Health & Hygiene Audit:** Examines worker exposure to noise, dust, fumes, heat, vibration, ergonomics, ventilation, and PPE adequacy.

5. **Environmental Safety Audit:** Reviews emissions, effluents, solid waste handling, and Environmental Management System (EMS) compliance as per ISO 14001.

6. **Management System Audit:** Evaluates the Safety Management System itself — policy, organization, planning, implementation, checking, and management review (PDCA).

7. **External / Certification Audit:** Conducted by accredited third-party bodies (e.g., for ISO 45001, ISO 14001) for unbiased certification and regulatory submission.

**(b) Step-by-Step Safety Audit Procedure (7 Marks):**

The audit procedure is organized into **three major phases**.

**Phase 1 — Pre-Audit (Planning):**
1. Define the **scope, objectives, and criteria** of the audit.
2. **Appoint the audit team** — a Lead Auditor and 2–4 competent members independent of the area.
3. Develop the **audit plan, schedule**, and **checklists**.
4. Issue **formal notification** to the auditee with document requests.

**Phase 2 — On-Site (Execution):**
5. Conduct an **opening meeting** to confirm scope and introduce the team.
6. Perform a **site tour and physical inspection** of equipment, housekeeping, signage, and PPE use.
7. **Review documents** — SOPs, logbooks, training records, maintenance records, incident reports.
8. **Interview personnel** at all levels — operators, supervisors, contractors, managers.
9. **Collect evidence** — photographs, copies of records, witness statements.
10. Conduct a **closing meeting** to present preliminary findings.

**Phase 3 — Post-Audit (Reporting & Follow-up):**
11. Prepare the **audit report** with findings classified as Major, Minor, or Observation.
12. Develop a **Corrective Action Plan (CAPA)** with responsibilities and timelines.
13. Distribute the report to **senior management, EHS committee, and regulators** as required.
14. **Verify corrective actions** through follow-up audits.
15. **Close the audit** with sign-off once all NCs are addressed and document lessons learned.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is an **Audit Checklist**? Explain its structure and importance with a suitable example. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The following data was obtained from a safety audit of a chemical plant. Calculate the **Compliance Percentage**, **Total Non-Conformities**, and **Non-Conformity Density** (given audit duration = 60 man-hours). (7 Marks)

| Audit Item Type | Count |
| :--- | :---: |
| Major Non-Conformities | 12 |
| Minor Non-Conformities | 28 |
| Observations | 15 |
| Compliant Items | 245 |
| **Total Items Audited** | **300** |

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Audit Checklist (7 Marks):**

An **Audit Checklist** is a structured list of questions or items used by auditors to evaluate compliance against predetermined standards systematically. It ensures **consistency, completeness, objectivity, and traceability** of the audit process.

**Structure of a Safety Audit Checklist:**

A professional checklist contains the following columns:
- **Serial Number** — unique identifier for each item.
- **Audit Area** — Mechanical, Electrical, Fire, Process Safety, Housekeeping, etc.
- **Audit Question** — what is being checked.
- **Reference Standard** — IS 2190, NFPA 10, OISD-105, etc.
- **Observation** — auditor's actual finding.
- **Compliance Status** — Compliant / NC Major / NC Minor / Observation / N/A.
- **Auditor Remarks and Risk Level**.
- **Photograph / Evidence Reference**.

**Example:**

> *"Are fire extinguishers inspected monthly and tagged?"*
> *Reference: IS 2190 / NFPA 10*
> *Observation: Extinguisher near Reactor R-201 not tagged since 6 months.*
> *Status: Non-Conformity (Minor).*

**Importance:**
- Ensures **no critical area is missed** during the audit.
- Provides **legal and documentary evidence**.
- **Standardizes** audits across multiple plants.
- Saves time and improves **auditor confidence**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given:
- Total audit items, $N_t = 300$
- Major NC = 12
- Minor NC = 28
- Observations = 15
- Compliant items = 245
- Audit man-hours = 60

**Step 1:** Verify total.
$$
N_t = 12 + 28 + 15 + 245 = 300 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2:** Compliance Percentage.
$$
\text{CP} = \left( \dfrac{245}{300} \right) \times 100 = 81.67\%
$$

**Step 3:** Total Non-Conformities.
$$
\text{Total NC} = 12 + 28 = 40
$$

**Step 4:** Non-Conformity Density.
$$
\text{NCD} = \dfrac{40}{60} = 0.667 \text{ NCs per hour}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.
The plant's compliance is **81.67%**, which is **below the industry benchmark of 95%**. The 12 Major NCs demand **immediate corrective action within 72 hours**, and the 28 Minor NCs must be closed within 30 days. Management must initiate a root-cause analysis to identify systemic weaknesses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Skipping the definition:** When asked about safety audits, students often jump straight to the types. Always begin with the **formal definition** — *"systematic, documented, objective evaluation against predetermined criteria."* This earns you **1–2 easy marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up audit types:** A common error is confusing **PSSR (Pre-Startup Safety Review)** with **HAZOP**. PSSR is an audit conducted before commissioning; HAZOP is a hazard analysis technique, not an audit.
> 3. **Forgetting the closing meeting:** Many students miss mentioning the **closing meeting** as part of the on-site audit phase. This is a frequent deduction area.
> 4. **Unit mistakes in numericals:** In RPN or Risk Score calculations, **forgetting to state the risk category** after calculating the numerical value loses 1 mark easily.
> 5. **Neglecting follow-up:** A safety audit is **not complete** without verification of corrective actions. Always mention the **follow-up/closure step**.
> 6. **Plagiarism risk:** Never reproduce copied text from the internet. KTU uses Turnitin-style tools; **paraphrase every definition in your own words**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 60-Second Summary**

- ☐ **Safety Audit = Systematic, Documented, Objective Evaluation against Predetermined Criteria.**
- ☐ **Seven Major Types:** Compliance, PSSA, Process Safety, Occupational Health, Environmental, Management System, External Certification.
- ☐ **Three Phases:** Pre-Audit (Planning) → On-Site (Execution) → Post-Audit (Reporting & Follow-up).
- ☐ **PDCA Cycle** is the underlying philosophy of every audit program.
- ☐ **Audit Team** must be **independent, competent, and trained**; led by a Lead Auditor.
- ☐ **Checklist Structure:** S.No. | Area | Question | Standard | Observation | Status | Remarks.
- ☐ **Findings Classification:** **Major NC** (immediate danger, 24–72 h) → **Minor NC** (30 days) → **Observation** (90 days) → **OFI** (discretionary).
- ☐ **Key Formulas:** $\text{CP} = (N_c / N_t) \times 100$; $R = S \times P \times L$; $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$.
- ☐ **Risk Categories:** $R \geq 50$ = Critical; $25 \leq R < 50$ = High; $10 \leq R < 25$ = Medium; $R < 10$ = Low.
- ☐ **Statutory Standards to Remember:** Factories Act 1948, MSIHC Rules 1989, EP Act 1986, OISD Standards, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, ISO 45001, ISO 14001, IEC 61511, NFPA, IS Codes.
- ☐ **Audit is incomplete without:** Opening meeting → Site tour → Document review → Interviews → Closing meeting → Report → CAPA → Verification → Closure.
- ☐ **Always close the loop:** A safety audit is only valuable when corrective actions are **verified and closed**.

> **End of Module 4 — Safety Audits | OEBBT723 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Safety Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Analysis** is a systematic, structured, and documented methodology used to identify, evaluate, and control hazards throughout the life cycle of a process plant. It combines qualitative reasoning (HAZOP, What-If) with quantitative probabilistic logic (Fault Tree, Event Tree) to estimate accident frequencies and consequences, enabling risk-informed decision-making in accordance with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM) and IEC 61511 (Functional Safety) standards.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Safety Analysis is treated as the **operational backbone of Process Safety Management (PSM)**. It is not a single technique but a *family of analytical tools* applied sequentially during the design, commissioning, operation, and decommissioning phases of any chemical, petrochemical, or process plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Safety Analysis matters for KTU Examiners:**
> The KTU 2024 scheme evaluates students on their ability to **select the correct methodology** based on the plant life-cycle stage and the **type of hazard** (process, mechanical, human-factor). Marks are awarded for linking the technique to its specific application context — not just rote definitions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Medical Diagnosis" View

Imagine a doctor treating a critically ill patient. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor performs several diagnostic tests:

| Diagnostic Step | Safety Analysis Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Patient history check | **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** | Initial screening |
| Blood test & MRI scan | **HAZOP Study** | Detailed systemic examination |
| Specialist consultations | **What-If Analysis** | Expert brainstorming of failure modes |
| Genetic risk profiling | **FMEA / FMECA** | Component-level failure prioritization |
| Probability of relapse | **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Quantitative top-event probability |
| Recovery pathway mapping | **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** | Post-initiating event outcomes |

Just as a doctor combines multiple diagnostic tools to form a complete clinical picture, a process safety engineer uses a **portfolio of safety analysis techniques** to build a defensible safety case for a plant.

## 1.3 Classification of Safety Analysis Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Safety Analysis (CCPS Classification)**

1. **Qualitative Methods** — Identify *what* can go wrong.
   - HAZOP, What-If, Checklist Analysis, PHA
2. **Semi-Quantitative Methods** — Rank *how bad* it can be.
   - FMEA, FMECA, Dow's Fire & Explosion Index, Mond Index
3. **Quantitative Methods (QRA / LOPA)** — Calculate *how likely* it is.
   - Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA), Bow-Tie, Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)

## 1.4 Core Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standard metrics must be memorized by every KTU 2024 student:

- **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** = (Number of fatalities × 10⁸) / (Total exposure hours) — Unitless index
- **Individual Risk (IR)** = **1 × 10⁻⁶** per year (typical acceptable tolerability limit per HSE, UK)
- **F-N Curve** = Societal risk plot of cumulative frequency (F) vs. number of fatalities (N)
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** = Discrete level (1 to 4) per IEC 61508 for specifying safety instrumented function reliability
- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** — Target values: **SIL 1: 10⁻² to 10⁻¹**, **SIL 2: 10⁻³ to 10⁻²**, **SIL 3: 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³**, **SIL 4: 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)

PHA is the **first-pass safety review** performed during the conceptual design stage. It is an inductive (forward-looking) brainstorming technique that uses checklists compiled from past accident histories and engineering judgment.

### Structured Logic Steps:
1. Review the process description, raw materials, products, and operating conditions.
2. Divide the plant into logical units (e.g., reactor, storage, distillation).
3. For each unit, identify hazards across **five canonical categories**:
   - **Thermal** (fire, runaway reaction)
   - **Chemical** (toxic release, corrosion)
   - **Mechanical** (pressure rupture, vibration)
   - **Human-factor** (operator error)
   - **Environmental** (gas dispersion, spillage)
4. Assign an initial severity and likelihood ranking.
5. Recommend preliminary safeguards.

## 2.2 HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZOP is the most frequently asked 14-mark question in KTU ESE.** Master the guidewords and their meanings.

HAZOP is a **systematic, team-based, deductive technique** formalized by the **ICI (Imperial Chemical Industries)** in the 1960s and later standardized under **IEC 61882**.

### The 7 Standard HAZOP Guidewords:

| Guide Word | Meaning | Parameter Deviation Example |
|---|---|---|
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of design intent | **No** flow in a transfer line |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | **More** pressure in a reactor |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | **Less** cooling water flow |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | **Reverse** flow direction |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity occurs | Air ingress **as well as** nitrogen |
| **PART OF** | Achievement only partially | Only **part of** composition reached |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | **Other than** intended phase |

### HAZOP Process Parameters (Standard List):
Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Mixing, Reaction, Time, Sequence, Start/Stop, Maintenance, Sampling, Relief, Instrumentation.

### HAZOP Node Procedure:
1. Define **node** (a section of pipe + equipment with uniform intent).
2. Apply each guideword to each parameter.
3. Identify **causes** (deviations), **consequences** (outcomes), and **safeguards**.
4. Generate **recommendations** and **actions**.

## 2.3 What-If Analysis

A less formal, more flexible brainstorming technique. The team raises hypothetical questions of the form: *"What if the cooling pump fails?"*, *"What if the operator opens valve V-101 manually during a shutdown?"*

**Strength:** Rapid, low-cost, ideal for early-stage design or screening.
**Weakness:** Highly dependent on team expertise; no structured guidewords → high chance of missing deviations.

## 2.4 FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)

Component-level, bottom-up, inductive analysis. Originally developed by the **U.S. military (MIL-STD-1629)** in 1949, later adopted by automotive (AIAG-VDA FMEA, 2019) and process industries.

### The RPN (Risk Priority Number) Formula:

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

Where:
- **S** = Severity (1 to 10)
- **O** = Occurrence (1 to 10)
- **D** = Detection (1 to 10)

When the analysis includes a **Criticality** term, it is termed **FMECA** (Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis).

## 2.5 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)

Developed by **H.A. Watson (1961)** for the U.S. Air Force **Minuteman missile** system. Standardized under **IEC 61025**.

FTA is a **deductive (top-down)**, logic-based, quantitative method. You begin with a **Top Event** (e.g., "Reactor Overpressure Rupture") and decompose it using Boolean logic gates into **Basic Events** (component failures, human errors, external events).

### Standard FTA Logic Gates:

| Gate Symbol | Logic Expression | Top Event Occurs When |
|---|---|---|
| **AND** | $T = A \cap B$ | **All** input events occur simultaneously |
| **OR** | $T = A \cup B$ | **Any one or more** input events occur |
| **Voting (k/n)** | $T = \binom{n}{k}$ | Exactly **k** of **n** inputs fail |

## 2.6 Event Tree Analysis (ETA)

Inductive (bottom-up), used to model the **propagation of consequences** *after* an initiating event occurs. Each branch represents a success/failure of a safety function.

The endpoint of each branch is a **consequence category** (e.g., safe shutdown, minor release, major fire, catastrophic explosion).

## 2.7 Bow-Tie Analysis

A modern hybrid that fuses FTA (left side, causes) with ETA (right side, consequences), anchored at a central **Top Event**. Each side carries **escalation factors** and **escalation path controls** (proactive + reactive barriers). Standardized under **CENTER FOR CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY (CCPS)** guidelines.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-day revision. Every formula here has been asked in a KTU ESE question paper since 2019.**

| # | Method | Formula / Relation | Key Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FMEA | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | S=Severity, O=Occurrence, D=Detection | Dimensionless |
| 2 | FTA AND | $P_{TOP} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Probability of AND gate = product of input probabilities | Dimensionless |
| 3 | FTA OR | $P_{TOP} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)$ | Probability of OR gate (rare-event approximation: $P_{TOP} \approx \sum P_i$) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | FTA (k/n voting) | $P = \sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j} p^j (1-p)^{n-j}$ | Exactly k of n identical components fail | Dimensionless |
| 5 | ETA | $P_i = P_{\text{init}} \times \prod P_{\text{branch},j}$ | Branch probability = product of all preceding branch probabilities | Dimensionless |
| 6 | LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) | $\text{Mitigated Frequency} = \text{Initiating Event} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$ | PFD = Probability of Failure on Demand per IEC 61511 | /year |
| 7 | SIL Determination | $\text{Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)} = \dfrac{f_{\text{initiating}}}{f_{\text{tolerable}}}$ | Inverse of PFD for SIF | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Individual Risk | $\text{IR} = \sum_i f_i \times p_i$ | f = frequency, p = probability of fatality given accident | /year |
| 9 | F-N Curve (Societal Risk) | $\text{Expected Fatalities} = \sum f_i \times N_i$ | Cumulative frequency vs N | — |
| 10 | Coincidence Probability | $P_{\text{both}} = P_1 \times P_2$ | Two independent events occur together | Dimensionless |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas power real-world decisions like whether to install a SIL-3 emergency shutdown valve (cost ~₹50 lakhs) versus accepting a SIL-2 instrumented system. The economic breakeven is determined precisely through LOPA.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation: Fault Tree Boolean Algebra

**Problem Statement:** A reactor overpressure rupture event **T** has three immediate causes based on operator logs:
- **A** = Pressure transmitter fails high
- **B** = Pressure safety valve fails to open on demand
- **C** = Operator fails to respond to high-pressure alarm

The logic is: *The rupture occurs if (B fails) AND (either A fails OR C fails), because if A works the alarm alerts the operator and B works as the automatic relief.*

### Step 1: Translate English Logic to Boolean Algebra

The English statement is parsed into a structured Boolean form:

$$T = B \cap (A \cup C)$$

This means: T occurs when **B** occurs AND at least one of (**A** or **C**) occurs.

### Step 2: Expand Using the Distributive Law

Applying the **distributive law** of Boolean algebra: $X \cap (Y \cup Z) = (X \cap Y) \cup (X \cap Z)$

$$T = (B \cap A) \cup (B \cap C)$$

### Step 3: List the Minimal Cut Sets

A **cut set** is a combination of basic events whose simultaneous failure causes the top event. A **minimal cut set** has no redundant events.

From the expanded Boolean expression, the **two minimal cut sets** are:

$$\text{MCS}_1 = \{A, B\} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{MCS}_2 = \{B, C\}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Quantitative Probability

Let the failure probabilities per demand be:
- $P_A = 0.01$
- $P_B = 0.005$
- $P_C = 0.10$

Since the two minimal cut sets are **disjoint** (they share event B but are otherwise independent), we apply the **exact OR-gate formula**:

$$P_T = P(A \cap B) + P(B \cap C) - P(A \cap B \cap C)$$

Since the events are statistically independent:

$$P_T = (P_A \times P_B) + (P_B \times P_C) - (P_A \times P_B \times P_C)$$

### Step 5: Substitute Numerical Values

$$P_T = (0.01 \times 0.005) + (0.005 \times 0.10) - (0.01 \times 0.005 \times 0.10)$$

$$P_T = 0.00005 + 0.0005 - 0.00000005$$

$$\boxed{P_T \approx 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per demand}}$$

### Step 6: Compare with Rare-Event Approximation

For low probabilities, $P_T \approx P_A P_B + P_B P_C = 5.5 \times 10^{-4}$ — the triple intersection term is negligible.

If the plant demands the overpressure protection system **once per year**, the top event frequency is approximately $5.5 \times 10^{-4}$ per year, which exceeds the typical tolerable risk of $10^{-6}$ per year → **SIL-3 upgrade is mandatory**.

## 3.2 Derivative Derivation: (k/n) Voting Gate Probability

**Problem Statement:** A safety instrumented function uses **2-out-of-3 (2oo3)** voted pressure transmitters. Each transmitter has an independent failure probability $p = 0.02$. Find the system failure probability.

The system fails if **2 or more** transmitters fail:

$$P_{2oo3} = \binom{3}{2} p^2 (1-p)^{1} + \binom{3}{3} p^3 (1-p)^{0}$$

Substituting values:

$$P_{2oo3} = 3 \times (0.02)^2 \times 0.98 + 1 \times (0.02)^3 \times 1$$

$$P_{2oo3} = 3 \times 0.0004 \times 0.98 + 0.000008$$

$$P_{2oo3} = 0.001176 + 0.000008$$

$$\boxed{P_{2oo3} \approx 1.184 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Compared with a single transmitter ($p = 0.02$), the voted system is approximately **17 times more reliable**, validating the use of redundancy in SIL-2/3 applications.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Safety Analysis Method to Apply?

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is a frequent 14-mark KTU question — "Compare and select appropriate safety analysis methods for the following scenarios."**

| Analysis Method | Stage of Plant Life Cycle | Hazard Type | Output Type | Team Size | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PHA** | Conceptual / Front-End Engineering | Broad hazards, siting | Qualitative + Initial Risk | 3–5 | Low |
| **Checklist** | Any phase, periodic | Standard known hazards | Pass/Fail | 1–3 | Very Low |
| **What-If** | Early design, modifications | Procedural & operational | Qualitative | 4–8 | Low |
| **HAZOP** | Detailed design, pre-commissioning, periodic | Process deviations | Qualitative + Recommendations | 5–8 | Medium |
| **FMEA / FMECA** | Equipment design, procurement | Component-level failure | RPN / Criticality ranking | 3–6 | Medium |
| **FTA** | Detailed design, SIL determination | Complex multi-cause failures | Boolean model + Quantitative probability | 2–4 | High |
| **ETA** | Emergency planning, LOPA | Post-initiating event propagation | Frequency of outcomes | 2–4 | Medium |
| **Bow-Tie** | Operations, barrier management | Major accident hazards | Visual barrier map + KPIs | 4–6 | Medium |
| **QRA / LOPA** | Design approval, regulatory submission | Major accident hazards | Individual & societal risk | 5–10 | Very High |

### Step-by-Step Selection Logic for KTU Examiners:

1. **What stage of life cycle?** → PHA for conceptual; HAZOP for detailed.
2. **Is the hazard process-related?** → HAZOP.
3. **Is the hazard equipment-component-related?** → FMEA.
4. **Do you need to compute top-event probability?** → FTA.
5. **Do you need to map outcome consequences?** → ETA.
6. **Do you need both + barrier management?** → Bow-Tie.
7. **Do you need regulatory numeric risk targets?** → LOPA / QRA.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAZOP Node-Based Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Define Study Scope and Objectives] --> A2[Divide Plant into P&ID Nodes]
    A2 --> A3[Select First Node]
    A3 --> A4[Define Design Intent for Node]
    A4 --> A5[Apply First Guideword to First Parameter]
    A5 --> A6{Deviation Identified?}
    A6 -- No --> A7[Apply Next Guideword/Parameter]
    A6 -- Yes --> A8[Document Cause of Deviation]
    A8 --> A9[Document Consequence]
    A9 --> A10{Existing Safeguards Sufficient?}
    A10 -- Yes --> A11[Record and Continue]
    A10 -- No --> A12[Generate Recommendation and Action]
    A12 --> A13[Assign Responsible Person and Deadline]
    A13 --> A7
    A11 --> A7
    A7 --> A14{All Nodes and Parameters Covered?}
    A14 -- No --> A3
    A14 -- Yes --> A15[Compile HAZOP Report and Sign Off]
    A15 --> A16[End]
```

## 4.2 Fault Tree Analysis Architecture (AND-OR Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TopEvent[Top Event: Reactor Overpressure Rupture]
    Gate1[AND Gate]
    Gate2[OR Gate]
    Gate3[Voting 2 out of 3]
    BasicA[Basic Event A: Pressure Transmitter Fails High]
    BasicB[Basic Event B: Safety Valve Fails to Open]
    BasicC[Basic Event C: Operator Fails to Respond]
    BasicD[Basic Event D: Sensor 1 Fails]
    BasicE[Basic Event E: Sensor 2 Fails]
    BasicF[Basic Event F: Sensor 3 Fails]

    TopEvent --> Gate1
    Gate1 --> Gate2
    Gate1 --> BasicB
    Gate2 --> BasicA
    Gate2 --> BasicC
    Gate3 --> BasicD
    Gate3 --> BasicE
    Gate3 --> BasicF
```

> **Interpretation:** This tree shows that the top event requires both (1) at least one of A or C to occur AND (2) Basic Event B to occur. The 2oo3 voted sensor bank is shown as a separate branch.

## 4.3 Bow-Tie Analysis Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT[Left Side: Threat Pathways]
        T1[Threat 1: Pipe Rupture] -->|P1| TE
        T2[Threat 2: Pump Overpressure] -->|P2| TE
        T3[Threat 3: Thermal Expansion] -->|P3| TE
    end

    TE[Top Event: Loss of Containment]

    TE -->|P4| C1[Consequence 1: Toxic Gas Dispersion]
    TE -->|P5| C2[Consequence 2: Pool Fire]
    TE -->|P6| C3[Consequence 3: Vapour Cloud Explosion]

    subgraph PROACTIVEPROACTIVE[Proactive Barriers]
        B1[Preventive Barrier 1: Process Design] -->|Blocks P1| T1
        B2[Preventive Barrier 2: Maintenance Program] -->|Blocks P3| T3
    end

    subgraph REACTIVEREACTIVE[Reactive Barriers]
        RB1[Mitigation Barrier 1: Emergency Shutdown] -->|Blocks P4| TE
        RB2[Mitigation Barrier 2: Fire Water Deluge] -->|Blocks P5| C2
    end
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic for Safety Analysis Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start: Hazard Identification Required?] --> Q2{Is the Hazard Process-Related?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is Quantitative Probability Required?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Need Component-Level Failure Mode?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q5[Use FTA and ETA combined with LOPA]
    Q3 -- No --> Q6[Use HAZOP Study]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q7[Use FMEA and FMECA]
    Q4 -- No --> Q8[Use What-If Analysis]
    Q5 --> Q9[Final Output: Numeric Risk Profile]
    Q6 --> Q9
    Q7 --> Q9
    Q8 --> Q9
    Q9 --> Q10[End: Risk Informed Decision]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define HAZOP. List any four standard guidewords used in a HAZOP study with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a systematic, team-based, deductive safety analysis technique used to identify process hazards and operability problems by applying a structured set of guidewords to process parameters.

**Four Guidewords with Examples (2 Marks):**

| Guideword | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **NO** | Complete negation | **No** flow in transfer line → pump failure |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | **More** pressure → blocked outlet |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | **Less** temperature → cooling water failure |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | **Reverse** flow → check valve failure |

*(One mark for correct definition; one mark for guideword listing; one mark for valid examples.)*

---

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) and Event Tree Analysis (ETA).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) | Event Tree Analysis (ETA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Logic Direction** | Deductive (top-down) | Inductive (bottom-up) |
| **Starting Point** | Top Event | Initiating Event |
| **Gates Used** | AND, OR, Voting | Branch probabilities |
| **Output** | Boolean expression + cut sets | Consequence frequency |
| **Application** | SIL determination, system reliability | Emergency planning, LOPA |

*(One mark each for: 1. Direction difference, 2. Gate vs Branch logic, 3. Application difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question 1A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step methodology of conducting a HAZOP study** for an ammonia storage tank system. Mention the team composition, guidewords used, and how recommendations are tracked. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **Fault Tree** for a "Fire in Reactor Jacket" scenario with at least **two AND gates and one OR gate**, and determine the minimal cut sets. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer 1A:

**Part (a) — HAZOP Methodology for Ammonia Storage Tank (7 Marks):**

1. **Define Scope and Objectives (0.5 Mark):** Identify the ammonia storage tank, associated pipework, valves, pressure relief devices, and instrument air system.
2. **Team Composition (0.5 Mark):** A multidisciplinary team of 5–8 members including: process engineer (leader), operations supervisor, instrument engineer, safety officer, and a scribe.
3. **Obtain Updated P&ID and Process Data (0.5 Mark):** Operating pressure, temperature, design pressure, relief valve set pressure, ammonia inventory.
4. **Divide into Nodes (1 Mark):**
   - Node 1: Inlet pipework (liquid ammonia feed)
   - Node 2: Storage tank shell
   - Node 3: Vapor return line
   - Node 4: Relief and flare header
5. **Apply Guidewords to Parameters (2 Marks):** Use guidewords (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, OTHER THAN, PART OF) on parameters (Pressure, Temperature, Level, Flow, Composition).
   - Example: **MORE PRESSURE** on Node 2 → Cause: blocked vapor return valve → Consequence: tank rupture → Safeguard: pressure relief valve, rupture disc.
   - Example: **LESS TEMPERATURE** on Node 2 → Cause: refrigeration system failure → Consequence: pressure drop, possible vacuum collapse → Safeguard: vacuum breaker.
6. **Document Findings in HAZOP Worksheet (0.5 Mark):** Use standardized columns: Deviation, Cause, Consequence, Safeguard, Recommendation, Action.
7. **Generate Recommendations and Assign Actionees (1 Mark):** Each recommendation gets a responsible person, a deadline, and a tracking ID. A follow-up HAZOP meeting verifies closure.
8. **Sign-off and Report Compilation (1 Mark):** Final HAZOP report signed by all team members and the plant manager, archived for the lifetime of the plant.

**Part (b) — Fault Tree Construction (7 Marks):**

**Top Event:** Fire in Reactor Jacket

**Logical Decomposition:**
- The jacket fire occurs if **Cooling water flow is lost AND Ignition source is present** (AND gate G1).
- Cooling water flow is lost if **Pump fails OR Valve closes inadvertently OR Pipe ruptures** (OR gate G2).
- Ignition source is present if **Hot surface temperature exceeds auto-ignition OR Static discharge occurs** (OR gate G3).
- Additionally, the jacket contains oil that auto-ignites only if **Pump seal leaks AND Temperature sensor fails** (AND gate G4).

**Fault Tree Structure:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TopEvent[Top Event: Fire in Reactor Jacket]
    G1[AND Gate]
    G2[OR Gate]
    G3[OR Gate]
    G4[AND Gate]
    A1[Basic Event: Cooling Pump Fails]
    A2[Basic Event: Cooling Valve Closed Inadvertently]
    A3[Basic Event: Cooling Pipe Ruptures]
    A4[Basic Event: Hot Surface Exceeds Auto-Ignition Temp]
    A5[Basic Event: Static Discharge Occurs]
    A6[Basic Event: Pump Seal Leaks]
    A7[Basic Event: Temperature Sensor Fails]

    TopEvent --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G1 --> G3
    G2 --> A1
    G2 --> A2
    G2 --> A3
    G3 --> A4
    G3 --> A5
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> A6
    G4 --> A7
```

**Boolean Expression (1.5 Marks):**

$$T = [A_1 \cup A_2 \cup A_3] \cap [A_4 \cup A_5 \cup (A_6 \cap A_7)]$$

**Minimal Cut Sets (1.5 Marks):**

Expanding via distributive law:

$$T = (A_1 \cap A_4) \cup (A_1 \cap A_5) \cup (A_1 \cap A_6 \cap A_7) \cup (A_2 \cap A_4) \cup (A_2 \cap A_5) \cup (A_2 \cap A_6 \cap A_7) \cup (A_3 \cap A_4) \cup (A_3 \cap A_5) \cup (A_3 \cap A_6 \cap A_7)$$

**Minimal Cut Sets (MCS):**

| MCS Number | Cut Set | Order |
|---|---|---|
| MCS 1 | {A1, A4} | 2 |
| MCS 2 | {A1, A5} | 2 |
| MCS 3 | {A2, A4} | 2 |
| MCS 4 | {A2, A5} | 2 |
| MCS 5 | {A3, A4} | 2 |
| MCS 6 | {A3, A5} | 2 |
| MCS 7 | {A1, A6, A7} | 3 |
| MCS 8 | {A2, A6, A7} | 3 |
| MCS 9 | {A3, A6, A7} | 3 |

**Interpretation (1 Mark):** The six 2nd-order cut sets dominate the risk. The cheapest mitigation is to install a redundant cooling pump and a high-temperature interlock.

---

### Question 1B: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]** (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** methodology. For a centrifugal pump used in a hydrocarbon service, compute the RPN for the following failure modes and rank them. **(7 Marks)**

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bearing seizure | 9 | 4 | 3 |
| Mechanical seal leak | 7 | 6 | 5 |
| Impeller erosion | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| Coupling misalignment | 5 | 5 | 6 |

**(b)** Describe the **Bow-Tie Analysis** methodology with a neat sketch. Explain how proactive and reactive barriers differ. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer 1B:

**Part (a) — FMEA Methodology + RPN Computation (7 Marks):**

**FMEA Steps (3 Marks):**
1. Define the system boundary and functional block diagram.
2. Identify all components (pump casing, bearings, seal, impeller, coupling, motor).
3. For each component, list all potential failure modes.
4. Determine the effect of each failure mode on system function.
5. Assign **S** (Severity 1–10), **O** (Occurrence 1–10), **D** (Detection 1–10) ratings.
6. Compute **RPN = S × O × D**.
7. Rank by RPN and assign corrective actions.

**RPN Calculation (3 Marks):**

| Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN = S×O×D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bearing seizure | 9 | 4 | 3 | **108** |
| Mechanical seal leak | 7 | 6 | 5 | **210** |
| Impeller erosion | 6 | 7 | 8 | **336** |
| Coupling misalignment | 5 | 5 | 6 | **150** |

**Ranking (1 Mark):**
1. Impeller erosion (RPN = 336) — Highest priority
2. Mechanical seal leak (RPN = 210)
3. Coupling misalignment (RPN = 150)
4. Bearing seizure (RPN = 108) — Lowest priority in this list

**Note:** Although bearing seizure has the highest severity (S=9), its low occurrence and easy detection give it a lower RPN.

**Part (b) — Bow-Tie Analysis Methodology (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Bow-Tie is a visual risk-assessment tool that combines Fault Tree Analysis (left side, threats → top event) with Event Tree Analysis (right side, top event → consequences). The central knot is the Top Event.

**Sketch (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Threat 1] -->|Cause P1| TE[Top Event]
    T2[Threat 2] -->|Cause P2| TE
    T3[Threat 3] -->|Cause P3| TE
    TE -->|Consequence Q1| C1[Consequence 1]
    TE -->|Consequence Q2| C2[Consequence 2]
    TE -->|Consequence Q3| C3[Consequence 3]
    PB1[Proactive Barrier 1] -.Blocks P1.-> T1
    PB2[Proactive Barrier 2] -.Blocks P2.-> T2
    RB1[Reactive Barrier 1] -.Blocks Q1.-> C1
    RB2[Reactive Barrier 2] -.Blocks Q2.-> C2
```

**Proactive vs Reactive Barriers (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Proactive (Left Side) | Reactive (Right Side) |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Prevent the top event from occurring | Mitigate the consequences after the top event |
| **Examples** | Process design pressure, maintenance programs, alarms | Emergency shutdown systems, firewater deluge, evacuation |
| **Failure Type** | "Failure to prevent" | "Failure to mitigate" |
| **Performance Metric** | Frequency reduction | Consequence severity reduction |
| **Time Horizon** | Before incident | During and after incident |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU Safety Analysis Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FTA and ETA directions:** Students often write FTA as bottom-up. Deductive means *top-down*; Inductive means *bottom-up*. This costs 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting Boolean distribution in cut sets:** Writing $T = A \cap (B \cup C)$ but not expanding it to minimal cut sets is a frequent half-mark loss.
> 3. **Using guidewords as deviations (not parameters):** The correct phrasing in HAZOP is "MORE PRESSURE" (parameter = pressure, guideword = MORE), not "MORE" alone. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Neglecting the rare-event approximation:** When computing OR-gate probability for low-probability events, students forget that $P_T \approx \sum P_i$, which is the formula examiners expect for SIL calculations.
> 5. **Forgetting team composition:** A HAZOP question without mentioning the **multi-disciplinary team** (process engineer, instrument engineer, safety officer, operator, scribe) is considered incomplete. 1 mark is reserved for this.
> 6. **Quantitative FMEA mistake:** RPN must be computed for **all** four failure modes in the table; missing one row leads to deduction of 0.5 mark per omission.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Safety Analysis (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- [ ] **Safety Analysis** is a portfolio of techniques (qualitative + quantitative) used across the entire plant life cycle.
- [ ] **PHA** is the first-pass analysis during conceptual design.
- [ ] **HAZOP** uses **7 guidewords** (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) on standard process parameters.
- [ ] **HAZOP team** must be **multi-disciplinary** (process, instrument, operations, safety, scribe).
- [ ] **FMEA** computes **RPN = S × O × D**, range 1 to 1000.
- [ ] **FTA** is **deductive, top-down**, uses AND/OR/Voting gates, and yields minimal cut sets.
- [ ] **FTA AND gate:** $P_{TOP} = \prod P_i$ (multiplication).
- [ ] **FTA OR gate:** $P_{TOP} = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$ (exact), or $\approx \sum P_i$ (rare-event).
- [ ] **FTA Boolean distributive law:** $A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)$ — must be applied to find minimal cut sets.
- [ ] **ETA** is **inductive, bottom-up**, models post-initiating-event consequence propagation.
- [ ] **Bow-Tie** merges FTA (left) and ETA (right) with **proactive barriers** (prevention) and **reactive barriers** (mitigation).
- [ ] **LOPA** uses equation: $\text{Mitigated Frequency} = \text{Initiating Event} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$ for SIL determination.
- [ ] **SIL 1 to SIL 4** corresponds to **PFD** ranges of $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ per IEC 61508.
- [ ] **Individual Risk** tolerability threshold: $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-6}}$ per year (HSE, UK).
- [ ] **Acceptable accident frequency** for chemical industry: $\mathbf{10^{-5}}$ per year for injuries, $\mathbf{10^{-6}}$ per year for fatalities.
- [ ] Always select safety analysis method based on **plant life-cycle stage**, **hazard type**, and **decision output** required (qualitative vs quantitative).
- [ ] For KTU 14-mark questions, **always draw a labeled diagram** (HAZOP node, fault tree, bow-tie) — visual representation fetches easy 1–2 marks.
- [ ] **RPN ranking** in FMEA must follow descending order, with the **highest RPN action priority**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Survey and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hazard Survey and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hazard Survey and Analysis** is a systematic, structured, and iterative engineering methodology employed during the design, operation, and modification of chemical and process plants to **identify, evaluate, and control potential hazards** arising from process materials, equipment, operating conditions, and human factors. The objective is to proactively reduce risk to As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP) by examining deviations from design intent, quantifying the probability and consequence of failures, and recommending engineered or procedural safeguards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Focus):**
> *Hazard survey and analysis involves the identification of potential hazards in a process plant through a systematic review of process flow diagrams (PFDs), piping and instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs), and material properties, followed by the application of qualitative, semi-quantitative, and quantitative techniques to estimate frequency and consequence of undesired events.*

### 1.2 Hierarchical Classification of Hazard Analysis Techniques

The techniques are conventionally classified into three tiers, based on the depth of data required and the lifecycle phase of the project:

| Tier | Type | Example Techniques | Lifecycle Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Qualitative (Index-based) | Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI), Mond Index, Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PrHA) | Conceptual / R&D |
| **II** | Semi-Quantitative | Checklist, What-If Analysis, Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA), Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) | Design / Engineering |
| **III** | Quantitative (Risk-Based) | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA), Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) | Detailed Engineering / Operation |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Patient Health Checkup"

Imagine a **chemical process plant is a human body**, and a Hazard Survey is a **comprehensive medical checkup**:

- **Blood Test → Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PrHA):** A general screening that flags potential health issues early.
