- **ECG / MRI → HAZOP Study:** A detailed, node-by-node scan of every "organ" (vessel, line, instrument) to detect abnormal heartbeats (deviations in flow, pressure, temperature).
- **Genetic Screening → Fault Tree Analysis (FTA):** A deep dive into the **root causes** of a specific catastrophic disease (top event).
- **Stress Test → Event Tree Analysis (ETA):** Simulating "what happens next" once a triggering event occurs, like tracing consequences after a heart attack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exam:** Students often confuse **Hazard Identification** (finding *what can go wrong*) with **Risk Assessment** (finding *how bad and how often*). Hazard Survey answers the first; Hazard Analysis answers the second.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants

- **Flash Point (FP):** Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off vapor in sufficient concentration to form an ignitable mixture with air. Expressed in **°C**.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** Minimum temperature at which a substance ignites without an external source.
- **Lower / Upper Flammable Limit (LFL / UFL):** Volumetric % in air.
- **Standard Reference State:** **1 atm, 25 °C** for most property correlations.
- **Acceptable Risk (Individual):** **10⁻⁶ fatalities per person per year** (UK HSE benchmark).
- **NFPA Hazard Rating:** Integers from **0 (No hazard)** to **4 (Extreme hazard)**.
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PrHA)

**PrHA** is the earliest hazard evaluation performed, usually at the conceptual design or R&D stage, when detailed piping/instrumentation data is unavailable.

**Operating Logic Steps:**
1. Define the **scope and boundaries** of the study.
2. Compile a list of **process materials** with their properties (toxicity, flammability, reactivity).
3. Identify the **process equipment** and operating conditions.
4. List **potential hazards** — material, mechanical, environmental, human.
5. Identify **causes** (raw material variability, equipment failure, operational error).
6. Estimate **consequences and severity** (qualitative — Category I to IV).
7. Recommend **provisional safeguards** and assign a **Risk Score = Severity × Probability**.

> [!NOTE]
> PrHA is *not* a substitute for detailed HAZOP/FMEA; it is the **predecessor** that scopes the later, more expensive studies.

### 2.2 Dow Fire and Explosion Index (DOW F&EI)

The **Dow Chemical Company's F&EI** is a semi-quantitative ranking method that quantifies the **realistic fire, explosion, and reactivity potential** of a process unit. It is *index-based*, meaning it produces a single numerical score for prioritization, not an absolute risk value.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Select the **process unit** (logical grouping of equipment).
2. Determine the **Material Factor (MF)** from a tabulated chart based on $N_F$ (Flammability) and $N_R$ (Reactivity) ratings.
3. Compute the **General Process Hazards Factor (F₁)** — penalties for exothermic reactions, endothermic processes, material handling, enclosed units, access, drainage.
4. Compute the **Special Process Hazards Factor (F₂)** — penalties for toxic materials, sub-atmospheric pressure, low/high temperature, quantity of material, corrosion, leakage, use of fired heaters, hot oil systems, rotating equipment.
5. Calculate the **Unit Hazard Factor F₃ = F₁ × F₂**.
6. Calculate the **Process Unit Risk Factor F₄ = MF × F₃**.
7. Compute the **Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) = F₄ × 0.1** (or simply MF × F₃ in the 7th edition).
8. Calculate **Damage Radius** and **Loss Control Credit Factor (LCCF)** to derive the **Monetary Loss**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state the *edition* of the DOW Index you are using (e.g., 7th or 8th edition), as the formula for F&EI differs. The **8th edition** simplified the procedure but retained the MF-based logic.

### 2.3 Mond Index (ICI Method)

The **Mond Index** is an extension of the Dow F&EI developed by Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI). It adds **toxicity** and **layout** as separate scoring dimensions and uses a *layout-weighted* compensation factor.

**Distinctive Features vs. Dow F&EI:**
- Independent scoring for **Fire, Explosive, Toxic, and Overall** indices.
- Detailed **Air, Water, and Gaseous** sub-indices.
- Computes a **Dominant Hazard Index (D)** and a **Operating Hazard Index (O)**.

### 2.4 Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)

HAZOP is the **industry gold standard** for qualitative hazard analysis on continuous process plants, particularly in the chemical, petrochemical, and pharmaceutical sectors. It was developed by **ICI in the 1970s** and is codified in **IEC 61882**.

**Operational Logic — The HAZOP "Deviation" Method:**
1. Divide the plant into **Nodes** (sections of pipe, vessels, columns).
2. Apply the **Guide Words** systematically to each Process Parameter.
3. Identify **Causes → Consequences → Safeguards → Recommendations**.

**Standard Guide Words and Meanings:**

| Guide Word | Meaning | Example Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| NO / NOT | Negation of design intent | NO FLOW |
| MORE | Quantitative increase | MORE PRESSURE |
| LESS | Quantitative decrease | LESS TEMPERATURE |
| REVERSE | Logical opposite | REVERSE FLOW |
| AS WELL AS | Additional condition present | CONTAMINATION AS WELL AS |
| PART OF | Part of design intent missing | PART OF COMPOSITION |
| OTHER THAN | Complete substitution | OTHER THAN (maintenance mode) |

**Process Parameters Studied:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Mixing, Reaction, Start, Stop.

### 2.5 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

A **bottom-up**, inductive, hardware-focused technique that examines **each component's failure modes** and traces their effect on the system and the mission.

**Key Output Parameter — Risk Priority Number (RPN):**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Severity** (1 to 10) — consequence seriousness.
- $O$ = **Occurrence** (1 to 10) — failure rate frequency.
- $D$ = **Detection** (1 to 10) — probability of detection before the failure propagates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students sometimes confuse **Detection** with **Occurrence**. *Occurrence* = how often it fails; *Detection* = how easily we catch it **before** it reaches the customer/process.

### 2.6 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)

A **top-down**, deductive technique introduced by **H. A. Watson of Bell Labs in 1961** for the Minuteman missile system. It starts from a single **Top Event** (e.g., "Reactor Rupture") and works backward through Boolean logic to find the **Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)**.

**Boolean Logic Gates Used:**

| Gate | Symbol | Boolean Expression |
|---|---|---|
| AND | ∩ or ∧ | Output occurs if **all** inputs occur |
| OR | ∪ or ∨ | Output occurs if **any** input occurs |
| NOT / Inhibit | ¬ | Inverts input |

**Probability Calculations (for independent events):**
- For an **AND** gate: $P_{top} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$
- For an **OR** gate with low probabilities: $P_{top} \approx 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i) \approx \sum P_i$

### 2.7 Event Tree Analysis (ETA)

A **forward (inductive)** logic tree that begins with an **Initiating Event** and branches through successive **safety function successes/failures** to reach possible outcomes.

- Used to compute the **Frequency of Each Outcome** = (Frequency of Initiating Event) × (Conditional Probability of Branch Path).
- The sum of all branch outcome frequencies must equal the **frequency of the initiating event**.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Technique | Key Formula / Output | Standard Units / Range | Critical Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DOW F&EI** | $F\&EI = MF \times F_3 \times 0.1$ | Dimensionless, 0 to > 200 | $F_3 = F_1 \times F_2 \geq 1.0$ |
| **DOW Radius** | Radius $= 0.304 \times F\&EI$ (ft) | meters / feet | Used to define exposure circle |
| **DOW MPPD** | $MPPD = 0.83 \times (\text{Damage Factor}) \times (\text{Value of Unit})$ | USD (millions) | Updated by replacement value |
| **FMEA RPN** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Dimensionless, 1 to 1000 | High RPN $\geq 100$ triggers action |
| **FTA — AND** | $P_{AND} = \prod_{i} P_i$ | Probability (0 to 1) | Assumes **statistical independence** |
| **FTA — OR** | $P_{OR} = 1 - \prod_i (1 - P_i)$ | Probability (0 to 1) | For rare events, $\approx \sum P_i$ |
| **ETA Outcome** | $f_j = f_{init} \times \prod P_{branch}$ | events/year | $\sum f_j = f_{init}$ |
| **Mond Overall Index** | $D = B \times (1 + M/P)$ | Dimensionless | $D \geq 1000$ is "Catastrophic" |
| **Thermal Radiation** | $q = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot T^4$ | kW/m² | Stefan-Boltzmann: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ |
| **TNT Equivalence** | $W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot W \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ | kg | $H_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **vertical bar symbol `\|`** for absolute value is intentionally avoided in tables. Use the LaTeX command **`\vert`** (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) when writing equations.

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HAZOP** is *mandatory* in the UK (COMAH regulations) and EU (Seveso-III Directive) for hazardous installations.
- **FTA** is used in aerospace (NASA, ISRO) and nuclear safety (e.g., Three Mile Island, post-incident studies).
- **DOW F&EI** is the basis for **insurance premium calculation** for process plant coverage.
- **ETA** is the backbone of **probabilistic safety assessment (PSA)** for nuclear reactors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DOW Fire and Explosion Index Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Standard):**
A process unit handles acetone. The material is stored at atmospheric pressure and ambient temperature. Given:
- $N_F = 3$ (Flammability rating)
- $N_R = 1$ (Reactivity rating)
- $N_H = 1$ (Health rating)
- General Process Hazard Factor, $F_1 = 2.0$
- Special Process Hazard Factor, $F_2 = 3.5$
- Replacement value of unit = ₹ 8,000,000
- Damage Factor $DF = 0.6$

Compute the **F&EI, Damage Radius, and Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)**.

**Step 1 — Determine the Material Factor (MF):**
Using the DOW 7th edition table for a compound with $N_F = 3$ and $N_R = 1$:

$$MF = 16 \text{ (read from DOW table for acetone)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Unit Hazard Factor $F_3$:**

$$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$$

Substituting $F_1 = 2.0$ and $F_2 = 3.5$:

$$F_3 = 2.0 \times 3.5 = 7.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute Process Unit Risk Factor $F_4$:**

$$F_4 = MF \times F_3 = 16 \times 7.0 = 112$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Fire and Explosion Index:**

$$F\&EI = F_4 \times 0.1 = 112 \times 0.1 = 11.2$$

> [!NOTE]
> Per DOW 7th edition conventions, an F&EI between **1 and 60** corresponds to a **"Light" hazard** classification. Our result of **11.2** thus places this unit in the Light category.

**Step 5 — Compute Damage Radius:**

The DOW correlation for radius of exposure is:

$$R = 0.304 \times F\&EI \quad (\text{feet, original imperial form})$$

For the KTU 2024 Scheme (SI convention), this is converted to meters using the updated DOW 8th edition factor:

$$R_{\text{m}} = 0.84 \times F\&EI = 0.84 \times 11.2 = 9.41 \text{ meters}$$

**Step 6 — Compute MPPD:**

$$MPPD = 0.83 \times DF \times \text{Replacement Value}$$

$$MPPD = 0.83 \times 0.6 \times 8{,}000{,}000$$

$$MPPD = 3{,}984{,}000 \text{ INR} \approx \text{₹ 39.84 Lakhs}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Material Factor | $MF$ | **16** |
| Unit Hazard Factor | $F_3$ | **7.0** |
| Process Unit Risk Factor | $F_4$ | **112** |
| Fire & Explosion Index | $F\&EI$ | **11.2** |
| Damage Radius | $R$ | **9.41 m** |
| MPPD | $MPPD$ | **₹ 39.84 Lakhs** |

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Fault Tree Probability Calculation (AND-OR)

**Problem Statement:**
A reactor overpressure event is the top event $T$. It requires the simultaneous failure of:
- Pump P1 (fails to stop) — Probability $P_{P1} = 0.01$
- Relief Valve RV (fails to open) — Probability $P_{RV} = 0.02$

OR, alternatively, the failure of a backup cooling system (single failure):
- Heat Exchanger HEX fails — Probability $P_{HEX} = 0.005$

**Step 1 — Identify Gate Structure:**
- The Pump-Relief branch is an **AND** gate: $T_1 = P_1 \cap RV$
- The HEX branch is a single event: $T_2 = HEX$
- The Top Event is an **OR** gate: $T = T_1 \cup T_2$

**Step 2 — Probability of $T_1$ (AND gate, assuming independence):**

$$P(T_1) = P_{P1} \times P_{RV} = 0.01 \times 0.02 = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Probability of Top Event $T$ (OR gate):**

$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - P(T_1))(1 - P(T_2))$$

Substituting values:

$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - 2 \times 10^{-4})(1 - 0.005)$$

$$P(T) = 1 - (0.9998)(0.995)$$

$$P(T) = 1 - 0.994801$$

$$P(T) = 5.199 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Approximation (since $P \ll 1$):**

$$P(T) \approx P(T_1) + P(T_2) = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 0.005 = 5.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

The small difference ($\approx 1 \times 10^{-6}$) confirms the **rare-event approximation** is valid for low probabilities.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Event Tree Outcome Frequencies

**Problem Statement:**
A storage tank failure (initiating event) has an expected frequency $f_{init} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ events/year. The event tree branches through:
- Bund wall containment: $P_{\text{success}} = 0.95$
- Foam fire suppression: $P_{\text{success given bund OK}} = 0.90$
- Foam fire suppression: $P_{\text{success given bund FAIL}} = 0.60$

Compute the frequency of each terminal outcome.

**Step 1 — Define Branch Probabilities:**
- $P_1 = P(\text{Bund OK}) = 0.95$
- $P_2 = P(\text{Foam OK} \mid \text{Bund OK}) = 0.90$
- $P_3 = P(\text{Foam OK} \mid \text{Bund FAIL}) = 0.60$

**Step 2 — Compute Branch Path Probabilities:**

Path 1 — Bund OK, Foam OK:
$$P_A = P_1 \times P_2 = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855$$

Path 2 — Bund OK, Foam FAIL:
$$P_B = P_1 \times (1 - P_2) = 0.95 \times 0.10 = 0.095$$

Path 3 — Bund FAIL, Foam OK:
$$P_C = (1 - P_1) \times P_3 = 0.05 \times 0.60 = 0.030$$

Path 4 — Bund FAIL, Foam FAIL:
$$P_D = (1 - P_1) \times (1 - P_3) = 0.05 \times 0.40 = 0.020$$

**Step 3 — Verify Total Probability:**

$$P_A + P_B + P_C + P_D = 0.855 + 0.095 + 0.030 + 0.020 = 1.000 \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by Initiating Frequency:**

$$f_A = 0.855 \times 10^{-3} = 8.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /yr}$$

$$f_B = 0.095 \times 10^{-3} = 9.50 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

$$f_C = 0.030 \times 10^{-3} = 3.00 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

$$f_D = 0.020 \times 10^{-3} = 2.00 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

**Sanity check:** $\sum f_i = 10.00 \times 10^{-4} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3}$ ✓ (matches $f_{init}$).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (HAZOP vs. FMEA vs. FTA)

| Attribute | HAZOP | FMEA | FTA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Deviation-based | Component-based | Top-event deductive |
| **Direction** | Neither (lateral) | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| **Logic Tool** | Guide Words | Failure Modes | Boolean Gates |
| **Data Type** | Qualitative | Semi-Quantitative | Quantitative |
| **Best For** | Continuous processes | Hardware / mechanical | Complex systems / software |
| **Output** | List of issues + actions | RPN ranking | Minimal Cut Sets |
| **Limitation** | Tedious for batch | Misses system interactions | Can explode combinatorially |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: HAZOP Study Workflow

The following block diagram represents the standard HAZOP study execution flow as per **IEC 61882**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Initiation And Scope Definition] --> A1[Assemble Multidisciplinary HAZOP Team]
    A1 --> A2[Divide Plant Into Process Nodes]
    A2 --> A3[Select Design Intent And Parameters]
    A3 --> A4[Apply Guide Words To Each Parameter]
    A4 --> A5{Deviation Identified Question}
    A5 -- No --> A4
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Identify Causes]
    A6 --> A7[Determine Consequences]
    A7 --> A8[Evaluate Existing Safeguards]
    A8 --> A9[Recommend Actions And Responsibilities]
    A9 --> A10[Record In HAZOP Worksheet]
    A10 --> A11[Move To Next Node]
    A11 --> A12{All Nodes Covered Question}
    A12 -- No --> A2
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Compile Final HAZOP Report]
    A13 --> A14[Management Review And Follow Up]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Define Top Undesired Event] --> B1[Identify Immediate Causative Events]
    B1 --> B2[Connect Events With Logic Gates]
    B2 --> B3{Event Further Developable Question}
    B3 -- Yes --> B1
    B3 -- No --> B4[Mark As Basic Event]
    B4 --> B5[Obtain Failure Probability Data]
    B5 --> B6[Apply Boolean Algebra]
    B6 --> B7[Derive Minimal Cut Sets]
    B7 --> B8[Compute Top Event Probability]
    B8 --> B9[Perform Sensitivity And Importance Analysis]
    B9 --> B10[Recommend Risk Reduction Measures]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Event Tree Analysis (ETA) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Initiating Event] --> C1{Safety Function 1}
    C1 -- Success --> C2[Outcome Path A]
    C1 -- Failure --> C3{Safety Function 2}
    C3 -- Success --> C4[Outcome Path B]
    C3 -- Failure --> C5[Outcome Path C]
    C2 --> C6[Compute Outcome Frequencies]
    C4 --> C6
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7[Identify Dominant Contributors]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture: Selection Logic for Hazard Analysis Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Start Selection Process] --> D1{Available Data Type Question}
    D1 -- Qualitative Only --> D2[Use PrHA Or Checklist]
    D1 -- P And ID Available --> D3[Use HAZOP]
    D1 -- Hardware List --> D4[Use FMEA]
    D1 -- Specific Top Event --> D5[Use FTA]
    D1 -- Initiating Event --> D6[Use ETA]
    D2 --> D7[Verify With Dow F And EI]
    D3 --> D8[Quantify With FTA]
    D4 --> D9[Quantify With FTA]
    D5 --> D10[Cross Check With ETA]
    D6 --> D11[Cross Check With FTA]
    D7 --> D12[Document Final Risk Register]
    D8 --> D12
    D9 --> D12
    D10 --> D12
    D11 --> D12
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define HAZOP study. List any four guide words used in HAZOP.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a structured, systematic, team-based qualitative hazard analysis technique used to identify potential deviations from design intent in a process plant, evaluate their causes and consequences, and recommend safeguards.

**Four Guide Words:**
1. **NO / NOT** — Negation (e.g., NO FLOW)
2. **MORE** — Quantitative increase (e.g., MORE PRESSURE)
3. **LESS** — Quantitative decrease (e.g., LESS TEMPERATURE)
4. **REVERSE** — Logical opposite (e.g., REVERSE FLOW)

> Additional valid answers: AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.

---

#### **Question 2: What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? Write its formula and explain each term.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
RPN is a numerical score used in FMEA to rank failure modes based on their relative risk significance, so that high-risk items are addressed with priority.

**Formula:**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

- **$S$ — Severity (1 to 10):** The seriousness of the consequence of the failure to the next higher level, the end user, or the environment.
- **$O$ — Occurrence (1 to 10):** The likelihood or frequency with which a particular failure mode will occur.
- **$D$ — Detection (1 to 10):** The probability that the failure will be detected **before** it propagates to the customer or causes harm (higher $D$ = lower detectability, so worse).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): HAZOP and FTA in Depth**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4]`

**(a) With a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a HAZOP study on a chemical reactor cooling water line. Apply the guide words 'NO', 'MORE', and 'LESS' to the parameter 'FLOW' and tabulate the causes, consequences, and safeguards.**
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure (HAZOP Steps):**
1. Define scope and objectives; select a multidisciplinary team (process, mechanical, instrumentation, operations, safety).
2. Obtain updated P&IDs, PFDs, and operating procedures.
3. Divide the cooling water circuit into nodes (e.g., "CW supply line from pump to jacket inlet").
4. State design intent for each node (e.g., "To deliver cooling water at 5 m³/h, 30 °C, 3 bar").
5. Apply guide words systematically to each parameter (FLOW, PRESSURE, TEMPERATURE, etc.).
6. For each deviation, identify causes, consequences, existing safeguards, and recommend actions.
7. Record findings in the HAZOP worksheet.
8. Repeat until all nodes are covered; compile the report.

**Application — Parameter: FLOW; Node: Reactor Cooling Water Line**

| Guide Word | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Existing Safeguards | Recommendations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NO** | NO FLOW | Pump failure, blocked strainer, valve closed, pipe rupture | Loss of cooling, runaway reaction, vessel overpressure | Flow indicator (FI), low-flow alarm, interlock to shut down feed | Install flow switch with hard interlock, redundant pump |
| **MORE** | MORE FLOW | Control valve stuck open, pump oversize, set point drift | Erosion of jacket, poor temperature control, shell cracking | Manual block valve, flow control loop | Install flow limiter, high-flow alarm |
| **LESS** | LESS FLOW | Partial blockage, partial pump failure, leak | Inadequate cooling, hot spot formation, product degradation | Flow transmitter, low-low alarm | Install temperature interlock, preventive cleaning schedule |

**(b) Develop a Fault Tree for "Reactor Vessel Rupture" showing at least one AND gate and one OR gate. Calculate the top event probability given:** Pump fails to stop ($P_1 = 0.01$), Relief valve fails to open ($P_2 = 0.02$), Operator fails to act ($P_3 = 0.10$).
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Fault Tree Structure (Text Description):**
- **Top Event (T):** Reactor Vessel Rupture
  - **Input A (AND gate):** Overpressure Condition
    - $E_1$: Pump fails to stop ($P_1 = 0.01$)
    - $E_2$: Relief valve fails to open ($P_2 = 0.02$)
  - **Input B (OR gate):** External Impact OR Human Error
    - $E_3$: Operator fails to act ($P_3 = 0.10$)
    - $E_4$: Mechanical impact (assume $P_4 = 0.005$)
- The top event occurs if (Overpressure) **OR** (External Impact) occurs.

**Probability Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Probability of Overpressure (AND gate):**
$$P_A = P_1 \times P_2 = 0.01 \times 0.02 = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of External Impact (OR gate):**
$$P_B = 1 - (1 - P_3)(1 - P_4) = 1 - (0.90)(0.995) = 1 - 0.8955 = 0.1045$$

**Step 3 — Probability of Top Event (OR gate):**
$$P_T = 1 - (1 - P_A)(1 - P_B) = 1 - (1 - 0.0002)(1 - 0.1045)$$
$$P_T = 1 - (0.9998)(0.8955) = 1 - 0.895321 = 0.1047$$

**Step 4 — Rare-Event Approximation Check:**
$$P_T \approx P_A + P_B = 0.0002 + 0.1045 = 0.1047 \checkmark$$

**Final Answer: $P_T \approx 0.1047$ (≈ 10.47% per demand event).**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): DOW Index and ETA in Depth**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4]`

**(a) Explain the DOW Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) method in detail. Compute the F&EI, Damage Radius, and MPPD for a process unit with the following data:**
- Material Factor (MF) = 21
- General Process Hazards Factor ($F_1$) = 2.5
- Special Process Hazards Factor ($F_2$) = 4.0
- Damage Factor (DF) = 0.55
- Replacement Value = ₹ 12,000,000
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theory (DOW F&EI Method):**
The Dow F&EI is a semi-quantitative hazard ranking method that calculates a numerical index representing the realistic fire, explosion, and reactivity potential of a process unit. The steps are:
1. Select the process unit.
2. Determine the Material Factor (MF) from $N_F$ and $N_R$ ratings.
3. Compute General Process Hazard Factor ($F_1$).
4. Compute Special Process Hazard Factor ($F_2$).
5. Compute Unit Hazard Factor $F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$.
6. Compute Process Unit Risk Factor $F_4 = MF \times F_3$.
7. Compute $F\&EI = F_4 \times 0.1$.
8. Compute damage radius and MPPD.

**Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Unit Hazard Factor:**
$$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2 = 2.5 \times 4.0 = 10.0$$

**Step 2 — Process Unit Risk Factor:**
$$F_4 = MF \times F_3 = 21 \times 10.0 = 210$$

**Step 3 — Fire and Explosion Index:**
$$F\&EI = F_4 \times 0.1 = 210 \times 0.1 = 21.0$$

**Step 4 — Damage Radius (DOW 8th edition):**
$$R = 0.84 \times F\&EI = 0.84 \times 21.0 = 17.64 \text{ meters}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Probable Property Damage:**
$$MPPD = 0.83 \times DF \times \text{Replacement Value}$$
$$MPPD = 0.83 \times 0.55 \times 12{,}000{,}000 = 5{,}478{,}000 \text{ INR} \approx \text{₹ 54.78 Lakhs}$$

**Hazard Degree Interpretation:** $F\&EI = 21.0$ falls in the **"Light"** category (per DOW 7th edition thresholds of 1–60).

---

**(b) An Event Tree has an initiating event "LPG Tank Leakage" with frequency $f_{init} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$/year. The first safety barrier (Gas Detection) succeeds with $P = 0.95$. The second barrier (Emergency Shutdown Valve, ESDV) succeeds with $P = 0.90$ if Detection succeeds, and $P = 0.50$ if Detection fails. Calculate the frequency of each terminal outcome.**
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Branch Structure:**
- **Outcome A:** Detection OK, ESDV OK
- **Outcome B:** Detection OK, ESDV Fails
- **Outcome C:** Detection Fails, ESDV OK
- **Outcome D:** Detection Fails, ESDV Fails

**Step 2 — Branch Path Probabilities:**

**Path A:**
$$P_A = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.8550$$

**Path B:**
$$P_B = 0.95 \times (1 - 0.90) = 0.95 \times 0.10 = 0.0950$$

**Path C:**
$$P_C = (1 - 0.95) \times 0.50 = 0.05 \times 0.50 = 0.0250$$

**Path D:**
$$P_D = (1 - 0.95) \times (1 - 0.50) = 0.05 \times 0.50 = 0.0250$$

**Verification:** $0.8550 + 0.0950 + 0.0250 + 0.0250 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Multiply by Initiating Frequency:**

$$f_A = 0.8550 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 4.275 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /yr}$$

$$f_B = 0.0950 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 4.750 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

$$f_C = 0.0250 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.250 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

$$f_D = 0.0250 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.250 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

**Sanity Check:** Sum $= 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ ✓

**Conclusion:** Outcome A (both barriers succeed) is the **most likely scenario** at $4.275 \times 10^{-4}$/yr, while the catastrophic Outcome D occurs at $1.25 \times 10^{-5}$/yr.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Confusing HAZOP guide words with parameters** (e.g., writing "High Pressure" as a guide word — WRONG; "MORE + Pressure" is correct).
> 2. **Forgetting to verify the rare-event approximation** in FTA — always show that $P \ll 1$ before applying $P_{OR} \approx \sum P_i$.
> 3. **Not stating the edition of DOW Index** used — F&EI formula changes between 7th and 8th editions.
> 4. **Skipping the Sum-Check** in Event Tree problems — the total must always equal the initiating frequency; failure to verify loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up Severity vs. Detection** in RPN — the "Detection" rating is **inverse** to actual detectability.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HAZOP** uses 7 standard guide words: NO/NOT, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.
- **FMEA** is **bottom-up** and component-driven; **FTA** is **top-down** and event-driven; **ETA** is **forward-branching** from an initiating event.
- **RPN = S × O × D**; a value **> 100** typically demands corrective action in most industry standards.
- **DOW F&EI** steps: $MF \rightarrow F_1 \rightarrow F_2 \rightarrow F_3 \rightarrow F_4 \rightarrow F\&EI \rightarrow R \rightarrow MPPD$.
- **Fault Tree AND-gate** probability is the **product** of input probabilities; **OR-gate** is approximately the **sum** for rare events.
- **Event Tree** outcome frequency = initiating frequency × product of branch probabilities along the path.
- **Hazard Severity Categories** (PrHA): I (Negligible), II (Marginal), III (Critical), IV (Catastrophic).
- **ALARP** principle: risk must be reduced "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" — the regulatory benchmark in the EU, UK, and increasingly in Indian PSUs.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** integrates FTA + ETA + consequence analysis to compute individual and societal risk contours.
- **Hazard Identification (HAZID)** is the *first* step; **Hazard Analysis (HAZAN)** is the *second*; **Risk Evaluation** is the *third* — many KTU students write these interchangeably, losing marks.
- The **Dow F&EI** is for **fire/explosion** only; the **Mond Index** additionally covers **toxicity** and **layout** factors.
- **HAZOP** requires a **multidisciplinary team** (typically 5–8 people) — solo HAZOP is not a valid study.
- **NFPA Diamond** uses four colored quadrants: Blue (Health), Red (Flammability), Yellow (Reactivity), White (Special Hazard).
- **Hazard Survey** includes workplace inspections, **Hazard Analysis** includes mathematical/logical evaluation — remember this distinction in 3-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HAZOP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** HAZOP # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a systematic, structured, and rigorous qualitative risk-assessment technique used to identify potential hazards, operability problems, and deviations from design intent in process systems. It is a **team-based brainstorming methodology** that uses standardized **guide words** applied to **process parameters** at specific **nodes (study nodes)** along a process flow to discover potential deviations and their causes and consequences. The methodology was originally developed in the **1970s** by the **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI)** of the United Kingdom, specifically at the **Widnes plant**, in response to the **Flixborough disaster (1974)**. The technique has since been formalized under standards such as: - **IEC 61882** – *Hazard and operability studies (HAZOP studies) – Application guide* - **BS IEC 61882:2016** - **AIChE/CCPS HAZOP Guidelines** - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (Process Safety Management – mandates HAZOP for highly hazardous chemicals) > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (KTU Examiner Focus):** HAZOP is fundamentally a **qualitative** technique, although it may be supplemented with semi-quantitative risk ranking (frequency × severity matrices). It is **not** a Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) tool like Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) or Event Tree Analysis (ETA). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of HAZOP like a **medical check-up for a chemical plant**: > Imagine a doctor examining a patient. The doctor doesn't just check one thing; they go through every organ systematically — "Is the heart beating **too fast** (MORE)?" "Is the lung **not** working (NO)?" "Is there something **extra** in the blood (AS WELL AS)?" "Is the temperature **less** than normal (LESS)?" The doctor applies **standard questions** to **standard body parts** and documents **abnormalities**. > > HAZOP does the same for a process plant. The **process engineer is the doctor**, the **plant is the patient**, the **guide words are the standard medical questions**, the **process parameters are the organs**, and the **nodes are the anatomical regions** being examined. Any **deviation** found is a **symptom** that must be diagnosed (cause), understood (consequence), and treated (recommendation/safeguard). This systematic questioning ensures that **no abnormal scenario is overlooked** — even ones that experienced operators might dismiss as "impossible." --- ## 1.3 Core Terminology (Definition Box) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Essential HAZOP Vocabulary (MUST MEMORIZE for KTU):** > > | Term | Definition | > |------|------------| > | **Node** | A specific section of the process (a vessel, a line segment, a control loop) where process parameters are well-defined and deviations are analyzed. | > | **Design Intent** | The expected/predicted behavior of the process at that node (e.g., "flow of 10 m³/h of benzene from Pump P-101 to Reactor R-201"). | > | **Deviation** | A departure from the design intent, expressed as a combination of Guide Word + Parameter (e.g., "NO FLOW"). | > | **Guide Word** | A standardized word (NO, MORE, LESS, etc.) used to qualitatively describe a deviation type. | > | **Process Parameter** | A physical/chemical variable associated with the process (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, etc.). | > | **Cause** | The reason(s) why the deviation might occur (e.g., pump failure, valve closed, instrument failure). | > | **Consequence** | The resulting outcome of the deviation (e.g., overheating, pump cavitation, overpressure). | > | **Safeguard** | Existing protection (alarm, interlock, relief valve, SOP) that prevents or mitigates the consequence. | > | **Recommendation/Action** | A new or improved safeguard proposed by the HAZOP team to reduce risk to As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP). | --- ## 1.4 Historical Context & Industrial Significance - **Origin:** 1974, ICI, Widnes, UK - **Catalyst:** Flixborough Disaster (1st June 1974) — A **cyclohexane** vapor cloud explosion killed **28 people** and injured **89**. The disaster was caused by a temporary 20-inch bypass pipe connecting two reactors, which failed due to inadequate engineering review. - **Standardization:** Adopted globally; now mandatory in **Process Safety Management (PSM)** regulations worldwide. - **Industry Applications:** Petrochemical, pharmaceutical, oil & gas, nuclear, food processing, and increasingly in **battery manufacturing, hydrogen systems, and semiconductor fabrication**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Metric:** According to industry studies, a typical HAZOP study reduces process plant incidents by **30–50%** during the operational life of the facility. The cost of a HAZOP study is typically **0.1–0.5%** of the total plant capital cost — an exceptional return on investment for safety. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logic of HAZOP — Step-by-Step Operational Flow The HAZOP methodology follows a **rigorous 8-step logical framework**: ### **STEP 1: Define Scope and Objectives** - Identify the boundaries of the study (a whole plant, a specific unit, a single line). - Define the **Phase of Study**: Conceptual, Design (FEED/Detailed), Operation/Modification, or Decommissioning. - Obtain all relevant documentation: **P&IDs (Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams)**, PFDs (Process Flow Diagrams), Cause & Effect matrices, operating procedures, and previous incident reports. ### **STEP 2: Assemble the HAZOP Team** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard HAZOP Team Composition (5–8 members, NEVER alone):** > > | Role | Responsibility | > |------|---------------| > | **Team Leader (Facilitator)** | Drives the study, ensures methodology is followed, keeps the team focused. Must be **independent** of the design. | > | **Process Engineer** | Explains design intent, answers process questions. | > | **Operations/Plant Operator** | Provides practical operational perspective, past incident experience. | > | **Instrument/Control Engineer** | Addresses control loop and interlock questions. | > | **Mechanical/Rotating Equipment Engineer** | Addresses pump, compressor, vessel integrity. | > | **Safety Engineer** | Evaluates risk, ensures compliance with standards. | > | **Scribe (Documentation)** | Records the HAZOP worksheet entries. | > | **Project Manager** | Decides on recommendation implementation (often not present full-time). | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** A HAZOP performed by **only one person** is **NOT a valid HAZOP**. The technique is fundamentally a **multi-disciplinary team exercise**. ### **STEP 3: Divide the Plant into Nodes** - A **node** is a logical section of the process where parameters and design intent are reasonably constant. - **Common Node Boundaries:** - A vessel (reactor, tank, column) - A pipeline section between two vessels - A control loop - A pump/compressor skid - A heat exchanger - The team walks the **P&ID** and defines nodes sequentially. ### **STEP 4: Define Design Intent for Each Node** - For each node, articulate **exactly** what the process is supposed to do. - Example: *"Liquid toluene at 5 bar and 80°C is pumped from Tank T-101 to Reactor R-201 at a flow rate of 12 m³/h."* ### **STEP 5: Apply Guide Words to Parameters → Generate Deviations** - For each **process parameter**, apply each **guide word** to generate a deviation. - Deviation = Guide Word + Parameter - Examples: **NO FLOW**, **MORE PRESSURE**, **LESS TEMPERATURE**, **REVERSE FLOW**, **AS WELL AS CONTAMINANTS** ### **STEP 6: Identify Causes for Each Deviation** - The team brainstorms: "What could cause this deviation?" - Causes are typically: - Equipment failure (pump trip, valve failure, instrument failure) - Human error (valve left closed, wrong routing) - External events (loss of power, loss of instrument air, cooling water failure) - Process upset (upstream disturbance, exothermic runaway) ### **STEP 7: Determine Consequences** - "If this deviation occurs, what happens next?" - Consequences can be: Safety (fire, explosion, toxic release), Environmental (spill, emission), Operational (loss of production, equipment damage), or Quality (off-spec product). ### **STEP 8: Identify Safeguards and Recommend Actions** - Existing safeguards: Alarms, interlocks, relief valves, routine inspections, SOPs. - If risk is **not ALARP**, the team issues a **Recommendation** to add or improve safeguards. --- ## 2.2 The Guide Words — The Heart of HAZOP > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 7 Standard HAZOP Guide Words (IEC 61882):** > > | Guide Word | Meaning | Example with FLOW | > |------------|---------|--------------------| > | **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of the design intent | No flow when flow is intended | > | **MORE** | Quantitative increase | More flow than designed | > | **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | Less flow than designed | > | **REVERSE** | Logical opposite of intent | Backflow (reverse direction) | > | **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity/compound occurring | Contaminant present with intended material | > | **PART OF** | Less than complete — only part of intent achieved | Partial composition, partial phase change | > | **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution — something else entirely | Wrong material routed (e.g., water instead of acid) | > [!NOTE] > **Memorization Tip:** The first three (NO, MORE, LESS) handle the **"absence/quantity"** category. The next three (REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF) handle **"direction/quality"** issues. The last (OTHER THAN) is the **"complete substitution"** category. A mnemonic: **"No More Lousy Recipes, As Smart Pastry-makers"** or just memorize the 7 systematically. ### 2.2.1 Standard Process Parameters > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Process Parameters Used in HAZOP:** > 1. **Flow** (rate, direction) > 2. **Pressure** (vessel, line) > 3. **Temperature** (vessel, line) > 4. **Level** (vessel) > 5. **Composition / Concentration** (mixture purity) > 6. **Phase** (vapor, liquid, solid, two-phase) > 7. **Reaction** (intended chemical conversion) > 8. **Time / Sequence** (batch operations) > 9. **Start / Stop / Maintenance** (operational states) > 10. **Service / Utility** (steam, instrument air, cooling water, nitrogen) > 11. **Agitation / Mixing** (rotating equipment) > 12. **Instrumentation / Control** (loop performance) --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > HAZOP is a **qualitative** technique, so it does not have heavy numerical formulas. However, the **risk-ranking** done alongside HAZOP uses a simple matrix. Below is the consolidated reference: | Concept | Equation / Definition | Units | Application | |---------|----------------------|-------|-------------| | **Risk (R)** | $R = f \times C$ where $f$ = Frequency, $C$ = Consequence | Dimensionless or score (1–5) | Risk ranking matrix | | **ALARP Principle** | Risk must be reduced **As Low As Reasonably Practicable** | — | Decision-making for recommendations | | **Deviation Construction** | $\text{Deviation} = \text{Guide Word} + \text{Process Parameter}$ | Qualitative | Core HAZOP logic | | **Cause Set** | $\text{Cause}_i = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n\}$ | Set notation | Brainstorming | | **Consequence Severity Score (1–5)** | 1 = Negligible, 2 = Minor, 3 = Major, 4 = Critical, 5 = Catastrophic | Score | Risk matrix | | **Likelihood Score (1–5)** | 1 = Rare, 2 = Unlikely, 3 = Possible, 4 = Likely, 5 = Almost Certain | Score | Risk matrix | | **Total Risk Score** | $\text{Risk} = S \times L$ | Dimensionless | Semi-quantitative ranking | | **HAZOP Team Size (N)** | $5 \leq N \leq 8$ typically | Persons | Best practice per IEC 61882 | | **HAZOP Cost** | $0.1\% \text{ to } 0.5\%$ of plant capital cost | % of CAPEX | Industry benchmark | ### 2.3.1 Sample 5×5 Risk Matrix | Severity $\downarrow$ / Likelihood $\rightarrow$ | 1 Rare | 2 Unlikely | 3 Possible | 4 Likely | 5 Almost Certain | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **5 Catastrophic** | M | H | E | E | E | | **4 Critical** | M | M | H | E | E | | **3 Major** | L | M | M | H | E | | **2 Minor** | L | L | M | M | H | | **1 Negligible** | L | L | L | M | M | *Where: L = Low Risk (Accept), M = Medium (Manage), H = High (Mitigate), E = Extreme (Stop/Engineer Out)* --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Design Phase:** HAZOP identifies design flaws *before* construction — extremely cost-effective. A $10,000 HAZOP recommendation can prevent a **$10 million incident**. - **Modification Management (MOC):** Any plant change requires a HAZOP (or simpler technique like What-If) re-review. - **Incident Investigation:** HAZOP is used retrospectively to identify why a safeguard failed. - **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory for **Seveso III Directive** in Europe, **EPA Risk Management Program (RMP)** in the US, and increasingly in **Indian Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC)**. - **Operational Discipline:** Reinforces the understanding of **why** a particular alarm, interlock, or relief device exists. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Field Applications:** HAZOP is used in designing **ammonia plants, LNG terminals, ethylene crackers, pharmaceutical batch reactors, offshore platforms, hydrogen refueling stations**, and even **battery gigafactories** (for electrolyte handling and thermal runaway scenarios). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Deviation Matrix — Exhaustive Guide Word × Parameter Grid The most important deliverable in HAZOP is the **Deviation Matrix**, which systematically pairs every **Guide Word** with every **Process Parameter**. Below is the **complete analytical matrix** a HAZOP team would walk through. | Parameter $\downarrow$ \ Guide Word $\rightarrow$ | **NO / NOT** | **MORE** | **LESS** | **REVERSE** | **AS WELL AS** | **PART OF** | **OTHER THAN** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Flow** | No flow | More flow | Less flow | Reverse flow | Contaminant in flow | Partial flow (e.g., two-phase) | Wrong fluid routed | | **Pressure** | Vacuum / no pressure | High pressure | Low pressure | — | — | Partial vacuum | — | | **Temperature** | No heating (cold) | High temperature | Low temperature | — | Hot spot / cold spot | — | — | | **Level** | Empty (vessel) | High level | Low level | — | Foam / second liquid phase | — | — | | **Composition** | — | More concentration | Less concentration | — | Impurity present | Partial conversion | Wrong ingredient | | **Phase** | No phase change | — | — | — | Two-phase instead of single | Partial phase change | — | | **Reaction** | No reaction | Runaway reaction | Slow reaction | Reversed reaction | Side reaction | Partial reaction | Wrong reaction | | **Time** | No time / stuck | More residence time | Less residence time | — | — | — | — | | **Agitation** | No agitation | High RPM | Low RPM | Reverse rotation | — | — | — | | **Service (Utility)** | Loss of utility | More utility flow | Less utility flow | — | Contaminant in utility | — | Wrong utility | > [!NOTE] > Some cells in the matrix are **meaningless** (e.g., REVERSE PRESSURE, OTHER THAN TEMPERATURE) — the team must recognize and **skip** these intelligently, not force-fit deviations. --- ## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Toluene Feed Line to a Nitration Reactor > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scenario:** A pump (P-101) pumps liquid toluene from a day tank (T-101) to a nitration reactor (R-201). The line has a flow indicator (FI), a flow control valve (FCV), and a manual block valve (V-101). A pressure safety valve (PSV) protects the line. ### 3.2.1 Design Intent *"Liquid toluene at 25°C, 4 bar gauge, flows from T-101 to R-201 at a controlled rate of 10 m³/h via Pump P-101, with FCV-101 modulating the flow."* ### 3.2.2 Selected Deviation Analysis: **"NO FLOW"** | Step | Analysis | Outcome | |------|----------|---------| | **1. Deviation** | NO FLOW | Identified by guide word "NO" + parameter "FLOW" | | **2. Causes** | (a) Pump P-101 fails (mechanical seizure, motor trip, loss of power)
(b) V-101 manually closed
(c) FCV-101 fails shut
(d) Line blockage (plugged strainer)
(e) Loss of suction (T-101 empty) | 5 potential causes identified | | **3. Consequences** | (a) Loss of feed to nitration reactor → reaction stalling → potential accumulation of unreacted nitric acid → violent runaway upon restoration of flow
(b) Pump cavitation/damage if running against closed valve
(c) Loss of production | High severity: Catastrophic (reactor runaway can lead to explosion) | | **4. Existing Safeguards** | (a) Low flow alarm (FAL-101) at 5 m³/h
(b) Pump motor current trip on high load
(c) Operator rounds every 2 hours
(d) PSV on discharge line | 4 safeguards present | | **5. Risk Level** | Severity = 5, Likelihood = 2 | Risk Score = 10 → **High** | | **6. Recommendation** | Add a **low-low flow interlock** (FALL-101) that automatically shuts down the nitric acid feed pump on detection of toluene flow loss, preventing acid accumulation. | New safeguard proposed | | **7. Action Item** | Modify control logic in DCS, install new low-low flow transmitter, update Cause & Effect matrix. | Tracked via Management of Change (MOC) | > [!NOTE] > **Real Incident Parallel:** The **Schöller Bleicherode nitration reactor explosion (1948)** in Germany was a classic **"NO FLOW → Acid Accumulation → Re-feed → Runaway"** scenario, killing 22 people. HAZOP, if performed in 1948, would have flagged this exact deviation and likely prevented the disaster. ### 3.2.3 Selected Deviation Analysis: **"MORE FLOW"** | Step | Analysis | Outcome | |------|----------|---------| | **1. Deviation** | MORE FLOW (excessive flow) | | | **2. Causes** | (a) FCV-101 fails open
(b) Operator opens V-101 fully and overrides FCV
(c) Pump running at higher speed than design | | | **3. Consequences** | Overfeeding nitration reactor → excess toluene + nitric acid → exotherm out of control → potential thermal runaway → explosion
Possible hydraulic overload of reactor internals | High severity | | **4. Existing Safeguards** | (a) High flow alarm (FAH-101) at 12 m³/h
(b) Reactor temperature interlock | | | **5. Risk Level** | Severity = 4, Likelihood = 2 | **Medium-High** | | **6. Recommendation** | Add independent high flow shutdown (FASL-101) at 14 m³/h independent of the control loop. | | ### 3.2.4 Selected Deviation Analysis: **"REVERSE FLOW"** | Step | Analysis | Outcome | |------|----------|---------| | **1. Deviation** | REVERSE FLOW (backflow from R-201 to T-101) | | | **2. Causes** | (a) R-201 pressure exceeds pump discharge head
(b) P-101 trips while R-201 is at high pressure
(c) Thermal expansion of liquid in blocked line | | | **3. Consequences** | (a) Contamination of toluene in T-101 with nitric acid/nitro-toluene
(b) Violent reaction in day tank if acid mixes with toluene
(c) Possible tank rupture | Catastrophic | | **4. Existing Safeguards** | (a) Check valve (C/V-101) in line
(b) Pressure gauge on line | | | **5. Risk Level** | Severity = 5, Likelihood = 1 | **Medium** (rare, but catastrophic) | | **6. Recommendation** | Install **double block and bleed** configuration or a **slam-shut valve** to ensure positive isolation. | | --- ## 3.3 Algebraic Representation of the HAZOP Risk-Ranking Logic The risk score for each deviation is computed as a **multiplicative product** of severity and likelihood. This is the semi-quantitative backbone that allows HAZOP teams to **prioritize** recommendations. $$ R = S \times L $$ Where: - $R$ = Risk Score (dimensionless integer, 1 to 25) - $S$ = Severity Score (1 to 5) - $L$ = Likelihood Score (1 to 5) For the **toluene NO FLOW** case above: - $S = 5$ (catastrophic — explosion potential) - $L = 2$ (unlikely under normal operation) $$ R = S \times L = 5 \times 2 = 10 $$ A risk score of **10** falls in the **High (H)** zone of the 5×5 risk matrix, justifying a **mandatory recommendation**. After implementation of the new low-low flow interlock, the team re-evaluates: - $L_{\text{new}} = 1$ (likelihood reduced due to new safeguard) - $R_{\text{new}} = 5 \times 1 = 5$ → now in the **Medium (M)** zone → **ALARP achieved** > [!NOTE] > The **reduction in risk** can be expressed as a **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**: $$ \text{RRF} = \frac{R_{\text{initial}}}{R_{\text{residual}}} $$ For our example: $$ \text{RRF} = \frac{10}{5} = 2 $$ A Risk Reduction Factor of 2 means the new safeguard halves the residual risk — meeting the **ALARP** test if the cost of implementation is not grossly disproportionate to this reduction. --- ## 3.4 Time and Coverage Estimation for a HAZOP Study Engineers planning a study often need to estimate the duration. A widely used industry heuristic is: $$ T_{\text{HAZOP}} = k \times N_{\text{nodes}} \times T_{\text{node}} $$ Where: - $T_{\text{HAZOP}}$ = Total study time (hours) - $k$ = Complexity factor (1.0 to 1.5 for a continuous process, 1.5 to 2.5 for a batch process) - $N_{\text{nodes}}$ = Number of nodes - $T_{\text{node}}$ = Time per node (typically 2 to 4 hours) **Example:** A study with $N_{\text{nodes}} = 15$ nodes, $k = 1.2$ for a continuous plant, and $T_{\text{node}} = 3$ hours/node: $$ T_{\text{HAZOP}} = 1.2 \times 15 \times 3 = 54 \text{ hours} $$ At 6 productive hours per day, this is approximately **9 working days** for a 5–8 member team. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 HAZOP Methodology Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Define Scope and Objectives] --> B[Step 2: Assemble Multi-Disciplinary HAZOP Team] B --> C[Step 3: Collect P&IDs, PFDs, Operating Procedures] C --> D[Step 4: Divide Process into Study Nodes] D --> E[Step 5: Define Design Intent for Each Node] E --> F[Step 6: Apply Guide Words to Parameters] F --> G[Step 7: Generate Deviations] G --> H[Step 8: Identify Causes for Each Deviation] H --> I[Step 9: Determine Consequences] I --> J[Step 10: Identify Existing Safeguards] J --> K[Step 11: Calculate Risk Score R = S x L] K --> L{Risk Acceptable?} L -- No --> M[Step 12: Issue Recommendation and Action Item] M --> N[Step 13: Document in HAZOP Worksheet] L -- Yes --> N N --> O[Step 14: Move to Next Deviation] O --> P{All Deviations Covered?} P -- No --> F P -- Yes --> Q[Step 15: Move to Next Node] Q --> R{All Nodes Covered?} R -- No --> E R -- Yes --> S[Step 16: Final HAZOP Report and Action Tracking] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above flowchart represents the **functional workflow** of an actual HAZOP session as conducted in industry. The team iterates through deviations within a node before moving to the next node. --- ## 4.2 HAZOP Team Composition Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD TL[HAZOP Team Leader / Facilitator] --> SC[Scribe / Documentation Officer] TL --> PE[Process Engineer] TL --> OP[Plant Operations Expert] TL --> IE[Instrument and Control Engineer] TL --> ME[Mechanical and Rotating Equipment Engineer] TL --> SE[Safety Engineer] TL --> PM[Project Manager Optional] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Team Leader** sits at the **center** of the diagram because they facilitate the entire study, ensuring that every team member's expertise is leveraged. --- ## 4.3 Deviation Generation Logic (Guide Word × Parameter) ```mermaid flowchart LR GW[Guide Word] --> COMB[Combine] PP[Process Parameter] --> COMB COMB --> DEV[Deviation] DEV --> CAUSE[Cause Analysis] CAUSE --> CONS[Consequence Evaluation] CONS --> SAFE[Safeguard Identification] SAFE --> RISK{Risk = Severity x Likelihood} RISK -->|High| REC[Issue Recommendation] RISK -->|Low/Acceptable| DOC[Document and Continue] REC --> DOC ``` --- ## 4.4 Schematic of a Process Section with HAZOP Nodes ```mermaid flowchart LR T101[T-101 Toluene Day Tank] -->|Node 1: Suction Line| P101[P-101 Transfer Pump] P101 -->|Node 2: Discharge Line| V101[V-101 Manual Block Valve] V101 -->|Node 3: Controlled Section| FCV101[FCV-101 Control Valve with FI-101] FCV101 -->|Node 4: Reactor Feed Line| R201[R-201 Nitration Reactor] R201 -->|Node 5: Reactor Vessel| PROD[Product and Effluent System] ``` > [!NOTE] > Each labeled segment in the schematic represents a **HAZOP node**. The team examines each node individually, applying the 7 guide words to relevant parameters (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, etc.) within that node. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define HAZOP. List the seven standard HAZOP guide words. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a systematic, team-based, qualitative risk-assessment technique used to identify potential hazards and operability problems in process plants by applying standardized guide words to process parameters at defined study nodes. **Seven Guide Words (2 Marks):** 1. **NO / NOT** — Complete negation of the design intent 2. **MORE** — Quantitative increase 3. **LESS** — Quantitative decrease 4. **REVERSE** — Logical opposite of intent 5. **AS WELL AS** — Additional activity or component 6. **PART OF** — Less than the complete intent 7. **OTHER THAN** — Complete substitution --- ### **Question 2: What is a HAZOP node? What is design intent? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Node (1.5 Marks):** A HAZOP node is a specific, clearly defined section of the process (such as a vessel, a line segment, a control loop, or a heat exchanger) where the process parameters and design intent are reasonably constant, and at which deviations are systematically analyzed. **Design Intent (1.5 Marks):** The design intent is a precise description of **how the process is expected to operate** at a particular node — including the materials, flow rates, temperatures, pressures, phases, and compositions — against which deviations are compared. **Example:** *"Liquid toluene at 4 bar and 25°C is pumped from T-101 to R-201 at 10 m³/h."* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A: Comprehensive HAZOP Study Description [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, End Semester Examination]** **(a) Explain the HAZOP methodology in detail. Discuss the team composition, the role of the team leader, and the step-by-step procedure. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **1. HAZOP Methodology Definition (1 Mark):** HAZOP is a structured, qualitative, team-based risk-assessment technique used to identify hazards and operability issues by systematically applying guide words to process parameters at defined nodes. **2. Team Composition (2 Marks):** A typical HAZOP team consists of **5–8 members** from diverse disciplines: - **Team Leader (Facilitator):** Independent of design, drives the study, manages the team, ensures methodology compliance, summarizes findings. - **Process Engineer:** Explains the design intent and process chemistry. - **Operations/Plant Operator:** Provides real-world operational insights and incident history. - **Instrument/Control Engineer:** Addresses control loops, interlocks, and alarms. - **Mechanical Engineer:** Addresses rotating equipment and vessel integrity. - **Safety Engineer:** Evaluates risk and regulatory compliance. - **Scribe:** Documents the entire HAZOP worksheet. **3. Step-by-Step Procedure (4 Marks):** - **Step 1:** Define the scope and objectives of the study. - **Step 2:** Collect all relevant documentation (P&IDs, PFDs, operating procedures, C&E matrix). - **Step 3:** Form the multi-disciplinary team. - **Step 4:** Divide the plant into study nodes (vessels, line segments, control loops). - **Step 5:** For each node, define the design intent. - **Step 6:** Apply each guide word to each process parameter to generate deviations. - **Step 7:** For each deviation, identify causes and consequences. - **Step 8:** Identify existing safeguards (alarms, interlocks, relief valves, SOPs). - **Step 9:** Evaluate risk using $R = S \times L$ matrix. - **Step 10:** Issue recommendations if risk is not ALARP. - **Step 11:** Document in the HAZOP worksheet and prepare the final report. --- **(b) Explain the seven standard HAZOP guide words with one example deviation each. Discuss the advantages and limitations of HAZOP. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **1. Seven Guide Words with Examples (3.5 Marks):** | Guide Word | Meaning | Example Deviation (in a Reactor Feed Line) | |------------|---------|----------------------------------------------| | **NO / NOT** | Complete negation | **No Flow** to the reactor | | **MORE** | Quantitative increase | **More Pressure** in the line | | **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | **Less Temperature** in the feed | | **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | **Reverse Flow** from reactor to feed tank | | **AS WELL AS** | Additional element | **Contaminant As Well As** feed material | | **PART OF** | Incomplete fulfillment | **Part Of Composition** (only partial reaction) | | **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | **Other Than** feed material (wrong chemical routed) | **2. Advantages of HAZOP (2 Marks):** - **Comprehensive:** Systematically covers all process deviations. - **Team-based:** Leverages multi-disciplinary expertise. - **Documentation:** Provides a thorough record of risk decisions. - **Cost-effective:** Cheap relative to plant cost; high ROI. - **Regulatory acceptance:** Recognized by OSHA, EPA, IEC, etc. - **Educational:** Trains team members on process hazards. **3. Limitations of HAZOP (1.5 Marks):** - **Time-consuming:** Large plants may take weeks. - **Dependent on team quality:** Poor team → poor results. - **Qualitative:** Doesn't quantify risk precisely (without supplementary tools). - **Reactive to design:** Cannot evaluate alternative designs comparatively. - **Limited to procedural deviations:** Struggles with rare events like domino effects. - **No software/automation:** Heavily dependent on human facilitator. --- ### **Question B: HAZOP Application to a Specific Process [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]** **(a) Consider a process where a centrifugal pump transfers a flammable solvent (acetone) from a storage tank to a reactor through a flow control valve. Prepare a HAZOP worksheet (with design intent, deviations, causes, consequences, safeguards, and recommendations) for the deviations: (i) NO FLOW and (ii) MORE FLOW. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **Design Intent:** *"Liquid acetone is pumped at 30°C and 3 bar gauge from Tank T-201 to Reactor R-301 at a controlled rate of 8 m³/h through Pump P-201 and FCV-201."* ### (i) NO FLOW — HAZOP Worksheet Entry | Column | Entry | |--------|-------| | **Node** | Discharge line of Pump P-201 (between pump and FCV-201) | | **Design Intent** | Liquid acetone flowing at 8 m³/h, 3 bar, 30°C | | **Deviation** | **NO FLOW** | | **Causes** | (i) P-201 mechanical failure or motor trip
(ii) V-201 (manual block valve) inadvertently closed
(iii) FCV-201 fails shut
(iv) Strainer blockage
(v) T-201 empty / loss of suction | | **Consequences** | (i) Loss of acetone feed → reaction stalling in R-301
(ii) Pump cavitation and possible seal failure (acetone leak → fire hazard)
(iii) Accumulation of unreacted reagents in R-301 → potential runaway | | **Safeguards** | (i) Low flow alarm (FAL-201)
(ii) Pump current trip on high load
(iii) Operator rounds
(iv) PSV on discharge line | | **Risk Score** | $S = 5, L = 2, R = 10$ → **High** | | **Recommendation** | Install a low-low flow interlock (FALL-201) to shut down the reagent feed pump on detection of acetone flow loss. | ### (ii) MORE FLOW — HAZOP Worksheet Entry | Column | Entry | |--------|-------| | **Node** | Discharge line, downstream of FCV-201 | | **Deviation** | **MORE FLOW** (greater than 8 m³/h) | | **Causes** | (i) FCV-201 fails open
(ii) Operator manually opens V-201 wide, overrides FCV
(iii) Pump P-201 running above design RPM | | **Consequences** | (i) Overfeeding of acetone → exotherm in R-301
(ii) Possible hydraulic overload, overflow, or runaway reaction
(iii) Flash-back of flame into storage tank | | **Safeguards** | (i) High flow alarm (FAH-201) at 10 m³/h
(ii) Reactor temperature interlock
(iii) R-301 level high-high interlock | | **Risk Score** | $S = 4, L = 2, R = 8$ → **High** | | **Recommendation** | Install a high flow safety instrumented function (SIF) independent of the basic control loop, with shutdown at 11 m³/h (SIL-1 minimum). | --- **(b) Discuss the application of HAZOP in batch processes. How does it differ from HAZOP on continuous processes? (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **1. HAZOP in Batch Processes (3.5 Marks):** Batch processes (used extensively in **pharmaceuticals, fine chemicals, paints, food, and specialty chemicals**) present unique HAZOP challenges because the process state changes with time, not just with position. **Key Steps:** - **Time as a Parameter:** In batch HAZOP, **time** and **sequence** become critical process parameters. Deviations like **MORE TIME** (over-cooking) or **LESS TIME** (incomplete reaction) are unique to batch operations. - **Phased Approach:** The batch is divided into **operational phases** (charging, mixing, heating, reacting, transferring, discharging) — each phase is treated as a separate "node." - **Procedure-Based:** Batch HAZOP is closely tied to the **recipe/procedure** (ISA-88 batch standard). - **State-Based Deviations:** Deviations such as **WRONG ADDITION** (wrong material charged), **WRONG ORDER** (sequence error), **AS WELL AS CONTAMINANTS** (cross-contamination from previous batch) are common. - **Manual Operations:** Batch plants rely heavily on **manual valve operations** and **operator interventions** — increasing the **human error** probability. **2. Key Differences from Continuous HAZOP (3.5 Marks):** | Aspect | Continuous HAZOP | Batch HAZOP | |--------|-------------------|-------------| | **Node Definition** | Spatial (vessel, line) | Temporal (phase of batch) | | **Key Parameters** | Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level | Time, Sequence, Step Completion, Charge Order | | **Procedure Reliance** | Low (steady-state) | High (recipe-driven) | | **Human Error** | Moderate (operator rounds) | Very High (many manual steps) | | **HAZOP Duration** | Shorter (1–2 weeks for medium plant) | Longer (more iterations due to phases) | | **Additional Guide Words** | Standard 7 | Often **"AS WELL AS"** (extra charge), **"PART OF"** (incomplete addition), **"OTHER THAN"** (wrong material) heavily used | | **Outcome Documentation** | Node-based worksheet | Phase-based worksheet, often in recipe format | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway for Batch HAZOP:** The team must examine the **entire operational cycle**, including **start-up, normal operation, emergency shutdown, and maintenance phases** — not just the steady-state reaction. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in HAZOP Questions:** > > 1. **Listing only 5 or 6 guide words** instead of all 7 — the standard set is **NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN**. Missing even one costs marks. > 2. **Confusing "Hazard" with "Operability":** HAZOP addresses **both** — *Hazards* are safety/environmental concerns; *Operability* issues are production/quality problems. Examiners expect you to recognize both. > 3. **Forgetting the team aspect:** If a question asks "Who performs HAZOP?" — answering "the process engineer" alone is **incomplete**. HAZOP is a **team-based** activity. > 4. **Skipping safeguards:** A complete HAZOP entry must list **existing safeguards** before recommending new actions. > 5. **Confusing HAZOP with HAZAN (Hazard Analysis) or QRA:** HAZOP is **qualitative**; HAZAN/QRA are **quantitative**. > 6. **Not stating the ALARP principle** when justifying recommendations. > 7. **Mixing up nodes and deviations:** A *node* is a physical/process location; a *deviation* is what is analyzed at that node. Examiners will deduct marks for using these interchangeably. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — HAZOP in 60 Seconds:** - ✅ **Full Form:** Hazard and Operability Study - ✅ **Origin:** ICI, 1974, post-Flixborough disaster - ✅ **Standard:** IEC 61882 - ✅ **Type:** Qualitative, team-based, systematic - ✅ **Team Size:** 5–8 members, multi-disciplinary - ✅ **7 Guide Words (Memorize!):** NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN - ✅ **Key Process Parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Reaction, Time - ✅ **Conceptual Building Blocks:** Node + Design Intent → Deviation = Guide Word + Parameter → Cause + Consequence + Safeguard → Risk ($S \times L$) → Recommendation (if not ALARP) - ✅ **Risk Formula:** $R = S \times L$ (5×5 matrix) - ✅ **ALARP:** As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the decision principle - ✅ **Batch vs Continuous:** Batch adds **time/sequence** as critical parameters - ✅ **Output:** HAZOP worksheet + HAZOP report + Action Item register - ✅ **Mandatory Application:** Seveso III Directive, OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, Indian MSIHC Rules - ✅ **Cost Benchmark:** 0.1–0.5% of plant capital cost - ✅ **Limitation:** Time-consuming, dependent on team quality, qualitative only - ✅ **Most Critical Deviation in Process Safety:** **NO FLOW** in exothermic reactors (risk of acid/reagent accumulation and runaway) - ✅ **Key Industrial Lesson:** Flixborough (1974), Bhopal (1984), Pasadena (1989), Texas City (2005) — all could have been prevented or mitigated by thorough HAZOP.

topics.title: Bow Tie Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bow Tie Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bow-Tie Diagram (BTD)** is a universally accepted, semi-quantitative risk assessment and safety barrier-based management tool that graphically integrates the concepts of **causality** (what causes an incident) and **consequence** (what happens if it occurs) into a single, unified visual representation shaped like a bow-tie.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Process Safety Engineering syllabus, the bow-tie method is positioned as a logical extension of HAZOP and Fault Tree / Event Tree analyses. It was originally developed by the chemical process industry (CPI) in the late 1970s and later standardized by organizations such as the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** of the AIChE and **IEC 31010:2019** (Risk management — Risk assessment techniques), where it is formally listed as a "bow-tie analysis."

Formally, a bow-tie diagram consists of:
- A **Top Event** in the center (the loss of control / undesired event)
- A **Left-Hand Side (LHS) — Fault Tree (Cause Tree)**: the paths of escalating threats
- A **Right-Hand Side (RHS) — Event Tree (Consequence Tree)**: the paths of escalating outcomes
- **Threats**, **Consequences**, and **Barriers (Controls / Safeguards)** placed on the relevant pathways

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Ready):** A Bow-Tie Diagram is a graphical representation of an incident scenario that depicts the **causes (threats)** leading to a **Top Event**, the **Top Event** itself (loss of containment or process upset), the **pathways of escalation**, and the resulting **consequences**, along with the **preventive and mitigative barriers** that interrupt these pathways.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **sailor navigating a dangerous strait**:
- On the **left bank** are the **rocks (threats)** — waves, storms, careless navigation — that could push your ship toward disaster.
- The **center of the strait** is the **point of no return** — the moment your ship hits a rock (the **Top Event** — e.g., ship collision, hull breach).
- On the **right bank** are the **cascading disasters (consequences)** — oil spill, sinking, loss of life, environmental damage.
- The **lighthouses, breakwaters, life jackets, rescue boats, and firefighting tugs** placed along both banks are the **barriers** — engineered, administrative, and human controls that **prevent** the ship from hitting the rock (left side) or **mitigate** the damage if it does (right side).

The Bow-Tie Diagram is exactly this strait — a single picture that shows an engineer *every possible thing that can go wrong, what to do to prevent it, and what to do if it does happen*. 

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

- **Top Event**: The undesired event (e.g., release of flammable toxic gas, loss of containment — LOC). **Typically considered to have a frequency/probability > 10⁻⁶/year for a major hazard**.
- **Threat**: A latent hazard or initiating cause (e.g., pump seal failure, corrosion, human error). Units: **events/year** or **probability**.
- **Barrier (Control)**: A system, equipment, or procedure that prevents a threat from reaching the Top Event (preventive) or mitigates a consequence from unfolding (mitigative). Classified as **passive, active, or procedural (human)**.
- **Degradation Factor (V):** An efficiency factor (0 ≤ V ≤ 1) representing barrier reliability. The **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of a barrier is **1 − V**.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** As per **IEC 61511**, a risk-based measure of barrier reliability ranging from SIL 1 (PFD 10⁻² to 10⁻¹) to SIL 4 (PFD 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The KTU Process Safety module emphasizes that bow-tie diagrams convert abstract risk matrices (Likelihood × Severity) into **actionable safety management tools** by explicitly identifying each barrier's *owner*, *performance standard*, and *verification mechanism* — a concept called the **"Bow-Tie XP"** (Extended Performance) model.

### 1.4 Why Bow-Tie is Taught in KTU Module 4

The syllabus positions Bow-Tie in Module 4 after HAZOP (Module 2) and Fault Tree/Event Tree (Module 3). The pedagogical rationale is:
1. HAZOP identifies *what* deviations can occur.
2. Fault Tree and Event Tree compute *probabilities* of failure.
3. **Bow-Tie synthesizes the above into a single management graphic that operational staff can use, audit, and continuously improve.**

This bridges the gap between **analytical risk assessment** (done by engineers) and **operational safety management** (done by plant operators and supervisors) — a critical competency expected of a KTU graduate engineer entering a process plant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Bow-Tie Diagram

A complete bow-tie diagram is built from **five mandatory components**:

1. **Top Event (TE)** — The single loss-of-control event at the bow-tie's "knot."
2. **Threats** — Causes positioned on the LHS, each linked to the Top Event via a logical AND/OR gate.
3. **Consequences** — Outcomes positioned on the RHS, each linked to the Top Event via a logical AND/OR gate.
4. **Preventive Barriers (PBs)** — Placed *between* each threat and the Top Event.
5. **Mitigative Barriers (MBs)** — Placed *between* the Top Event and each consequence.

Additional (advanced) components:
- **Escalation Factors (EFs)** — Conditions that increase the likelihood of barrier failure (e.g., "high ambient temperature," "rain," "inadequate maintenance").
- **Safety Critical Tasks / Performance Standards** — Quantitative metrics for each barrier (e.g., "test valve every 6 months; isolation response time < 30 seconds").
- **Recovery / De-escalation Factors** — Conditions that reduce consequence severity.

### 2.2 Logic Step — How to Read a Bow-Tie

Reading the diagram follows a deterministic seven-step logic:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Hazard**: e.g., "Stored toluene in atmospheric tank T-101."
- **Step 2 — Identify the Top Event**: e.g., "Loss of containment of toluene through tank shell rupture." A bow-tie has *one* Top Event; if multiple exist, draw multiple bow-ties.
- **Step 3 — List Threats on the LHS**: e.g., (a) external corrosion, (b) overfilling due to level instrument failure, (c) lightning strike, (d) vehicle impact.
- **Step 4 — Link Threats via Fault Tree Logic**: Threats are typically connected by **OR gates** (any one threat can cause the TE). Sub-causes within each threat may use **AND** logic.
- **Step 5 — Insert Preventive Barriers**: For each path from threat to TE, insert at least one PB. Multiple PBs in series form **defence-in-depth**.
- **Step 6 — List Consequences on the RHS**: e.g., (a) vapour cloud explosion, (b) pool fire, (c) toxic exposure to operator, (d) environmental release to drainage.
- **Step 7 — Insert Mitigative Barriers**: e.g., (a) bund wall for fire, (b) firewater deluge system, (c) gas detection with isolation, (d) emergency evacuation plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Defence-in-Depth Principle:** A KTU 2024 board favourite — the requirement that **at least two independent barriers** separate the threat from the Top Event, and at least two separate the Top Event from each major consequence. This is the **Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990)** visually embedded in a bow-tie.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Bow-Tie Shape

The choice of the bow-tie shape is **mathematically isomorphic to a Fault Tree + Event Tree hybrid**:

- The **left triangle (fault tree)** aggregates initiating event probabilities using:
$$
P(\text{Top Event}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - P(T_i) \cdot V_{PB,i}\right)
$$
- The **right triangle (event tree)** computes realized consequence probability:
$$
P(\text{Consequence}_j) = P(\text{Top Event}) \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{m} \left(1 - V_{MB,k}^{(j)}\right)
$$

This is *why* a bow-tie is more than a cartoon: it preserves the **probabilistic arithmetic** of the underlying fault and event trees while presenting it in a single, intuitive graphic.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Definitions & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|---------------------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Top Event Probability (OR-gated threats)** | $P_{TE} = 1 - \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i V_{PB,i})$ | $P_i$ = frequency of threat $i$ (per year); $V_{PB,i}$ = efficiency of preventive barrier $i$ (dimensionless, 0–1) | Calculates TE frequency when multiple threats are connected by OR logic |
| 2 | **AND-gated Threat Contribution** | $P_{TE,AND} = \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i V_{PB,i}$ | Same as above; used when all sub-threats must occur together | Sub-events in a fault-tree branch |
| 3 | **Consequence Probability (with mitigative barriers)** | $P_{C_j} = P_{TE} \cdot \displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{m}(1 - V_{MB,k}^{(j)})$ | $V_{MB,k}^{(j)}$ = efficiency of $k$-th barrier mitigating consequence $j$ | Risk quantification per outcome path |
| 4 | **Barrier Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | $\text{PFD} = 1 - V$ | $V$ = barrier efficiency; PFD is dimensionless | IEC 61511 SIL classification |
| 5 | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}} = \dfrac{1}{1-V}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Measures barrier effectiveness |
| 6 | **Individual Risk (IR)** | $\text{IR} = \displaystyle\sum_{j} P_{C_j} \cdot f_j$ | $f_j$ = fatality rate per consequence $j$ (deaths per event) | Per-person risk per year |
| 7 | **Societal Risk (F-N curve)** | Plot of $F(N)$ vs $N$ | $F(N)$ = cumulative frequency of $N$ or more fatalities | ALARP demonstration |
| 8 | **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) link** | $\text{Mitigated Frequency} = P_{TE} \cdot \displaystyle\prod_{k}\text{PFD}_k$ | Frequency in events/year | Validates barrier layer in bow-tie |
| 9 | **Defence-in-Depth Condition** | $\sum_{i=1}^{L} V_{PB,i} \geq 0.999$ for L layers | $L$ = number of independent PB layers | Minimum safety target for major hazards |
| 10 | **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** test | $\text{Risk} \leq \text{Cost} \cdot \text{Disutility factor}$ | Economic balancing | UK HSE / KTU reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Scheme Tip:** In 14-mark Part B questions, examiners expect students to draw a *complete* bow-tie and then **explicitly state the equations for $P_{TE}$ and $P_{C_j}$** for the specific scenario in the question. Generic verbal answers without equations score at most 7/14.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Process Safety

In professional practice (Refineries, Petrochemical plants, LNG terminals), bow-tie diagrams are used for:

- **Safety Case Development** — Required by COMAH (UK) and OSHA PSM (US) regulations.
- **Management of Change (MOC)** — Visualising how modifications alter barrier integrity.
- **Audit & Inspection Planning** — Each barrier has an assigned *owner* and *performance standard* verifiable during safety audits.
- **Emergency Response Planning** — The mitigative side becomes the operational blueprint for emergency drills.
- **Training & Communication** — Operators, contractors, and managers all "read" the same risk picture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference:** Bow-tie analysis is published as a formal methodology in **IEC 31010:2019** and the **CCPS "Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures" (3rd Edition, 2008)**. KTU examiners consider this the de-facto syllabus standard.

### 2.6 Bow-Tie vs. Other Methods (KTU Comparison Table)

| Method | Direction | Probability? | Best Use | Limitation |
|--------|-----------|--------------|----------|------------|
| **HAZOP** | Forward | No (qualitative) | Identify deviations | No quantification |
| **Fault Tree** | Backward | Yes | Compute TE probability | No consequences shown |
| **Event Tree** | Forward | Yes | Compute consequence frequency | Single initiating event |
| **Bow-Tie** | Both | Semi-quantitative | Risk communication & barrier management | Less rigorous for complex logic |
| **LOPA** | Forward | Yes (order of magnitude) | Validate SIL of barriers | Detailed event data required |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Top Event Frequency for an OR-Gated Bow-Tie Left Side

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A toluene storage tank has two identified threats leading to shell rupture:
- **Threat A** (External Corrosion): frequency $P_A = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ per year; protected by one preventive barrier (corrosion inspection) with efficiency $V_{PB,A} = 0.95$.
