- **Threat B** (Overfilling): frequency $P_B = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ per year; protected by one preventive barrier (high-level trip) with efficiency $V_{PB,B} = 0.99$.

Assuming the two threats are **independent and OR-gated**, derive the Top Event frequency.

**Step 1 — Identify Gate Logic and Threat Structure**

The LHS of the bow-tie is a fault tree with two basic events (A and B) feeding an OR gate into the Top Event. Each basic event passes through its respective preventive barrier.

The probability that Threat $i$ *reaches* the Top Event despite the barrier:
$$
P_{i,\text{effective}} = P_i \cdot V_{PB,i}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Effective Threat Probabilities**

For Threat A (corrosion):
$$
P_{A,\text{effective}} = P_A \cdot V_{PB,A} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.95
$$
$$
P_{A,\text{effective}} = 4.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per year}
$$

For Threat B (overfilling):
$$
P_{B,\text{effective}} = P_B \cdot V_{PB,B} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.99
$$
$$
P_{B,\text{effective}} = 1.98 \times 10^{-2} \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the OR-Gate Fault Tree Formula**

For independent threats connected by an OR gate, the probability that the Top Event occurs is:
$$
P_{TE} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - P_{i,\text{effective}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Numerical Values**

$$
P_{TE} = 1 - \left(1 - 4.75 \times 10^{-3}\right)\left(1 - 1.98 \times 10^{-2}\right)
$$

$$
P_{TE} = 1 - (0.99525)(0.9802)
$$

$$
P_{TE} = 1 - 0.97553
$$

$$
P_{TE} = 2.447 \times 10^{-2} \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

The Top Event (tank rupture) is expected roughly **2.45 times per 100 years**, which is unacceptable for a major hazard facility. The dominant contributor is Threat B (overfilling). KTU examiners will mark the **identification of the dominant threat** as a key insight worth 2 marks.

$$
P_B \text{ contribution} = \frac{1.98 \times 10^{-2}}{2.447 \times 10^{-2}} \times 100\% \approx 80.9\%
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Consequence Probability with Mitigative Barriers

**Problem Statement (KTU Style Continued):** Given the Top Event frequency derived above, the ruptured tank may lead to:
- **Consequence C1** (Pool Fire → Worker Fatality): protected by mitigative barrier MB-1 (firewater deluge, $V_{MB,1} = 0.90$) and MB-2 (gas detection + emergency shutdown, $V_{MB,2} = 0.80$).
- **Consequence C2** (Vapour Cloud Explosion): protected by MB-3 (vapour cloud control via foam blanketing, $V_{MB,3} = 0.95$).

Each consequence event is assumed to produce 1 fatality if it occurs. Calculate the **Individual Risk (IR)** for an on-site operator.

**Step 1 — Calculate Probability of C1 with Two Series Mitigative Barriers**

For two independent mitigative barriers in series, the probability that the consequence *still occurs* is:
$$
P_{C1} = P_{TE} \cdot (1 - V_{MB,1}) \cdot (1 - V_{MB,2})
$$

$$
P_{C1} = 2.447 \times 10^{-2} \times (1 - 0.90) \times (1 - 0.80)
$$

$$
P_{C1} = 2.447 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.10 \times 0.20
$$

$$
P_{C1} = 4.894 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Probability of C2**

$$
P_{C2} = P_{TE} \cdot (1 - V_{MB,3}) = 2.447 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.05
$$

$$
P_{C2} = 1.2235 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Individual Risk**

The Individual Risk (assuming operator fatality occurs with probability = 1 in each consequence event):
$$
IR = P_{C1} + P_{C2} = 4.894 \times 10^{-4} + 1.2235 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
IR = 1.7129 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to Tolerable Risk Criteria**

The UK HSE tolerable risk for workers is $\leq 10^{-3}$ per year (upper), and ALARP region is $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ per year.

Since $IR = 1.71 \times 10^{-3}$ per year **exceeds** the $10^{-3}$ upper tolerable limit, the risk is in the **intolerable region** — additional mitigative barriers are mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This numerical exercise is the exact "type" the KTU board sets. Memorise the OR-gate formula and the consequence-with-mitigative-barriers formula; the 14-mark question always includes a numerical part.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Barrier Classification (Essential for KTU Viva)

| Barrier Type | Sub-class | Example | Failure Mode | Typical PFD |
|--------------|-----------|---------|--------------|-------------|
| **Preventive (PB)** | Passive | Bund wall, dikes, fireproofing | Degradation over time | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| | Active (engineered) | Relief valve, SIS, alarm | Hardware failure, bypass | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ (SIL 1–3) |
| | Procedural (human) | Permit-to-work, inspection | Human error | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{0}$ |
| **Mitigative (MB)** | Passive | Fire-resistant insulation | Material degradation | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| | Active | Deluge system, ESD, foam pour | Sensor/pump failure | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| | Procedural | Evacuation, firefighting | Training failure, delay | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{0}$ |

### 3.4 KTU Problem-Solving Template (Write This in Exams)

A complete 14-mark answer in the KTU 2024 Scheme should follow this six-step structure:

1. **Define the Top Event** (2 marks) — single sentence identifying the loss-of-control event.
2. **List Threats on LHS** (2 marks) — minimum 3 realistic threats for the chosen process.
3. **List Consequences on RHS** (2 marks) — minimum 3 consequences, separated into safety, environmental, and economic.
4. **Insert Barriers** (3 marks) — at least one PB per threat, at least one MB per consequence; classify type.
5. **Apply the Probability Formulae** (3 marks) — compute $P_{TE}$ and $P_{C_j}$ if numerical data is given.
6. **Comment on Risk and ALARP** (2 marks) — compare to tolerable risk, suggest additional barriers if needed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical Bow-Tie Diagram — Toluene Storage Tank T-101

The following Mermaid flowchart renders the full bow-tie structure for the toluene tank case used in Section 3. The diagram uses an explicit OR-gate and AND-gate structure to preserve fault-tree logic on the LHS, and a branched event-tree on the RHS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% ===== LEFT SIDE - FAULT TREE =====
    T1["Threat T1: External Corrosion"]:::threat
    T2["Threat T2: Overfilling"]:::threat
    T3["Threat T3: Vehicle Impact"]:::threat
    
    PB1["PB1: Cathodic Protection\nV = 0.90"]:::pb
    PB2["PB2: High-High Level Trip\nV = 0.99"]:::pb
    PB3["PB3: Crash Barrier Wall\nV = 0.85"]:::pb
    
    PB1a["PB1a: Quarterly UT Scan\nV = 0.95"]:::pb
    PB2a["PB2a: Operator Round Check\nV = 0.70"]:::pb
    
    OR1{{"OR GATE"}}:::gate
    AND1{{"AND GATE"}}:::gate
    
    TE(("TOP EVENT\nToluene Release\nP_TE = 2.45E-2 / yr")):::topevent
    
    %% ===== RIGHT SIDE - EVENT TREE =====
    MB1["MB1: Bund Wall\nV = 0.95"]:::mb
    MB2["MB2: Firewater Deluge\nV = 0.90"]:::mb
    MB3["MB3: Foam Blanket\nV = 0.95"]:::mb
    MB4["MB4: Gas Detection+ESD\nV = 0.80"]:::mb
    MB5["MB5: Emergency Egress\nV = 0.75"]:::mb
    
    C1["Consequence C1\nPool Fire - Fatality\nP = 4.89E-4 / yr"]:::consequence
    C2["Consequence C2\nVapour Cloud Explosion\nP = 1.22E-3 / yr"]:::consequence
    C3["Consequence C3\nSoil+Water Pollution\nP = 1.22E-3 / yr"]:::consequence
    
    %% ===== CONNECTIONS - LEFT =====
    T1 --> PB1 --> AND1
    T2 --> PB2 --> OR1
    T2 --> PB2a --> OR1
    T3 --> PB3 --> OR1
    AND1 --> OR1
    OR1 --> TE
    
    %% ===== CONNECTIONS - RIGHT =====
    TE --> MB1 --> C3
    TE --> MB2 --> C1
    TE --> MB3 --> C2
    TE --> MB4 --> C1
    TE --> MB5 --> C2
    
    %% ===== STYLING =====
    classDef threat fill:#FFD9D9,stroke:#C00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pb fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#FFE599,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef topevent fill:#C00000,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef mb fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#1155CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef consequence fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bow-Tie Construction Workflow

The following diagram maps the *engineering workflow* used to construct a bow-tie, useful for KTU viva questions on "How is a bow-tie built in industry?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Define System Boundary\nHazard Identification HAZID"]:::phase --> B["Step 2: Define Top Event\nSingle loss-of-control event"]:::phase
    B --> C["Step 3: Develop Fault Tree LHS\nIdentify all credible threats"]:::phase
    C --> D["Step 4: Develop Event Tree RHS\nIdentify all credible consequences"]:::phase
    D --> E["Step 5: Identify Preventive Barriers\nPlace between threats and TE"]:::phase
    E --> F["Step 6: Identify Mitigative Barriers\nPlace between TE and consequences"]:::phase
    F --> G["Step 7: Assign Barrier Owners\n+ Performance Standards"]:::phase
    G --> H["Step 8: Quantify via LOPA\nCompute P_TE and P_C"]:::phase
    H --> I["Step 9: Validate vs Tolerable Risk\nALARP Test"]:::phase
    I --> J{"Risk\nTolerable?"}:::decision
    J -- No --> K["Add/Upgrade Barriers\nReturn to Step 5"]:::phase
    J -- Yes --> L["Publish Bow-Tie\nDocument in Safety Case"]:::phase
    L --> M["Step 10: Review Cycle\nEvery 3-5 years or post-incident"]:::phase
    
    classDef phase fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Barrier Degradation and Escalation Factors

A common KTU viva question: *"What are escalation factors in a bow-tie?"* This block diagram shows how degradation mechanisms (escalation factors) compromise barrier effectiveness.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EF1["EF1: Inadequate\nMaintenance"]:::ef
    EF2["EF2: Extreme Weather"]:::ef
    EF3["EF3: Operator\nFatigue"]:::ef
    EF4["EF4: Corrosion"]:::ef
    
    PB["Preventive\nBarrier"]:::pb
    MB["Mitigative\nBarrier"]:::mb
    
    TE(("Top Event")):::topevent
    C(("Consequence")):::consequence
    
    EF1 -. reduces V .-> PB
    EF2 -. reduces V .-> PB
    EF3 -. reduces V .-> PB
    EF4 -. reduces V .-> MB
    
    PB ==>|V reduced| TE
    TE ==>|V reduced| MB
    MB ==>|V reduced| C
    
    classDef ef fill:#F8E1E1,stroke:#A00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef pb fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mb fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#1155CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef topevent fill:#C00000,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef consequence fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Dashed lines** in the Mermaid diagram represent the *negative* influence of escalation factors (EFs) on barrier efficiency $V$. The **solid thick lines** represent the main barrier flow. This is the convention used in CCPS Bow-Tie XP publications.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Define a **Bow-Tie Diagram**. List any **four essential components** of a bow-tie.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Bow-Tie Diagram is a **graphical risk assessment tool** that depicts, on a single sheet, the **causes (threats)** that can lead to a specific **loss-of-control event (Top Event)** and the **potential consequences** that may follow, together with the **preventive and mitigative barriers** in place.

Four essential components are:
1. **Top Event** — the central undesired event (e.g., loss of containment).
2. **Threats** — the initiating causes on the left side.
3. **Consequences** — the undesired outcomes on the right side.
4. **Barriers** — preventive (left side) and mitigative (right side) controls.

*(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the four components — half mark each)*

---

#### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> Differentiate between a **Preventive Barrier** and a **Mitigative Barrier** in a bow-tie diagram with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Preventive Barrier | Mitigative Barrier |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Position** | Between threat and Top Event (LHS) | Between Top Event and consequence (RHS) |
| **Function** | Stops the threat from developing into the Top Event | Reduces severity / frequency of the consequence after the Top Event has occurred |
| **Timing** | Acts *before* the loss of control | Acts *after* the loss of control |
| **Example** | High-High Level Trip on a tank | Firewater deluge system post-spill |

*(1 mark for correct definition of each, 1 mark for the example)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction of a Bow-Tie Diagram** for a pressurised LPG storage bullet. List at least **three threats**, **three consequences**, and the corresponding **preventive and mitigative barriers**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** If the LPG release frequency is $2 \times 10^{-2}$ per year, the BLEVE consequence has a single mitigative barrier (water spray cooling) with efficiency $V = 0.90$, and the Jet Fire consequence has two mitigative barriers in series with efficiencies $V_1 = 0.85$ and $V_2 = 0.80$, calculate the **probability of each consequence** and comment on the **ALARP status** given the tolerable individual risk is $10^{-4}$ per year. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (B-A):**

##### Part (a) — Construction of Bow-Tie for LPG Bullet (7 Marks)

**Top Event:** Sudden catastrophic release of LPG from the pressurised bullet (loss of containment).

**Left-Hand Side — Threats (3):**
1. **T1:** External corrosion of the bullet shell.
2. **T2:** Overpressure due to blocked relief valve.
3. **T3:** Mechanical impact (vehicle collision).

**Preventive Barriers (one per threat):**
1. **PB1:** Cathodic protection + annual thickness survey ($V = 0.90$).
2. **PB2:** Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) + redundant PSV ($V = 0.99$).
3. **PB3:** Earthen mound + crash barrier wall ($V = 0.85$).

**Right-Hand Side — Consequences (3):**
1. **C1:** BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion).
2. **C2:** Jet Fire impinging on adjacent tank.
3. **C3:** Unconfined Vapour Cloud Explosion (UVCE).

**Mitigative Barriers:**
1. **MB1:** Water spray deluge cooling ($V = 0.90$).
2. **MB2:** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) + gas detection ($V = 0.85$ + $0.80$).
3. **MB3:** Bund wall + foam pourer ($V = 0.95$).

**Sketch:** (To be drawn in the answer sheet — see Section 4.1 for the standard structure.)

*(Mark split: 2 marks for threats + barriers classification, 2 marks for consequences + MB, 1 mark for Top Event, 1 mark for sketch quality, 1 mark for barrier classification labels — PB/MB, passive/active/procedural.)*

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $P_{TE} = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ per year.
- BLEVE (C1) has **one** MB: $V_{MB,1} = 0.90$.
- Jet Fire (C2) has **two** series MBs: $V_{MB,2} = 0.85$, $V_{MB,3} = 0.80$.
- Tolerable individual risk = $10^{-4}$ per year.

**Step 1 — Probability of C1 (BLEVE):**

$$
P_{C1} = P_{TE} \cdot (1 - V_{MB,1}) = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times (1 - 0.90)
$$

$$
P_{C1} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.10 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of C2 (Jet Fire):**

$$
P_{C2} = P_{TE} \cdot (1 - V_{MB,2}) \cdot (1 - V_{MB,3}) = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.15 \times 0.20
$$

$$
P_{C2} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.03 = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — ALARP Comparison:**

Both $P_{C1} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ and $P_{C2} = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ per year are **greater than $10^{-4}$ per year**, the tolerable individual risk limit. The risk is therefore in the **intolerable / must-reduce region**.

**Recommendations to achieve ALARP:**
- Add a third independent mitigative barrier for C1 (e.g., exclusion zone enforcement, $V = 0.50$).
- Upgrade V of the existing MBs by improved inspection and testing regimes.

$$
P_{C1,\text{upgraded}} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.10 \times 0.50 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per year}
$$

$$
P_{C1,\text{further upgraded}} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.10 \times 0.50 \times 0.30 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year (tolerable)}
$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks for setting up equations, 2 marks for numerical substitution and final P values, 1 mark for ALARP comparison, 2 marks for engineering recommendations.)*

---

#### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]`

> **(a)** Explain the **Swiss Cheese Model** in the context of a bow-tie diagram. How does a bow-tie visualise **defence-in-depth**? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A reactor contains a flammable solvent with the following hazard data:
> - Threat frequency (overpressure due to runaway reaction): $P = 3 \times 10^{-2}$ per year.
> - Preventive Barrier 1: Pressure relief valve with $V = 0.92$.
> - Preventive Barrier 2 (independent, in series): Rupture disc with $V = 0.88$.
> - Mitigative barrier for the Vapour Cloud Explosion consequence: gas detection + isolation with $V = 0.90$.
>
> Calculate the **Top Event frequency** and the **probability of the UVCE consequence**. State whether the consequence risk is **ALARP** if the corporate tolerable risk is $10^{-4}$ per year. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (B-B):**

##### Part (a) — Swiss Cheese Model and Bow-Tie (7 Marks)

The **Swiss Cheese Model**, proposed by James Reason in 1990, conceptualises an organisation's safety as **multiple layers of defence**, each represented as a slice of Swiss cheese with holes. The **holes** in each slice represent the failure modes of that particular layer (latent conditions, active failures, procedural lapses, equipment breakdowns). An incident occurs only when the **holes in all slices momentarily align**, allowing the hazard trajectory to pass through.

In a bow-tie diagram:
- The **left side (fault tree)** of the bow-tie shows how the hazard (threat) must pass through **multiple preventive barriers** before reaching the Top Event. Each PB is a "slice of cheese."
- The **right side (event tree)** shows how the consequence trajectory must pass through **multiple mitigative barriers** after the Top Event has occurred. Each MB is another slice.
- **Defence-in-depth** is therefore *visually embedded* in the bow-tie as a **series of barriers on each pathway**.

**Engineering implications:**
1. **Independence of barriers** is critical — redundant barriers fail from common-cause if they share sensors, power, or operators.
2. **Continuous monitoring of barrier integrity** is needed so that "holes" do not grow (e.g., corrosion, sensor drift).
3. The **number of barriers** between a threat and the Top Event is a measure of safety margin.

*(Mark split: 2 marks Swiss Cheese concept, 2 marks translation to bow-tie, 1 mark independence requirement, 1 mark continuous monitoring, 1 mark diagram/sketch.)*

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Effective Threat Probability with Two Series PBs:**

Two independent preventive barriers in series give a combined effective threat probability that reaches the Top Event:
$$
P_{TE} = P \cdot (1 - V_{PB1}) \cdot (1 - V_{PB2})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values:**

$$
P_{TE} = 3 \times 10^{-2} \times (1 - 0.92) \times (1 - 0.88)
$$

$$
P_{TE} = 3 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.08 \times 0.12
$$

$$
P_{TE} = 3 \times 10^{-2} \times 9.6 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P_{TE} = 2.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of UVCE Consequence:**

The mitigative barrier is in series with the Top Event. The probability that the UVCE consequence is *realised* (i.e., both the Top Event occurs and the MB fails):
$$
P_{\text{UVCE}} = P_{TE} \cdot (1 - V_{MB}) = 2.88 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.10
$$

$$
P_{\text{UVCE}} = 2.88 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 4 — ALARP Comparison:**

- Tolerable individual risk: $10^{-4}$ per year.
- Computed $P_{\text{UVCE}} = 2.88 \times 10^{-5}$ per year.

Since $2.88 \times 10^{-5} < 10^{-4}$, the consequence risk is **within the ALARP region** (i.e., tolerable, but further reduction is desirable if grossly disproportionate cost is not involved).

**Engineering Verdict:** The current two-PB + one-MB configuration reduces the risk to within corporate ALARP limits. **No additional barriers are mandatory**, but the inspector must ensure the **independence** of the PSV and rupture disc (no common pressure-sensing line or maintenance crew).

*(Mark split: 2 marks for setting up combined PB equation, 2 marks for numerical computation, 1 mark for ALARP comparison, 2 marks for engineering verdict and independence commentary.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Drawing a bow-tie with arrows in the wrong direction** — the convention is *threats → PBs → TE → MBs → consequences*. Reversing it loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing PB and MB** — Preventive barriers are on the **LHS only**, Mitigative on the **RHS only**. Mixing them is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the OR-gate formula** for multiple threats — the correct formula is $P_{TE} = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i V_i)$, **not** $\sum P_i V_i$. The latter is only valid for very small probabilities and examiners explicitly test the correct form.
> 4. **No Top Event clearly defined** — every bow-tie must have **one** clearly stated Top Event. A bow-tie with multiple Top Events loses the structure of the diagram.
> 5. **Barriers without owners or performance standards** — the KTU 2024 Scheme (OBE-aligned) requires students to assign a barrier owner (operator, maintenance, instrument) and a performance standard (test interval, response time) to score full marks.
> 6. **Skipping the ALARP discussion** — even with correct numerics, a 14-mark answer must end with risk interpretation against a stated tolerable limit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Bow-Tie Diagram) — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Definition**: A bow-tie is a single graphic combining a fault tree (left) and an event tree (right) anchored at a **Top Event**.
- **Top Event**: The single loss-of-control event at the centre of the bow-tie (e.g., loss of containment, runaway reaction, explosion).
- **LHS (Fault Tree side)** contains **Threats** and **Preventive Barriers (PBs)**.
- **RHS (Event Tree side)** contains **Consequences** and **Mitigative Barriers (MBs)**.
- **Three Barrier Classes**:
  - **Passive** — always active, no operator action (e.g., bund wall, fireproofing).
  - **Active** — require activation (e.g., PSV, ESD, gas detector).
  - **Procedural (Human)** — depend on human action (e.g., permit-to-work, evacuation).
- **Barrier Efficiency $V$** is dimensionless, $0 \leq V \leq 1$. Higher $V$ = better barrier.
- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand) = $1 - V$** is the IEC 61511 metric.
- **OR-gated Top Event formula**: $P_{TE} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i V_{PB,i})$.
- **AND-gated Top Event formula**: $P_{TE,AND} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i V_{PB,i}$ (use only when *all* sub-events must occur together).
- **Consequence probability** with series MBs: $P_{C_j} = P_{TE} \cdot \prod_{k}(1 - V_{MB,k}^{(j)})$.
- **Defence-in-Depth** = multiple independent barriers on each pathway; derived from Reason's **Swiss Cheese Model**.
- **ALARP** test: Risk is tolerable only if further reduction would cost "grossly disproportionate" effort relative to the safety gain. KTU benchmark: $10^{-4}$ per year (workers), $10^{-5}$ per year (public).
- **Escalation Factors (EFs)** are conditions that reduce barrier $V$ (e.g., poor maintenance, weather, training lapses).
- **Escalation Factors in Bow-Tie XP** are shown as dashed arrows pointing *into* the barriers they degrade.
- **Bow-Tie XP** (CCPS, 2008) adds three layers: (i) barrier owner, (ii) performance standard, (iii) degradation / escalation control.
- **Comparison with related tools**:
  - HAZOP = *what* can deviate.
  - Fault Tree = *probability* of Top Event.
  - Event Tree = *probability* of consequence.
  - **Bow-Tie = all of the above in one picture, plus barrier management**.
- **KTU board exam mantra**: Always state the **Top Event first**, then draw **symmetrically**, label every barrier with **PB or MB**, and end with a **risk interpretation** in numbers.
- **Standards Reference** (no fabrication — well-established): IEC 31010:2019 (Risk assessment techniques); CCPS *Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures* (3rd Ed., 2008).
- **Real-World Use**: Mandatory for COMAH (UK) safety cases, OSHA PSM audits, and major-hazard facility pre-startup safety reviews (PSSR).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive, top-down, graphical failure analysis methodology used in process safety engineering to determine the combinations of equipment failures, human errors, and external events that can lead to an undesired top event (typically a major process accident, loss of containment, or catastrophic system failure). The technique was originally developed in 1962 by **H. A. Watson** of Bell Telephone Laboratories for the U.S. Air Force Minuteman missile system, and was later formalized in **NUREG-0492** (Nuclear Regulatory Commission) and adopted in **IEC 61025**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> Fault Tree Analysis is a systematic, logical, and diagrammatic procedure that progresses from a predefined undesired Top Event backwards to identify all possible combinations of basic root causes (basic events) that can result in that Top Event, using Boolean logic gates (AND, OR, etc.).

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol/Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Top Event (TE)** | T | The undesired event being analyzed (e.g., reactor overpressure, BLEVE) |
| **Intermediate Event** | — | An event resulting from a combination of lower events via a logic gate |
| **Basic Event (BE)** | Xi | A failure event requiring no further development (component failure, human error) |
| **Undeveloped Event** | — | An event not fully explored due to lack of information or insignificance |
| **External Event** | — | An event normally expected to occur (e.g., earthquake, lightning) |
| **Logic Gate** | — | A symbol defining the relationship between input and output events |
| **Cut Set** | — | A combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the Top Event |
| **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** | — | A cut set where removal of any single basic event prevents the Top Event |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective Investigation" Viewpoint

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine a detective investigating a house fire. The fire (the **Top Event**) has already happened. Instead of starting with potential causes, the detective works **backwards**: "What set of conditions must have occurred for this fire to happen?" The house must have had a fault AND there must have been an ignition source AND the fire alarm must have failed — working backwards, branching out, combining AND/OR conditions — until reaching the simplest root causes (electrical short circuit, gas leak, absent smoke detector, etc.). That backward-branching logic tree is exactly what a **Fault Tree** is.

In process safety:
- The **fire** = the Top Event (e.g., "Vessel Rupture").
- The **AND/OR branches** = the logic gates.
- The **root causes** = the Basic Events (valve fails closed, pump fails on, operator error, etc.).

### 1.4 Why FTA? (Engineering Utility)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Identification of single-point failures** (minimal cut sets of size 1) — the most critical vulnerabilities.
> - **Quantitative risk assessment**: combining failure probabilities of basic events to estimate Top Event probability.
> - **Prioritization** of safety modifications by focusing on the smallest, highest-probability cut sets.
> - **Documentation** of system failure logic for regulatory compliance (OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, COMAH).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logic Gates Used in Fault Trees

| Gate | Symbol | Boolean Expression | Plain Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AND** | Flat D-shape | $T = A \cdot B \cdot C$ | Top Event occurs **only if** ALL inputs occur |
| **OR** | Crescent-shape | $T = A + B + C$ | Top Event occurs **if any one** input occurs |
| **NOT / Inhibit** | Diamond with bubble | $T = A \cdot B \cdot \bar{C}$ | Conditional logic (rare in classical FTA) |
| **Exclusive OR** | OR with extra curve | $T = A \oplus B$ | Top event occurs if exactly one occurs |
| **Voting (k/n)** | Rectangular block | $T = (A, B, C; k=2)$ | Top event occurs if k of n inputs occur (used in redundancy) |

### 2.2 Boolean Algebra Laws Used in FTA

> [!NOTE]
> FTA is built entirely on **Boolean algebra**. The following identities are essential for KTU problem-solving:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Idempotent: } A + A = A, \quad A \cdot A = A \\
&\text{Complement: } A + \bar{A} = 1, \quad A \cdot \bar{A} = 0 \\
&\text{Identity: } A + 0 = A, \quad A \cdot 1 = A \\
&\text{Dominance: } A + 1 = 1, \quad A \cdot 0 = 0 \\
&\text{Commutative: } A + B = B + A, \quad A \cdot B = B \cdot A \\
&\text{Associative: } A + (B + C) = (A + B) + C \\
&\text{Distributive: } A \cdot (B + C) = A \cdot B + A \cdot C \\
&\text{Absorption: } A + A \cdot B = A, \quad A \cdot (A + B) = A
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relation | Expression | Variables / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AND-gate probability (independent events) | $P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i)$ | Product of basic event probabilities |
| 2 | OR-gate probability (independent events) | $P(T) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - P(X_i)\right)$ | Complementary survival probability |
| 3 | Rare-event approximation (OR gate) | $P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i)$ | Valid only when $P(X_i) \ll 1$ |
| 4 | Inclusion–Exclusion (exact OR) | $P(A+B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(AB)$ | Used for non-rare events |
| 5 | Minimal Cut Set probability (rare) | $P(MCS_j) = \prod_{i \in MCS_j} P(X_i)$ | Product within each MCS |
| 6 | Top Event (rare, disjoint MCS) | $P(T) \approx \sum_{j=1}^{m} P(MCS_j)$ | Upper bound (rare event approx.) |
| 7 | Top Event (exact) | $P(T) = P(MCS_1 \cup MCS_2 \cup \dots \cup MCS_m)$ | Inclusion–exclusion required |
| 8 | Failure Rate (constant hazard) | $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ | For exponential distribution |
| 9 | Unreliability (time t) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure by time t |
| 10 | Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of survival to time t |
| 11 | Unavailability (repairable) | $q \approx \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \lambda \cdot MT T R$ | $\mu$ = repair rate, MTTR = mean time to repair |
| 12 | Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | For non-repairable, $1/\lambda$ |
| 13 | Birnbaum Importance | $I_i^B = P(T \mid X_i=1) - P(T \mid X_i=0)$ | Sensitivity of TE to component i |
| 14 | Vesely–Fussel Importance | $I_i^{VF} = \frac{\text{Contribution of } X_i \text{ to } P(T)}{P(T)}$ | Criticality measure |
| 15 | Risk Achievement Worth (RAW) | $RAW_i = \frac{P(T \mid X_i=1)}{P(T)}$ | Risk increase if i fails |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For **OR gates**, do NOT naively add probabilities. Always use the **rare-event approximation** (linear sum) only when explicitly stated. Otherwise, use the complementary formula $1 - \prod(1 - p_i)$.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Plant Design Phase**: Engineers use FTA to identify weaknesses before construction.
- **Operating Plant**: Quantitative FTA informs **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** determination per **IEC 61508/61511**.
- **Regulatory Audits**: OSHA and EPA accept FTA as a recognized hazard analysis technique (alongside HAZOP, FMEA, What-If).
- **Insurance & Risk**: FTA-generated probabilities feed into **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** for loss prevention.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of AND-Gate Probability (Independent Events)

> [!NOTE]
> For two **independent** basic events $A$ and $B$ feeding an AND gate, the probability of the output event $T$ is:

$$P(T) = P(A \cdot B)$$

By the **multiplication rule of independent events**:

$$P(T) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

Extending to $n$ independent events:

$$P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2) \cdots P(X_n)$$

**Justification:** Two events are independent when $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. This is the default assumption in classical FTA unless dependence is explicitly identified.

### 3.2 Derivation of OR-Gate Probability (Independent Events)

For two independent basic events $A$ and $B$ feeding an OR gate:

$$P(T) = P(A + B)$$

Using **De Morgan's law** and the complement rule:

$$P(T) = 1 - P(\bar{A + B}) = 1 - P(\bar{A} \cdot \bar{B})$$

Since $A$ and $B$ are independent, so are $\bar{A}$ and $\bar{B}$:

$$P(T) = 1 - P(\bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{B}) = 1 - [1 - P(A)] \cdot [1 - P(B)]$$

For $n$ independent events:

$$P(T) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - P(X_i)\right]$$

**Rare-event approximation derivation:** If $P(X_i) \ll 1$ (typically $P(X_i) < 0.1$), then $(1 - P(X_i)) \approx 1$, and using Taylor expansion $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$:

$$\prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P(X_i)) \approx 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i)$$

Therefore:

$$P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i)$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Simple Two-Level Fault Tree

> **Problem:** A reactor overpressure Top Event (T) occurs if **BOTH** conditions occur:
> - Event A: Pressure relief valve fails to open (AND), AND
> - Event B: High-pressure interlock fails.
>
> Given: $P(A) = 0.01$ and $P(B) = 0.005$ per year. Find $P(T)$.

**Step 1: Identify the gate structure**
Top Event T = A (under AND gate) → A is itself the output of an OR gate with no — let us re-define clearly.

T occurs if A occurs AND B occurs, where A is "Pressure relief valve fails" and B is "High-pressure interlock fails."

**Step 2: Write Boolean expression**

$$T = A \cdot B$$

**Step 3: Apply AND-gate probability formula**

$$P(T) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

**Step 4: Substitute values**

$$P(T) = 0.01 \times 0.005 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

**Answer:** $P(T) = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$ (Failing-On-Demand probability).

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — OR Gate with Three Basic Events

> **Problem:** A pump failure (Top Event T) occurs if any of the following basic events occur:
> - X1: Mechanical seizure, $P(X_1) = 0.002$
> - X2: Seal failure, $P(X_2) = 0.003$
> - X3: Motor burnout, $P(X_3) = 0.001$
>
> Calculate (a) the exact probability, and (b) the rare-event approximation.

**Step 1: Boolean expression**

$$T = X_1 + X_2 + X_3$$

**Step 2 (a): Exact formula**

$$P(T) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{3} (1 - P(X_i))$$

$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.002)(1 - 0.003)(1 - 0.001)$$

$$P(T) = 1 - (0.998)(0.997)(0.999)$$

$$P(T) = 1 - 0.99401... $$

Let us compute precisely:

$$(0.998)(0.997) = 0.995006$$

$$(0.995006)(0.999) = 0.99401199...$$

$$P(T) = 1 - 0.994012 = 0.005988 \approx 5.99 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 (b): Rare-event approximation**

$$P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{3} P(X_i) = 0.002 + 0.003 + 0.001 = 0.006 = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Compare**
- Exact: $5.988 \times 10^{-3}$
- Approximate: $6.000 \times 10^{-3}$
- Error: $\approx 0.2\%$ (very small — approximation is valid for these low probabilities)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** When individual probabilities are very small ($< 10^{-2}$), the rare-event approximation is **acceptable** in board examinations. Always specify which method you are using.

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Minimal Cut Set Probability (OR of ANDs)

> **Problem:** A vessel rupture Top Event T has two minimal cut sets:
> - $MCS_1 = X_1 \cdot X_2$ (overpressure AND relief failure)
> - $MCS_2 = X_3$ (single wall corrosion)
>
> Given: $P(X_1) = 0.05$, $P(X_2) = 0.02$, $P(X_3) = 0.001$
>
> Compute $P(T)$ using (a) rare-event approximation, and (b) the exact inclusion-exclusion.

**Step 1: Identify structure**

$$T = MCS_1 + MCS_2 = (X_1 \cdot X_2) + X_3$$

**Step 2: Compute individual MCS probabilities**

$$P(MCS_1) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2) = 0.05 \times 0.02 = 0.001$$

$$P(MCS_2) = P(X_3) = 0.001$$

**Step 3 (a): Rare-event approximation**

$$P(T) \approx P(MCS_1) + P(MCS_2) = 0.001 + 0.001 = 0.002$$

**Step 3 (b): Exact formula**

$$P(T) = 1 - [1 - P(MCS_1)] \cdot [1 - P(MCS_2)]$$

$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.001)(1 - 0.001)$$

$$P(T) = 1 - (0.999)(0.999) = 1 - 0.998001 = 0.001999$$

**Answer:** $P(T) \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ per year (both methods yield nearly identical results).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Scenario | Method | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| AND gate, independent, low probs | Direct product | $\prod P(X_i)$ |
| OR gate, independent, low probs (< 0.1) | Rare-event approx. | $\sum P(X_i)$ |
| OR gate, independent, high probs | Complementary product | $1 - \prod(1 - P(X_i))$ |
| Multiple MCS, low probs | Sum of MCS probabilities | $\sum P(MCS_j)$ |
| Multiple MCS, non-rare | Inclusion–exclusion (rare in KTU) | Full expansion |
| Common-cause / dependent events | Use Beta-factor model | $P(\text{CCF}) = \beta \cdot Q$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Fault Tree for "Reactor Overpressure" Top Event

```mermaid
graph TD
    TE[Top Event: REACTOR OVERPRESSURE]
    G1{{AND Gate}}
    G2{{OR Gate}}
    G3{{OR Gate}}
    G4{{AND Gate}}
    BE1[BE1: Relief Valve Fails Open]
    BE2[BE2: Rupture Disk Fails]
    BE3[BE3: Blocked Outlet]
    BE4[BE4: Excess Feed Pump Failure]
    BE5[BE5: High-Pressure Interlock Fails]
    BE6[BE6: Operator Fails to Respond]

    TE --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G1 --> G3
    G2 --> BE1
    G2 --> BE2
    G3 --> G4
    G3 --> BE6
    G4 --> BE3
    G4 --> BE4
    G4 --> BE5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The Top Event at the top is the undesired outcome. Each gate (diamond/rounded rectangle) defines a Boolean operation on the events feeding into it from below. The bottom-most events (rectangles) are the **Basic Events** — root causes requiring no further breakdown.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Generic AND vs OR Gate Logic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[A] --> AND{{AND}}
    A2[B] --> AND
    AND --> T1[Output: T occurs if A AND B]

    O1[A] --> OR{{OR}}
    O2[B] --> OR
    OR --> T2[Output: T occurs if A OR B]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level FTA Methodology Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Define System Boundary and Top Event]
    S2[Step 2: Identify Immediate Causes of Top Event]
    S3[Step 3: Construct Fault Tree with AND OR Gates]
    S4[Step 4: Resolve to Basic Events]
    S5[Step 5: Derive Minimal Cut Sets using Boolean Algebra]
    S6[Step 6: Assign Failure Probabilities to Basic Events]
    S7[Step 7: Quantify Top Event Probability]
    S8[Step 8: Perform Importance Analysis Birnbaum Vesely RAW]
    S9[Step 9: Recommend Safety Improvements]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Example Boolean Reduction to Minimal Cut Sets

```mermaid
graph TD
    TE2[Top Event T]
    G5{{AND}}
    G6{{OR}}
    G7{{OR}}
    X1[X1]
    X2[X2]
    X3[X3]
    X4[X4]

    TE2 --> G5
    G5 --> G6
    G5 --> G7
    G6 --> X1
    G6 --> X2
    G7 --> X3
    G7 --> X4

    Note1[Boolean: T = X1+X2 AND X3+X4 equals MCS1 X1 X3 plus MCS2 X1 X4 plus MCS3 X2 X3 plus MCS4 X2 X4]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boolean Reduction Walk-Through:** The expression $T = (X_1 + X_2)(X_3 + X_4)$ expands (via distributive law) to $T = X_1X_3 + X_1X_4 + X_2X_3 + X_2X_4$, producing **four minimal cut sets of size 2**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define **Fault Tree Analysis**. List any four standard symbols (gates/events) used in FTA with a one-line description of each.

**Model Answer:**
Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) is a deductive, top-down, systematic failure analysis technique that uses Boolean logic to model the combinations of equipment failures, human errors, and external events that can lead to a predefined Top Event (undesired accident event).

Four standard symbols:
1. **Rectangle** — Represents a Top Event or Intermediate Event (further developed below).
2. **Circle** — Represents a Basic Event (a primary failure requiring no further development).
3. **Diamond** — Represents an Undeveloped Event (not explored due to insufficient data or insignificance).
4. **AND Gate** — D-shaped gate; output event occurs only when **ALL** input events occur simultaneously.
5. **OR Gate** — Crescent-shaped gate; output event occurs if **any one or more** input events occur.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between a **Cut Set** and a **Minimal Cut Set** with an example. Why are minimal cut sets important in process safety?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cut Set | Minimal Cut Set (MCS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Any combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the Top Event. | A cut set from which **no basic event can be removed** without preventing the Top Event. |
| **Size** | Larger or equal | Smallest (no redundant events) |
| **Example** | {X1, X2, X3} causing T | {X1, X2} is minimal, but {X1, X2, X3} is not, because X3 is redundant. |

**Importance in process safety:**
- The **smallest MCS** (size 1, 2, 3) reveals the most vulnerable paths to the Top Event.
- Single-point failures (MCS of size 1) are catastrophic and must be eliminated by redundancy.
- MCS guide **SIL allocation** and **safety instrumented function (SIF)** design.
- They enable **quantitative risk assessment** with manageable computation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**

---

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Worked Solution**

**Q(a) [7 Marks]:** Construct the Fault Tree for the Top Event **"Storage Tank Rupture"** given the following causes:
- Tank overpressure caused by blocked vent (X1) OR failure of pressure relief valve (X2).
- Tank corrosion (X3) — single failure mode.
- Rupture occurs only if **BOTH** overpressure (or corrosion) **AND** failure of the containment bund (X4) occur.

**Q(b) [7 Marks]:** Given failure probabilities per year: $P(X_1) = 0.02$, $P(X_2) = 0.01$, $P(X_3) = 0.005$, $P(X_4) = 0.001$. Compute the Top Event probability $P(T)$ using rare-event approximation.

---

**Model Solution to Q(a):**

**Step 1: Identify Boolean structure**

$$T = [(X_1 + X_2 + X_3)] \cdot X_4$$

Rupture occurs when (overpressure OR corrosion) **AND** bund failure.

**Step 2: Identify the gates**
- **OR Gate** (G1): combines X1, X2, X3.
- **AND Gate** (G2): combines G1 with X4.

**Step 3: Fault tree diagram (textual representation)**

```
                        T  (Tank Rupture)
                              |
                          [AND G2]
                          /       \
                         /         \
                  [OR G1]           X4 (Bund Fails)
                  /  |  \
                 /   |   \
               X1    X2    X3
            (Blocked (PRV    (Corrosion)
             Vent)   Fails)
```

**Mermaid representation:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    TT[Tank Rupture T]
    GG1{{AND}}
    GG2{{OR}}
    Y1[X1: Blocked Vent]
    Y2[X2: PRV Fails]
    Y3[X3: Corrosion]
    Y4[X4: Bund Fails]

    TT --> GG1
    GG1 --> GG2
    GG1 --> Y4
    GG2 --> Y1
    GG2 --> Y2
    GG2 --> Y3
```

**Minimal Cut Sets:**
- $MCS_1 = \{X_1, X_4\}$
- $MCS_2 = \{X_2, X_4\}$
- $MCS_3 = \{X_3, X_4\}$

All MCS are of size 2, meaning **no single-point failure** causes the rupture (good design feature — bund is the redundancy).

---

**Model Solution to Q(b):**

**Step 1: Compute probability of each MCS**

$$P(MCS_1) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_4) = 0.02 \times 0.001 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P(MCS_2) = P(X_2) \cdot P(X_4) = 0.01 \times 0.001 = 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P(MCS_3) = P(X_3) \cdot P(X_4) = 0.005 \times 0.001 = 5 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 2: Apply rare-event approximation for OR of MCS**

$$P(T) \approx \sum_{j=1}^{3} P(MCS_j)$$

$$P(T) \approx 2 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-5} + 5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P(T) \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3: Verify the approximation is valid**
All individual MCS probabilities are $< 0.1$, so rare-event approximation is justified.

**Final Answer:** $P(T) \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Full Worked Solution (Alternative Choice)**

**Q(a) [7 Marks]:** A chemical reactor has a cooling system with **two redundant pumps** (Pump A and Pump B). The Top Event **"Loss of Cooling"** occurs if both pumps fail. Each pump has an independent failure probability of $P(A) = P(B) = 0.05$ per demand. Construct the fault tree and compute the Top Event probability.

**Q(b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the concepts of **Birnbaum Importance** and **Vesely–Fussel Importance** measures. Why are importance measures used in process safety decision-making?

---

**Model Solution to Q(a):**

**Step 1: Boolean structure**

$$T = A \cdot B$$

The Top Event occurs only if **BOTH** pumps fail — this is an **AND gate**.

**Step 2: Fault tree**

```mermaid
graph TD
    TT2[Loss of Cooling T]
    GG3{{AND}}
    AA[Pump A Fails]
    BB[Pump B Fails]

    TT2 --> GG3
    GG3 --> AA
    GG3 --> BB
```

**Step 3: Compute Top Event probability**

For independent events under AND gate:

$$P(T) = P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.0025 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**
By adding a redundant pump, the system failure probability dropped from $5 \times 10^{-2}$ (single pump) to $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ — a **20-fold improvement** in reliability, demonstrating the value of redundancy.

---

**Model Solution to Q(b):**

**Birnbaum Importance ($I_i^B$):**

$$I_i^B = P(T \mid X_i = 1) - P(T \mid X_i = 0)$$

- It measures the **sensitivity** of the Top Event probability to the failure of component $i$.
- Physically: it is the difference in Top Event probability when component $i$ is forced to fail versus forced to succeed.
- Components with **higher Birnbaum importance** are more critical to the system's safety.

**Vesely–Fussel Importance ($I_i^{VF}$):**

$$I_i^{VF} = \frac{\text{Probability that } X_i \text{ contributes to TE occurrence}}{P(T)}$$

- Numerator: probability that $X_i$ is a member of a failing minimal cut set during operation.
- It represents the **fractional contribution** of component $i$ to the overall Top Event probability.
- Useful for **prioritizing inspection and maintenance** of components.

**Role in Process Safety Decision-Making:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Prioritization** — Limited maintenance budgets can be directed to components with the highest importance.
> 2. **Design Optimization** — Identifies components whose reliability improvements yield the greatest Top Event probability reduction.
> 3. **Regulatory Documentation** — Importance measures support SIL verification per **IEC 61511**.
> 4. **Risk Communication** — Quantitative basis for explaining to management *why* a particular component matters.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **OR-gate probability error:** Many students write $P(T) = P(X_1) + P(X_2)$ directly for OR gates. This is the **rare-event approximation** — valid ONLY when explicitly stated and when individual probabilities are < 0.1. Otherwise, the formula is $P(T) = 1 - \prod(1 - P(X_i))$.
> 2. **Confusing Cut Set and Minimal Cut Set:** A cut set of size 3 is **not** minimal if a size-2 cut set exists. Always reduce.
> 3. **Forgetting Boolean expansion:** Students often stop at $T = (X_1 + X_2)(X_3 + X_4)$ without expanding to $X_1X_3 + X_1X_4 + X_2X_3 + X_2X_4$ — examiners mark this down.
> 4. **Units and time frame:** Failure probabilities are typically per year or per demand. Always state the unit clearly.
> 5. **No Fault Tree diagram:** For 7-mark construction questions, ALWAYS draw the symbolic fault tree (AND/OR gates + rectangles for events). Verbal description alone loses 2-3 marks.
> 6. **Assuming dependence by default:** Standard FTA assumes independent events. Only address common-cause failure if specifically asked.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FTA is a deductive (top-down) method**, opposite of FMEA which is inductive (bottom-up).
- **Top Event must be precisely defined** — vague top events invalidate the entire analysis.
- **Boolean algebra is the language of FTA** — gate logic IS Boolean logic.
- **AND gate** → output only when ALL inputs occur → probability is the **product**.
- **OR gate** → output when ANY input occurs → probability is $1 - \prod(1 - p_i)$ (exact) or $\sum p_i$ (rare-event approx.).
- **Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)** are the *minimal* combinations of basic events causing the Top Event — found by Boolean expansion and absorption.
- **Single-point failure** = MCS of size 1 → **most critical**, must be eliminated.
- **Importance measures** (Birnbaum, Vesely–Fussel, RAW) prioritize safety investments.
- **FTA applications**: SIL determination, QRA, regulatory compliance (OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119, EPA RMP), design verification.
- **Standards**: IEC 61025 (FTA), NUREG-0492, IEC 61508/61511.
- **Limitation**: Assumes independent events; common-cause failures require special treatment (Beta-factor model).
- **Always quantify** — qualitative FTA alone is insufficient for KTU board exam full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT723 - PROCESS SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, bottom-up, inductive (forward-logic) analytical technique used in process safety engineering to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes of a system, component, process, or design. It examines **how** each component could fail (**failure mode**), **what** consequences would result (**effect**), **why** the failure occurs (**cause**), and **how** it can be detected, by assigning numerical rankings to **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, and **Detectability (D)** to compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, FMEA is classified as a **qualitative-quantitative hazard identification tool** falling under the broader umbrella of **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)**. It is most commonly applied during the **design phase** (Design FMEA / DFMEA) and during **manufacturing/processing** (Process FMEA / PFMEA) of chemical plants, oil & gas installations, pharmaceutical units, and other process industries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Family Road Trip Pre-Trip Inspection**

Imagine you are about to drive your family car from Kerala to Delhi (a 2,500 km journey). Before leaving, you sit down with a notepad and brainstorm: *"What could go wrong on this trip?"*

- **Failure Mode:** "What can fail?" → Tyre puncture, engine overheating, headlight failure, brake fade.
- **Effect:** "What happens if it fails?" → Loss of vehicle control, accident, traffic rule violation.
- **Cause:** "Why would it fail?" → Worn-out tyre, low coolant, blown fuse, worn brake pads.
- **Detection:** "How would I know?" → Tyre visual check, temperature gauge, headlight test, brake noise.

You then **rank** each issue:
- **Severity (S):** How bad is the consequence? (Tyre blowout at 100 km/h = catastrophic = 9/10)
- **Occurrence (O):** How often does this happen? (Brake fade in mountains = rare = 3/10)
- **Detection (D):** How easily can I catch it before departure? (Engine overheating = hard to predict = 7/10)

You multiply these → **RPN** → Fix the highest RPN items first. **This is exactly FMEA!**

In a chemical plant, we replace "car" with "reactor/heat exchanger/distillation column" and the logic remains identical.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following standardized scales are universally used in industrial FMEA per **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)** and **IEC 60812**:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Severity | S | 1 – 10 | Dimensionless Rank |
| Occurrence | O | 1 – 10 | Dimensionless Rank |
| Detection | D | 1 – 10 | Dimensionless Rank |
| Risk Priority Number | RPN | 1 – 1000 (or 1 – 1000 in S×O×D) | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** RPN is a **relative** ranking tool, not an absolute risk measure. Two systems with identical RPN = 100 may carry vastly different real-world risks because of the qualitative nature of S, O, D scales.

## 1.4 FMEA — Where Does It Sit in Process Safety?

FMEA belongs to the family of **Proactive Hazard Identification Tools**, alongside:
- **HAZOP** (Hazard and Operability Study)
- **What-If Analysis**
- **Checklist Analysis**
- **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — *deductive, top-down*
- **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** — *inductive, bottom-up*

**FMEA is itself inductive (forward logic)**: starts from a single component failure and traces its consequences outward.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Steps of Conducting an FMEA

> [!NOTE]
> The following **10-step FMEA procedure** is the standard adopted across process industries and is the most frequently asked question in KTU examinations (worth **7–14 marks**).

**Step 1 — Define the Scope and Boundaries**
- Decide whether it is a **System FMEA**, **Design FMEA (DFMEA)**, or **Process FMEA (PFMEA)**.
- Establish system boundaries, interfaces, and the level of analysis (component, sub-assembly, full plant).

**Step 2 — Assemble a Cross-Functional Team**
- Team leader (FMEA facilitator), process engineer, design engineer, maintenance engineer, quality engineer, operator, and safety officer.
- A good FMEA team has **5–8 members** with diverse expertise.

**Step 3 — Acquire System Documentation**
- P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram), PFD (Process Flow Diagram), process datasheets, material specifications, operating procedures, control narratives.

**Step 4 — Identify Functions of Each Component**
- Every component has a **function** and **functional specification** (e.g., "Centrifugal pump P-101 delivers 50 m³/h of water at 4 bar discharge pressure").

**Step 5 — Identify Potential Failure Modes**
- A **failure mode** is the manner in which a component fails to perform its intended function. Examples:
  - Pump: *fails to start, fails to run, runs backwards, leaks, cavitates*
  - Valve: *fails to open, fails to close, leaks internally, leaks externally, plugged*

**Step 6 — Determine Effects of Each Failure Mode**
- **Effect** is the consequence on the system, process, or customer (end-user). Effects cascade from local → next higher level → final consequence.

**Step 7 — Determine Causes of Each Failure Mode**
- **Cause** is the root mechanism initiating the failure (corrosion, fatigue, operator error, power failure, design error, improper maintenance).

**Step 8 — Assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) Rankings**
- Use standardized 1–10 ranking tables (see Section 2.3).

**Step 9 — Compute RPN and Prioritize**
- Calculate Risk Priority Number for each failure mode and sort in descending order.

**Step 10 — Recommend Actions and Re-evaluate**
- For high-RPN items, propose corrective actions (design change, additional safeguards, inspection, training), then recalculate RPN to verify risk reduction.

## 2.2 Types of FMEA

| Type | Application | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **System FMEA** | Entire system-level analysis | Conceptual design |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Product/equipment design | Detailed engineering |
| **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing/processing operations | Operations & commissioning |
| **Service FMEA (SFMEA)** | After-sales service | Logistics |
| **Software FMEA (SW-FMEA)** | Control systems / DCS / PLC logic | Process automation |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are high-yield for KTU University Exam 2024.**

The fundamental FMEA formula is:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity ranking (1–10) — impact of the failure
- $O$ = Occurrence ranking (1–10) — likelihood of the failure
- $D$ = Detection ranking (1–10) — difficulty of detecting the failure *before* it reaches the customer/process

### Standard S, O, D Ranking Tables

**Severity (S) Scale — Process Safety Adapted**

| Rank | Severity Level | Description (Process Safety) |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | Catastrophic | Loss of life, major release of toxic gas, plant explosion |
| 9 | Catastrophic | Permanent injury, major environmental damage |
| 8 | Major | Major injury, fire with significant loss |
| 7 | Major | Significant injury, regulatory non-compliance |
| 6 | Moderate | Lost-time injury, partial production loss |
| 5 | Moderate | Minor injury, customer dissatisfaction |
| 4 | Low | Minor inconvenience, repairable damage |
| 3 | Low | Minor defect noticed by few customers |
| 2 | Minor | No injury, noticed by alert customer only |
| 1 | Negligible | No effect, no impact on operation |

**Occurrence (O) Scale**

| Rank | Failure Rate | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | ≥ 1 in 2 | Very high — failure almost inevitable |
| 9 | 1 in 8 | Failure persistent |
| 8 | 1 in 20 | High — frequent failure |
| 7 | 1 in 80 | High |
| 6 | 1 in 400 | Moderate |
| 5 | 1 in 2,000 | Moderate |
| 4 | 1 in 15,000 | Low — occasional failure |
| 3 | 1 in 150,000 | Low |
| 2 | 1 in 1,500,000 | Remote — rare failure |
| 1 | ≤ 1 in 1,500,000 | Almost impossible |

**Detection (D) Scale**
*Note: Higher D = harder to detect (counterintuitive but standardized).*

| Rank | Detection Probability | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | Almost impossible | No known detection method |
| 9 | Very remote | Very low chance of detection |
| 8 | Remote | Low chance |
| 7 | Very low | Very low detection capability |
| 6 | Low | Low detection capability |
| 5 | Moderate | Moderate detection capability |
| 4 | Moderately high | Moderately high detection |
| 3 | High | High detection capability |
| 2 | Very high | Very high detection, alarms + auto shutdown |
| 1 | Almost certain | Detected 100% by automatic protection system |

### Action Priority (AP) — Modern AIAG-VDA (2019) Approach

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 expects awareness of the **Action Priority (AP)** method which supplements/replaces pure RPN.

| Action Priority | Color Code | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **H (High)** | Red | Must take action — top priority |
| **M (Medium)** | Yellow | Action required — moderate priority |
| **L (Low)** | Green | May take action — low priority |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

FMEA is a **mandatory deliverable** in:
- **Automotive** (per IATF 16949) — original DFMEA by AIAG
- **Aerospace** (per AS9100 and SAE ARP5580)
- **Medical devices** (per ISO 14971)
- **Chemical/process industries** (per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 Process Safety Management — *PSM*)
- **Pharmaceuticals** (per ICH Q9 Quality Risk Management)

In the **chemical process industry**, FMEA supports:
- HAZOP verification (cross-check)
- Mechanical integrity programs
- Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) — provides failure rate data
- Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) — provides initiating event frequencies
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of RPN Thresholds and Reduction Logic

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the RPN Computation Method

The RPN is a **composite risk score** that mathematically combines the three independent risk dimensions into a single comparable scalar value.

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

- **Severity (S)** represents the *magnitude* of harm. A higher S means worse consequence. To preserve proportionality, S is used linearly.
- **Occurrence (O)** represents the *frequency* of failure. A higher O means more frequent failure. Used linearly.
- **Detection (D)** represents the *inability to detect* (i.e., the *probability of escape*). A higher D means the failure escapes current controls. Used linearly.

The **multiplicative form** is chosen because:
- It captures **interaction effects** between S, O, and D (a severe AND frequent AND undetectable failure is exponentially more risky).
- It naturally penalizes failures that score high on multiple dimensions simultaneously.
- It produces a **bounded range** of $1 \le RPN \le 1000$, which is convenient for ranking.

> **Logical justification:** If a failure mode has low severity but extremely high occurrence (e.g., a minor leak occurring daily), it may still need attention. RPN captures this because the product magnifies the issue even when only one dimension is high.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of RPN Threshold for Action

KTU 2024 often asks: *"What is the RPN threshold above which action is required?"*

Industry-standard threshold derivation:

$$
RPN_{threshold} = \alpha \cdot RPN_{max}
$$

For the 1–10 scale (max = 1000), common thresholds are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{threshold, low} &= 0.10 \times 1000 = 100 \\
RPN_{threshold, medium} &= 0.20 \times 1000 = 200 \\
RPN_{threshold, high} &= 0.30 \times 1000 = 300
\end{aligned}
$$

**Default KTU-accepted industry threshold:** $RPN \geq 200$ requires action.

> [!NOTE]
> A failure mode with $S \geq 9$ (regardless of RPN) is also a mandatory action item — this is the **Severity Override Rule**.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Risk Reduction After Corrective Action

After an action is taken, RPN is recomputed:

$$
RPN_{after} = S_{after} \times O_{after} \times D_{after}
$$

**Percentage Risk Reduction (PRR):**

$$
PRR = \frac{RPN_{before} - RPN_{after}}{RPN_{before}} \times 100\%
$$

**Numerical Worked Example — Reactor Cooling System FMEA**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** Consider a jacketed CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) with cooling water supply line. A failure mode is "Cooling water flow rate falls below 30% of design value."

**Step 1 — Assign Severity:**
A cooling water failure in an exothermic reactor can cause runaway reaction → catastrophic.
$\Rightarrow S = 9$

**Step 2 — Assign Occurrence:**
Based on historical data, flow reduction occurs ~1 in 800 startups due to pump strainer blockage.
$\Rightarrow O = 6$

**Step 3 — Assign Detection:**
Currently, only a temperature indicator exists, but operator response is slow. Detection is moderate.
$\Rightarrow D = 6$

**Step 4 — Compute Initial RPN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{before} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 9 \times 6 \times 6 \\
&= 324
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Risk classification:**
Since $RPN = 324 > 200$, **immediate action is required**.

**Step 6 — Recommended Action:** Install a flow transmitter (FT) with low-low alarm and automatic ESD (Emergency Shutdown) on feed.

**Step 7 — Re-assign Detection after Action:**
With FT + ESD, detection becomes highly reliable.
$\Rightarrow D_{after} = 2$

**Step 8 — Re-assign Occurrence:**
Plant chooses to do preventive strainer cleaning weekly.
$\Rightarrow O_{after} = 4$

**Step 9 — Severity unchanged** (failure consequence remains the same):
$\Rightarrow S_{after} = 9$

**Step 10 — Compute New RPN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{after} &= 9 \times 4 \times 2 \\
&= 72
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11 — Compute Percentage Risk Reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PRR &= \frac{RPN_{before} - RPN_{after}}{RPN_{before}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{324 - 72}{324} \times 100\% \\
&= 77.78\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **77.78%** risk reduction is achieved by installing flow transmitter + ESD and adopting weekly strainer cleaning.

### 3.1.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| RPN Range | Action Decision | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $1 \le RPN \le 50$ | Accept / monitor | Low risk |
| $51 \le RPN \le 100$ | May accept, document decision | Marginal risk |
| $101 \le RPN \le 200$ | Action required (low priority) | Moderate risk |
| $201 \le RPN \le 400$ | Action required (high priority) | High risk |
| $401 \le RPN \le 1000$ | Immediate action — design review | Critical risk |

**Severity Override:** Any failure with $S \geq 9$ is treated as critical regardless of RPN.

## 3.2 FMEA Worksheet Template (Industry Standard)

The FMEA is documented in a **Worksheet** with the following columns (10-column standard):

| Col | Header | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Item / Function | Component ID and intended function |
| 2 | Failure Mode | How it could fail |
| 3 | Effect | Consequence of failure |
| 4 | Severity (S) | 1–10 ranking |
| 5 | Cause | Root cause of failure |
| 6 | Occurrence (O) | 1–10 ranking |
| 7 | Current Controls | Existing prevention/detection methods |
| 8 | Detection (D) | 1–10 ranking |
| 9 | RPN | S × O × D |
| 10 | Recommended Action | Corrective measures |

## 3.3 Critical FMEA Equations Summary

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

$$
1 \leq RPN \leq 1000
$$

$$
PRR = \frac{RPN_{before} - RPN_{after}}{RPN_{before}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\sum RPN_{system} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (S_i \times O_i \times D_i)
$$

$$
\overline{RPN} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} RPN_i
$$

Where $n$ = number of identified failure modes in the system.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level FMEA Process Architecture

The following functional flow captures the **10-step FMEA procedure** with all critical interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Scope and Boundaries] --> B[Step 2: Assemble Cross-Functional Team]
    B --> C[Step 3: Gather System Documentation]
    C --> D[Step 4: Identify Component Functions]
    D --> E[Step 5: Identify Failure Modes]
    E --> F[Step 6: Determine Failure Effects]
    F --> G[Step 7: Determine Failure Causes]
    G --> H[Step 8: Assign S, O, D Rankings]
    H --> I[Step 9: Calculate RPN = S times O times D]
    I --> J{RPN above 200 OR Severity above 9?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Step 10: Recommend Corrective Action]
    K --> L[Re-evaluate and Recalculate RPN]
    L --> M{New RPN acceptable?}
    M -->|No| K
    M -->|Yes| N[Document and Close Action]
    J -->|No| O[Document and Monitor]
    N --> P[FMEA Report Finalized]
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 FMEA Information Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Inputs: P and ID, PFD, Datasheets] --> FMEA[FMEA Analysis Engine]
    KNOWLEDGE[Team Knowledge and Past Data] --> FMEA
    FMEA --> S[Severity Score 1 to 10]
    FMEA --> O[Occurrence Score 1 to 10]
    FMEA --> D[Detection Score 1 to 10]
    S --> CALC[RPN Computation]
    O --> CALC
    D --> CALC
    CALC --> SORT[Ranking and Sorting]
    SORT --> PRIO[Prioritized Action List]
    PRIO --> ACT[Corrective Actions]
    ACT --> RECALC[Recompute RPN]
    RECALC --> REPORT[FMEA Worksheet and Report]
```

## 4.3 RPN Triadic Interaction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S_DIM[S Dimension - Severity]
        S_LOW[Low S = 1 to 3]
        S_MED[Medium S = 4 to 7]
        S_HIGH[High S = 8 to 10]
    end
    subgraph O_DIM[O Dimension - Occurrence]
        O_LOW[Low O = 1 to 3]
        O_MED[Medium O = 4 to 6]
        O_HIGH[High O = 7 to 10]
    end
    subgraph D_DIM[D Dimension - Detection]
        D_LOW[Low D = 1 to 3 = Easy Detect]
        D_MED[Medium D = 4 to 6]
        D_HIGH[High D = 7 to 10 = Hard Detect]
    end
    S_HIGH --> RPN_HIGH[High RPN Region]
    O_HIGH --> RPN_HIGH
    D_HIGH --> RPN_HIGH
    RPN_HIGH --> CRITICAL[Critical Action Required]
    S_LOW --> RPN_LOW
    O_LOW --> RPN_LOW
    D_LOW --> RPN_LOW
    RPN_LOW --> ACCEPT[Accept and Document]
```

## 4.4 FMEA — HAZOP — FTA Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PHA[Process Hazard Analysis PHA] --> FMEA[Inductive FMEA - Bottom Up]
    PHA --> HAZOP[HAZOP - Guide Word Based]
    PHA --> FTA[Deductive FTA - Top Down]
    PHA --> WHATIF[What If Analysis]
    PHA --> CHK[Checklist Analysis]
    FMEA --> LO[Link to LOPA]
    HAZOP --> LO
    FTA --> QRA[Quantitative Risk Assessment]
    LO --> QRA
    FMEA --> QRA
```

## 4.5 Plant-Level FMEA — Functional Block Diagram (CSTR Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED[Feed Tank] --> P1[Feed Pump P101]
    P1 --> CSTR[Reactor R101]
    CW[Cooling Water Header] --> CP[Cooling Water Pump P102]
    CP --> JACKET[Reactor Jacket]
    JACKET --> CSTR
    CSTR --> V1[Control Valve on Outlet]
    V1 --> PROD[Product Receiver]
    CSTR --> T1[Temperature Sensor TT101]
    T1 --> DCS[DCS Controller]
    DCS --> V1
    CW --> FT1[Flow Transmitter FT102]
    FT1 --> DCS
    DCS --> ESD[Emergency Shutdown System]
    ESD -.Interlocks.-> P1
    ESD -.Interlocks.-> V1
```

In this diagram, FMEA would be conducted for each of the labelled components: `P101`, `P102`, `R101`, `V1`, `T1`, `FT102`, `DCS`, `ESD`, and the `JACKET` — each having multiple potential failure modes that are systematically scored.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Define FMEA. List any three applications of FMEA in process industries.**

**Model Answer:**

FMEA stands for **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis**. It is a systematic, bottom-up, inductive technique used to identify potential failure modes of a component, system, or process, evaluate their effects, and prioritize them for corrective action using the **Risk Priority Number (RPN = S × O × D)**.

**Three applications in process industries:**
1. Identification of potential equipment failures in chemical reactors, heat exchangers, pumps, and valves during design phase.
2. Evaluation of process deviations in oil & gas refineries, pharmaceutical plants, and petrochemical units.
3. Support to **Process Safety Management (PSM)** audits and compliance with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119.
4. (Optional) Inputs to **LOPA** and **QRA** studies as failure frequency data.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Explain the significance of the Detection (D) ranking in FMEA. Why is a higher D value undesirable?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Detection (D) ranking** in FMEA quantifies the **difficulty of detecting a failure** before it propagates to cause harm. It is rated on a 1–10 scale where **D = 1 means almost certain detection** and **D = 10 means detection is almost impossible**.

**Significance:**
- A high D value indicates that the existing **control, alarm, inspection, or monitoring systems** are unable to catch the failure in time.
- A high D value directly increases the **RPN** (since $RPN = S \times O \times D$), making the failure a higher priority for action.
- It highlights the need for **additional safeguards** such as automatic shutdowns, redundant sensors, or enhanced inspection programs.

**Why higher D is undesirable:**
- A high D means the failure is likely to reach the customer/process/environment before being noticed.
- It increases the **risk of propagation** to severe consequences (fires, explosions, toxic releases).
- From a process safety standpoint, undetected failures are the most dangerous.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 4 Internal Choice A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA). Mention the role of the cross-functional team.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A centrifugal pump in a chemical plant has the following FMEA data:
- Severity (S) = 8
- Occurrence (O) = 5
- Detection (D) = 7

Calculate the Risk Priority Number (RPN). If a vibration monitoring system is installed which reduces Detection to 2 and preventive maintenance reduces Occurrence to 3, calculate the new RPN and the **Percentage Risk Reduction (PRR)**.

---

### Model Answer to Question A

**(a) Step-by-Step FMEA Procedure [7 Marks]**

1. **Define scope and boundaries** — Decide on system/design/process scope and limit of analysis.
2. **Assemble a cross-functional team** — A 5–8 member team comprising FMEA facilitator, design engineer, process engineer, maintenance engineer, quality engineer, operator, and safety officer. The team brings diverse expertise to comprehensively identify failure modes.
3. **Gather system documentation** — P&ID, PFD, equipment datasheets, operating procedures, control narratives, failure history.
4. **Identify component functions** — Define the intended function and functional specifications of each component.
5. **Identify potential failure modes** — The manner in which a component could fail to perform its intended function (e.g., pump fails to start, valve fails to close).
6. **Determine failure effects** — Trace the consequence of each failure mode to the next higher level and to the final system/customer.
7. **Determine failure causes** — Identify the root causes (corrosion, operator error, design error, power loss, wear, fatigue).
8. **Assign S, O, D rankings** — Use standardized 1–10 scales.
9. **Compute RPN and prioritize** — Multiply S × O × D and rank failure modes in descending order of RPN.
10. **Recommend actions and re-evaluate** — Propose corrective actions, recalculate RPN, and verify risk reduction.

**Role of cross-functional team:** Multi-disciplinary expertise ensures no failure mode is missed, severity is judged correctly from both design and operations perspective, and corrective actions are practical and implementable.

**(b) RPN Calculation and PRR [7 Marks]**

**Initial RPN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{before} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 8 \times 5 \times 7 \\
&= 280
\end{aligned}
$$

**After corrective action:** $S = 8$ (unchanged), $O_{after} = 3$, $D_{after} = 2$

**New RPN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{after} &= S \times O_{after} \times D_{after} \\
&= 8 \times 3 \times 2 \\
&= 48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage Risk Reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PRR &= \frac{RPN_{before} - RPN_{after}}{RPN_{before}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{280 - 48}{280} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{232}{280} \times 100\% \\
&= 82.86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The corrective actions (vibration monitoring + preventive maintenance) achieve an **82.86% reduction in risk** for the centrifugal pump failure mode.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 4 Internal Choice B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **FMEA** and **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)**. State the application areas where each is most suitable in the process industry.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a heat exchanger in a hydrocarbon service, the following failure modes have been identified. Complete the FMEA worksheet and rank the failure modes in order of priority.

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tube leak | 8 | 4 | 6 |
| Shell-side fouling | 5 | 7 | 3 |
| Tube rupture | 10 | 2 | 5 |
| Gasket failure | 7 | 5 | 4 |
| Pressure relief valve stuck closed | 9 | 3 | 8 |

---

### Model Answer to Question B

**(a) FMEA vs FTA [7 Marks]**

| Parameter | FMEA | FTA |
|---|---|---|
| **Logic direction** | Inductive (bottom-up) | Deductive (top-down) |
| **Starting point** | Component failure mode | Undesirable top event |
| **Output** | List of failure modes + RPN | Boolean logic tree + cut sets |
| **Quantification** | Qualitative (RPN) | Quantitative (failure probability) |
| **Complexity** | Suitable for simpler systems | Suitable for complex multi-pathway systems |
| **Best suited for** | Component-level, manufacturing | System-level, complex safety systems |
| **Symbolic representation** | Tabular worksheet | Logic gates (AND, OR) |

**Application areas:**
- **FMEA** is most suitable for design verification of pumps, valves, heat exchangers, single equipment items, and manufacturing process analysis.
- **FTA** is most suitable for analyzing complex systems with multiple initiating events, e.g., reactor runaway, BLEVE, and emergency shutdown system analysis.

**(b) RPN Calculation and Ranking [7 Marks]**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

| Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tube leak | 8 | 4 | 6 | 192 | 3rd |
| Shell-side fouling | 5 | 7 | 3 | 105 | 4th |
| Tube rupture | 10 | 2 | 5 | 100 | 5th |
| Gasket failure | 7 | 5 | 4 | 140 | (not in top 5) |
| Pressure relief valve stuck closed | 9 | 3 | 8 | **216** | 1st |

Wait — let me re-rank by computing all five:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{tube\_leak} &= 8 \times 4 \times 6 = 192 \\
RPN_{fouling} &= 5 \times 7 \times 3 = 105 \\
RPN_{tube\_rupture} &= 10 \times 2 \times 5 = 100 \\
RPN_{gasket} &= 7 \times 5 \times 4 = 140 \\
RPN_{PRV} &= 9 \times 3 \times 8 = 216
\end{aligned}
$$

**Corrected ranking (highest to lowest RPN):**

1. **Pressure relief valve stuck closed** — RPN = 216
2. **Tube leak** — RPN = 192
3. **Gasket failure** — RPN = 140
4. **Shell-side fouling** — RPN = 105
5. **Tube rupture** — RPN = 100

> **Critical observation:** Although **tube rupture** has the highest severity (S = 10), its RPN is the lowest because it is rare (O = 2) and reasonably detectable (D = 5). The **Severity Override Rule** still requires that tube rupture be addressed (since S = 10), even though RPN is low.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Confusing D = 1 with high risk:** Students mistakenly think a small D means less risky. **D = 1 means easy detection = LESS risk.** Higher D = harder detection = MORE risk.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the Severity Override Rule:** A failure with S = 9 or 10 must be addressed *regardless* of RPN value.
> 3. **Using linear scales incorrectly:** Always use the **standard 1–10 ranking tables**. Ad-hoc numbering is a frequent error.
> 4. **Confusing FMEA with HAZOP:** HAZOP uses **guide words** (No, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than) on process parameters. FMEA uses **component failure modes**.
> 5. **Computing PRR with absolute RPN difference instead of percentage:** PRR is a ratio; express as a percentage.
> 6. **Omitting the cross-functional team step:** Examiners specifically award 1 mark for emphasizing team composition.
> 7. **Forgetting to recategorize O after action:** Many students reduce D (easiest) but forget to reduce O with preventive maintenance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — FMEA**

- **FMEA = Failure Mode and Effects Analysis** — a **bottom-up, inductive** hazard identification technique.
- **Core equation:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$, with $1 \le RPN \le 1000$.
- **Three dimensions of RPN:**
  - **S (Severity)** = consequence magnitude (1–10).
  - **O (Occurrence)** = failure frequency (1–10).
